PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Macronutrients: Digestion and Absorption
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Celiac disease is an immune-mediated reaction to gluten, a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye. Exposure to gluten in the digestive track over time leads to damage of the small intestine's lining and malabsorption. The intestinal damage often causes diarrhea, fatigue, weight loss, bloating, excess gas and anemia. Human leukocyte antigen (HLA) types HLA-DQ2 and HLA-DQ8 are necessary but not sufficient to initiate celiac disease.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 18: Carbohydrate Transport, Carbohydrate Malabsorption, and Lactose Intolerance.

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch.  34: Digestion of Dietary Protein and Net Synthesis of Protein in the Body.

Moshiree B, Drossman D, Shaukat A. AGA Clinical Practice Update on Evaluation and Management of Belching, Abdominal Bloating, and Distention: Expert Review. Gastroenterology. 2023;165(3):791-800.e3. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37452811/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When you eat foods with gluten (found in wheat, bread, and pasta), your immune system attacks your small intestine by mistake. This damages the intestine lining, making it hard to absorb nutrients and causing gas, bloating, and a swollen belly.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Fructose and Galactose Metabolism
Genetic Inheritance
Inborn Errors of Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (deficiency of Aldolase B, OMIM) results in trapping of fructose-1-phosphate in the liver causing hepatomegaly and liver damage,which in turn could contribute to abdominal distention.
Classic Galactosuria (deficiency of Galactose-1-phosphate uridyltransferase, OMIM) results in accumulation of galactose-1-phosphate and galactitol in the liver causing hepatomegaly and further liver damage, which in turn could contribute to abdominal distention.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch. 12: Monosaccharide and Disaccharide Metabolism

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch.  20: Fructose and Galactose Metabolism: Hereditary Fructose Intolerance and Galactosemia

Los E, Ford GA. Galactose-1-Phosphate Uridyltransferase Deficiency. 2023 Jul 17. [Updated 2023 Jul 17]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan–. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441957/

Pinheiro FC, Sperb-Ludwig F, Schwartz IVD. Epidemiological aspects of hereditary fructose intolerance: A database study. Hum Mutat. 2021;42(12):1548-1566.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34524712/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When the body can't break down certain sugars like fructose or galactose, these sugars get trapped in the liver. This causes the liver to get bigger and damaged, which can make the belly swell up.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Glycogen Metabolism
Inborn Errors of Metabolism
Lysosomes and Peroxisomes
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Glycogen storage diseases (GSD) affecting liver enzymes metabolizing glycogen result in increased accumulation of glycogen or various metabolites of its metabolism in hepatocytes that results in hepatomegaly. In some cases, secondary storage of additional lipid and/or protein metabolites may occur (Pompe, see special notes). 

The following GSDs result in hepatomegaly: 
GSD Type I: Von Gierke disease (Glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency; OMIM)
GSD Type III: Cori disease (Debranching enzyme (4:4 transferase) deficiency; OMIM)
GSD Type IV: Andersen disease (Branching enzyme (4:6 transferase) deficiency; OMIM)
GSD Type VI: Hers disease (Liver Glycogen phosphorylase deficiency; OMIM)
GSD Type IX: Liver phosphorylase kinase deficiency (OMIM). 

The liver enlargement in severe hepatomegaly caused by GSDs is likely to result in abdominal distension, while mild hepatomegaly caused by a GSD would not.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch. 17: Phospholipid, Glycosphingolipid, and Eicosanoid Metabolism 

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 24: Glycogen Metabolism and Glycogen Storage Diseases

Massese M, Tagliaferri F, Dionisi-Vici C, Maiorana A. Glycogen storage diseases with liver involvement: a literature review of GSD type 0, IV, VI, IX and XI. Orphanet J Rare Dis. 2022;17(1):241. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35725468/
SPECIAL NOTES: GSD Type II: Pompe disease (Acid α-glucosidase (acid maltase) deficiency; OMIM) is also classified as a lysosomal storage disease. Normal byproducts of glycogen breakdown include maltose and isomaltose, which are degraded in the lysosome by acid maltase. Acid α-glucosidase deficiency leads to a build-up of glycogen in the lysosome and can lead to mild hepatomegaly, although this is secondary to the main presentation of cardiomyopathy.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The body stores energy as glycogen in the liver. When an enzyme needed to break down this stored energy is missing, glycogen piles up in the liver making it very large, which can cause the belly to swell.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Heme Proteins
Genetic Inheritance
Membrane Proteins and Lipids
Protein Structure and Folding
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Patients with hereditary spherocytosis have inherited mutations in one of five genes: spectrin alpha or beta (OMIM; OMIM), ankyrin (OMIM), Band 3 (anion exchanger AE1; OMIM), protein 4.2  (EPB42; OMIM) that encode red blood cell (RBC) cytoskeletal scaffolding or membrane proteins that are critical to maintain the stability and structure of the cell membrane and the cell shape. Mutations in these proteins lead to rigid, inflexible, spherically-shaped RBCs, which get trapped in the spleen and increase RBC hemolysis. The strain of removing the faulty RBCs leads to splenomegaly which may manifest as abdominal distention.
RESOURCES: Perrotta S, Gallagher PG, Mohandas N. Hereditary spherocytosis. Lancet. 2008;372(9647):1411-1426. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18940465/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Red blood cells become round and stiff instead of flexible. These abnormal cells get stuck in the spleen, causing it to work overtime and get bigger, which can make the belly swell.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Heme Proteins
Genetic Inheritance
Protein Structure and Folding
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Patients with sickle cell disease (OMIM) inherit Glu-to-Val point mutation at position 6 of the beta globin chain of Hb, which results in the production of HbS hemoglobin. HbS possesses an exposed hydrophobic surface that results in polymerization of Hb leading to formation of sickled erythrocytes. Resultant increased RBC turnover leads to splenomegaly presenting in the first decade of life, which can lead to abdominal distention. Later, the spleen undergoes progressive atrophy due to repeated vaso-occlusive attacks. Splenic enlargement in adults is considered rare.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 17: Hemoglobinopathies

Al-Salem AH. Splenic complications of sickle cell anemia and the role of splenectomy.  ISRN Hematol. 2011;2011:864257.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22084706/

Williams Hematology, 10e., 2021. Chapter 50: Disorders of Hemoglobin Structure: Sickle Cell Anemia and Related Abnormalities
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A genetic change makes red blood cells become curved like crescents. In children, these sickle-shaped cells get stuck in the spleen, making it enlarge and causing belly swelling. As people get older, the spleen usually shrinks.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (primarily)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Heme Proteins 
Genetic Inheritance
Protein Structure and Folding
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: The thalassemias are a group of hemoglobinopathies in which the normal ratio of alpha globin to beta globin production is disrupted due to a genetic variant in one or more alpha or beta globin genes resulting in impaired Hb structure. Hepatosplenomegaly, which may manifest as abdominal distention, results from chronic hemolysis, extramedullary hematopoiesis in the liver and spleen, and hepatic iron deposition (also from transfusions).
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 17: Hemoglobinopathies

Taher AT, Weatherall DJ, Cappellini MD. Thalassaemia. Lancet. 2018;391(10116):155-167. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28774421/ 

Gosein M, Maharaj P, Balkaransingh P, et al. Imaging features of thalassaemia. Br J Radiol. 2019;92(1095):20180658. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30412423/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A genetic problem causes an imbalance in the proteins that carry oxygen in the blood. This damages red blood cells, causing the liver and spleen to work extra hard and get bigger, which makes the belly swell.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric or Adult

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Inborn Errors of Metabolism
Macronutrients: Digestion and Absorption
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Primary lactose intolerance is caused by the lack or decreased expression of the enzyme lactase (OMIM) that catalyzes the breakdown of lactose to glucose and galactose.  Since only monosaccharides can be absorbed, the passage of undigested carbohydrate into the large intestine upon consuming lactose containing products results in water being drawn into the large intestine from the mucosa, causing osmotic diarrhea. This is reinforced by the bacterial fermentation of the remaining carbohydrate to two- and three-carbon compounds (which are also osmotically active) plus large volumes of carbon dioxide and hydrogen gas (H2), causing abdominal cramps, diarrhea, bloating that may manifest as abdominal distention.  Secondary lactose intolerance may be associated with Celiac disease, Crohn disease, following gastroenetritis or antibiotic treatment.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott  Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch 7: Introduction to Carbohydrate

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 18: Carbohydrate Transport, Carbohydrate Malabsorption, and Lactose Intolerance

Malik TF, Panuganti KK. Lactose Intolerance. [Updated 2023 Apr 17]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from:
 https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532285/

Danialifar TF, Chumpitazi BP, Mehta DI, Di Lorenzo C. Genetic and acquired sucrase-isomaltase deficiency: A clinical review. J Pediatr Gastroenterol Nutr. 2024;78(4):774-782.
 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38327254/
SPECIAL NOTES: A similar phenomenon is observed in sucrase–isomaltase deficiency (OMIM 222900), which results in the inability to digest sucrose.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Some people can't make the enzyme needed to digest the sugar in milk. When they drink milk, bacteria in their intestines eat the sugar instead, creating gas and bloating that causes stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Protein Structure and Folding
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Alpha 1 antitrypsin (AAT; OMIM), is produced in the liver, and transported via the blood to the lungs where it inhibits neutrophil elastase to prevent potential lung damage. Certain inherited variants of alpha 1 antitrypsin (S or Z) can result in pathologic polymerization of the variant AAT, resulting in intrahepatocyte accumulation of misfolded AAT molecules causing liver damage, fibrosis and hepatomegaly.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 13: Pathologic Alterations of the Extracellular Matrix that Involve Fibrillin, Elastin, or Proteoglycans

Mornex JF, Traclet J, Guillaud O, et al. Alpha1-antitrypsin deficiency: An updated review. Presse Med. 2023;52(3):104170.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37517655/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A protein made in the liver gets folded wrong and gets stuck inside liver cells instead of going into the blood. The stuck proteins damage liver cells, causing scarring and making the liver bigger.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric or Adult

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Protein Structure and Folding
Protein Turnover
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Multiple factors can lead to amyloidosis, which is characterized by the accumulation of abnormally structured serum Amyloid A (Amyloid A (AA), OMIM) parent protein and Immunoglobulin light chain (Amyloid L (AL), OMIM) that form amyloid deposits. These deposits are often derived from soluble precursors which have undergone conformational changes that lead to the adoption of a predominantly antiparallel beta-pleated sheet configuration. Deposition of these abnormal amyloids into tissues such as liver results in hepatomegaly and subsequent organ dysfunction.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 9: The Structure of Proteins and Protein Aggregates in Degenerative Diseases

Gertz MA, Lacy MQ, Dispenzieri A, Hayman SR. Amyloidosis. Best Pract Res Clin Haematol. 2005;18(4):709-27.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16026746/

Sikora J, Kmochová T, Mušálková D, et al. A mutation in the SAA1 promoter causes hereditary amyloid A amyloidosis. Kidney Int. 2022;101(2):349-359. 
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34560138/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Abnormally shaped proteins pile up in organs like the liver. These deposits make the liver bigger and stop it from working properly, which can cause belly swelling.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Alcohol Metabolism
Nutrition
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Ascites results from portal hypertension and/or hypoalbuminemia, and peritoneal diseases. The portal hypertension may be due to cirrhosis of the liver, alcoholic hepatitis and metabolic dysfunction-associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD), chronic liver diseases, acute liver failure and others. Hypoalbuminemia may be due to nephrotic syndrome, severe malnutrition or protein-losing enteropathy. Both conditions leading to fluid build up/leakage into the surrounding abdominal/interstitial tissues result in abdominal distention.
RESOURCES: Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 21e., 2022. Chapter 50: Abdominal Swelling and Ascites
SPECIAL NOTES: Some hereditary metabolic disorders characterized by hepatomegaly may also present with ascites which may also contribute to abdominal distension ( Example hereditary fructose intolerance, Glycogen storage disorder type IV, etc).
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When the liver gets severely damaged or doesn't make enough proteins, fluid leaks out and collects in the belly area, causing swelling and making the abdomen look bigger.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
Iron Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hereditary hemochromatosis is an adult onset autosomal recessive inherited disorder of iron metabolism that results from homozygosity for the C282Y variant in the homeostatic iron regulator (HFE; OMIM) gene which controls hepcidin activity. Hepcidin's normal role is to regulate iron absorption. HFE pathogenic variants lead to increased intestinal iron absorption and can also lead to total-body iron overload. Increased iron absorption results in progressive iron overload in multiple organs (heart, liver, pancreas, pituitary gland, and other endocrine organs). Since liver is the primary site for iron storage in the body, iron overload may result in hepatomegaly which may manifest as abdominal distention.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 15: Iron Metabolism: Iron-Deficiency Anemia and Iron Overload

Crownover BK, Covey CJ. Hereditary hemochromatosis. Am Fam Physician. 2013 Feb 1;87(3):183-90. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23418762/
SPECIAL NOTES: Secondary hematochromatosis could arise during frequent blood transfusions and could lead to abdominal distension.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A genetic problem causes too much iron to build up in the liver. The extra iron damages liver cells and makes the liver bigger, which can cause belly swelling.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Membrane Transport
Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Wilson disease is caused by mutations in the ATP7B gene (OMIM) encoding human ATPase copper transporting beta protein, which results in copper accumulation in hepatocytes. Since liver is the initial site of copper accumulation in patients with this disease it results in hepatomegaly which may manifest as abdominal distention.
RESOURCES:
Penning LC, Berenguer M, Czlonkowska A, et al. A Century of Progress on Wilson Disease and the Enduring Challenges of Genetics, Diagnosis, and Treatment. Biomedicines. 2023;11(2):420.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36830958/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A missing protein means copper gets trapped in liver cells instead of being removed. Too much copper damages the liver, making it bigger and causing belly swelling.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both (often presents in adolescence/young adulthood)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Cholesterol and Lipoprotein Metabolism
Genetic Inheritance
Inborn Errors of Metabolism
Lysosomes and Peroxisomes
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Gaucher disease (beta-glucosidase deficiency; OMIM) results in lysosomal accumulation of glucosylceramide and glucocerebrosides in the cells of different organs and tissues including liver and spleen resulting in hepatosplenomegaly.

Niemann-Pick disease, type A and B (Acid sphingomyelinase deficiency; OMIM and OMIM) results in lysosomal accumulation of sphingomyelin in the cells of different parenchymal tissues including liver and spleen resulting in hepatosplenomegaly. 

Cholesteryl ester storage disease (Wolman disease - Lysosomal acid lipase deficiency; OMIM) is characterized by hepatic accumulation of cholesteryl esters and triglycerides that may cause fatty liver and subsequent hepatomegaly in childhood or adulthood, depending on the severity of the mutation.
RESOURCES:
Sen Sarma M, Tripathi PR. Natural history and management of liver dysfunction in lysosomal storage disorders. World J Hepatol. 2022;14(10):1844-1861.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9627439/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A missing enzyme causes fatty substances to build up inside cells throughout the body, including the liver and spleen. This makes these organs bigger and causes the belly to swell.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Gaucher disease (beta-glucosidase deficiency; OMIM) results in lysosomal accumulation of glucosylceramide and glucocerebrosides in the cells of different organs and tissues including liver and spleen resulting in hepatosplenomegaly.

Niemann-Pick disease, type A and B (Acid sphingomyelinase deficiency; OMIM and OMIM) results in lysosomal accumulation of sphingomyelin in the cells of different parenchymal tissues including liver and spleen resulting in hepatosplenomegaly. 

Cholesteryl ester storage disease (Wolman disease - Lysosomal acid lipase deficiency; OMIM) is characterized by hepatic accumulation of cholesteryl esters and triglycerides that may cause fatty liver and subsequent hepatomegaly in childhood or adulthood, depending on the severity of the mutation.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Nutritional protein energy deficiency leads to the following:
1). Inability of hepatocytes to produce and secrete albumin and other plasma proteins resulting in edema due to the decreased oncotic pressure and leakage of fluid into interstitial tissues.
2). Inability of hepatocytes to synthesize ApoB resulting in inability to package lipids into VLDL and secrete them from the liver. This in turn results in enlarged fatty liver and hepatomegaly.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:

MPS VII – Sly Syndrome (Beta-glucuronidase deficiency; OMIM) results in lysosomal accumulation of heparan sulfate, and dermatan sulfate in different tissues including liver and spleen resulting in hepatosplenomegaly.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Cholesterol and Lipoprotein Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Deposition of cholesterol stones (due to changes in bile composition and cholesterol solubility) can lead to obstructive jaundice and defective lipid digestion in intestinal lumen. The resultant distention of the bile duct, peristaltic smooth muscle contractions as well as inflammatory response (cholecystitis) and potential for infection can all lead to abdominal pain.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 30: Metabolism of Ethanol and the Consequences of Alcohol Dependence Syndrome.

Coucke EM, Akbar H, Kahloon A, et al. Biliary Obstruction. [Updated 2022 Nov 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539698/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Hard chunks made of cholesterol can block the tubes that carry digestive juices from your liver. This blockage causes the tubes to stretch and squeeze, gets them swollen and infected, which causes stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Heme Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Conditions that cause excessive production of bilirubin (such as hemolytic anemia) result in gallbladder pigment stones comprised of Ca-Bilirubinate. Resultant distention of the bile duct, smooth muscle contractions as well as inflammatory response (cholecystitis) and potential for infection can all lead to abdominal pain.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 29: Cholesterol Metabolism and Hypercholesterolemia. 

Lammert F, Gurusamy K, Ko CW, et al. Gallstones. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2016;2:16024. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27121416/
SPECIAL NOTES: Pigment gallstones include both black pigment stones and brown pigment stones.  Black pigment stones contain calcium bilirubinate and other calcium salts, and are associated with older age, total parenteral nutrition and hemolytic diseases.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When your body breaks down too many red blood cells, it creates dark stones in your gallbladder. These stones block tubes, causing stretching, squeezing, swelling, and infections that hurt your stomach.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Heme Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Brown pigment stones are related to low-calorie, vegetable-rich diets, and are highly associated with cholangitis and cholangiohepatitis, and postcholecystectomy cholelithiasis. Brown gallstones form when the biliary tree becomes infected with colonic anaerobic microbiota producing β-glucuronidase.  β-glucuronidase hydrolyzes bis glucuronosyl bilirubin to unconjugated bilirubin. Brown pigment stones are laminated, containing unpolymerized calcium hydrogen bilirubinate  (Ca(HUCB)2) coprecipitated with amorphous calcium salts of palmitate and stearate (calcium ‘soaps’) derived from bacterial phospholipase A1 hydrolysis of biliary phosphatidylcholines. Brown gallstones also contain unconjugated (free) bile acids and Ca bile salts and variable amounts of cholesterol. Resultant distention of the bile duct, smooth muscle contractions as well as inflammatory response (cholecystitis) and potential for infection can all lead to abdominal pain.
RESOURCES: Vítek L, Carey MC. New pathophysiological concepts underlying pathogenesis of pigment gallstones. Clin Res Hepatol Gastroenterol. 2012;36(2):122-129. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3311771/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Bacteria infect the tubes connected to your gallbladder and change the digestive juices into brown stones. These stones block the tubes, causing stretching, squeezing, swelling, and pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Defects in Fibrillin-1 gene product (FBN1; OMIM), a connective tissue protein involved in elasticity of the tissue, lead to Marfan Syndrome. Patients with Marfan Syndrome report higher rates of  abdominal symptoms than control groups.  Two of the main pathophysiological mechanisms thought to lead to abdominal pain from the connective tissue defects in Marfan syndrome are aortic dissection and dural ectasia.  Aortic dissection, a sudden tearing of the layers in the aorta wall, can result from a dilatation of the aorta.  Dissection of abdominal aorta can lead to abdominal pain. Dural ectasia results from the membrane that surrounds the brain and spinal cord (dura) becomes abnormally enlarged or dilated. In some instances, spinal dural ectasia could lead to abdominal pain.
RESOURCES:
Marks’ Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e. 2023. Ch. 18: An Introduction to Human Genetics.

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 14: Heme Metabolism, Porphyrias, and Hyperbilirubinemia

MedlinePlus [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Library of Medicine (US). Marfan syndrome; [updated 2018 May 1; cited 2024 Nov 1]. Available from: https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/marfan-syndrome/   

Inayet N, Hayat JO, Kaul A, Tome M, Child A, Poullis A. Gastrointestinal Symptoms in Marfan Syndrome and Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome. Gastroenterol Res Pract. 2018;2018:4854701. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6087563/
SPECIAL NOTES: Marfan syndrome is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner and is present in the worldwide population at a frequency of approximately 1 in 5000.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: People with Marfan syndrome have weak stretchy tissue throughout their body. This can cause a major blood vessel in the belly to tear, or the covering around the spinal cord to stretch too much, both causing stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Diabetic ketoacidosis is a complication of Diabetes and can be triggered by multiple factors. In this, hyperglycemia and uncontrolled fatty acid oxidation, caused by the absence of insulin, leads to buildup of ketones.  A number of complex factors, including metabolic acidosis, can lead to electrolyte imbalance and intestinal paralysis or diabetic gastroparesis, which leads to abdominal pain.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 39: Diabetes.

Ahmed MSO, Forde H, Smith D. Diabetic gastroparesis: clinical features, diagnosis and management. Ir J Med Sci. 2023;192(4):1687-1694. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36266392/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When diabetics don't have enough insulin, their blood sugar gets too high and their body makes harmful acids called ketones. This messes up the body's chemical balance and can paralyze the stomach and intestines, causing pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Glycogen storage disease type 1B is caused due to deficient glucose 6-phosphate translocase (OMIM). GSD type Ib leads to hepatomegaly, pronounced fasting hypoglycemia with lactic acidosis, hyperlipidemia, and hyperuricemia.  Hepatomegaly is largely due to excessive storage of glycogen. In addition to metabolic issues, GSD1B patients also present with neutropenia and neutrophil dysfunction, although the mechanisms are not well understood.  As a result, some patients have frequent bacterial infections and Crohn's disease-like inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), with bloody diarrhea, abdominal pains, histological inflammation of the intestinal mucosa, and mucocutaneous manifestations such as oral and perianal ulcerations.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e., 2025, Ch 11: Glycogen Metabolism

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 24: Glycogen Metabolism and Glycogen Storage Diseases

Wicker C, Roda C, Perry A, et al. Infectious and digestive complications in glycogen storage disease type Ib: Study of a French cohort. Mol Genet Metab Rep. 2020;23:100581. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32300528/
SPECIAL NOTES:
1. Type I glycogen storage disease is an autosomal recessive genetic disorder and occurs at frequency of approximately 1 in 100,000 in the general population.

2.  The observation of abdominal pain was specific to glycogen storage disease type 1B.  Abdominal pain was NOT observed in the following liver specific GSDs: Type 1a (von Gierke), Type III (Cori, debranching), Type IV (branching), Type VI (Hers) as well as  Lysosomal Storage Disease (Gauchers, glucocerebrosidase)
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A missing protein means the body can't properly use stored sugar. This causes the liver to get too big and problems with infection-fighting cells. Some people get a disease similar to Crohn's that causes intestine inflammation, bloody diarrhea, and stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Pompe disease can be categorized as both a lysosomal storage disease and a glycogen storage disease caused by a deficiency in acid α-glucosidase (GAA; OMIM) activity that leads to buildup of glycogen in lysosomes. The most consequential risk of Pompe disease is cardiomegaly and death from heart failure.  However, individuals with milder (adult onset) forms of the disease develop gastrointestinal symptoms, including abdominal pain, later in life.  The mechanism of how the deficiency causes pain is unknown.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e., 2025. Ch 11: Glycogen Metabolism

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 24: Glycogen Metabolism and Glycogen Storage Diseases

SPECIAL NOTES:
1.  Pompe disease is an autosomal recessive genetic disorder and occurs at frequency of approximately 1 in 40,000 in the United States.

2.  Infants with the classic infantile-onset subtype present with severe hypotonia and cardiomegaly, and do not live longer than 1 year.  It is only individuals with partial deficiency that go on to develop abdominal pain later in adulthood.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A missing enzyme causes sugar to build up in cells' recycling centers. People with milder versions of this disease develop stomach problems and pain later in life, but doctors don't know exactly why.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Infantile and adult-onset forms

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: lnfants born with genetic lactase deficiency (OMIM), individuals that have non-persistence of lactase expression, or individuals with acquired lactase deficiency are unable to digest and absorb lactose in breast milk.  Lack of lactase activity leads to bloating and flatulence as bacteria digest lactose, which can lead to abdominal pain.
RESOURCES:

Catanzaro R, Sciuto M, Marotta F. Lactose intolerance: An update on its pathogenesis, diagnosis, and treatment. Nutr Res. 2021 May;89:23-34. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33887513/
SPECIAL NOTES:
1.  Non-persistence of lactase expression, in varying degrees, is extremely common in most of the world's population.

2.  Congenital lactase deficiency is rare autosomal recessive condition, and its exact incidence is unknown. It is most common in Finland, where it affects approximately 1 in 60,000 newborns.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Some people can't make the enzyme needed to digest the sugar in milk. When they drink milk, bacteria in their intestines eat the sugar instead, creating gas and bloating that causes stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Ethanol can lead to multiple pathologies such as fatty liver (steatosis) and hepatitis, which may eventually lead to cirrhosis. Ethanol metabolism can lead to cirrhosis via different mechanisms: (1) direct hepatotoxicity from metabolites (acetaldehyde) and oxidative damage;  (2) inhibition of fatty acid oxidation and inhibition of Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (G-3P DH) in the liver, due to a high NADH/NAD ratio, resulting in increased triglyceride production and  hepatic steatosis; (3) decreased VLDL secretion from hepatocytes caused by the alcohol metabolite (acetaldehyde) increasing triglyceride accumulation in liver; (4) and the resultant inflammatory response to all of the above and fibrosis.   Several of the common features of cirrhosis, including ascites,splenomegaly, and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, can potentially lead to abdominal pain.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 30: Metabolism of Ethanol and the Consequences of Alcohol Dependence Syndrome 

Mark's Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e., 2023. Ch. 33: Metabolism of Ethanol

Klinge M, Coppler T, Liebschutz JM, et al. The assessment and management of pain in cirrhosis. Curr Hepatol Rep. 2018;17(1):42-51. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7557016/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Drinking too much alcohol damages the liver in several ways: the alcohol and its breakdown products poison liver cells, cause fat buildup, and create scarring. This leads to fluid in the belly, enlarged spleen, and infections that cause pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin (α-1-AT) is made in the liver and inhibits proteases that are released by neutrophils fighting infections. The liver disease associated with A1ATD (OMIM) results from a toxic gain of function mechanism. The misfolded insoluble globular  mutant α-1-AT proteins accumulate in the endoplasmic reticulum, leading to hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis.  Common features of cirrhosis include ascites, splenomegaly, and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, which can cause abdominal pain.
RESOURCES:
Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease, 10th Ed., 2020. Ch.A13: Atlas of Liver Biopsies

Mark's Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e., 2023. Ch.1: Illustrated Glossary of Histologic and Pathologic Terms

Mitchell EL, Khan Z. Liver Disease in Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency: Current Approaches and Future Directions [published correction appears in Curr Pathobiol Rep. 2018;6(1):97. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5780543/
SPECIAL NOTES: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency is inherited in an autosomal codominant pattern. This disorder affects about 1 in 1,500 to 3,500 individuals with European ancestry.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Abnormally shaped proteins get stuck in liver cells and pile up, damaging the liver and causing scarring. This leads to fluid in the belly, enlarged spleen, and infections that cause stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Can present in childhood or adulthood)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Iron Metabolism
Reactive Oxygen Species
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: A toxic amount of iron accumulates in the liver  hereditary hemochromatosis. Hereditary hemochromatosis is most often caused by homozygosity for the C282Y mutation of HFE gene (High Fe gene = Human Homeostatic Iron Regulator Protein; OMIM), which fails to stimulate hepcidin secretion.  Without hepcidin, ferroportin survives and moves an excessive amount of absorbed iron from the intestine to the extracellular space.  In severely iron-overloaded hepatocytes, iron gives rise to excessive production of damaging reactive radicals.  A high liver iron content predisposes a person to cirrhosis and liver cancer.  Common features of cirrhosis include ascites, splenomegaly, and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, which can cause abdominal pain.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 15:  Iron Metabolism: Iron-Deficiency Anemia and Iron Overload

Barton JC, Barton JC, Patel N, McLaren GD. Abdominal pain and cirrhosis at diagnosis of hemochromatosis: Analysis of 219 referred probands with HFE p.C282Y homozygosity and a literature review. PLoS One. 2021;16(12):e0261690. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8691644/
SPECIAL NOTES:
1. Type 1 hemochromatosis, HFE deficiency, is one of the most common genetic disorders in the United States and is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.  It affects about 1 million people. It most often affects people of Northern European descent.

2.  In addition to hemochromatosis, iron in the liver can result from excessive iron in the diet, excessive infusion of iron preparations, chronic & frequent blood transfusions (especially in patients with beta-thalassemia), or ineffective erythropoiesis.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A genetic problem causes too much iron to build up in the liver. The extra iron damages liver cells like rust damages metal. This leads to liver scarring, fluid in the belly, enlarged spleen, and infections that cause pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Metabolic conditions such as obesity, type II Diabetes, etc. can lead to hepatic triglyceride accumulation (fatty liver), and under chronic conditions leads to inflammation and fibrosis, which are prequels to cirrhosis, which can lead to dull aching pain in abdomen. Additionally, common features of cirrhosis include ascites, splenomegaly, and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, which can potentially lead to abdominal pain.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 28: Triglycerides and Hypertriglyceridemia

Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 21e. 2022. Ch. 343: Nonalcoholic Fatty Liver Diseases and Nonalcoholic Steatohepatitis. 

Milić S, Lulić D, Štimac D. Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and obesity: biochemical, metabolic and clinical presentations. World J Gastroenterol. 2014;20(28):9330-9337. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4110564/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When people have conditions like obesity or diabetes, fat builds up in their liver. Over time this causes scarring. The scarred liver can cause fluid buildup in the belly, an enlarged spleen, and infections that cause stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Wilson disease is a deficiency in the P-type ATPase protein that is primarily responsible for copper homeostasis affected by the liver. This protein is encoded by the ATPase, Cu2+-transporting, beta polypeptide (ATP7B; OMIM) gene.  Deficiencies in ATP7B result in increased levels of apoceruloplasmin and a toxic build-up of copper in hepatocytes.  When the copper level in the liver overwhelms the proteins that normally bind it, it results in oxidative damage that causes chronic active hepatitis, fibrosis, and cirrhosis. Common features of cirrhosis include ascites, hepatosplenomegaly, and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, which can potentially lead to abdominal pain.
RESOURCES: Wu F, Wang J, Pu C, Qiao L, Jiang C. Wilson's disease: a comprehensive review of the molecular mechanisms. Int J Mol Sci. 2015;16(3):6419-6431. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4394540/
SPECIAL NOTES: Wilson disease affects approximately 1 in 30,000 individuals and is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A genetic problem means the liver can't get rid of copper properly. Too much copper builds up and damages liver cells, causing scarring. This leads to fluid in the belly, enlarged liver and spleen, and infections that cause stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both (often presents in adolescence/young adulthood)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Fabry disease is a lysosomal storage disease, defined by a deficiency of alpha-galactosidase (OMIM), leads to the accumulation of a glycolipid, globotriaosylceramide (GL3), and its metabolites within the lysosomes of many types of cells, including endothelial, cardiac and neuronal.  Neuronal and vascular dysfunction caused by GL3 accumulation can lead to ischemia and inflammation, and impact multiple systems. Impacts on the autonomic nervous system (ANS) and GI tract can lead to abdominal pain.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry (Ferrier, 7th Ed), Ch 17 Phospholipid, Glycosphingolipid, and Eicosanoid Metabolism

Zar-Kessler C, Karaa A, Sims KB, Clarke V, Kuo B. Understanding the gastrointestinal manifestations of Fabry disease: promoting prompt diagnosis. Therap Adv Gastroenterol. 2016;9(4):626-634. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27366228/
SPECIAL NOTES:
1.  GI symptoms are some of the most frequent and early general presentations among both adults and children with Fabry disease.

2.  Fabry is an X-linked recessive disorder that affects an estimated 1 in 1,000 to 9,000 people. Milder, late-onset forms of the disorder are probably more common than the classic, severe form.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A missing enzyme causes fatty substances to pile up inside many types of cells, including nerve and blood vessel cells. This damages nerves and blood vessels in the digestive system, causing stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Niemann-Pick type C (NPC; OMIM) is a lysosomal storage disease (LSD) that is characterized by intracellular accumulation of unesterified cholesterol in the lysosomes and late endosomes. The disease is caused by deficiency in the NPC1 and NPC2 proteins, which are involved in transporting cholesterol out of the lysosome into cellular membranes including ER, Golgi, and the plasma membrane.  It is thought that unbalanced lipid homeostasis in NPC can be associated with digestive and subsequent absorptive malfunction in the intestine due to impaired trafficking and reduced lipid raft association of sucrase-isomaltase and other disaccharidases.  In addition, splenomegaly is a common feature of NPC.  Splenomegaly can result in abdominal pain by encroachment of the enlarged spleen on the stomach.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch. 17: Phospholipid, Glycosphingolipid, and Eicosanoid Metabolism

SPECIAL NOTES: Niemann-Pick disease type C is an autosomal recessive condition that affects approximately 1 in 150,000 individuals. The disease occurs more frequently in people of French-Acadian descent in Nova Scotia.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Missing proteins mean cholesterol gets trapped inside cells instead of being moved where it needs to go. This messes up how the intestines digest food and causes the spleen to get too big, both causing stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric (pediatric more common)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Acute pancreatitis involves premature activation of pancreatic digestive enzymes, including the activation of trypsinogen to trypsin which activates other proteases. The activated digestive enzymes cause pancreatic cellular injury and death, as well as inflammatory responses. 70-80% of acute pancreatitis are triggered by alcohol and gallstones. Accumulation of triglycerides in the blood can cause decreased blood flow to abdominal organs can also lead to pancreatitis.  The resulting acute inflammation of the pancreas can lead to neuropathic, inflammatory and visceral pain in the abdomen.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Digestive enzymes in the pancreas turn on too early and start digesting the pancreas itself instead of food. This is usually caused by alcohol or gallstones. The damaged, inflamed pancreas causes severe stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Chronic pancreatitis is associated with irreversible and permanent fibrosis in the pancreas due to activation of stellate cells after multiple episodes of acute inflammation.  Alcohol and tobacco use are among the most dominant causes of chronic pancreatitis. Hypertriglyceridemia causes acute pancreatitis, but commonly leads to chronic pancreatitis.  The abdominal pain is largely due to damage to pancreatic nociceptive nerves and complex neuroimmune interactions driven by chronic inflammation.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: After repeated episodes of pancreas inflammation (usually from alcohol or smoking), permanent scarring develops. The constant inflammation damages pain nerves in the pancreas, causing ongoing stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

The accumulation of delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) is known to be associated with the symptoms of acute intermittent porphyria (AIP).  In AIP, the pain is either neuropathic in origin or caused by visceral ischemia, which may lead to obstruction and abdominal pain during acute phase.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: During a sickle cell crisis, which is triggered by multiple factors, the polymerization of HbS (OMIM) and the sickling of cells leads to vaso-occlusive blockages. Vaso-occlusive crises including splenic ischemia are common in sickle cell disease.  Intestinal segments at the end of their respective arterial supplies are particularly susceptible to ischemia, including the splenic flexure where the superior and inferior mesenteric arterial circulations terminate. Resultant ischemia in these intestinal regions leads to abdominal pain.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Abnormally shaped red blood cells get stuck together and block blood vessels. This cuts off blood supply to the spleen and intestines, causing stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric (pediatric more common)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Lead inhibits delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) dehydratase, which specifically leads to the accumulation of ALA.  ALA is known to be associated with the symptoms of lead poisoning. By inhibition of ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase, lead also leads to a decrease in heme biosynthesis and anemia.  Though the mechanism of abdominal pain in lead poisoning is unknown it appears to be either neuropathic in origin or caused by visceral ischemia, which may lead to obstruction and pain during acute phase of poisoning.
RESOURCES:
Harrison’s Principles of Internal Medicine 19th Ed., 2022. Ch. 15: Abdominal Pain

SPECIAL NOTES:
1.  Chronic lead poisoning (CLP) is a rare cause of abdominal pain and is more common in young children, in whom the incidence is higher than it is in adults. As the symptoms of CLP are nonspecific, misdiagnoses or missed diagnoses often occur, especially in sporadic cases.

2. Other major targets of lead toxicity are blood and bone marrow resulting in microcytic, hypochromic anemia because lead impairs incorporation of iron during heme synthesis. Blood cells are further more fragile, leading to increased hemolysis and elevated free protoporphyrin levels.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Lead poison stops the body from making healthy blood and causes toxic chemicals to build up. This damages nerves or reduces blood flow to intestines, causing blockages and stomach pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric (pediatric more common historically)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar syndrome (HHS) - a potentially life-threatening complication of uncontrolled diabetes mellitus type 2 which may be characterized by marked hyperglycemia, dehydration, electrolyte derangements, and hyperosmolality and reduced mental alertness in the absence of significant ketoacidosis.  Triggers include infection, insulin deficiency, severe dehdration, inflammatory conditions, renal failure, and certain drugs. Relative insulin deficiency (still enough to inhibit ketogenesis but not enough to inhibit hepatic gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis or to promote peripheral glucose uptake) leads to hyperglycemia.   Underlying illness causes increased counterregulatory hormones which can lead to extreme hyperglycemia, causing osmotic diuresis.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Acromegaly is a condition that results from increased levels of growth hormone levels. Growth hormone affects glucose metabolism by increasing gluconeogenesis, glycogenolysis, and lipolysis. It also induces the expression of IGF2, which normally counterbalances those effects by promoting glucose uptake, however, in the increased levels in acromegaly lead to insulin resistance both in the liver and periphery. Together these lead to hyperglycemia.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Too much growth hormone makes the liver produce extra sugar and causes cells to ignore insulin's signals. This means sugar builds up in the blood instead of being used by cells.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Angelman Syndrome most commonly results from microdeletion of region 15q11-13 of the maternally-derived chromosome 15 that includes deletion of the Ubiquitin-Protein Ligase E3A; UBE3A (UBE3A) gene (OMIM). 2. Some individuals with Angelman syndrome may have seizures, others may not. An early finding in most children with Angelman syndrome is a movement or balance abnormality that includes jerky movements due to an inability to coordinate voluntary movements.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A piece of chromosome 15 is missing, which affects a gene that helps control connections between brain cells. This imbalance can cause abnormal electrical activity in the brain, leading to seizures.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Uncontrolled diabetes mellitus (DM) can trigger ketoacidosis. While ketoacidosis is more common in individuals with Type 1 DM, it may occur in individuals with type 2 DM. The electrolyte imbalance associated with ketoacidosis, particularly hypokalemia, dehydration and development of cerebral edema could lead to seizures in DM. Rapid changes in the blood glucose levels including rapid correction of blood glucose levels during ketoacidosis can also trigger seizures. The Kussmaul breathing associated with severe diabetic ketoacidosis leads to hypocapnia (reduced CO₂ in the blood).  It has been proposed that hypocapnia leads to cerebral vasoconstriction, decreased cerebral blood flow, and seizure.
RESOURCES:

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Guanidinoacetate methyltransferase (GAMT; OMIM) is required for the synthesis of creatine from glycine, arginine, and methionine. GAMT catalyze the transfer a methyl group from S-adenosyl methionine to the glycine breakdown product guanidinoacetate (GAA) to form creatine. Thus, the absence of GAMT results in a deficiency of creatine.  Creatine-phosphate is an important energy source and its deficiency has particularly detrimental effects on the brain, resulting in intellectual disability, as well as seizures, autism, and speech delay.
RESOURCES:
Q. Sun, Chapter 14 - Cerebral creatine deficiency syndromes, Editor(s): Uttam Garg, Laurie D. Smith, In Clinical Aspects and Laboratory Determination, Biomarkers in Inborn Errors of Metabolism, Elsevier, 2017,Pages 331-341.

SPECIAL NOTES: GAMT deficiency is an autosomal recessive condition and has an estimated prevalence from 1 out of 250,000 to 1 out of 550,000.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A missing enzyme means the body can't make creatine, an important energy source for the brain. Without enough energy, brain cells don't work properly, leading to seizures and other brain problems.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Neonates with severe hyperammonemia may have seizures, some without overt clinical manifestations. Hyperammonemia in these individuals may be triggered by multiple secondary factors. Ammonia can cause brain damage through a variety of proposed mechanisms, a major component of which is cerebral edema through increased glutamine. Increased ammonia causes increased conversion of glutamate by shifting the equilibrium of the reaction catalyzed by glutamine synthase. Neurotoxicity, and seizures, specifically result from the increased glutamine synthesis causing swelling of astrocytes and brain edema.  By definition, all urea cycle disorders (the most common being ornithine transcarbamoylase deficiency), can result in hyperammonemia.
RESOURCES:

SPECIAL NOTES:
1.  The incidence of urea cycle disorders is estimated to be at least 1:35,000 births; partial defects may make the number much higher.

2.  Seizures in neonates with hyperammonemia can be as high as 50%.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When the body can't get rid of ammonia properly, it builds up in the blood. High ammonia levels cause brain cells to swell by making too much of a substance called glutamine, which leads to seizures.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hypoglycemia can result from a variety of physiological, pathophysiological, and iatrogenic conditions. It has been proposed that the ATP deficiency impacts ATP dependent ion channels leading to intracellular disturbance of sodium, potassium, and calcium, causing abnormal neuronal membrane action potentials and seizure.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: In Niemann-Pick Type A (OMIM), a sphingomyelinase enzyme deficiency results in the abnormal accumulation sphingomyelin in different tissues. In Niemann-Pick type C (NPC), the disease is caused by pathogenic variants in the NPC1 (OMIM) and NPC2 (OMIM) genes, which impair the movement of lipids (e.g.sphingomyelin and cholesterol) out of cells, leading to their accumulation within the cells.  Sphingomyelin is a fatty substance that is a component of most cell membranes. The abnormal accumulation of sphingomyelin within lysosomes, mainly in macrophages, leads to the deposition of lipid-laden macrophages in the brain causing neurologic symptoms such as seizures.

Bajwa H, Azhar W. Niemann-Pick Disease. [Updated 2023 Mar 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Sep 1.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Pathogenic variants in the HEXA gene cause Tay-Sachs (OMIM) disease.  HEXA encodes the beta-hexosaminidase A enzyme, which helps break down GM2 ganglioside within lysosomes. MECHANISM EXPLANATION: A genetic deficiency in the branched chain ketoacid dehydrogenase complex causes maple syrup disease.  Pathogenic variants in the genes encoding the subunits of the BCKAD complex (OMIM) prevent the normal breakdown of leucine, isoleucine, and valine. As a result, these amino acids and their byproducts build up in the brain. Because high levels of these substances, specifically leucine and α-ketoisocaproic acid, are neurotoxic, their accumulation can lead to seizures.  Leucine competes with nine other amino acids for entry into the brain via the facilitative SLC7A5 transporter and can lead to cerebral edema.  Increased α-ketoisocaproic acid depletes cells of glutamate by inhibiting the branched chain amino acid transaminase.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Variants  in at least 12 genes have been found to cause Zellweger spectrum disorder (OMIM search). These genes encode for peroxins, which are essential for the formation and normal functioning of peroxisomes.  If the production of peroxisomes is altered, these structures cannot perform their usual functions, which includes production of fats (lipids) used in the nervous system. Seizures (and other neurological issues) are caused by reduced myelin, a covering that protects nerves and promotes efficient transmission of nerve impulses.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a metabolic disorder, characterized by accumulation of phenylalanine (Phe) due to phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH; OMIM) deficiency or tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) deficiency. If untreated, PKU can present with symptoms that include growth failure, intellectual impairment and seizures. PKU patients may experience different types of seizures, including generalized ones.  The mechanisms linking Phe levels and seizures are unclear, but the high Phe concentrations are associated with a decreased brain levels of norepinephrine since phenylalanine inhibits tyrosine hydroxylase and the synthesis of norepinephrine from tyrosine.  In non-classical PKU a BH4 deficiency additionally inhibits the synthesis of the neurotransmitter serotonin.
RESOURCES:
Guida M, Pesaresi I, Fabbri S, Sartucci F, Cosottini M, Giorgi FS. Epilepsy and phenylketonuria: a case description and EEG-fMRI findings. Funct Neurol. 2014;29(1):75-79.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Isoniazid is a drug used to treat patients with TB.  Acute isoniazid toxicity can manifest as seizures, and the important mechanism is a deficiency of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), which is an inhibitory neurotransmitter. Isoniazid induces a vitamin B6 deficiency by forming  an inactive derivative with pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP), the active form of vitamin B6.  The pyridoxine deficiency induced by the use of isoniazid leads to reduced production of GABA, as it is usually a product of pyridoxine-dependent decarboxylation reaction.

Seizures are thought to originate from reduced production of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), as it is usually a product of pyridoxine-dependent decarboxylation reaction.  The common aspect in all these conditions is that the seizures can be treated with large doses of Vitamin B6.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Short stature: A point mutation in the gene coding for the transmembrane portion of FGFR3 (fibroblast growth factor receptor 3; OMIM), which resides on the short arm of chromosome 4, results in achondroplasia. The genetic mutation of FGFR3 (p.Gly380Arg) results in a gain-of-function, constitutive activation of the receptor protein, and a significant decrease in endochondral bone formation via increased inhibition of chondrocyte proliferation and differentiation leading to phenotypic features.

It is an autosomal dominant genetic condition. This gene limits the conversion of cartilage to bone, particularly in the long bones. In achondroplasia, this gene becomes overactive and bone formation is severely impacted in the extremities.
RESOURCES:
Jorde LB, Carey JC, Bamshad MJ, Medical Genetics, 6e. 2020. Ch. 10: Genetic Basis of Development. 

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 12: Collagen, Collagenopathies, and Diseases of Mineralization

McDonald EJ, De Jesus O. Achondroplasia. [Updated 2023 May 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559263/
SPECIAL NOTES: Another cause of disproprtionate (short-limb) short stature is diastrophic dysplasia.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Achondroplasia causes short stature. In your body, a protein called FGFR3 normally helps control how cartilage turns into bone, especially in the long bones of your arms and legs. In achondroplasia, a small change (mutation) in the gene that makes FGFR3 causes this protein to be stuck in the "on" position all the time. When FGFR3 is overactive like this, it puts the brakes on the cells that build bone, so the long bones of the arms and legs cannot grow properly. This condition is passed down in families and only one copy of the changed gene is needed to cause it.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Tall stature can be seen in children with the rare kyphoscoliotic EDS, which is due to mutant lysyl hydroxylase, a critical enzyme involved in posttranslational modification of collagen. The resulting impaired cross linking, especially in the long bones, leads to tall stature. 

Patients with the rare spondylodysplastic type of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome often present with short stature due to skeletal abnormalities such as curved spine and limbs, contractures, etc.  This type of EDS is due to a problem with glycosaminoglycan synthesis.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 12: Collagen, Collagenopathies, and Diseases of Mineralization

https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/ehlers-danlos-syndrome/

https://rarediseases.info.nih.gov/diseases/6322/ehlers-danlos-syndrome

Agrawal P, Kaur H, Kondekar A, Rathi S. A case of Ehlers-Danlos syndrome presenting as short stature: a novel mutation in SLC39A13 causing spondylodysplastic Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. Oxf Med Case Reports. 2023;2023(1):omac107.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36727144/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (EDS) can cause either tall or short stature, depending on the type. In a rare type of EDS called kyphoscoliotic EDS, a faulty enzyme called lysyl hydroxylase fails to properly finish building collagen — the protein that gives your body structure and support. Without proper collagen, the long bones of the body are not held together well, which can lead to tall stature. In another rare type called spondylodysplastic EDS, the body cannot properly make important building materials called glycosaminoglycans. Without these, the skeleton develops abnormally — including a curved spine and curved limbs — which can lead to short stature.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Tall stature: Loeys–Dietz syndrome is a congenital disorder with an abnormal increase of collagen and extracellular matrix caused by an activated TGF-β signal pathway. 
It is caused by mutations in at least six genes (OMIM search) that are all known to influence the activity of TGF-β (TGFBR1, TGFBR2, SMAD3, TGFB2 or TGFB3)
It affects the connective tissue in many parts of the body, especially skeleton, heart, blood vessels, and skin, and causes Marfanoid features (tall stature).
The genes in this pathway are important for gene expression, cell growth, differentiation and controlled cell death (apoptosis). Notably, the gene SMAD3 (OMIM) has been associated with a condition known as aneurysm-osteoarthritis syndrome (AOS).

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Loeys-Dietz syndrome causes tall stature. This condition is caused by mutations in genes that control a signaling pathway involving a protein called TGF-β. This pathway normally tells cells when to grow, specialize, or die. When one of these genes is mutated, the TGF-β signaling is overactive, leading to an abnormal buildup of collagen and other structural materials throughout the body. This affects the skeleton, heart, blood vessels, and skin, and causes a body shape similar to Marfan syndrome, including tall stature.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Tall stature: Defects in Fibrillin-1 (FBN1 gene; OMIM) a connective tissue protein involved in elasticity, lead to reduced resistance from connective tissue such that invidivuals show a tendency to be tall and slender, have elongated fingers and toes (arachnodactyly), have loose joints, and have an arm span that exceeds their body height.
 
Fibrillin-1 can bind to Latent Transforming Growth Factor-β Binding Protein (LTBP-1). Via LTBP-1, fibrillin can control the bioavailability of TGF-β. Defects in TGF-β signalling impact collagen synthesis and development of smooth muscle in vessel walls. Altered TGFβ signaling is a major contributor to the pathology of Marfan syndrome (MFS) and related diseases.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Marfan syndrome causes tall stature. It is caused by a faulty version of a protein called Fibrillin-1, which is part of the stretchy, supportive framework of your body's connective tissue. When Fibrillin-1 does not work properly, the connective tissue becomes too loose. This looseness means there is less resistance holding the body compact, so people with Marfan syndrome tend to be tall and slender, have very long fingers and toes, have loose joints, and have an arm span wider than their height. Fibrillin-1 also normally controls the activity of another important protein called TGF-β, which affects how collagen is made and how blood vessel walls develop. When Fibrillin-1 is faulty, TGF-β signaling goes wrong, making the problems in Marfan syndrome even worse.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Short stature: Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI) is a rare disease affecting the connective tissue and is characterized by extremely fragile bones that break or fracture easily (brittle bones). The abnormal growth of bones is often referred to as a bone dysplasia. Four main types of OI (the collagen types) have been identified based on clinical features and severity. These types account for 85-90 percent of OI cases and are caused by mutations in the COL1A1 (OMIM) or COL1A2 (OMIM) genes. These genes code for type I collagen, the most abundant collagen in the human body. 

The etiology for short stature in OI is multifactorial. Scoliosis, kyphosis, vertebral fractures, recurrent long bone fractures, and bone deformities are important contributing factors. The incidence of scoliosis and the rate of progression of curvature are significantly higher in the more severe forms of OI.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Osteogenesis Imperfecta (OI) causes short stature. OI is a rare condition where the bones are extremely fragile and break or fracture very easily, which is why it is sometimes called "brittle bone disease." It is caused by mutations in genes called COL1A1 or COL1A2, which are instructions for making Type I collagen — the most common structural protein in the human body. Without normal collagen, bones cannot form properly. Short stature in OI happens for several reasons together: fractures of the spine and long bones, abnormal curving of the spine (scoliosis or kyphosis), and other bone deformities all prevent normal growth. The more severe the form of OI, the greater the impact on height.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Short stature: Defective CFTR (cystic fibrosis transmembrane regulator; OMIM) is an ATP-Binding Casette Cl-Transport protein located on epithelial cells of the lungs, pancreas, intestines, and skin. Dysfunction of exocrine glands leads to high Na+ and Cl- in sweat and to highly viscous mucus due to dehydration. In the case of inadequate nutritional management, patients exhibit failure to thrive due to malabsorption and malnutrition, chronic inflammation, decreased  chondrocyte proliferation and differentiation and increased appoptosis due to growth hormone-insulin-like growth factor 1 (GH-IGF1) axis disruption and recurrent respiratory infections due to P. aeruginosa (which is often the cause of death). All these factors together can lead to short stature.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Cystic fibrosis (CF) can cause short stature. CF is caused by a faulty protein called CFTR, which normally acts as a doorway for salt (chloride) to move in and out of cells that line the lungs, pancreas, intestines, and skin. When CFTR does not work, mucus in the body becomes very thick and sticky because of dehydration, and the body cannot absorb nutrients properly. Without enough nutrients, the body cannot grow well. On top of that, chronic inflammation and repeated lung infections — often caused by a bacteria called P. aeruginosa — and disruption of the growth hormone–IGF-1 system all work together to prevent normal growth, leading to short stature.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Short stature: Mutations in the  adrenal 21-hydroxylase (CYP21A2; OMIM) gene or 11-beta hydroxylase (CYP11B1; OMIM) cause deficiency of these enzymes in the adrenal glands, leading to reduced synthesis of cortisol and aldosterone. Precursors build up and are converted into androgens.  Cortisol is the main feedback inhibitor of adrenal activity.  Thus, the lack of cortisol leads to hyperactive adrenal glands, which includes increased androgen production.  Short stature is a frequent complication of these forms of CAH; excess androgens can lead to precocious puberty, leading to early epiphyseal fusion and short stature.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) causes short stature. Normally, the adrenal glands make a hormone called cortisol, which acts as a brake on the adrenal glands' activity. In CAH, mutations in enzymes called 21-hydroxylase or 11-beta hydroxylase prevent the adrenal glands from making cortisol and another hormone called aldosterone. Without cortisol to act as that brake, the adrenal glands go into overdrive and produce too many male sex hormones called androgens. Too many androgens cause the body to go through puberty too early. When puberty happens too early, the growth plates in the bones close sooner than they should, stopping growth before the child reaches full height, leading to short stature.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Short stature: Pituitary dwarfism is due to GH deficiency.  Growth hormone deficiency can result from multiple causes and is a frequent cause of dwarfism. The hypothalamic-pituitary axis maintains the levels of growth hormones in the body, which then directly or indirectly, through insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1), stimulate bone elongation, and growth of soft tissue and cartilage. Lower levels of IGF-1 are linked with short stature.
Laron dwarfism is due to GH receptor deficiency (OMIM).  Laron dwarfism is characterized by an inadequate secretion of IGF-1 in response to GH.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Growth hormone (GH) deficiency causes short stature. The brain has a control center called the hypothalamic-pituitary axis that manages the amount of growth hormone in the body. Growth hormone works directly, and also indirectly through a helper molecule called IGF-1, to help bones grow longer, and to support the growth of soft tissue and cartilage. When there is not enough growth hormone, IGF-1 levels fall, and the body cannot grow normally, leading to short stature (also called pituitary dwarfism). In a related condition called Laron dwarfism, the body makes growth hormone but has a faulty growth hormone receptor, so the body cannot respond to it properly — meaning IGF-1 is not made in adequate amounts, and short stature results.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Short stature: Thyroid hormones, along with insulin, growth hormone, glucocorticoids, insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) and other hormones, regulate body protein metabolism and are closely linked to the processes involved in growth and development. Almost all chronic thyroid diseases of childhood impair growth and development. Every disease may have a unique mechanism to halt linear growth, but reduced serum concentration or diminished local availability of thyroid hormones seems to be a common pathway.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Hypothyroidism causes short stature. Thyroid hormones work alongside other important hormones — including insulin, growth hormone, glucocorticoids, and IGF-1 — to control how the body uses protein and how it grows and develops. When thyroid hormone levels are too low, or when thyroid hormone cannot reach the places in the body where it is needed, growth slows down. Nearly all long-term thyroid diseases in childhood can impair growth, and while each type of thyroid disease may affect growth in a slightly different way, low thyroid hormone is the shared factor that slows normal growth and development.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Short stature: Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder is a consequence of maternal alcohol use during pregnancy.  The mechanism of intrauterine alcohol damage is not established.  Key features are short stature, craniofacial dysmorphism, plus varying degrees of other developmental, cognitive, and behavioral impairments, along with a history of alcohol abuse by the mother during pregnancy.
Ethanol, acting by a dual mechanism [blockade of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) glutamate receptors and excessive activation of GABA, receptors], triggers widespread apoptotic neurodegeneration in the developing rat forebrain. Vulnerability coincides with the period of synaptogenesis, which in humans extends from the sixth month of gestation to several years after birth. During this period, transient ethanol exposure can delete millions of neurons from the developing brain. This can explain the reduced brain mass and neurobehavioral disturbances associated with human fetal alcohol spectrum disorder.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD) causes short stature. FASD happens when a baby is exposed to alcohol before birth because the mother drank alcohol during pregnancy. Alcohol harms the developing baby by blocking certain brain receptors (called NMDA receptors) and over-activating others (called GABA receptors). This triggers widespread destruction of brain cells during a critical window of brain development, which in humans spans from the sixth month of pregnancy to several years after birth. This loss of millions of brain cells reduces brain size and causes a range of behavioral and thinking problems. Along with short stature and characteristic facial features, the damage to the developing brain is a core feature of FASD.

Erythropoiesis is impaired by decreased hemoglobin production and by toxic homotetramers that impair red blood cell production and survival (OMIM). The pathogenesis of growth failure is multifactorial and is mainly due to chronic anemia and hypoxia, chronic liver disease, zinc and folic acid deficiency, iron overload, intensive use of chelating agents, emotional factors, and endocrinopathies (hypogonadism, delayed puberty, hypothyroidism, and GH-IGF-1 axis dysregulation).

This is because of mutations that lead to low production of the building blocks (called globin chains) of hemoglobin. The result is chronic anemia (not enough healthy red blood cells), which means the body's tissues do not get enough oxygen. Short stature in thalassemia happens for many reasons working together: chronic anemia and low oxygen, liver disease, deficiencies of zinc and folic acid, iron buildup in organs, and problems with several hormone systems including the growth hormone–IGF-1 axis, the thyroid, and the reproductive hormones.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Tall stature: Classic homocystinuria is due to cystathionine beta synthase (CBS; OMIM) deficiency, associated with recessively inherited mutations of the CBS gene on chromosome 21q22.3 and involves four major organ systems: eye, skeleton, central nervous system, and vascular system. Accumulation of homocysteine disrupts disulfide bond formation on Cys of many proteins (aka thiolation). Examples of proteins that get thiolated include lysyl oxidase, which is required for maturation of collagen and elastin.  Defects in these proteins can lead to connective tissue defects, impacting arteries, bones, and skin, and resulting in Marfanoid outcomes.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Homocystinuria causes tall stature. Classic homocystinuria is caused by a deficiency of an enzyme called cystathionine beta synthase (CBS), leading to a buildup of a molecule called homocysteine in the body. High levels of homocysteine interfere with the normal building of many proteins by disrupting their chemical structure through a process called thiolation. One important protein affected is lysyl oxidase, which is needed to finish building collagen and elastin — the stretchy structural proteins that support the body's arteries, bones, and skin. MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Short stature: Kallmann syndrome arises from failed migration of GnRH-producing neuronal cells and olfactory nerves from the nasal placodes to the hypothalamus.  Unusual cases of Kallmann syndrome presenting with short stature are reported but the exact pathophysiology remains to be explored. A possible mechanism inlcudes hypoplastic olfactory bulbs associated with abnormalities of the pituitary gland that in turn might be associated with growth hormone deficiency. Evaluation of other hormone deficiencies in cases of short stature prior to making a definitive Kallmann syndrome diagnosis is warranted especially considering short stature being attributed to constitutional delay of growth.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Kallmann syndrome can sometimes cause short stature. Normally, special nerve cells that make a hormone called GnRH travel from the nose area to a part of the brain called the hypothalamus during fetal development. In Kallmann syndrome, this migration fails. In some unusual cases, this failed migration is also associated with abnormalities of a nearby brain structure called the pituitary gland, which may then fail to produce enough growth hormone. Because growth hormone is essential for normal height, a shortage of it can lead to short stature. Short stature in Kallmann syndrome may also sometimes be related to a general delay in growth and puberty rather than a direct hormone deficiency.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Tall stature: Klinefelter syndrome is the most common sex aneuploidy with most affected individuals having an extra X chromosome (47,XXY). Classic features include tall stature, sparse hair, gynecomastia, and hypogonadism, as well as neurodevelopmental or psychological abnormalities. Although extensively studied, the link between the extra X and the phenotype largely remains unexplained. One possible mechanism includes excessive expression of growth-related genes by the SHOX transcription factor (OMIM) that is acting on embryonic development to control the formation of many body structures and the skeleton, along with its important role in maturation of bones in arms and legs. Normally one copy of SHOX is located on each of the sex chromosomes. As a result, both females and males have two functional copies of the SHOX gene in each cell. Since individuals with a 47,XXY karyotype carry three copies, Klinefelter syndrome can be considered an example of a gene-doseage effect of the pseudoautosomal gene SHOX.

Tüttelmann F, Gromoll J. Novel genetic aspects of Klinefelter's syndrome. Mol Hum Reprod. 2010;16(6):386-395.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Klinefelter syndrome causes tall stature. Klinefelter syndrome occurs when a male is born with an extra X chromosome (47,XXY instead of the usual 46,XY). One possible reason for tall stature in Klinefelter syndrome involves a gene called SHOX, which is found on the sex chromosomes and acts as a master switch that controls how the skeleton and bones develop — especially the bones in the arms and legs. Normally, both males and females have two working copies of SHOX. Because individuals with Klinefelter syndrome have three sex chromosomes, they carry three copies of SHOX. Having an extra copy of SHOX means it is more active than normal, and this extra activity drives increased bone growth, contributing to tall stature.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric
