MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Short stature: A low height-for-age is known as stunting. It may be the result of chronic or recurrent undernutrition, usually associated with poor socioeconomic conditions, poor maternal health and nutrition, frequent illness, and/or inappropriate infant and young child feeding and care in early life. Stunting holds children back from reaching their physical and cognitive potential.

Protein-energy malnutrition can present as Marasmus or Kwashiorkor, both of which can lead to stunting.  Marasmus occurs due to a global deficiency of calories and protein. As a result, the body draws on its own stores, resulting in emaciation and muscle wasting.  Kwashiorkor is primarily due to protein deficiency, which leads to decreased synthesis of visceral proteins. The resulting hypoalbuminemia contributes to extravascular fluid accumulation. Impaired synthesis of B-lipoprotein results in a fatty liver.  In case of Kwashiorkor, the weight loss is not always evident (arm/thigh circumference is a measure of weight loss instead of BMI). Clinically, a combination of these two can occur and is referred to as marasmic kwashiorkor.
RESOURCES:
Malnutrition. World Health Organization. Accessed November 18, 2025 –
https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/malnutrition

Atassi H.  Protein-Energy Malnutrition. Medscape. Published Sep 15, 2025. Accessed Nov 24, 2025.
https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1104623-overview
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Kwashiorkor causes short stature (called stunting). Kwashiorkor is mainly caused by not getting enough protein in the diet. Without enough protein, the body cannot make the important proteins it needs to function, including visceral proteins. Low levels of a protein called albumin cause fluid to leak out of the blood vessels and collect in the body's tissues, causing swelling. The liver also fills up with fat because it cannot make the proteins needed to transport fat away. Because weight loss is hidden by the fluid buildup, arm or thigh circumference is used instead of BMI to measure malnutrition. When a child does not get enough nutrition over a long period of time — especially in early life — growth slows down significantly, and the child does not reach a normal height for their age.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

Because the body is not getting enough fuel from food, it starts breaking down its own stored fat and muscle to survive, resulting in severe weight loss, a very thin appearance, and muscle wasting. When a child does not get enough nutrition over a long period of time — especially in early life — growth slows down significantly, and the child does not reach a normal height for their age.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Short stature: Among the mitochondrial diseases that arise from mutant mtDNA, KSS and MERRF are most often associated with short stature. 

KSS is due to deletions of mtDNA of about 1-10 kb, with the single most common deletion involving 5 kb of a region that encodes several proteins of oxidative phosphorylation.

Mutations in the mitochondrially encoded tRNA lysine gene (MT-TK, OMIM) are the most common cause of MERRF, occurring in more than 80 percent of all cases. These mutations lead to impaired translation of mitochondrial proteins and defects in oxidative phosphorylation.

A reduced capacity for oxidative phosphorylation can lead to muscle weakness, muscle atrophy, and exercise intolerance (exhaustion caused by physical exertion).  Common presentations for mitochondrial disease in children include short stature, neurosensory hearing loss, ophthalmoplegia, ptosis, neuropathy or nerve disease, diabetes, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy or enlarged heart muscle, and renal tubular acidosis.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS) causes short stature. KSS is caused by large deletions in the DNA found inside mitochondria — the parts of your cells that produce energy. The most common deletion removes a section of mitochondrial DNA that contains instructions for several proteins needed for a process called oxidative phosphorylation, which is how cells make most of their energy. When the cells cannot produce enough energy through oxidative phosphorylation, muscles become weak and waste away, and the body cannot tolerate physical activity. In children, this reduced energy production contributes to short stature, along with hearing loss, eye muscle weakness, drooping eyelids, nerve problems, diabetes, heart muscle thickening, and kidney problems.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Short stature: Mucopolysaccharidosis type I (MPS I/Hurler Syndrome; OMIM) is a life-threatening, autosomal recessive disease caused by deficiency of α-L-iduronidase (IDUA), a lysosomal enzyme responsible for metabolizing the glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) dermatan sulfate and heparan sulfate.

Distended lysosomes accumulate in the cell, interfere with cell function, and lead to a characteristic pattern of clinical, radiologic, and biochemical abnormalities. Excessive GAG storage disrupts bone connective tissue and disrupts growth leading to short stature.  Other structural, metabolic, or endocrine abnormalities may also contribute to short stature.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Hurler syndrome causes short stature. This condition is caused by a deficiency of an enzyme called alpha-L-iduronidase (IDUA), which lives inside the cell's recycling centers called lysosomes. This enzyme is responsible for breaking down large sugar-based molecules called glycosaminoglycans (GAGs). Without this enzyme, GAGs build up inside the lysosomes, causing them to swell. These overfilled lysosomes interfere with how cells work throughout the body. When GAGs accumulate in bone and connective tissue, they disrupt the structure and growth of the skeleton, leading to short stature. Other problems with the body's structure, metabolism, or hormone systems may also contribute to short stature.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Short stature: Noonan syndrome (OMIM) is a genetically inherited disease with gene mutations involved in the RAS/MAPK (mitogen-activated protein kinase) signaling pathway normally involved in cellular proliferation and differentiation. 
The mutations involved in Noonan syndrome are considered gain of function mutations, causing inappropriate prolongation of the RAS/MAPK signaling, leading to abnormal growth. This can lead to early epiphyseal fusion and short stature. Multiple genes in the RAS pathway can mutate to cause Noonan syndrome, with the most common being PTPN11 (OMIM).
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 33: Signaling

Allen MJ, Sharma S. Noonan Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jan 9]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532269/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Noonan syndrome causes short stature. This condition is caused by mutations in genes involved in a communication pathway inside cells called the RAS/MAPK pathway, which normally tells cells when to grow and develop. In Noonan syndrome, these mutations cause the pathway to stay switched "on" for too long — a gain-of-function effect — leading to abnormal patterns of growth. One result is that the growth plates in the bones close too early, stopping height increase before it should end, and leading to short stature. The most commonly mutated gene in this pathway is called PTPN11, though several other genes in the same pathway can also be responsible.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Short stature: Low levels of vitamin D in children lead to poor absorption of calcium and dysregulation of calcium and phosphate, leading to bone abnormalities (including softening of the bones), spine abnormalities (e.g. kyphoscoliosis), and disruption in bone growth, which can lead to short stature.
RESOURCES:
Marks' Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e. 2023, Ch. 1: Metabolic Fuels and Dietary Components

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin D deficiency causes short stature in children. Vitamin D is needed for the body to absorb calcium properly and to keep calcium and phosphate levels in balance. Without enough vitamin D, the bones do not get the minerals they need to stay strong and hard. The bones soften, and the spine can develop abnormal curves (kyphoscoliosis). These bone abnormalities interfere with how the bones grow, which disrupts normal growth in height, leading to short stature.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Short stature: The disorder is characterized by loss of paternally expressed genes in the imprinted region of chromosome 15q11-q13 (OMIM). This can result from deletions within the paternal chromosome 15 involving the SNRPN gene, maternal uniparental disomy 15 (UPD 15; inheritance of both copies of chromosome 15 from the mother and none from the father), or (infrequently) imprinting defects.  No single gene in the critical region has been identified that on its own results in PWS, though candidates include snoRNAs involved in RNA processing.

Infants with Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) are characterized by severe hypotonia and failure to thrive due to feeding difficulties. Another phenotype is small feet and hands.

Patients with PWS have lower amounts of growth hormone, which can lead to short stature, but the true mechanism is unknown.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) causes short stature. PWS is caused by the loss of genes that are normally active only on the chromosome 15 inherited from the father. This can happen because of a deletion in the father's chromosome 15, because a child inherits both copies of chromosome 15 from the mother (with none from the father), or because of imprinting defects. Babies with PWS have very low muscle tone and difficulty feeding. They also have smaller hands and feet than normal. Patients with PWS have lower levels of growth hormone, which is needed for normal bone and body growth, and this contributes to short stature — though the full reason for the growth hormone deficiency in PWS is not yet completely understood.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Short stature: Stunted growth is a hallmark of Trisomy 21 (Down Syndrome), with decreased growth velocity being more pronounced during infancy and adolescence. Individuals show significantly decreased levels of all three subunits of the insulin-like growth factor-1 ternary complex (IGF1/IGFBP3/IGFALS) leading to IGF-1 downstream signaling disruption as a potential contributor to stunted growth and neurodegeneration.

IGF-1 is a master regulator of human growth and development, acting downstream of the pituitary growth hormone (GH). Upon release of GH-releasing hormone (GHRH) from the hypothalamus, the pituitary gland releases GH1 into the bloodstream. In the liver, GH stimulates the production of IGF-1, and several IGFBPs. IGFALS, the acid labile subunit of the IGF-1 signaling ternary complex, stabilizes and increases the bioavailability of IGF-1 in circulation.
RESOURCES:
Robbins and Kumar Basic Pathology, 11e. 2023. Ch. 4: Genetic and Pediatric Diseases

Araya P, Kinning KT, Coughlan C, et al. IGF1 deficiency integrates stunted growth and neurodegeneration in Down syndrome. Cell Rep. 2022;41(13):111883.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36577365/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) causes short stature. Normally, a hormone called IGF-1 acts as a master switch for human growth and development. Growth hormone (GH), released by the pituitary gland after receiving a signal from the brain (GHRH), travels to the liver and triggers IGF-1 production. IGF-1 then travels through the bloodstream as part of a three-part complex that includes two helper proteins — IGFBP3 and IGFALS — which stabilize IGF-1 and keep it available in the blood to promote growth. In individuals with Down syndrome, levels of all three parts of this complex are significantly lower than normal, disrupting the IGF-1 signaling that the body needs to grow properly and contributing to short stature.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Short: Partial or complete monosomy of the X chromosome leads to short stature, webbing of the neck, and other malformations, most likely due to haploinsufficiency of the SHOX (pseudoautosomal homeobox-containing osteogenic) gene (OMIM), which is located on each type of sex chromosome.  SHOX encodes a transcription factor that is involved in bone growth.

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21925981/
SPECIAL NOTES: Conversely, overdosage of the SHOX gene leads to tall stature.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Turner syndrome causes short stature. Turner syndrome occurs when a female is born with a missing or partially missing X chromosome. Short stature in Turner syndrome is most likely due to having only one working copy of a gene called SHOX, located on the sex chromosomes. SHOX acts as a master switch that controls the development of many body structures, especially bone growth in the arms and legs. Normally, both males and females have two working copies of SHOX — one on each sex chromosome. In Turner syndrome, having only one working copy of SHOX means the gene cannot do its full job of driving bone growth, leading to short stature.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Insulin deficiency (e.g., in Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus) leads to hyperglycemia and may lead to diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). Multiple factors can trigger DKA. DKA may lead to cerebral edema and thus can affect brain function. The exact mechanisms underlying cerebral edema in diabetic ketoacidosis are not fully understood, but a commonly accepted hypothesis attributes it to factors such as rapid intravenous rehydration, administration of hypotonic fluids, rapid normalization of blood glucose levels, and changes in oncotic pressures, which collectively lead to osmotic fluid shifts into cells, causing swelling.

Altered mental state is associated with more severe cases.
Primary treatment is hydration and insulin which may in itself exacerbate the cerebral edema.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Type 1 Diabetes, the body doesn't make enough insulin, so blood sugar gets very high. Without insulin, the body can't use sugar properly for energy. This causes the brain to swell, which can affect how a person thinks and acts. The brain swelling may be made worse by the treatments used, such as giving fluids too quickly, using certain types of fluids, or lowering blood sugar too fast — all of which can cause fluid to shift into brain cells, making them puff up.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric

Glucose Homeostasis
pH, Water, Electrolytes
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Very high blood glucose causes dehydration due to diuresis leading to hypotension that can result in altered mental status (or confusion and coma). Conditions that increase water loss can accelerate the onset of hyperosmolarity and hypotension. In cases of severe dehydration and hyperosmolarity, hypotension triggers significant activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system, which can ultimately lead to renal failure. Reduced urine output limits the kidneys' ability to excrete glucose, helping to preserve circulating volume but worsening hyperglycemia. Additionally, hypotension impairs tissue perfusion. When severe electrolyte imbalances occur alongside hypotension, the progression of this hyperglycemic state may result in coma.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 39: Diabetes

Avichal D. Pediatric Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State. Medscape. Published 2024 Jul 18, Accessed Jan. 3, 2025
https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1914705-overview

Stoner GD. Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State. Am Fam Physician. 2017;96(11):729-736.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29431405/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When blood sugar gets extremely high, the body tries to get rid of the extra sugar by making a lot of urine. This causes the body to lose too much water, leading to dangerous dehydration and low blood pressure. The low blood pressure triggers the body's emergency systems, which can eventually cause the kidneys to stop working well. When the kidneys can't get rid of sugar anymore, blood sugar climbs even higher. Low blood pressure also means the body's tissues aren't getting enough blood flow. When severe dehydration, very high blood sugar, and serious imbalances in the body's minerals all happen together, a person can become confused or even fall into a coma.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Valproic acid is an antiseizure medication that inhibits N-acetyl glutamate synthase and leads to decreased levels of N-acetyl glutamate.  Since N-acetyl glutamate is an allosteric activator of the rate limiting enzyme of the urea cycle (carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I), a decreased concentration of N-acetyl glutamate can lead to hyperammonemia.

Excess ammonia crosses the blood brain barrier and reacts with alpha-ketoglutarate to form glutamate in a reaction catalyzed by glutamate dehydrogenase.  The depletion of alpha-ketoglutarate by this reaction inhibits the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle. An impaired TCA cycle reduces the production of NADH, which  affects neurons. Ammonia can also react with glutamate to form glutamine (via glutamine synthetase).  This reaction depletes glutamate and gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), a neurotransmitter that is synthesized from glutamate.  An increased glutamine concentration changes the osmotic balance, pulling water primarily into astrocytes, thus inhibiting their function.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Valproic acid is a medication used to prevent seizures. It blocks a molecule that the body needs to run its waste-removal system for nitrogen (called the urea cycle). When this system slows down, a waste product called ammonia builds up in the blood. Ammonia can travel into the brain, where it disrupts the brain's energy supply and throws off the balance of important chemical messengers. It also causes brain support cells to swell with water. All of these changes together can lead to confusion and other mental changes.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric

This reaction depletes glutamate and gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), a neurotransmitter that is synthesized from glutamate.  An increased glutamine concentration changes the osmotic balance, pulling water primarily into astrocytes.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The brain depends almost entirely on sugar (glucose) for its energy. When blood sugar drops too low, brain cells can't make enough energy to work properly. This lack of energy in brain cells leads to confusion and other changes in mental function.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Hypoxic conditions arising from multiple factors can lead to a decreased rate of processing substrate through the electron transport chain (ETC) and oxidative phosphorylation. The resulting decreased production of ATP can lead to ion channel dysfunction in neurons, lowering synaptic transmission and leading to brain dysfunction.  
Hyperlipidemia leading to atherosclerosis is an important cause of hypoxia.  Atherosclerotic plaques in different locations can affect blood supply to the brain.  In addition blood clots can form at sites of plaque rupture leading to stroke.
RESOURCES: Goodall S, Twomey R, Amann M. Acute and chronic hypoxia: implications for cerebral function and exercise tolerance. Fatigue. 2014;2(2):73-92. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4292893/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The brain needs a steady supply of oxygen to make energy. When oxygen levels fall — whether from breathing problems, a blocked blood vessel, or other causes — brain cells can't produce enough energy to keep working normally. This causes the brain's signaling systems to break down, leading to confusion or other mental changes. Clogged blood vessels caused by fatty buildup (atherosclerosis) are an important reason why blood and oxygen may not reach the brain. Blood clots can also form at these sites of fatty buildup, which can cause a stroke.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Maple syrup urine disease (MSUD) is caused by mutations in components of the branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase complex (BCKD), specifically BCKDHA (OMIM), BCKDHB (OMIM), or DBT (OMIM). This complex is required to convert the toxic ketoacid intermediates of branched-chain amino acids (leucine, isoleucine, and valine) metabolism to non-toxic by-products. These ketoacids have a distinctive smell, akin to maple syrup, when secreted into the urine, giving the condition its name. Accumulation of the ketoacids, outcompetes other amino acids for transport into oligodendrocytes, which leads to cerebral edema.  This amino acid deficiency may impair protein synthesis and thus cause demyelination. In cases of mutations leading to a severe loss of BCKD function, if untreated, the edema rapidly leads to coma and death.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is a metabolic disorder, characterized by accumulation of phenylalanine (Phe) due to phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) deficiency (OMIM) or tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) deficiency.  Phenylalanine outcompetes other amino acids for transport into oligodendrocytes.  The resulting amino acid deficiency may impair protein synthesis and thus cause demyelination, or it may impair the synthesis of the neurotransmitters dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin. Acute exacerbations can occur with infection and other stresses.  Otherwise, chronic presentations are observed in poorly controlled disease.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Ethanol metabolism leads to increased NADH levels in the mitochondria and cytoplasm. In the fasting state, the high cytoplasmic NADH promotes the conversion of pyruvate to lactate, thus inhibiting the use of lactate or alanine as gluconeogenic substrates. The conversion of malate to oxaloacetate is also inhibited by high NADH, further inhibiting gluconeogenesis. The resultant lactic acidosis and hypoglycemia can lead to brain dysfunction.  

Alcohol is a gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) receptor agonist.  Ethanol binding to GABA receptors enhances their inhibitory function by increasing chloride ion influx into the cell, reducing neuronal excitability and potentially leading to impaired brain function.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When the body breaks down alcohol, it creates a chemical imbalance that prevents the liver from making new sugar (glucose) for the brain. This leads to low blood sugar and a buildup of acid in the body, both of which can interfere with how the brain works. Alcohol also directly acts on the brain by latching onto receptors that calm brain activity, making brain cells less excitable and impairing normal brain function.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Lead inhibits ALA dehydratase, which leads to the accumulation of delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA).  ALA is known to be associated with the symptoms of lead poisoning. Generally, lead exposure is more chronic in nature, but once a threshold level is reached, or if the exposure is in a single large dose, then acute changes in mental status may be observed.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Thiamine deficiency leads to a reduction in intracellular thiamine pyrophosphate, which impairs the function of critical metabolic pathways like the TCA cycle (pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase) and the pentose phosphate pathway. Thiamine deficiency decreases the production of NADH, which is used for generation of ATP in oxidative phosphorylation. Decreased ATP production particularly affects cells with a high ATP demand for their cellular functions. Those cells are muscle (especially cardiac muscle) and neurons (brain). A decreased production of ATP in the neuronal cells leads to altered mental status (confusion). Additionally, toxic metabolic byproducts, including lactate, alanine, and glutamate, accumulate, causing a drop in cellular pH and disrupting electrolyte balance. These changes contribute to cytotoxic edema and further neuronal damage.  Vitamin B1 deficiency may result from GI tract surgery, or from chronic alcohol use, which is associated with poor nutrition and poor absorption.

SPECIAL NOTES: Thiamine is a cofactor for transketolase in the pentose phosphate pathway. Transketolase activity is measured to measure thiamine levels.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin B1 (thiamine) is needed to keep important energy-making pathways in the body running smoothly. Without enough thiamine, the body can't make enough energy for brain cells, which need a lot of energy to work properly. Harmful byproducts like lactic acid also build up, making the inside of cells more acidic and throwing off the balance of minerals. This causes brain cells to swell and become damaged, leading to confusion and other mental changes. Thiamine deficiency can result from stomach or intestinal surgery, or from long-term heavy alcohol use, which is linked to poor nutrition and poor absorption of vitamins.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Iron (non-heme, non-Fe-S) is a crucial cofactor for the enzymes tyrosine hydroxylase and tryptophan hydroxylase, which are responsible for the synthesis of the neurotransmitters dopamine and serotonin, respectively.

Additionally, monoamine oxidase, a key enzyme in the degradation pathways of these neurotransmitters, also requires iron for its activity. Consequently, monoamine oxidase activity is reduced in individuals with iron deficiency anemia.

Gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA), an inhibitory neurotransmitter derived from glutamate, is also affected by iron levels. Iron deficiency has been shown to lead to increased levels of GABA and heightened GABAergic activity in the brain.

The balance between monoamines and GABA plays a critical role in regulating mood, neuronal activity, and anxiety. Thus, alterations in brain iron levels, particularly under conditions of iron deficiency, which can result from multiple etiologies, have significant implications for emotional behaviors and mental health. 

Additionally, clinical features of iron deficiency anemia, such as shortness of breath and palpitations, can further exacerbate symptoms of anxiety.
RESOURCES:
Kim J, Wessling-Resnick M. Iron and mechanisms of emotional behavior. J Nutr Biochem. 2014;25(11):1101-1107.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Iron helps your brain make important chemicals called neurotransmitters that control your mood and help you feel calm. When you don't have enough iron, your brain can't make the right balance of these mood chemicals, which can make you feel anxious. Low iron also causes tiredness, rapid heartbeat, and shortness of breath, which can make anxiety worse.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Certain essential nutrients, such as B vitamins, vitamin C, magnesium, and zinc, play a crucial role in regulating the body's stress responses. These nutrients are involved in the production and metabolism of neurotransmitters like serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine, balance of which is critical for mental wellness and for managing stress and anxiety levels. Micronutrient deficiency paired with chronic stress can disrupt the synthesis of these neurotransmitters, increasing the risk of developing severe anxiety disorders. Furthermore, these nutrients also influence the structure of neuronal membranes and the release of neurotransmitters. Additionally, B vitamins, vitamin C, magnesium, and zinc are involved in the conversion of alpha-linolenic acid into longer-chain omega-3 fatty acids.Higher levels of these beneficial omega-3 fatty acids have been associated with a lower risk of experiencing anxiety disorders.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Several mechanisms were proposed and are being investigated to clearly understand the pathogenesis of Huntington disease. Huntingtin (HTT; OMIM) protein is normally involved in neurogenesis. Huntington disease is a consequence of a CAG-trinucleotide repeat expansion that leads to the production of an abnormally long huntingtin (HTT) protein with poly-Gln. Beta sheets form in the abnormally expanded region leading to misfolding and formation of toxic aggregates. The aggregates overload the ubiquitin proteasome degradation system.  HTT, in Huntington disease, impairs production, transport, and release of BDNF (brain derived neurotrophic factor), thus reducing downstream signalling via neurotrophic tyrosine receptor kinase B (TrkB). Disruption of serotonin signaling by the poly-Gln-expanded HTT, leading to defective neurogenesis in the hippocampus, is implicated in mood disorders.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI; OMIM search) is caused by inherited mutations in collagen genes and/or genes involved in collagen processing (e.g. hydroxylation, glycosylation, chaperones) resulting in abnormal collagen that gets incorporated into bone and other connective tissue. With collagen being an important part of connective tissue, abnormalities lead to brittle bones, improper dentition, and blue (thin) sclera. Bone fragility and other OI clinical manifestations, such as short stature, muscle weakness, and ligament laxity, can lead to mobility limitations and gait deviations.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body uses a special building material called collagen to make bones and other tissues strong. In Osteogenesis Imperfecta, genes that control how collagen is made or processed are not working correctly, so the collagen that gets built into your bones is abnormal. This makes bones very fragile and easy to break. Along with brittle bones, this condition can also cause short height, weak muscles, and loose joints — all of which can make it harder to walk normally and keep your balance.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric; affects individuals of all ages from birth onward

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Acquired Copper deficiency associated with GI surgery, malabsorption, or nutritional deficiency can lead to peripheral neuropathy, but the mechanisms are unclear. Copper plays a crucial role in the central nervous system as an essential component of various proteins and metalloenzymes. Copper deficiency has long been known to cause sensory ataxia, commonly referred to as swayback disease. 

Common enzymes requiring Cu are cytochrome oxidase, dopamine hydroxylase, superoxide dismutase, and tyrosinase. Cytochrome oxidase deficiency impairs cellular respiration, leading to central nervous system degeneration and ataxia. Additionally, autonomic symptoms such as hypotension and hypothermia arise from abnormal catecholamine synthesis, which is caused by a deficiency in dopamine beta-hydroxylase. These eventually progress to demyelination of the CNS, leading to myelopolyneuropathy, including gait and balance issues.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body needs a mineral called copper to keep many important proteins and enzymes working properly. These enzymes help your cells make energy, produce important brain chemicals, and protect your body from damage. When you don't get enough copper — for example, after certain stomach or intestinal surgeries, or due to poor nutrition — these enzymes stop working well. Without enough copper, the cells that surround and protect your nerves begin to break down, and the protective coating around your nerves (called myelin) starts to wear away. When nerves lose their protective coating, they can't send signals properly, which makes it hard to keep your balance and walk steadily.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; associated with GI surgery, malabsorption, or nutritional deficiency

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Multiple, non-mutually-exclusive mechanisms have been proposed for diabetic peripheral neuropathy. One mechanism to the neuropathy is that hyperglycemia (primarily in type 1 diabetes) or accumulation of lipids (primarily in type 2 diabetes) leads to buildup of lactate or acylcarnitine, respectively, in Schwann cells. These products are then shuttled to  Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) neurons, causing mitochondrial dysfunction and cell death. Another contributing mechanism is that excess glucose gets converted by the enzyme aldose reductase in the Schwann cells to sorbitol. Sorbitol cannot be further metabolized and puts osmotic stress on the cell, causing cell damage and decreased innervation in the peripheral tissues. Finally, high levels of glucose can lead to non-enzymatic modification of proteins. These glycation end products are thought to bind RAGE (Receptor for advanced glycation end products) and trigger inflammatory cascades. Glycation end products can also form crosslinks with collagen and disrupt PNS structure.  Peripheral nerve damage in patients with Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy results in delayed reflex responses when changing posture, leading to impaired balance and increased fall risk.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When blood sugar stays too high for a long time — as happens in diabetes — it can damage the nerves in your arms and legs in several ways. First, certain cells that protect your nerves can build up harmful waste products from the extra sugar or fat in the blood, which hurts the nerves from the inside. Second, extra sugar gets converted into a substance called sorbitol inside these protective cells; sorbitol builds up and causes the cells to swell and get damaged. Third, high sugar levels cause sticky, damaging changes to proteins throughout the body, which can trigger inflammation and damage the structure around your nerves. When the nerves in your legs are damaged, they are slow to send signals when your body position changes, making it harder to react quickly and keep your balance, which raises your risk of falling.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; associated with long-standing type 1 or type 2 diabetes mellitus

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Friedreich Ataxia (FA) is caused by an autosomal recessively inherited GAA trinucleotide repeat expansion in the frataxin gene, intron #1, located at chromosome 9q13 (affecting splicing of frataxin RNA). The frataxin gene product OMIM is an inner mitochondrial membrane protein required for the assembly of Fe-S Clusters for the electron transport chain complex I and complex II.  Frataxin deficiency leads to impaired mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and mitochondrial iron overload. This, in turn, can lead to oxidative stress and cell or tissue damage, leading to neuronopathy and degeneration of peripheral nerve fibers and the dorsal spinal cord. Clinical phenotypes begin in childhood with an ataxic (clumsy) gait as the typical initial observation.
RESOURCES:
Robbins and Cotran Pathological Basis of Disease, 10e.  2021. Ch. 28: The Central Nervous System

SPECIAL NOTES:
Progression of disease involves ataxia, muscle weakness, loss of limb and plantar reflex,loss of position and vibration sensory loss.  Patients become wheelchair bound and usually develop cardiomyopathy (typical cause of death).  

A subset (~6-19%) of patients with Friedreich Ataxia also develop diabetes and it is thought this may be due to mitochondrial dysfunction leading to insulin deficiency (mechanism not clear).
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Inside your cells, there are tiny structures called mitochondria that act like power plants, making the energy your body needs to work. A protein called frataxin normally helps these power plants run properly and keeps iron levels in balance inside them. In Friedreich Ataxia, a change in the frataxin gene — inherited from both parents — means that frataxin is not made correctly. Without enough frataxin, the mitochondria cannot make energy properly, and iron builds up inside them, causing damage from harmful molecules. Over time, this damages nerve cells in the outer nerves of the body and in the spinal cord. The first sign most people notice is a clumsy, unsteady walk, which gets worse over time.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric onset; symptoms typically begin in childhood or adolescence

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Deficiency in neutral amino acid transporter solute carrier family (SLC6A19 gene; OMIM) present in intestines (absorption) and kidneys (reabsorption), leading to deficiency in the transport of 8 of 10 essential amino acids, including tryptophan. Excessive tryptophan is excreted in urine and feces leading to tryptophan wasting. Hartnup disease is characterized by both neuropsychiatric and pellagra-like symptoms, including ataxia, owing to deficient neurotransmitter and niacin synthesis from tryptophan.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your small intestine and kidneys work together to absorb and save important building blocks from food called amino acids. In Hartnup disease, a protein that is supposed to carry certain amino acids — including one called tryptophan — is not working correctly because of an inherited gene change. Because of this, tryptophan is lost in the urine and stool instead of being kept and used by the body. Tryptophan is important because your body uses it to make brain chemicals and a vitamin called niacin. Without enough tryptophan, you can develop symptoms similar to a niacin deficiency, including an unsteady, clumsy walk (called ataxia) and other nervous system and skin problems.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric; symptoms typically appear in childhood

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Wilson Disease (WD) is a Cu toxicity disease of the liver. However, patients can exhibit neurological symptoms in the advanced stages of the disease.  Mutations in the ATP7B gene cause Wilson's Disease (OMIM) encoding human ATPase copper transporting beta protein, which results in initial copper accumulation in hepatocytes.  Extrahepatic copper accumulation in brain tissue (e.g., basal ganglia) can lead to neurotoxicity via mechanisms such as mitochondrial toxicity, oxidative stress, cell membrane damage, and DNA cross-linking.  The most common neurological phenotype in neuro-WD is tremor, and it can be accompanied by features of movement disorders -  dystonia, parkinsonism, cerebellar ataxia, chorea, dysarthria, and dysphagia.  Resultant motor dysfunction can lead to balance issues in these patients.

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31179301/
SPECIAL NOTES: Autosomal recessive inheritance
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Copper is a mineral your body needs in small amounts, but too much copper is harmful. In Wilson disease, an inherited gene change means that a protein that normally moves copper out of liver cells is not working. This causes copper to build up first in the liver, and then, as the disease gets worse, copper spills out and builds up in other parts of the body — including a part of the brain called the basal ganglia. When copper collects in the brain, it damages brain cells by harming their energy-making machinery, causing harmful chemical reactions, and damaging cell membranes. This brain damage causes movement problems, including tremors, stiffness, uncontrolled movements, and trouble with balance and walking.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both pediatric and adult; typically presents in individuals aged 5–35 years

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Niemann-Pick disease type C (NPC) is characterized by autosomal recessive inheritance of mutations in NPC1 (OMIM) and NPC2 (OMIM), which encode the NPC intracellular cholesterol transporters 1 and 2, respectively. This is a rare lysosomal lipid storage disorder causing accumulation of unesterified cholesterol and glycosphingolipid (neutral glycolipids such as glucosylceramide and lactosylceramide, and the monosialic gangliosides, GM2 and GM3) intracellularly in brain, liver, spleen and other tissues. In the brain, these lipids accumulate in the gray matter, leading to neuroaxonal dystrophy manifested as axon spheroid formation, demyelination, neurodegeneration, and brain atrophy. Included among multiple neurological symptoms are supranuclear gaze palsy, cerebellar ataxia, and dystonia that progressively lead to gait, balance, and coordination difficulties. Additional presentations include developmental delay, increased falls, clumsiness, and cataplexy.
RESOURCES:
Sansare A, Zampieri C, Alter K, et al. Gait, Balance, and Coordination Impairments in Niemann Pick Disease, Type C1. J Child Neurol. 2018;33(1):114-124. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29246094/    

Vincent I, Bu B, Erickson RP. Understanding Niemann-Pick type C disease: a fat problem. Curr Opin Neurol. 2003;16(2):155-161. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12644742/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your cells have a recycling system that uses small compartments called lysosomes to break down and reuse fats and other materials. In Niemann-Pick type C, inherited changes in certain genes mean that two proteins needed to move a type of fat called cholesterol out of the lysosomes are not working properly. As a result, cholesterol and other fats build up inside cells — especially in the brain, liver, and spleen. In the brain, this fat buildup damages nerve fibers and causes nerve cells to break down and die over time. This leads to a range of nervous system problems, including an unsteady walk, poor balance and coordination, difficulty controlling eye movements, and increased clumsiness and falls.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric; symptoms often begin in childhood, though onset can vary

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Abnormal production, misfolding, and build-up of proteins (amyloid protein and tau protein) in the brain lead to neuronal cell death. The major phenotype of cognitive impairment in Alzheimer disease can result in gait and balance deficits. A quantitative measure of dynamic balance called the Index of Postural Stability (IPS) was shown to be diminished in patients with mild Alzheimer disease, and brain network analysis showed this may reflect the progression of Alzheimer disease pathology via hippocampal degeneration and neuronal pathway impairment. Vestibular function has also been linked to balance and fall risk in patients with Alzheimer disease, and vestibular therapy may be an option for lowering fall risk.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your brain cells communicate through complex networks, and they depend on proteins being made and folded correctly to stay healthy. In Alzheimer disease, two proteins — called amyloid and tau — are made incorrectly, fold into the wrong shape, and build up in the brain. This buildup causes brain cells to die, especially in areas involved in memory and movement. As more brain cells are lost, people have trouble not only with memory and thinking, but also with keeping their balance and walking steadily. The part of the brain that helps manage balance becomes disrupted, and the inner ear system that also helps with balance may not work as well either, increasing the risk of falls.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; most commonly affects older adults, typically over age 65

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Huntington Disease is an inherited neurodegenerative disorder that presents in mid-adulthood. Several mechanisms have been proposed and are being investigated to clearly understand the pathogenesis of Huntington disease.  The huntingtin (HTT) protein (OMIM) is normally involved in neurogenesis. Huntington Disease is a CAG-Trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder leading to the production of huntingtin (HTT) protein with an abnormally long poly-Gln. Beta sheets form in the abnormally expanded region, leading to misfolding and formation of toxic aggregates. This overloads the ubiquitin-proteasome degradation system.  The toxic gain of function of HTT protein leads to cellular injury in the brain, specifically targeting the basal ganglia (caudate nucleus).  Dysfunction of the basal ganglia leads to suppression of voluntary movements and loss of inhibition of involuntary movements (causing chorea).  This leads to a neuronopathy of the dorsal root ganglion, and to degeneration of peripheral nerve fibers and the dorsal spinal columns. An ataxic gait is usually the presenting symptom, leading to issues maintaining balance, and to an increased fall rate.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Huntington disease, a gene that makes a protein called huntingtin has an abnormal repeated section that is much longer than it should be. This extra-long section causes the protein to fold into the wrong shape and clump together inside brain cells. The cell's normal system for breaking down and clearing away damaged proteins gets overwhelmed by these clumps and cannot keep up. The buildup of these abnormal clumps is especially harmful to a part of the brain called the basal ganglia, which normally helps control movement. When the basal ganglia are damaged, it becomes harder for the brain to control voluntary movements and easier for unwanted, involuntary movements — like jerking or writhing (called chorea) — to occur. Nerve fibers that carry signals from the brain down through the spinal cord also degenerate. An unsteady, clumsy walk is usually one of the first signs of the disease, and problems with balance and falls become more frequent over time.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; symptoms typically begin in mid-adulthood, between ages 30–50

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Parkinson disease (PD) is a neurodegenerative disorder of the basal ganglia in older adults, specifically affecting the pars compacta region of the substantia nigra.  A resultant loss of dopamine-producing cells (dopaminergic neurons) impacts motor cortex circuitry - reducing both indirect (inhibition) and direct pathways (excitation).  This leads to motor dysfunction (shuffling gait, resting tremor, slow/delayed movement).   

Theories on the etiology of Parkinson Disease include 1) exposure to environmental agents (e.g., 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine (MPTP), which accumulates in the mitochondria); 2) Free radical damage to thalamic nuclei caused by an inability to process toxic metabolites of dopamine metabolic products (neuromelanin); 3) a genetic predisposition to mutations in PARK loci. (a) The first genetic cause of PD discovered was mutant alpha-synuclein protein (encoded by the SNCA gene; the disorder is also known as PARK-SNCA or PARK1).  This protein is involved in PARP-1 (poly[ADP Ribose] Polymerase 1) signaling required for stress response (e.g., DNA repair and apoptosis).  In PD, alpha-synuclein peptides form inclusions in neuronal cell bodies (Lewy bodies) and processes (Lewy neurites), which leads to neurotoxicity.  The aggregation of alpha-synuclein is also proposed to act synergistically with amyloid deposits in Alzheimer disease, promoting neurotoxicity, and (b) Examples of inherited mutations in other PARK Loci include PRKN (encoding Parkin) and PINK1, two genes that normally promote lysosomal degradation of dysfunctional mitochondria via macrophages.  Tissues with mutant PRKN (PARK2) and PINK1 (PARK 6) gene products have impaired mitophagy.  PRKN (RBR E3 ubiquitin protein ligase) gene mutations lead to protein accumulation due to ubiquitin-proteasome dysfunction, which could lead to the death of dopamine-producing nerve cells. There are several other PARK Loci genes as well.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Parkinson disease, nerve cells in a specific part of the brain called the substantia nigra — which make an important chemical messenger called dopamine — slowly die off. Dopamine is needed to help control smooth, coordinated movement. When too many of these cells are lost, the brain's movement control circuits stop working properly, leading to a slow, shuffling walk, shaking at rest, and stiff, sluggish movements. Several things may cause these nerve cells to die. In some people, a protein called alpha-synuclein folds incorrectly and clumps together inside nerve cells, forming deposits called Lewy bodies that are toxic to the cell. In others, inherited gene changes mean that damaged mitochondria (the cell's power plants) are not cleared away properly, or that damaged proteins build up because the cell's waste disposal system is not working. Free radical damage from harmful byproducts of dopamine processing may also play a role. All of these processes can lead to the death of dopamine-producing cells, resulting in the movement difficulties seen in Parkinson disease.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; most commonly affects adults over age 60, though early-onset forms exist

Nutrition
Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Rickets/osteomalacia are due to osteoid (bone tissue) mineralization defects by calcium and phosphate arising from Vitamin D deficiency. The active form of vitamin D (1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol) is important for intestinal Ca absorption, maintenance of bone (formation/resorption), and allows Ca reabsorption in the kidneys.  Rickets is the pediatric condition, and Osteomalacia is the adult condition. Physical manifestations include softening and bowing of bones, which can lead to fractures, as well as balance and gait problems. Vitamin D deficiency can be caused by malnutrition, decreased exposure to sunlight, end-organ failure, or anti-convulsant therapy.  Studies have demonstrated that vitamin D supplementation can improve balance and gait difficulties in older adults with severe vitamin D deficiencies.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews, Biochemistry, 9e.2025 Ch 29: Micronutrients: Minerals

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 31: Steroid Hormones and Vitamin D

Sahin Alak ZY, Ates Bulut E, Dokuzlar O, Yavuz I, Soysal P, Isik AT. Long-term effects of vitamin D deficiency on gait and balance in the older adults. Clin Nutr. 2020;39(12):3756-3762. 
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32336527/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin D is a nutrient your body needs to absorb calcium and keep bones strong and hard. When you don't have enough vitamin D — because of poor diet, not enough sunlight, kidney or liver problems, or certain medications — your body cannot put enough calcium and phosphate into your bones. Without these minerals, bones become soft and weak. In children, this condition is called rickets and can cause the leg bones to bow outward. In adults, it is called osteomalacia. Soft, weak bones are more likely to bend, break, or cause pain, all of which can change the way you walk and make it harder to keep your balance. In older adults with very low vitamin D levels, taking vitamin D supplements has been shown to help improve balance and walking.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both pediatric (rickets) and adult (osteomalacia); affects all age groups

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency is due to inadequate nutritional intake due to various reasons and, in high-income countries, is most often associated with chronic alcohol use.  The variety of balance issues associated with thiamine deficiency is due to diminished ATP in the brain caused by decreased activity of two thiamine-requiring enzymes in mitochondria, pyruvate dehydrogenase complex and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, for which thiamine is a coenzyme.  Chronic B1 deficiency can lead to brain atrophy in different regions and a resultant triad of presenting symptoms, including ataxia, ophthalmoplegia, and a state of global confusion and amnesia. These lead to issues with both balance and gait.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 22: Citric Acid Cycle and Thiamine Deficiency, p. 238-9.

Lippincott Illustrated Reviews, Biochemistry, 9e.2025 Ch 28: Micronutrients: Vitamins

Akhouri S, Kuhn J, Newton EJ. Wernicke-Korsakoff Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jun 26. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430729/

Serhan HA, Alma'aitah HW, Irshaidat S, Ameer MA, Asghar MS, Tahir MJ. Ophthalmic manifestations of nutritional deficiencies: A mini review. J Family Med Prim Care. 2022 Oct;11(10):5899-5901.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36618247/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your brain needs a steady supply of energy to work properly, and it relies on a vitamin called thiamine (vitamin B1) to help make that energy. Thiamine is needed by two important enzymes inside the cell's power plants (mitochondria) to keep energy production running. When you don't get enough thiamine — most often because of heavy alcohol use over many years, but also from other causes of poor nutrition — these enzymes slow down and the brain doesn't get enough energy. Without enough energy, different regions of the brain begin to shrink and stop working correctly. This leads to three main problems happening together: an unsteady, clumsy walk; difficulty moving the eyes normally; and severe confusion and memory loss. These problems make both balance and walking very difficult.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; most commonly associated with chronic alcohol use in adults

Nutrition
Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Vitamin B12 deficiency can occur in many situations, including infection, malnutrition, pernicious anemia, and a vegan diet, and can result in megaloblastic anemia. Vitamin B12 is a coenzyme for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, which is required for the catabolism of methionine, valine, isoleucine, and odd-chain fatty acids.  In Vitamin B12 deficiency, the resultant high concentration of methylmalonyl-CoA  (MMA) serves as an alternate substrate for acetyl-CoA carboxylase and even fatty acid synthase, leading to the accumulation of alternative product branched-chain fatty acids.  Elevated MMA results in abnormal odd-chain and branched-chain fatty acids with subsequent abnormal myelination, possibly leading to defective nerve transmission.  Unusual, branched fatty acids get incorporated into cell membranes, leading to CNS defects, which can lead to issues with balance.  The latter are mainly observed in chronic B12 deficiency.  

Vitamin B12 is also required in methionine synthesis as a coenzyme for methionine synthase from homocysteine.   Homocysteine accumulation has been linked to neurodegenerative diseases and aging.  Some proposed mechanisms of homocysteine neurotoxicity involve perturbation of methylation and redox potential, leading to Ca influx, amyloid and tau accumulation, apoptosis, and neuronal death. The cognitive impairment resulting from neurodegeneration can lead to issues with balance. As homocysteine metabolism is linked to folate, B12, and B6 levels, deficiencies in these vitamins will affect its levels.   Homocysteine may also exert its effects by acting as an excitatory NMDA receptor-mediated neurotransmitter.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews, Biochemistry, 9e.2025 Ch 28: Micronutrients: Vitamins

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 36: One-Carbon Metabolism, Folate Deficiency, and Cobalamin Deficiency,

Singh NN. Vitamin B12-Associated Neurological Diseases. Medscape. Published 2025 Feb 4, Accessed Nov 30, 2025
https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1152670

Dewulf JP, Gerin I, Rider MH, Veiga-da-Cunha M, Van Schaftingen E, Bommer GT. The synthesis of branched-chain fatty acids is limited by enzymatic decarboxylation of ethyl- and methylmalonyl-CoA. Biochem J. 2019;476(16):2427-2447.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31416829/   

Obeid R, Herrmann W. Mechanisms of homocysteine neurotoxicity in neurodegenerative diseases with special reference to dementia. FEBS Lett. 2006;580(13):2994-3005. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16697371/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin B12 is a nutrient your body needs to keep nerves healthy and to process certain fats and amino acids correctly. When you don't have enough B12 — due to poor diet, certain infections, or conditions that prevent its absorption — a harmful substance called methylmalonyl-CoA builds up in the body. This causes the body to make abnormal fatty acids that get incorporated into the protective covering around nerves (called myelin), disrupting how nerve signals travel. At the same time, without B12, a harmful substance called homocysteine builds up in the blood. High homocysteine levels can damage brain cells through several harmful processes, including triggering inflammation and causing nerve cell death. Together, these changes can lead to problems with balance and coordination, especially when the deficiency has been present for a long time.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both pediatric and adult; can affect individuals of any age, though more common in adults, particularly older adults and those on vegan diets

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol) is a fat-soluble vitamin that acts as an antioxidant by scavenging lipid peroxyl radicals in the membrane and preventing their downstream effects.   Vitamin E deficiency is associated with ataxia (neurological condition associated with loss of balance, muscle control and coordination), and while the mechanism is still unclear, the ataxia is presumed to be related to progressive degeneration of the peripheral nervous system from oxidative damage. Vitamin E is known to be required for embryonic neurogenesis.  Vitamin E supplementation in adults can slow progression of nervous system degeneration. 

Inherited defects leading to vitamin E deficiency include: (1) Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency (AVED) an autosomally recessive inherited defect in the alpha-tocopherol transfer protein (TTPA gene) on chromosome 8q12.3.  This  protein is responsible for distribution and transport of vitamin E from the liver to other tissues in the body, including the brain.  Patients with AVED show "degeneration of sensory neurons, a progressive dying back of peripheral nerves resulting in spinocerebellar ataxia with Purkinje cell death" due to impaired vitamin E trafficking in this region. (2) Abetalipoproteinemia, an autosomal recessive disorder, also known as Bassen-Kornzweig syndrome, leading to malabsorption of fat and fat-soluble vitamins. The specific deficiency is in the microsomal triglyceride transfer protein, MTTP, resulting in an inability to assemble and lipidate apoB to form chylomicrons and VLDL particles.
RESOURCES:
Ouahchi K, Arita M, Kayden H, et al. Ataxia with isolated vitamin E deficiency is caused by mutations in the alpha-tocopherol transfer protein. Nat Genet. 1995;9(2):141-145.  https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7719340/ 

Lippincott Illustrated Reviews, Biochemistry, 9e. Ch 17: Phospholipid, Glycosphingolipid, and Eicosanoid Metabolism; Ch 18 Cholesterol, Lipoprotein, and Steroid Metabolism

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 28: Triglycerides and Hypertriglyceridemia

Ataxia with Vitamin E Deficiency. National Organization for Rare Disorders. Accessed January 23, 2025
https://rarediseases.org/rare-diseases/ataxia-with-vitamin-e-deficiency/

Traber MG. Vitamin E: necessary nutrient for neural development and cognitive function. Proc Nutr Soc. 2021 Aug;80(3):319-326.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33896432/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin E is a fat-soluble vitamin that acts like a shield inside your cell membranes, stopping harmful chemical reactions called oxidation from spreading and damaging cells. When you don't have enough vitamin E — whether from poor diet, inability to absorb fats, or an inherited gene change — the nervous system becomes vulnerable to this oxidative damage over time. The nerves that carry signals from your body to your brain and the nerve cells in the cerebellum (the part of the brain that helps coordinate movement) slowly break down. This leads to a progressive loss of balance, muscle coordination, and steady walking — a condition called ataxia. Some people inherit a gene change that stops a protein from properly distributing vitamin E from the liver to the rest of the body, including the brain; others inherit a condition that prevents fat and fat-soluble vitamins like vitamin E from being absorbed at all.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both pediatric and adult; inherited forms typically present in childhood or early adulthood; nutritional deficiency forms more common in adults

Cholesterol and Lipoprotein Metabolism
Genetic Inheritance
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Leading conditions that cause hypercholesterolemia such as 1) Type II or Familial hypercholesterolemia (LDL receptor deficiency; OMIM); 2) familial hypercholesterolemia caused by proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 serine protease (PCSK9,OMIM) gain-of-function mutations (increasing LDL receptor degradation); 3) Type III or familial dysbetalipoproteinemia (Apo-E deficiency; OMIM); 4) Type IV familial hyperlipidemia (VLDL metabolism defect; OMIM); metabolic syndrome] are risk factors for LDL accumulation in blood vessels that contribute to foam cell formation (via oxidized LDL), causing atherosclerosis and coronary artery disease.  Narrowing of coronary arteries due to the plaques can lead to angina. Thrombus formation at sites of plaque rupture leads to ischemia and infarction, which often manifest as chest pain.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry,9e. 2025. Ch 18: Cholesterol, Lipoprotein and Steroid Metabolism

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 29, Cholesterol Metabolism and Hypercholesterolemia

Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 22e. 2025. Ch: 419: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism
SPECIAL NOTES: Dyslipidemia commonly results in either one of two cardiovascular problems "angina pectoris" and "Myocardial infarction." They both show chest pain in similar locations and quality, but with significant differences in severity, timing, and relieving factors.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Some people are born with genes that cause too much bad cholesterol (LDL) to build up in their blood. When there is too much LDL in the blood, it can get stuck in the walls of blood vessels and form thick, hard buildups called plaques. These plaques narrow the blood vessels that bring blood to the heart, which can cause chest pain called angina. Sometimes a plaque can crack open and cause a blood clot to form, which can block blood flow to the heart entirely — this is a heart attack, and it causes severe chest pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; severe inherited forms may present in pediatric patients

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (vEDS) is most often due to an inherited collagen type III deficiency (OMIM), leading to clinical phenotype heterogeneity, mainly characterized by fragile blood vessels and lax skin.  The problems seen in patients with vEDS can be due to either the poor strength of collagen or the absence of sufficient amounts of structurally normal collagen.  Vascular EDS can lead to cardiovascular complications, such as aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection, or valve malformation, resulting in chest pain. Chest pain could also be musculoskeletal in origin with defects in collagen leading to joint instability and chest deformities. Connective tissue abnormalities in the lungs can lead to formation of blebs (air-filled sacs), which could potentially result in pneumothorax and thus chest pain.
RESOURCES:
Byers PH. Vascular Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome. 1999 Sep 2 [Updated 2019 Feb 21]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 1993-2024. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1494/ 

Shabani M, Abdollahi A, Brar BK, et al. Vascular aneurysms in Ehlers-Danlos syndrome subtypes: A systematic review. Clin Genet. 2023;103(3):261-267. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36210598/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, the body has a problem making a strong protein called collagen type III, which normally acts like a tough scaffold that holds blood vessels, skin, and other tissues together. Because this scaffold is weak or missing, blood vessels can become fragile and tear or bulge, causing chest pain. Joints can also become loose and unstable, and chest shape abnormalities can cause discomfort. Sometimes small air-filled sacs called blebs can form in the lungs and pop, letting air leak out — this also causes chest pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; may also affect pediatric patients

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Loeys–Dietz syndrome (LDS; OMIM search) is an inherited connective tissue disorder caused by mutations in several genes that lead to increased activity of the TGF-beta signaling pathway. LDS affects connective tissue in many parts of the body, including the skeleton, heart, skin, and blood vessels.  In adults, increased TGF-beta signaling may lead to weakening of the wall of the aorta, in part due to increased degradation of elastin.  Dissection of the aorta can cause severe chest pain, and it is life-threatening.  Some features of LDS are also seen in Marfan syndrome and in Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, and chest pain could also arise due to chest deformities and joint instability. Connective tissue abnormalities in the lungs can lead to formation of blebs (air-filled sacs), which could potentially result in pneumothorax and thus chest pain.
RESOURCES:
Loeys BL, Dietz HC. Loeys-Dietz Syndrome. 2008 Feb 28 [Updated 2018 Mar 1]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 1993-2024. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1133/  

MacCarrick G, Black JH 3rd, Bowdin S, et al. Loeys-Dietz syndrome: a primer for diagnosis and management. Genet Med. 2014;16(8):576-587.  https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24577266/ 

Luo X, Deng S, Jiang Y, et al. Identification of a Pathogenic TGFBR2 Variant in a Patient With Loeys-Dietz Syndrome. Front Genet. 2020;11:479. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32528524/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Loeys-Dietz syndrome is caused by gene mutations that make a signaling pathway in the body called TGF-beta work too hard. This over-activity affects connective tissue — the material that holds the body's parts together — in the heart, blood vessels, bones, and skin. Over time, the wall of the large blood vessel leaving the heart (the aorta) can weaken and tear, which causes sudden, very serious chest pain. Loose joints and chest shape problems can also cause discomfort. Additionally, small air-filled sacs called blebs can form in the lungs and pop, letting air leak into the chest and causing chest pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; may also affect pediatric patients

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Marfan syndrome is due to mutations in the gene for fibrillin-1 (FBN1; OMIM).  Fibrillin-1 is a connective tissue protein in microfibrils that plays a role in elasticity.  FBN1 mutations have pleiotropic effects.  In some patients, Marfan syndrome can lead to dilation of the aorta and other cardiovascular complications (aortic aneurysm, dissection, valve malformation), resulting in pain. Chest pain could also arise from chest abnormalities such as pectus excavatum and joint instability. Connective tissue abnormalities in the lungs can lead to formation of blebs (air-filled sacs), which could potentially result in pneumothorax and thus chest pain.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 13: Pathologic Alterations of the Extracellular Matrix That Involve Fibrillin, Elastin, or Proteoglycans

Gawinecka J, Schönrath F, von Eckardstein A. Acute aortic dissection: pathogenesis, risk factors and diagnosis. Swiss Med Wkly. 2017;147:w14489. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28871571/ 

Sanyal K, Sabanathan K.  Chest pain in Marfan syndrome.  BMJ Case Rep. 2009;2009:bcr07.2008.0431.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3027903/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Marfan syndrome is caused by a mutation in the gene for a protein called fibrillin-1, which helps give connective tissue its strength and stretchiness. Without enough working fibrillin-1, the large blood vessel leaving the heart (the aorta) can stretch and bulge or even tear, causing serious chest pain. Abnormal chest shape and loose joints can also cause discomfort. Sometimes small air-filled sacs called blebs form in the lungs and pop, letting air leak into the chest cavity, which also causes chest pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; may also affect pediatric patients

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Homocystinuria is caused by defects in methionine metabolism that lead to homocysteine accumulation. Classic homocystinuria, primarily due to cystathionine beta-synthase (CBS) deficiency (OMIM), affects multiple organs, with cardiovascular complications like atherosclerosis and thromboembolism driving morbidity and mortality.

Homocysteine promotes thrombosis by altering endothelial thrombomodulin and forming prothrombotic thioester bonds. Resulting ischemia may cause chest pain via lactate buildup, inflammation, and neuroendocrine pathways. It also weakens arterial walls, increasing aneurysm risk. Along with aneurysms, other marfanoid features are also seen, which could lead to chest pain. Chest pain could also arise due to involvement of the musculoskeletal system (chest deformities) and respiratory system (spontaneous pneumothorax).

Other genetic causes of homocystinuria include deficiencies in N(5,10)-methylenetetrahydrofolate dehydrogenase (OMIM), N(5,10)-methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase (OMIM), and methionine synthase reductase (OMIM).
RESOURCES:
Gawinecka J, Schönrath F, von Eckardstein A.  Acute aortic dissection: pathogenesis, risk factors and diagnosis.  Swiss Med Wkly. 2017 Aug 25;147:w14489. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28871571/

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 36: One-Carbon Metabolism, Folate Deficiency, and Cobalamin Deficiency.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Homocystinuria is caused by defects in the way the body breaks down a building block of protein called methionine. These defects cause a substance called homocysteine to build up in the blood. Too much homocysteine damages blood vessels and makes blood more likely to clot in the wrong place. When clots block blood flow, the tissue that is cut off from oxygen builds up lactic acid and triggers pain signals, causing chest pain. Weakened blood vessel walls can also bulge and cause chest pain. Additionally, chest shape problems and spontaneous lung air leaks can also cause chest pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric; cardiovascular complications extend into adult patients

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Mutations in the SERPINC1 gene (OMIM), encoding antithrombin III, lead to increased fibrin accumulation, resulting in a primary hyper-coagulated state, which is manifested most often with deep-vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism, the latter of which causes chest pain.
RESOURCES:
Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine 22e. 2025, Ch.69: Bleeding and Thrombosis

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Antithrombin III is a protein that normally helps stop blood from clotting too much. When the gene for antithrombin III is mutated, this braking system does not work properly, and blood clots can form too easily. These clots most commonly form in the deep veins of the legs, but they can also travel to the lungs — a condition called a pulmonary embolism — which causes chest pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; may also affect pediatric patients with the inherited form

A specific mutation called G20210A causes the body to make too much prothrombin, so the blood becomes too likely to clot. These clots can travel to the lungs — a condition called a pulmonary embolism — which causes chest pain.

SPECIFIC DIFFERENTIAL: Factor V Leiden (Activated Protein C resistance)
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: A pathogenic variant (R506Q) of clotting factor V (OMIM) renders factor Va resistant to inactivation by activated protein C, resulting in a primary hyper-coagulated state. This state is often associated with deep-vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism, the latter of which causes chest pain.
RESOURCES: Han E, Burdsall K, Escamilla O. Factor V Leiden Heterozygous: Complicated by Recurrent Venous Thromboembolism and Bilateral Pulmonary Embolism. Cureus. 2024;16(7):e63701. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39092354/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Factor V is a protein that helps blood clot. In Factor V Leiden, a mutation in this protein makes it resistant to being turned off by another protein called activated protein C. This means clotting goes unchecked and blood clots form too easily. These clots can travel to the lungs — a condition called a pulmonary embolism — which causes chest pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; may also affect pediatric patients with the inherited form

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Dominant and recessive mutations in protein C (OMIM) can lead to unregulated fibrin generation due to the inability of protein C to inactivate factors VIIIa and Va. Activated factors VIIIa and Va result in a primary hyper-coagulated state, which is manifested most often with deep-vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism, the latter of which causes chest pain.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Protein C normally acts like a braking system in the body by turning off two clotting proteins called factors VIIIa and Va. When the gene for protein C is mutated, this braking system does not work, and these clotting factors stay active too long, causing blood clots to form too easily. These clots most commonly develop in deep veins but can also travel to the lungs — a condition called a pulmonary embolism — which causes chest pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; may also affect pediatric patients with the inherited form

Pathogenic variants in PROS1 (OMIM) cause a deficiency of protein S that leads to unregulated fibrin generation due to the inability of protein C to inactivate factors VIIIa and Va. These clots most commonly develop in deep veins but can also travel to the lungs — a condition called a pulmonary embolism — which causes chest pain.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Alpha-1 antitrypsin (OMIM) is an antiprotease and functions as a protector of lungs from protease-mediated tissue destruction. Deficiency of alpha-1 antitrypsin causes increased protease-mediated tissue destruction in the lungs, resulting in emphysema.  Emphysema clinically manifests with excessive cough which can lead to chest pain.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 13: Pathologic Alterations of the Extracellular Matrix that Involve Fibrillin, Elastin, or Proteoglycans

Mornex JF, Traclet J, Guillaud O, et al. Alpha1-antitrypsin deficiency: An updated review. Presse Med. 2023;52(3):104170. 
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37517655/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Alpha-1 antitrypsin is a protective protein that shields the lungs from being damaged by enzymes called proteases. When someone does not have enough alpha-1 antitrypsin, these proteases break down lung tissue, leading to a condition called emphysema, where the lungs become damaged and overinflated. Emphysema causes frequent, hard coughing, which can lead to chest pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult; diagnosed in pediatric patients but symptoms typically emerge in adulthood

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Sickle cell disease is a collection of red blood cell sickling disorders linked to mutations in the beta-globin gene.
Sickle cell anemia, the most severe type of sickle cell disease, is caused by a single amino acid mutation from glutamic acid to valine, resulting in a hydrophobic patch on the surface of beta-globin (OMIM), leading to polymerization of deoxyhemoglobin and the sickle shape of red blood cells (RBCs). Sickling of RBCs leads to vessel occlusion and ischemic pain (sickle cell crisis).  Acute chest syndrome is characterized by chest pain, tachypnea, fever, cough and arterial oxygen desaturation due to in situ RBC sickling at the lung, which is exacerbated by hypoxia.
RESOURCES:

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Sickle cell disease is caused by a mutation in the gene for a blood protein called beta-globin, which is part of hemoglobin — the molecule in red blood cells that carries oxygen. This mutation causes hemoglobin to clump together when oxygen levels are low, making red blood cells change from their normal round shape to a stiff, crescent or "sickle" shape. These sickled cells get stuck in blood vessels and block blood flow, causing pain. When sickling happens inside the blood vessels of the lungs, it causes a dangerous condition called acute chest syndrome, which causes chest pain, fast breathing, fever, cough, and low oxygen levels.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric; complications continue into adult patients

Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) deficiency can lead to mild to moderate hyperhomocysteinemia due to decreased conversion of homocysteine to cysteine in methionine metabolism. The two enzymes catalyzing the two subsequent steps in this pathway, cystathionine β-synthase (CBS, OMIM ) and cystathionine γ-lyase (CSE, OMIM), require pyridoxine as a coenzyme. In its absence, homocysteine remains elevated in the blood, leading to hyperhomocysteinemia, which accelerates the progression of atherosclerosis and plaque formation via several mechanisms, such as increased inflammation, oxidative stress, epigenetic modification, and lipoprotein metabolism alterations. Narrowing of coronary arteries due to the plaques can lead to angina. Thrombus formation at sites of plaque rupture leads to ischemia/infarction, which often manifests as chest pain.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025. Ch 20: Amino Acid Degradation and Synthesis

Tian W, Ju J, Guan B, et al. Role of hyperhomocysteinemia in atherosclerosis: from bench to bedside. Ann Med. 2025;57(1):2457527. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39898976/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin B6 is needed by two enzymes that help convert a substance called homocysteine into another molecule in the body. Without enough vitamin B6, these enzymes cannot do their job properly, and homocysteine builds up in the blood. Too much homocysteine causes inflammation and other damage that speeds up the buildup of plaques inside blood vessels. These plaques narrow the vessels that supply blood to the heart, which can cause chest pain called angina. If a plaque breaks open and a blood clot forms, blood flow to the heart can be blocked, leading to a heart attack and more severe chest pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult
