MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin  B9 (folate) deficiency can lead to mild to moderate hyperhomocysteinemia due to decreased conversion of homocysteine to methionine. Folate as 5-methyl-THF acts as a methyl donor for the regeneration of methylcobalamin, which is a required coenzyme in the methionine synthase-catalyzed reaction that converts homocysteine to methionine (MS, OMIM). The absence of folic acid limits the availability of methyl groups to regenerate methylcobalamin, the active form of vitamin B12 that serves as a coenzyme for methionine synthase. As a result, homocysteine may remain elevated in the blood, leading to hyperhomocysteinemia, which accelerates the progression of atherosclerosis and plaque formation via several mechanisms, such as increased inflammation, oxidative stress, epigenetic modification, and alterations in lipoprotein metabolism. Narrowing of coronary arteries due to the plaques can lead to angina. Thrombus formation at sites of plaque rupture leads to ischemia and infarction, which often manifest as chest pain.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hypoxemia may arise from multiple causes including those of cardiovascular and respiratory origin. Some of the mechanisms of leading to hypoxemia are: V/Q mismatch, right-to-left shunt, diffusion impairment, hypoventilation, and low inspired oxygen. In each of these conditions, the lower partial pressure of oxygen in the blood leads to high concentration of deoxygenated hemoglobin that confers a blue color.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your blood doesn't have enough oxygen due to problems with your heart or lungs. When blood doesn't carry enough oxygen, it turns dark blue instead of bright red. This blue color shows through your skin, especially in your lips, fingers, and toes, making them look bluish.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Pathogenic variants in the alpha (OMIM) or beta chain (OMIM) of hemoglobins lead to the oxidation of the heme iron to Fe³⁺, which cannot bind O₂ (aka methemoglobin).  

Additionally, pathogenic variants that result in a deficiency in the enzyme NADH-cytochrome b5 reductase, which reduces heme Fe³⁺ to Fe²⁺ (the form that binds O₂), lead to a similar outcome - oxygen deprivation and cyanosis.

Acquired methemoglobinemia  is caused by exposure to oxidizing agents (such as aniline dyes, nitrobenzene, nitrate, nitrite, benzocaine, prilocaine, dapsone, pyridium, nitric oxide, nitrous oxide and naphthalene) that are high enough to overwhelm the capacity of reducing enzymes.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Genetic changes or exposure to certain chemicals or medications cause the iron in your blood to change form so it can't carry oxygen anymore. Even though you're breathing normally, your blood can't deliver oxygen to your body, making your skin look blue or gray.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Abnormalities in the kidney result from pathogenic variants in isoforms of Type IV Collagen (OMIM).  These variants lead to glomeruli that are unable to properly filter the blood.  Hence, blood (hematuria) and protein (proteinuria) enter the urine.  The blood in the urine gives it an uncharacteristically dark color.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Cystinuria is due to an inherited deficiency (autosomal recessive; OMIM) in the basic amino acid transporter responsible for the transport of lysine, arginine, histidine, and cysteine amino acids. Cysteine cannot be reabsorbed from the blood, which ultimately causes accumulation of cysteine in the kidneys. Increased cystine in the urine causes crystals and stones to form. The stones damage the lining cells of the urinary tract resulting in blood to leaking into urine, giving it an uncharacteristically dark color.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Any number of genetic deficiencies in red blood cells can lead to higher than normal turnover, and hemolytic anemia and jaundice. Increased bilirubin production leads to an increase in urobilinogen in the urine causing a darker color. Briefly, G6PD deficiency (OMIM) leads to a decrease in the reduced form of glutathione and oxidative damage to RBCs, pyruvate kinase deficiency (OMIM) leads to a defect in red cell glycolysis and an energy shortage in RBCs; and aggregated filaments of hemoglobin lead to misshapen red blood cells in sickle cell disease (OMIM). Hemolysis in these conditions could further be triggered by multiple factors.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Rhabdomyolysis (muscle damage and breakdown of skeletal muscle) can be secondary to decreased activity levels of ATP due to various causes. There are two effects:  1) Increased intracellular Na+ concentration, which draws water into the cell. Due to this osmotic effect, the integrity of cell membrane is impacted. 2) Membrane potential changes, leading to sustained muscle contractions and depletion of ATP.  Both will lead to acute muscle breakdown and cell lysis. Ultimately, the exercise intolerance is due to extreme pain associated with rhabdomyolysis from the inflammatory markers released during cell lysis.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Acute and often severe skeletal muscle damage due to various causes results in increased intracellular free ionized calcium, leading to muscle cell death through the activation of a number of mechanisms such as enzymatic activation and prolonged muscle fibre contraction. Damage releases myoglobin, which is filtered through the kidneys and released in the urine (myoglobinuria), resulting in a red-brown color.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Myoglobinuria/Rhabdomyolysis caused by genetic deficiency in fatty acid oxidation (i.e. VLCAD; OMIM) or mitochondrial import (i.e. CPT1; OMIM). ATP deficit results from lack of ability of muscles to use fat as energy during high levels of exertion, which interferes with the function of muscle cells. MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Failure of adrenal glands to secrete/release enough gluco-(cortisol) and mineralocorticoid (aldosterone) can results from various factors. The lack of glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid activity results in electrolyte and metabolic imbalances leading to fatigue, lack of interest and loss of appetite- symptoms collective of major depressive disorder. If left untreated, adrenal insufficiency becomes fatal often resulting in adrenal crisis.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your adrenal glands sit on top of your kidneys and make important hormones that help your body stay balanced. In Addison's disease, these glands don't make enough of those hormones. Without them, your body's chemistry gets out of balance, which can make you feel very tired, lose interest in things you used to enjoy, and not want to eat — all signs that can look just like depression. If not treated, it can become a medical emergency.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Reduced secretion of thyroid hormone can lead to symptoms collective of major depressive disorder. The exact underlying biochemical mechanism is unknown but appear to be complex including hypotheses such as (1) low thyroid hormone leading to low basal metabolic rate and loss of appetite and fatigue; (2) changes in the HPT axis leading to imbalance in somatostatin and serotonin levels in the CNS. (3) Reduced 5-HT (5-hydroxytryptamine) activity leading to a lower threshold for developing depressive disorders.

This can make you feel tired, slow, and sad. It also throws off the balance of certain brain chemicals — including one called serotonin — that help control your mood. When serotonin levels are low, you're more likely to feel depressed.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Deficiency in neutral amino acid transporter solute carrier family (SLC6A19; OMIM) present in intestines (absorption) and kidneys (reabsorption) leads to deficiency in the transport of 8 out of 10 essential amino acids, including tryptophan. Excessive tryptophan is excreted in urine and feces leading to tryptophan wasting. Characterized by both neuropsychiatric and pellagra-like symptoms owing to deficient neurotransmitter and niacin synthesis from tryptophan.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your intestines and kidneys have special "doors" (transporters) that help absorb and keep important building blocks called amino acids in your body. In Hartnup disease, one of those doors is broken, so your body loses too much of an amino acid called tryptophan. Your brain needs tryptophan to make mood-lifting chemicals. Without enough tryptophan, those chemicals run low, which can lead to mood and mental health symptoms like depression.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically identified in childhood)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Several mechanisms are proposed for the pathogenesis of Huntington disease. Huntingtin (HTT) protein is normally involved in neurogenesis. Huntington Disease is a CAG-Trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder leading to the production of an abnormally long huntingtin (OMIM) protein with poly-glutamine. Beta sheets form in the abnormally expanded region leading to misfolding and formation of toxic aggregates. This overloads the ubiquitin proteasome degradation system. 

HTT, in the context of Huntington disease is involved in impaired production/transport/release of the BDNF (brain derived neurotrophic factor), necessary for downstream signalling involving neurotrophic Tyrosine Receptor Kinase B (TrkB). Disruption of serotonin signaling leading to defective hippocampal neurogenesis is implicated in mood disorders.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Huntington disease is caused by a gene that makes an abnormal version of a protein called huntingtin. This faulty protein folds the wrong way and clumps together, damaging brain cells over time. The damage disrupts signals that help the brain stay healthy and affects mood-related brain chemicals, including serotonin. This can lead to depression and other mood problems.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults (typically onset in midlife)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Parkinson disease (PD) characterized by degradation of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra, leading to decreased levels of dopamine. Misfolding of alpha synuclein protein in these neurons is considered to be the hallmark of Parkinson disease. Low levels of dopamine is implicated in symptoms collective of major depressive disorder. Prevalence of mood disorders such as depression is ~40% and PD medicines that increase dopamine have been shown to ameliorate depressive symptoms in these patients.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Parkinson's disease, brain cells that make a chemical called dopamine slowly break down and die. Dopamine helps control movement, but it also plays a big role in mood. When dopamine levels drop too low, it can cause feelings of sadness and loss of interest — symptoms of depression. In fact, about 4 in 10 people with Parkinson's experience depression.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily older adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Clinical features of BH4 deficiency/dihydrobiopterin reductase deficiency (OMIM) are attributed to both Hyperphenylalaninemia (HPA) and a reduced monoamine neurotransmitter synthesis. 
BH4 is a cofactor of tyrosine and tryptophan hydroxylases which serve in the synthesis of monoamine neurotransmitters dopamine and serotonin and hence deficiency leads to reduced neurotransmitter levels implicated in both neurodegenerative and psychiatric disorders.

In addition to the above, high concentration of Phe results in competitive inhibition of the large neutral amino acid transporter, LAT-1, leading to reduced uptake of tyrosine and tryptophan into the brain, in turn leading to reduced neurotransmitter synthesis secondary to high Phe and low tyrosine and tryptophan levels.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body needs a helper molecule called BH4 to make mood-related brain chemicals like dopamine and serotonin. When BH4 is missing or low, your brain can't produce enough of these chemicals. On top of that, a buildup of a certain amino acid in the blood blocks the brain from getting other building blocks it needs to make these chemicals. The result is low dopamine and serotonin, which can cause depression and other mental health symptoms.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Low sunlight during winter months results in minimal exposure to Vitamin D. Vitamin D is identified to being a key regulator of Tryptophan hydroxylase 2 (TPH2) enzyme that catalyzes rate limiting step of serotonin synthesis from tryptophan. Deficiency in vitamin D leading to reduced transcription of TPH2 via VDRE (Vitamin D response elements) is implicated in neuropsychiatric symptoms.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: During winter, we get less sunlight, which means our bodies make less Vitamin D. Vitamin D helps control an enzyme that your brain uses to make serotonin — a chemical that helps keep your mood stable. When Vitamin D is low, less serotonin gets made, and that can lead to the low mood and sadness seen in seasonal depression.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults, though adolescents can also be affected

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Vitamin A and its derivatives such as isotretinoin (acne medication) work via retinoid receptors that are widely distributed throughout the CNS. Retinoid receptors are nuclear receptors involved in transcription for growth and maintenance of epithelial tissue. 

Excess usage/consumption of vitamin A derivatives are associated with mood disorders and are implicated in inducing depression-related behaviors by decreasing adult neurogenesis and/or altering the expression of components of serotonergic neurotransmitter system, thereby leading to impaired serotonin signaling. 

Suggested causal links between isotretinoin usage and depressive symptoms is speculative and warrants further investigation. No consistent link is reported through epidemiological studies.

When too much of this medication is used, it may reduce the growth of new brain cells and interfere with serotonin signaling — the brain's mood-regulating system. This may contribute to depression in some people, though the link is not fully proven and is still being studied.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adolescents and young adults (typical acne treatment age group)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Niacin deficiency can result from multiple factors that result in the absence of feedback inhibition of the kynurenine pathway. This will lead to shunting of Trp to elevate kynurenine pathway intermediates implicated in mood disorders. Trp deficiency, in turn, leads to reduced serotonin synthesis. This condition is characterized by three Ds called diarrhea, dermatitis and dementia. Dementia from chronic deficiency can lead to depression.
RESOURCES:

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30813414/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body uses an amino acid called tryptophan to make both serotonin (a mood chemical) and niacin (Vitamin B3). When niacin is lacking, the body sends most of the tryptophan down a different pathway, leaving very little to make serotonin. Low serotonin can lead to depression. Severe, long-term niacin deficiency also causes a condition called pellagra, which includes diarrhea, skin problems, and mental decline that can worsen into depression.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults, particularly those with poor nutrition or malabsorption

Without them, the brain's messaging system doesn't work properly, which can lead to mental health symptoms like depression.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Folate plays a key role in one carbon metabolism. 5-Methyltetrahydrofolate and homocysteine are combined in a reaction catalyzed by vitamin B12-dependent methionine synthase to produce L-methionine, which combines with ATP to form S-Adenosylmethionine (SAM). SAM is a key intermediate for several transmethylation reactions of catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) in the central nervous system. A defect in one-carbon metabolism via folate deficiency may be implicated in mood disorders.

SAM is used by the brain to keep important mood-related chemicals — like adrenaline and noradrenaline — working properly. When folate is low, this chain breaks down, SAM levels drop, and the brain's mood-regulating system can be disrupted, potentially leading to depression.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hartnup disease is caused by mutations in the SLC6A19 gene (OMIM) that encodes for a sodium-dependent neutral amino acid co-transporter protein, which is present in the kidneys and intestines. The resultant defective amino-acid transporter primarily results in the inability to reabsorb neutral amino acids by kidneys, leading to their loss in urine.  The defective amino-acid transporter also reduces the absorption of neutral amino-acids in the intestines, further contributing to loss of neutral amino acids. Loss of tryptophan particularly is responsible for the development of pellagra-like symptoms since tryptophan is a precursor for Niacin synthesis. As described in niacin deficiency, reduced levels of NAD affect DNA repair and levels of kynurenic acids and urocanic acid, which leads to photosensitive dermatitis.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Porphyria cutanea tarda is caused by defects in the heme synthesis pathway. A deficiency of uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase (OMIM) leads to the accumulation of intermediate porphyrins in the blood and tissues. Upon exposure to sunlight, these porphyrins absorb the light and get activated, which eventually leads to generation of reactive oxygen species that cause damage to the skin and other structures. Exposure to sunlight in patients with Porphyria cutanea tarda is associated with increased fragility and blistering of the skin.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: The retinoic acid (RA) form of Vitamin A acts as a transcription factor by binding and activating Retinoic acid receptor (RAR)/ retinoid X receptor (RXR) heterodimer. The RA bound heterodimer then regulates expression of genes through the retinoic acid response element (RARE). This transcriptional activity is required for normal growth and maintenance of epithelial tissue. Excess vitamin A, primarily due to excessive intake of supplements, therapeutic retinoids, or foods containing high-levels of Vitamin A, results in excessive proliferation of keratinocytes in the skin as well as abnormal differentiation. This leads to scaly, dry, red, and itchy skin. This is referred to as retinoid dermatitis.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Niacin deficiency or Pellagra may result from multiple factors. Photosensitive dermatitis is one of the clinical features of niacin deficiency. The derivative of niacin, NAD, is crucial for DNA repair as it acts as a coenzyme for the Poly ADP ribose polymerase-1 (PARP-1) enzyme. Lack of niacin thus results in improper DNA repair and increased turnover of cells in epithelial tissues, such as skin, leading to dermatitis. Additionally, a lack of niacin leads to increased production from Trp precursor, resulting in excessive kynurenic acid (Trp metabolite) and decreased urocanic acid (His metabolite) levels which could lead to photosensitivity.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Pyridoxal phosphate (PLP), an active form of Vitamin B6 is an important cofactor in many reactions, such as decarboxylation reactions, and transamination reactions. PLP is a cofactor in the kynurenic pathway, which is required for NAD synthesis. Hence, PLP deficiency, which could have multiple etiologies, can result in pellagra-like symptoms and can present as photosensitive dermatitis. PLP deficiency is more commonly associated with seborrheic dermatitis (in oily areas of the skin), however, the exact mechanism is not clear. Some proposed mechanisms are linked to its role in the synthesis of sphingolipids and collagen and also its antioxidant properties. Like many other B complex vitamins, B6 deficiency causes cheilosis (scaling and cracking of lips and corner of mouth).

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin K is mainly synthesized in adults by gut bacteria. Newborns, however, have minimal vitamin K reserves in their liver during the time of delivery and are not able to synthesize vitamin K due to a sterile gut. One of the main functions of vitamin K is gamma-carboxylation of coagulant factors- II, VII, XI, and X. This converts inactive clotting factors into an active state. Deficiency leads to the inadequate activity of these clotting factors, which results in bleeding in the gut and the subsequent melena. If the bleeding takes place in the lower part of the GI tract, then stools may appear either fresh bright red or dark red, depending on the location of the bleed.

SPECIAL NOTES: Vitamin K deficiency bleeding (VKDB) in neonates varies depending on the type of VKDB and whether the infant receives vitamin K prophylaxis.  Early and classical VKDB occurs in 1 in 60 to 1 in 250 newborns, but is higher for infants whose mothers took certain medications during pregnancy. Without vitamin K prophylaxis, the incidence is 0.25% to 1.7%.  Late VKDB occurs in 1 in 14,000 to 1 in 25,000 infants.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Newborn babies don't have the bacteria needed to make vitamin K, and they have very little vitamin K stored in their livers. Without vitamin K, their blood can't clot properly, which can cause bleeding in the digestive tract. This makes stools look black and tarry, or red, depending on where the bleeding occurs.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (newborns)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: One of the main functions of vitamin K is gamma-carboxylation of coagulant factors- II, VII, XI, and X. This carboxylation converts inactive clotting factors into an active state. By inhibiting vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKOR), warfarin blocks the regeneration of the functional form of vitamin K.  Vitamin K deficiency leads to the inadequate activity of these clotting factors, which results in bleeding in the gut and the subsequent melena. If the bleeding takes place in the lower part of the GI tract, then stools may appear either fresh bright red or dark red, depending on the location of the bleed.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Anemia is a condition that develops when the blood contains lower than a normal amount of red blood cells (RBC) and is not able to carry and deliver enough oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body. This may be due to blood loss, decrease in RBC production or an increase in RBC destruction. This in turn leads to lack of oxygen delivery to tissues, decreasing the cellular capacity for oxidative phosphorylation/ETC and ATP production. Decreased ATP generation in the brain commonly leads to the symptom of feeling lightheaded or dizzy.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch 29: Micronutrients: Minerals

Robbins and Cotran Pathological Basis of Disease, 10e. 2021. Ch. 14: Red Blood Cell and Bleeding Disorders

A.D.A.M. Medical Encyclopedia [Internet]. Johns Creek (GA): Ebix, Inc., A.D.A.M.; c1997-2023. Anemia; [reviewed 2021 Jul 1; cited 2024 Dec 17]
https://medlineplus.gov/anemia.html
SPECIAL NOTES: Normal range for RBC is 4 - 6 million cells/mcL. Hemoglobin (Hb) is the oxygen-carrying protein present in RBCs. Normal range of Hb is 12 - 15 gm/dL in adult women and 14 - 17 gm/dL in adult men. Normal ranges differ between men and women and may be affected by other factors such as age, race and high altitude. Anemia can be defined as an RBC count of less than 4.5 million/mcL for adult males and less than 4 million/mcL for adult females.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Anemia happens when your blood does not have enough red blood cells to carry oxygen from your lungs to the rest of your body. This can happen because of blood loss, your body not making enough red blood cells, or your body destroying too many red blood cells. When your body's cells — especially your brain cells — don't get enough oxygen, they can't make enough energy to work properly. This lack of energy in the brain is what causes you to feel lightheaded or dizzy.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by anemia.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: A number of drugs may cause dizziness or vertigo as an adverse drug reaction (ADR). These include anticonvulsants (e.g. voltage-gated sodium channel blockers like oxcarbazepine and carbamazepine), analgesics (acetaminophen), antihypertensive (e.g. ACE inhibitors, calcium-channel blockers, statins), antidiabetic, antibiotics (e.g. quinolones, aminoglycosides, macrolides); antifungals, and anti inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs, aspirin).  General mechanisms by which these drugs cause dizziness include a rapid or sudden fall in blood pressure or glucose levels, or dehydration due to water or mineral loss, which could lead to dizziness.
RESOURCES: Chimirri S, Aiello R, Mazzitello C, et al. Vertigo/dizziness as a Drugs' adverse reaction. J Pharmacol Pharmacother. 2013;4(Suppl 1):S104-S109. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3853661/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Some medicines can cause dizziness or a spinning feeling as a side effect. This can happen in a few different ways. Some medicines can cause your blood pressure to drop quickly and suddenly, which reduces the blood flow to your brain. Others can cause your blood sugar to fall too low, which means your brain doesn't get the fuel it needs to work properly. Some medicines can also cause your body to lose too much water or important minerals, which can also make you feel dizzy.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by drug-induced dizziness.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When your blood pressure is too low, your heart is not pumping enough blood to deliver oxygen to your brain. Without enough oxygen, your brain cannot work properly, which can make you feel lightheaded or dizzy. Sometimes this happens when you stand up quickly, because the blood temporarily pools in your legs before your body has a chance to adjust.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by hypotension.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: The leading conditions that cause hypercholesterolemia are: Type II Familial hypercholesterolemia (LDL receptor deficiency); Type III Familial dysbetalipoproteinemia hypercholesterolemia (Apo-E deficiency); and Type IV Familial hyperlipidemia (VLDL metabolism defect).  These are risk factors for LDL accumulation in blood vessels that contributes to foam cell formation, which can lead to atherosclerosis/coronary artery disease.  Blood clots can form at sites of plaque rupture leading to a lack of blood flow to the brain (an ischemic stroke).  Stroke can present as dizziness if patients lose balance or present as syncope.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Some people are born with genes that cause their bodies to build up too much bad cholesterol (LDL) in the blood. Over time, this extra cholesterol can stick to the walls of blood vessels and cause a buildup called plaque. If a piece of this plaque breaks off, it can cause a blood clot that blocks blood flow to the brain. When the brain doesn't get enough blood and oxygen, this can cause dizziness, loss of balance, or even fainting.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult patients are affected by atherosclerosis.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Carbon monoxide will compete for oxygen binding to heme and, at high enough concentrations, will displace the oxygen from binding the iron of the heme of hemoglobin in red blood cells. The ensuring hypoxic conditions can lead to lactic acidosis due to decreased rate of the electron transport chain (ETC) and oxidative phosphorylation, which consequently decreases production of ATP. Decreased ATP production causes osmotic imbalances due to increased intracellular sodium. High concentrations of intracellular sodium results in water retention and cell swelling that damages both the plasma membrane and the membranes of organelles. Decreased rate of ETC and ATP production impairs the function of brain and neurons that are most reliant on the ATP for cellular functions.  This can lead to dizziness and syncope.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 16: Erythropoiesis, Hemoglobin Function, and the CBC; Ch. 23: Oxidative Phosphorylation and Mitochondrial Diseases

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Carbon monoxide is a dangerous gas that competes with oxygen to attach to the red blood cells that carry oxygen through your body. When carbon monoxide takes the place of oxygen in your red blood cells, your body — especially your brain — is starved of oxygen. Without enough oxygen, your brain cells cannot make the energy they need to work properly. This also causes a harmful buildup of waste products inside your cells, which causes the cells to swell and become damaged. This damage to the brain and its cells is what causes dizziness and can even lead to fainting.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by carbon monoxide poisoning.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: B12 is a cofactor for methylmalonyl CoA mutase and homocysteine methyltransferase. In individuals with B12 deficiency, the elevated levels of methylmalonic acid (and homocysteine) contribute to myelin damage which can affect different parts of the spinal cord resulting in peripheral neuropathy, lack of proprioception and ataxia. Lack of proprioception can lead to disequilibrium (cervical vertigo/cervicogenic dizziness). Additionally, the anemia resulting from Vitamin B12 deficiency could also contribute dizziness as described in the 'Anemia' section.
RESOURCES:

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin B12 is an important nutrient that your body needs to keep the protective covering around your nerves — called myelin — healthy and intact. When you don't have enough Vitamin B12, this protective covering can get damaged. When the nerves that help your body sense its position in space are damaged, your body has trouble knowing where it is and keeping its balance, which can make you feel dizzy or unsteady. On top of this, Vitamin B12 deficiency can also cause anemia, which means your blood cannot carry enough oxygen to your brain, and this can add to the feeling of dizziness.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by Vitamin B12 deficiency.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Chronic excessive alcohol intake leads to fatty liver, which can then lead to alcoholic hepatitis, and eventually cirrhosis. Cirrhosis decreases blood flow through the liver, leading to portal hypertension and increased pressure in peripheral veins, which in turn causes fluid accumulation in the extracellular space. Additionally, a damaged liver cannot synthesis enough proteins, such as albumin, leading to hypoalbuminemia. This results in decreased plasma oncotic pressure edema and can lead to edema. Next, high alcohol intake can also lead to alcoholic cardiomyopathy. Alcoholic cardiomyopathy can first lead to episodes of left ventricle heart failure. In advanced cases, this progresses to right ventricle failure, further increasing pressure in peripheral veins and exacerbating fluid retention in the extracellular space.

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 30: Metabolism of Ethanol and the Consequences of Alcohol Dependence Syndrome.

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35593142/

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32098364/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Drinking too much alcohol over a long time damages the liver, causing fat buildup and scarring (cirrhosis). The scarred liver can't make enough proteins like albumin and blocks blood flow, causing fluid to leak into surrounding tissues. Heavy drinking can also weaken the heart muscle, making it unable to pump blood efficiently, which further causes fluid to back up in the legs and feet.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: A high concentration of LDL-cholesterol over many years increases a person's risk for atherosclerosis. A high concentration of LDL-cholesterol (and other factors) favors engulfment by macrophages of lipoproteins containing oxidized cholesterol, resulting in plaques on walls of arteries, leading to stenosis and occlusion. Plaques can also break off and lead to occlusion of a smaller vessel. Occlusion of coronary arteries can lead to heart failure. Right-sided heart failure leads to back pressure in veins and build-up of fluid tissues. Left-sided heart failure decreases the cardiac output. It can also lead to back-up of blood via lungs in the right ventricle, thereby damaging it. Heart failure, in general, also has systemic effects such as stimulating baroreceptors in the carotid sinus ultimately resulting in increased water retention by the kidneys.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 29, Cholesterol Metabolism and Hypercholesterolemia

Clark AL, Cleland JG. Causes and treatment of oedema in patients with heart failure. Nat Rev Cardiol. 2013;10(3):156-170. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23319101/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: High cholesterol over many years causes fatty deposits (plaques) to build up in arteries. These plaques can block blood flow to the heart, weakening the heart muscle. When the heart can't pump blood effectively, blood backs up in veins causing fluid to collect in the legs and feet. The weak heart also triggers the kidneys to hold onto more water and salt, worsening the swelling.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Chronic hyperglycemia, potentially through mechanisms involving advanced glycation end products and reactive oxygen species (ROS), can give rise to diabetic nephropathy. This nephropathy leads to large losses of protein into the urine, causing low serum albumin levels. Reduced concentration of albumin in blood decreases the oncotic pressure. As a result, a reduced amount of fluid is held in the bloodstream. This increases the volume of fluid in extracellular spaces, causing peripheral edema. 
The nephropathy can also give rise to dyslipidemia. This collection of symptoms is called nephrotic syndrome. The dyslipidemia seems to be due to decreased removal of triglycerides as well as LDL lipoprotein particles. The dyslipidemia can lead to atherosclerosis and heart failure as described above.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 39: Diabetes

Alicic RZ, Rooney MT, Tuttle KR. Diabetic Kidney Disease: Challenges, Progress, and Possibilities. Clin J Am Soc Nephrol. 2017;12(12):2032-2045.   https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28522654/ 

Politano SA, Colbert GB, Hamiduzzaman N. Nephrotic Syndrome. Prim Care. 2020;47(4):597-613.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33121631/ 

Agrawal S, Zaritsky JJ, Fornoni A, Smoyer WE.  Dyslipidemia in nephrotic syndrome: mechanisms and treatment.  Nat Rev Nephrol. 2018 Jan;14(1):57-70.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29176657/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Chronic high blood sugar damages the kidneys' filtering system. The damaged kidneys leak large amounts of protein (especially albumin) into the urine. Without enough protein in the blood to hold fluid in blood vessels, water leaks out into surrounding tissues, causing swelling in the legs and feet. The kidney damage can also cause cholesterol problems that may lead to heart disease.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult (can occur in pediatric type 1 diabetes after many years)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Chronic hypothyroidism leads to a decreased pulse rate and decreased breakdown of glycosaminoglycans (GAG), resulting in deposition of hyaluronic acid (nonsulfated GAG) in the extracellular space. The highly hydrophilic GAGs draw water, leading to accumulation of fluid in the extracellular space. This results in non-pitting edema and is referred to as myxedema.
RESOURCES:

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When the thyroid gland doesn't make enough thyroid hormone, the body slows down and can't break down certain sugar-based molecules (glycosaminoglycans) properly. These molecules pile up in tissues and act like sponges, attracting and holding water. This causes a special type of firm swelling called myxedema, especially in the legs and face.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD, formerly known as NAFLD) is most often caused by obesity. Individuals with MASLD can develop metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease (MASH, formerly NASH) and ultimately cirrhosis. Cirrhosis impairs blood flow through the liver, resulting in portal hypertension, which increases the pressure in peripheral veins. In turn, this leads to increased amounts of fluid in the extracellular space. Additionally, a damaged liver cannot synthesis enough proteins, such as albumin, leading to hypoalbuminemia. This results in decreased plasma oncotic pressure edema and can lead to edema.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Fat builds up in the liver (often due to obesity), which can lead to scarring (cirrhosis). The scarred liver blocks blood flow and can't make enough proteins like albumin. Low albumin means fluid leaks out of blood vessels into surrounding tissues. The blocked blood flow also increases pressure in veins, pushing more fluid into the legs and abdomen.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Insufficient protein intake reduces serum albumin levels, which lowers the oncotic pressure in the blood and increases water retention in the extracellular space. Protein-energy malnutrition (PEM) is a concern for patients with chronic renal failure undergoing dialysis, individuals with cachexia, and those facing food insecurity or deprivation. Systemic edema can lead to swelling in the intestinal lining, resulting in decreased nutrient absorption, which further exacerbates the edema.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Thiamine pyrophosphate is a cofactor of pyruvate dehydrogenase, alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase, and transketolase. Thiamine deficiency (e.g., due to alcohol-use disorder, gastric bypass surgery, poor nutrition, or malnutrition) can lead to high-output heart failure and dilated cardiomyopathy. The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system is activated and leads to fluid overload with accumulation of fluid in the extracellular space. Additionally, right-sided heart failure leads to back pressure in veins and build-up of fluid tissues. Thiamine deficiency is commonly measured using an assay measuring RBC transketolase activity.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Megaloblastic anemia can develop either due to folate or B12 deficiency. These vitamins are cofactors in one carbon metabolism, a network of reaction critical for DNA synthesis. Impaired nucleotide and DNA production due to these vitamin deficiency can affect the erythropoietic process causing delay in nuclear maturation compared to cytoplasmic maturation. This nuclear cytoplasmic dyssynchrony will lead to ineffective erythropoiesis. Impaired and immature RBC production leads to deficient oxygen delivery to tissues leading to reduced oxidative phosphorylation. Decreased ATP production can cause skeletal muscle fatigue and exercise intolerance.
RESOURCES:

https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/204066-overview
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body needs folate (Vitamin B9) to make DNA, which is the instruction manual for building new cells. Red blood cells are made constantly, and without enough folate, they can't develop properly — they end up oversized and immature. These abnormal red blood cells can't carry oxygen as well as healthy ones. Without enough oxygen reaching your muscles, your body can't make the energy it needs to exercise, leading to fatigue and exercise intolerance.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults, particularly those with poor diet or malabsorption

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Microcytic anemia can develop due to iron deficiency, vitamin B6 deficiency, or due to lead poisoning.  These conditions lead to impaired production of hemoglobin resulting in reduced hemoglobin levels in the erythrocytes. This leads to reduced oxygen delivery to tissues, decreasing the cellular capacity for oxidative phosphorylation/ETC. Decreased ATP generation can cause skeletal or respiratory muscular fatigue.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Red blood cells carry oxygen using a protein called hemoglobin. When iron or Vitamin B6 is too low, or when lead gets into the body, red blood cells can't make enough hemoglobin. With less hemoglobin, red blood cells can't carry as much oxygen to your muscles. Without enough oxygen, your muscles and even your breathing muscles run out of energy quickly, causing fatigue and exercise intolerance.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages; iron deficiency is especially common in children and women of childbearing age

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Regardless of etiology of the anemia, anemic individuals can develop exercise intolerance due to insufficient oxygen transport to tissues; e.g. exercising muscle.

Hemolytic anemia due to G6PD enzyme deficiency: Individuals with G6PD deficiency have increased risk of hemolysis due to a decreased ability to produce NADPH and, thus, decreased ability to maintain glutathione in its reduced state in erythrocytes. Oxidative stress causes ROS-mediated episodic hemolysis within hours. The most common triggers of oxidative stress are viral and bacterial infections, specific drugs such as antimalarial drugs and sulfonamide antibiotics, and certain foods such as fava beans. These common triggers cause reactions with O2, either enzymatically or nonenzymatically, to produce ROS. Extensive hemolysis can ultimately result in hemolytic anemia.

Hemolytic anemia due to pyruvate kinase enzyme deficiency: Genetic deficiency of pyruvate kinase results in an insufficient amount of ATP production and can cause hemolysis of the erythrocytes.
Since erythrocytes without ATP cannot maintain the function of the Na+/K+ ATPase pump in the plasma membrane. This results in a net accumulation of Na+ inside the cells, which subsequently attracts water causes hemolysis. Signs and symptoms of hemolytic anemia due to pyruvate kinase deficiency are increased concentrations of 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate and enlarged spleen.

Hemolytic anemia due hemoglobinopathies (Sickle cell disease, OMIM; or thalassemia): Sickle cell anemia is an autosomal recessive disorder, meaning that homozygotes develop sickle cell disease, whereas heterozygotes are generally asymptomatic carriers. Carriers do not develop symptoms unless there are extreme circumstances, such as severe hypoxia, deoxygenation of hemoglobin, high temperatures, or a low pH environment. The amino acid substitution from glutamic acid to valine in the β-globin gene of these individuals causes the formation of long polymers between the deoxyhemoglobin molecules. The valine residue in sickle hemoglobin (HbS) creates a hydrophobic patch that increases the hydrophobic interactions between deoxy- HbS molecules. This leads to formation of rod-shaped HbS polymers within erythrocytes, which gives rise to their characteristic sickle shape.

Deficiency of β-globin synthesis results in β-thalassemia (OMIM) and deficiency of α-globin synthesis results in α-thalassemia (OMIM). An imbalance in the ratio of alpha and beta chains of hemoglobin causes hemolysis in both thalassemias. 

In α-thalassemia, excess β-globin chains aggregate, while in β-thalassemia, excess α-globin chains aggregate. In either disease heterozygote are generally asymptomatic, although they may develop anemia in varying degrees due to both ineffective erythropoiesis and hemolysis.

Marks’ Basic Medical Biochemistry: A Clinical Approach, 6e., 2023 Ch. 42: The Biochemistry of Erythrocytes and Other Blood Cells

https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/200390-overview
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Regardless of the specific cause, when red blood cells are destroyed faster than the body can replace them, there aren't enough healthy red blood cells to carry oxygen to the muscles. Without enough oxygen, muscles can't make the energy they need, leading to fatigue and exercise intolerance. In G6PD deficiency, certain triggers like infections, specific medicines, or fava beans cause red blood cells to break down. In pyruvate kinase deficiency, red blood cells can't make enough energy to stay intact and burst open. In sickle cell disease, red blood cells change into a stiff, curved shape that blocks blood flow and breaks apart easily. In thalassemia, an imbalance in the building blocks of hemoglobin causes red blood cells to break down.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages; many of these are genetic conditions identified in childhood

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Exercise intolerance is important symptom of heart diseases or blood circulation problems. Patients with atherosclerosis have narrowing of arteries [such as coronary arteries] due to buildup of atherosclerotic plaque.  A risk factor for this are conditions that lead to high LDL cholesterol as well as low HDL cholesterol, impacting the clearance of cholesterol from the blood.  Higher LDL levels will remain in circulation longer, increasing risk of becoming deposited and oxidized at site of injury in blood vessel intima.  This can trigger an inflammatory response response including foam cell formation, a starting point for atherosclerosis.  Exercise intolerance can result from impaired myocardial function due to narrowed coronary arteries and reduced cardiac output in these patients.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your heart pumps blood through tubes called arteries to supply oxygen and nutrients to your entire body. In atherosclerosis, a waxy buildup called plaque narrows these tubes — especially the ones that feed the heart itself. A key risk factor is having too much "bad" (LDL) cholesterol in the blood, which can build up in artery walls and trigger inflammation. When the heart's own blood supply is reduced, it can't pump as strongly, meaning less blood and oxygen reach the muscles during exercise, causing exercise intolerance.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, especially middle-aged and older adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Muscular dystrophy is caused by pathogenic variants of the DMD gene located at X chromosome (OMIM). DMD gene encodes for a cytoskeletal protein dystrophin. Normal function of dystrophin protein is to anchor intracellular cytoskeletal proteins to the sarcolemma (the plasma membrane of muscle cells) and the extracellular proteins. This mechanical stability is critical for muscle contraction. Defects in dystrophin cause loss of myofibers and skeletal muscle weakness, which could result in exercise intolerance. Becker MD (OMIM) is also caused by partial loss-of-function mutations in the DMD gene, leading to milder phenotypes. Exercise intolerance phenotype is experienced in patients with both variants.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Muscles need a special protein called dystrophin to stay strong and work properly. Dystrophin acts like an anchor, holding the inside of muscle cells connected to the outside. Without it, muscle fibers break down over time, making the muscles weaker. This weakness makes it hard to exercise or even perform everyday activities. A related condition called Becker muscular dystrophy is caused by a partial loss of this same protein and leads to milder symptoms.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically diagnosed in early childhood; primarily affects males)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Mutations in the CPT2 gene affects the oxidation of long-chain fatty acids in the mitochondria (OMIM). For the oxidation of long-chain fatty acids carnitine is used in the carnitine shuttle to bring long-chain fatty acids into the mitochondria. Formation of fatty acyl-CoA, initial step of fatty acid oxidation, is catalyzed by the fatty acyl CoA synthetase enzyme. The fatty acyl CoA must then be transported into the mitochondrial matrix where the oxidation occurs. Short-chain (C4-C6) and medium chain (C8-C10) fatty acids are taken up by a monocarboxylate transporter and activated to acyl-CoA in the mitochondrial matrix. Long-chain (C12-C18) fatty acids require carrier carnitine in order to cross the inner mitochondrial membrane. The carnitine shuttle is facilitated by two important enzymes: carnitine palmitoyl transferase I (CPT1) and carnitine palmitoyl transferase II (CPT2). The rate-limiting enzyme CPT1 catalyzes the removal of the CoA and the addition of carnitine to the fatty acyl on the outer mitochondrial membrane. The acylcarnitine is transferred across the inner mitochondrial membrane into the mitochondrial matrix through a carnitine-acylcarnitine antiporter (aka carnitine translocase). Inside the matrix, carnitine is removed and recycled back. The CPT2 transfers the CoA back onto the fatty acid to form fatty acyl CoA, allowing for the initiation of the β-oxidation reactions. The lack of ATP generation due to impaired β-oxidation leads to exercise intolerance.

CPT2 deficiency will typically present in patients soon after puberty. The patient may have red or cola-colored urine, myoglobinuria, and will complain of recurrent episodic myalgia and muscle stiffness. 
Lab values will present a marked elevation of serum creatine kinase (CK) due to rhabdomyolysis. 
The patient will also complain that the symptoms are exacerbated by prolonged exercise, stress, infections, fasting and/or by a high-fat, low-carb diet.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body uses fat as a fuel source during prolonged exercise. To burn fat for energy, it first needs to be transported into a part of the cell called the mitochondria. A molecule called carnitine acts like a shuttle to carry fat in. CPT-II is a protein that helps complete this shuttle process. When CPT-II is faulty, fat gets stuck and can't be burned for energy. Without enough energy, muscles tire out quickly, causing pain, stiffness, and exercise intolerance. Symptoms are often triggered by prolonged exercise, stress, illness, fasting, or a high-fat diet.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adolescents and young adults (typically presenting after puberty)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Carnitine deficiency may occur if carnitine is not readily available in the diet or may occur due to a faulty plasma membrane carnitine transporter. Primary carnitine deficiency is autosomal recessive disorder, which is due to mutations in the organic cation/carnitine transporter (OCTN2/SLC22A5) gene (OMIM). A faulty plasma membrane carnitine transporter, either systemic or specifically in the muscle, would result in a primary deficiency of carnitine. The condition specifically in the muscle would prevent β-oxidation from occurring. The lack of ATP generation due to impaired β-oxidation leads to exercise intolerance.

A secondary carnitine deficiency may occur due to other metabolic disorders, such as other fatty acid oxidation disorders, organic acidemias, or in preterm newborns. The age of onset of carnitine deficiency varies between 1-month to 7-years of age. 

Patients with carnitine deficiency present with hypoketotic hypoglycemia, as gluconeogenesis cannot be supported by fatty acid oxidation. Other signs and symptoms include: skeletal myopathies, cardiomyopathies, encephalopathy, and hepatomegaly. Patients’ urine may be dark red, due to myoglobinuria.
RESOURCES:

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body uses fat as a fuel source, but fat needs a helper molecule called carnitine to get inside the part of the cell that burns it for energy. Without enough carnitine — either because it's missing from the diet or because the body's transporter for carnitine is broken — fat can't be burned properly. This means muscles don't get the energy they need, leading to weakness and exercise intolerance. Low blood sugar can also develop because the body can't fall back on fat when glucose runs out.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (onset typically between 1 month and 7 years of age)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
The McArdle disease/GSD V (OMIM) affects the skeletal muscles during strenuous exercise due to genetic deficiency of muscle glycogen phosphorylase. Due to the deficiency of this enzyme, glycogen molecules accumulate in the cytoplasm of the skeletal muscle cells and glucose is not made available to muscles. Lack of glucose results in ATP deficiency, which can lead to  exercise intolerance and frequent muscle cramps. Insufficient production of ATP in the muscle during strenuous exercise can lead to muscle breakdown and rhabdomyolysis. Rhabdomyolysis also results in pain and exercise intolerance. It is also associated high creatine kinase levels and release of myoglobin. Excretion of myoglobin in the urine turns the color of urine to red-brown color.

Glucose oxidation in this condition will be limited in the muscle during strenuous exercise, so the lactate does not accumulate. However, the muscle are able to oxidize fatty acids for energy (ATP) production, so patients can perform aerobic exercise.

• Exercise intolerance
• Muscle cramping
• Myoglobinuria (with strenuous exercise)
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your muscles store a sugar called glycogen to use as fuel during intense exercise. To use this stored sugar, a special enzyme called muscle glycogen phosphorylase is needed to break it down. In McArdle disease, this enzyme is missing, so the muscles can't access their stored fuel during hard exercise. Without fuel, muscles run out of energy quickly, causing cramps, fatigue, and exercise intolerance. During very intense exercise, the muscle cells can break down, causing dark-colored urine. Interestingly, muscles can still burn fat for energy, so steady, moderate aerobic exercise is usually tolerated better.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Typically identified in childhood but often not diagnosed until adulthood

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Due to deficiency of muscle isoform of phosphofructokinase 1 (PFK1) (OMIM), which catalyzes the rate-limiting step in glycolysis. This enzyme deficiency causes decreased glycolysis and accumulation of the substrate fructose-6-phosphate. This accumulated fructose 6-phosphate can isomerize to glucose 6-phosphate. Increased levels of glucose-6-phosphate leads to increased glycogen formation. Increased levels of glycogen in the muscle cells cause myopathy, muscle pain and exercise intolerance. Furthermore, the decreased glycolysis results in ATP deficiency during anaerobic exercise, which can lead to  exercise intolerance and frequent muscle cramps. Other symptoms include rhabdomyolysis, and myoglobinuria.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body breaks down sugars through a process called glycolysis to make energy for exercise. A key "gatekeeper" enzyme called PFK1 controls the speed of this process in muscles. When PFK1 is missing, sugar can't be broken down efficiently, and unused sugar building blocks pile up and get converted into glycogen, which accumulates in muscle cells. Both the buildup of glycogen and the lack of energy production cause muscle pain, cramps, and exercise intolerance.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically identified in childhood)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Individuals with lung problems may develop exercise intolerance due to difficulty of breathing and oxygenation of muscle cells. Alpha 1 antitrypsin deficiency (OMIM) increases the risk for developing panacinar emphysema due to the excessive destruction of elastin within the lungs by the uninhibited action of elastase.

Genetic mutations and variations in the α1-antitrypsin gene (SERPINA1) cause production of abnormal levels of α1-antitrypsin due to misfolding and aggregation, ranging from a reduced level to none. This reduced production of the antitrypsin in the liver may result in early-onset emphysema. Normally, the protease inhibitor α1-antitrypsin functions to limit the damage caused by activated neutrophils. The activated neutrophils release an enzyme called elastase, which causes degradation of the protein elastin. The protease inhibitor α1-antitrypsin essentially puts the brakes on elastase’s destructive power. 
Among the allelic variations of the SERPINA1 gene, the Z and S variants are most common. While variant M is the normal form of SERPINA1, the Z variant is the most severe form, resulting in only 10% α1-antitrypsin activity. Patients with the Z variant of this gene are at high risk for lung diseases such as early-onset of COPD or emphysema, as well as liver disease.
RESOURCES: Valastyan JS, Lindquist S. Mechanisms of protein-folding diseases at a glance. Dis Model Mech. 2014;7(1):9-14. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3882043/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your lungs have stretchy tissue called elastin that helps them expand and contract when you breathe. Your body makes a protective protein called alpha-1 antitrypsin that prevents immune cells from accidentally destroying this tissue. When a gene change causes this protein to be misshapen and not work properly, the protective brakes come off, and immune cells end up destroying the lung tissue. Over time this leads to a lung condition called emphysema, where breathing becomes difficult. When breathing is impaired, less oxygen reaches the muscles, causing exercise intolerance.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults (early-onset emphysema, typically younger adults with the most severe gene variant)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Due to defects in mitochondria ATP production (such as in the electron transport chain, ETC). Lack of ATP (or insufficient production of ATP) causes muscle fatigue, weakness, and exercise intolerance. 
Mitochondrial myopathies that may cause exercise intolerance and/or muscle weakness are:
1. Leigh syndrome  (includes deficiencies in TCA and ETC enzymes)
2. MERRF (Myoclonus epilepsy with ragged red fibers; OMIM)  -  caused by mito tRNA gene mutations which impact synthesis of ETC proteins encoded by the the mitochondrial genome.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your cells have tiny power plants called mitochondria that make the energy your body runs on. In Leigh syndrome, the enzymes that run these power plants are faulty, so cells — especially muscle cells — can't make enough energy. Without enough energy, muscles fatigue very easily, causing weakness and exercise intolerance.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically presenting in infancy or early childhood)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Myasthenia gravis is a neuromuscular disorder that is characterized by muscle weakness. It is an autoimmune disease in which the body produces autoantibodies against the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, thus preventing signaling at neuromuscular junctions. This binding prevents the neurotransmitter acetylcholine from interacting with its receptor, the muscle does not receive the signal to contract, thus causing a loss of muscle response.
RESOURCES:
Shah AK. Myasthenia Gravis. Medscape. Published 2023 Dec 5. Accessed Nov 24, 2025.
https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1171206-overview
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your brain sends signals to your muscles through chemical messengers. In myasthenia gravis, the body's own immune system mistakenly attacks the "receiving dock" on muscle cells that these chemical messengers attach to. When the dock is blocked, the signal from the brain never gets through, and the muscle doesn't contract properly. This causes muscle weakness that gets worse with activity and leads to exercise intolerance.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, though can affect all ages

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Rhabdomyolysis (muscle damage and breakdown of skeletal muscle) can be secondary to decreased activity levels of ATP due to various causes. There are two effects:  1) This leads to increased intracellular Na+ concentration, which draws water into the cell. Due to the osmotic effect, the integrity of cell membrane is impacted. 2) The membrane potential changes, leading to sustained muscle contractions and depletion of ATP.  Both of these will lead to acute muscle breakdown and cell lysis, which could result in exercise intolerance. Additionally, the exercise intolerance is due to extreme pain associated with rhabdomyolysis from the inflammatory markers released during cell lysis.

https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4522153/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Muscle cells need energy to keep their internal balance. When energy runs very low for any reason, salt and water build up inside muscle cells, causing them to swell and burst. The breakdown of muscle cells also releases substances that cause pain and inflammation. Together, the cell damage, pain, and loss of working muscle tissue make exercise extremely difficult or impossible.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages, depending on the underlying cause

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Rhabdomyolysis (muscle damage and breakdown of skeletal muscle) can be secondary to side effects of statin drugs.  Statin drugs inhibit HMG-CoA reductase in the cholesterol synthesis pathway also inhibiting farnesyl PP production. Farnesyl PP is precursor of ubiquinone (CoQ) in the electron transport chain (ETC). Because of this, the rate of the ETC is decreased leading to decreased production of ATP. which could result in exercise intolerance. Additionally, the exercise intolerance is due to extreme pain associated with rhabdomyolysis from the inflammatory markers released during cell lysis.
RESOURCES:
Torres PA, Helmstetter JA, Kaye AM, Kaye AD. Rhabdomyolysis: pathogenesis, diagnosis, and treatment. Ochsner J. 2015;15(1):58-69. 
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25829882/
SPECIAL NOTES:
Signs and symptoms:
• Muscle cramping
• Fatigue
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Statin drugs are used to lower cholesterol by blocking a key step in the cholesterol-making process. However, this same step also produces a building block needed for the energy-making process inside cells. When statins reduce this building block, the cells' power-generating system slows down, producing less energy. With less energy available, muscles can break down, causing pain and exercise intolerance.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults (statin use is most common in middle-aged and older adults)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Rhabdomyolysis (muscle damage and breakdown of skeletal muscle) can be secondary to depletion of muscle glycogen and decreased oxygen delivery during extreme exercise. The main cause of this damage is decreased production of ATP and depletion of it. This could result in exercise intolerance. Additionally, the exercise intolerance is due to extreme pain associated with rhabdomyolysis from the inflammatory markers released during cell lysis.
RESOURCES:
Torres PA, Helmstetter JA, Kaye AM, Kaye AD. Rhabdomyolysis: pathogenesis, diagnosis, and treatment. Ochsner J. 2015;15(1):58-69.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25829882/
SPECIAL NOTES:
Signs and symptoms:
• Muscle cramping
• Myoglobinuria (with strenuous exercise)
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: During extreme exercise, muscles burn through their stored fuel very rapidly and also receive less oxygen than they need. When the fuel and oxygen supply can't keep up with demand, the muscles can't make enough energy to keep working. This energy crash causes muscle cells to break down, releasing substances that cause pain and inflammation, making further exercise intolerant.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages, though most common in young adults and athletes

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Rhabdomyolysis (muscle damage and breakdown of skeletal muscle) can be secondary to ischemia caused by occlusion of major blood vessels supplying blood to skeletal muscle. Ischemic could be due to compression of blood vessels due to thromboses (for example from ruptured atherosclerotic plaques), emboli, compartment syndrome, carboxyhemoglobinemia, infection. Lack of blood supply to muscles could result in exercise intolerance. Additionally, the exercise intolerance is due to extreme pain associated with rhabdomyolysis from the inflammatory markers released during cell lysis.
RESOURCES:
Torres PA, Helmstetter JA, Kaye AM, Kaye AD. Rhabdomyolysis: pathogenesis, diagnosis, and treatment. Ochsner J. 2015;15(1):58-69.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25829882/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Muscles need a steady supply of blood to deliver oxygen and fuel. When blood flow to a muscle is cut off — due to a blockage in a blood vessel, a clot, or compression — the muscle is starved of oxygen and can't make energy. Without energy, muscle cells break down, releasing substances that cause intense pain and inflammation, making exercise intolerant.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, particularly those with cardiovascular disease or risk factors

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Bassen-Kornzweig syndrome is a rare inherited deficiency in the microsomal triacylglyceride-transfer protein (MTTP/MTP; OMIM) that leads to the inability of Apo-B containing lipoproteins (VLDLs and chylomicrons) to be loaded with lipids. The decreased VLDLs and chylomicrons results in poor blood Transport of dietary lipids and fat soluble vitamins (A,D,E,K) from the intestine and liver to the peripheral tissues.   These nutritional deficiencies lead to symptoms that appear in the first year including failure to gain weight and grow at expected rate along with other symptoms.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body uses special carriers called lipoproteins to move fats and fat-soluble vitamins from the intestines and liver to the rest of the body. In Bassen-Kornzweig syndrome, an inherited defect prevents these carriers from being properly loaded with fats. Without working carriers, dietary fats and important fat-soluble vitamins cannot reach the tissues that need them. This leads to poor weight gain and slow growth in the first year of life.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Exclusively a pediatric population condition. Symptoms appear in infancy, with failure to thrive among the earliest signs.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Mutations in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR; OMIM) lead to ion transportation defects at epithelial surfaces. One consequence of this is thickened pancreatic exocrine fluid. The thickening of the fluid inhibits secretion of pancreatic enzymes, including lipase, amylase, trypsin, and chymotrypsin leading to malabsorption of fats, carbohydrates and proteins, which in turn, can lead to malnutrition causing failure to thrive.

1. Cystic fibrosis occurs in 1 in 2,500 to 3,500 white newborns. It is less common in other ethnic groups, affecting about 1 in 17,000 African Americans and 1 in 31,000 Asian Americans.

2. Most infants with CF diagnosed by neonatal screening are already symptomatic in the first six weeks of life and the most frequent symptom is failure to thrive.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In cystic fibrosis, an inherited defect causes fluids in the body to become abnormally thick. In the pancreas, this thick fluid blocks the release of digestive enzymes into the intestine. Without these enzymes, the body cannot properly break down and absorb fats, carbohydrates, and proteins from food. This malabsorption leads to malnutrition and failure to thrive.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Exclusively a pediatric population condition, with most affected infants showing symptoms, including failure to thrive, within the first six weeks of life.

Depending on the variant and the gene, the disease can be inherited in an autosomal recessive or autosomal dominant pattern.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In permanent neonatal diabetes mellitus, an inherited defect causes certain channels in the pancreas to stay open when they should close. This prevents the pancreas from releasing enough insulin, the hormone that controls blood sugar. Without enough insulin, blood sugar stays too high, the body loses too much fluid, and the infant cannot gain weight and grow at the expected rate.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Exclusively a pediatric population condition, presenting from birth in affected infants.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) is primarily due to deficiency of the enzyme 21-hydroxylase (OMIM), causing a block in adrenal steroid production and a build-up of other hormones, specifically sex hormones (such as androgens). The core medical problem in CAH is the inability of the adrenal glands to make enough cortisol and aldosterone. Cortisol is important for maintenance of blood pressure, energy, blood sugar, and response to stress. Aldosterone works at the kidney to help retain salt and excrete potassium as well as to regulate blood pressure. The inability of the adrenal glands to produce these life essential hormones is the reason why newborns not receiving treatment get very sick with the salt-wasting form of CAH leading to dehydration, poor weight gain, failure to thrive, low blood sugar, shock, and lethargy.

In congenital adrenal hyperplasia, an inherited enzyme defect blocks the production of these hormones. Without enough cortisol and aldosterone, newborns become dehydrated, have low blood sugar, and cannot gain weight and grow at the expected rate, leading to failure to thrive.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Exclusively a pediatric population condition, presenting in the newborn period in affected infants.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Fatty acid oxidation disorders result from deficiencies in one of the enzymes involved in the β-oxidation of fatty acids. If adequate nonfat energy sources are not provided in the diet, infants can experience many symptoms including failure to thrive due to lack of ability to utilize fat as an energy source as well as abdominal discomfort.

SPECIAL NOTES:
1.  Collectively these disorders occur in about 1/10,000 individuals.

2.  Usually presents in infancy or early childhood, although less severe cases are sometimes not diagnosed until adulthood.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body can use fats as an energy source by breaking them down through a step-by-step process. In MCAD deficiency, an inherited defect in one of the enzymes needed for this process means the body cannot properly use fat for energy. If the diet does not provide enough energy from other sources, infants experience abdominal discomfort and cannot gain weight and grow at the expected rate, leading to failure to thrive.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Predominantly a pediatric population condition, usually presenting in infancy or early childhood.

Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Predominantly a pediatric population condition, usually presenting in infancy or early childhood.

The dietary manipulations required for managing these patients place patients at risk for an essential carbohydrate deficiency and failure to thrive.

1. Classic galactosemia is an autosomal recessive condition that occurs in 1 in 30,000 to 60,000 newborns.  Hereditary fructose intolerance is found in 1 in 20,000 to 30,000 individuals yearly.

2.  If infants with classic galactosemia are not treated promptly with a low-galactose diet, life-threatening complications appear within a few days after birth, including a failure to gain weight and grow as expected (failure to thrive).  Infants with hereditary fructose intolerance develop signs and symptoms of the disorder in infancy when fruits, juices, or other foods containing fructose are introduced into the diet. This necessary dietary restriction places infants at risk for not getting enough essential carbohydrates, which can lead to poor growth and failure to thrive if not carefully managed.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Exclusively a pediatric population condition, with life-threatening complications appearing within days of birth if untreated.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Urea cycle disorders are inherited deficiencies in the enzymes necessary to convert ammonia (a toxic waste product of amino acid catabolism) to urea that is disposed of in the urine.  The most common urea cycle disorder is ornithine transcarbamoylase (OTC, OMIM) deficiency. The dietary manipulations required for managing these patients place patients at risk for nutritional deficiency (protein insufficiency), which has mutisystem impacts.

1.  OTC deficiency is an X-linked condition and is its incidence in all populations is thought to be 1/14,000.

2.  Collectively (OTC deficiency, Carbamoyl phosphate synthetase deficiency, argininosuccinate synthetase deficiency, argininosuccinase deficiency and arginase deficiency) are thought to occur at frequency of 1/8,200 in the general population, with the cases exhibiting neonatal onset arising in 1/30,000 births.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When your body breaks down protein, it produces ammonia as a waste product. Normally, the liver converts ammonia into urea, which is safely removed in the urine. In urea cycle disorders, inherited enzyme defects block this conversion, allowing toxic ammonia to build up. To manage this, affected children must follow a strict low-protein diet. This dietary restriction places them at risk for not getting enough protein, leading to nutritional deficiency and failure to thrive.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Exclusively a pediatric population condition, with severe forms presenting in the newborn period.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Megaloblastic anemia is caused by deficiency of Vitamin B12 or folic acid, both of which are required for nucleotide biosynthesis, and it can result from multiple underlying factors. Impaired nucleotide biosynthesis inhibits DNA replication and can affect the erythropoietic process by causing delay in nuclear maturation compared to cytoplasmic maturation. This nuclear cytoplasmic dyssynchrony will lead to ineffective erythropoiesis. Impaired red blood cell production leads to deficient oxygen delivery to tissues leading to reduced oxidative phosphorylation and the resultant ATP deficiency leads to fatigue

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Folate is a nutrient your body needs to make new red blood cells. Without enough folate, your body cannot make red blood cells properly — the cells that do form are too large and do not work well. Because these abnormal red blood cells cannot carry enough oxygen to your body's tissues, your cells cannot make enough energy. This lack of energy is what causes you to feel fatigued and tired.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by folate deficiency-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD; OMIM) is a crucial housekeeping enzyme involved in the hexose monophosphate/pentose phosphate pathway, which is essential for the production of NADPH. NADPH plays a critical role in protecting cellular proteins and structures from damage caused by reactive oxygen species (ROS) via the glutathione reductase pathway. Red blood cells (RBCs) are particularly susceptible to ROS-induced damage due to their role in oxygen transport and their inability to repair damaged cellular components. A deficiency in G6PD can result in the destruction of RBCs, leading to reduced oxygen delivery to tissues and subsequent fatigue

In the United States, approximately 1 in 10 African American males is affected by this condition.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: G6PD is an enzyme that helps protect your red blood cells from damage. Without enough G6PD, your red blood cells are easily damaged and destroyed when they are exposed to certain triggers, such as some medicines or foods. When too many red blood cells are destroyed, your blood cannot carry enough oxygen to your body's tissues, and your cells cannot make enough energy. This is what causes the feeling of fatigue.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by G6PD deficiency-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Pyruvate kinase (PK; OMIM) is key enzyme in glycolysis and required for ATP production in RBCs (red blood cells) and other cells. in RBCs, anaerobic glycolysis is the only pathway for ATP production due to the lack of mitochondria. PK deficiency leads to defective glycolysis and causes hereditary nonspherocytic anemia. The enzymatic defect leads to decreased ATP generation in RBC, ATP is required to maintain the activity of membrane ATPase which maintains cellular integrity and flexibility. Deficient ATP production leads to excessive deformability and destruction of RBC and decreased oxygen carry capacity and fatigue.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Red blood cells need energy to keep their shape and do their job of carrying oxygen through your body. Unlike most other cells, red blood cells can only make energy through one pathway, and pyruvate kinase is a key enzyme in that pathway. When pyruvate kinase is deficient, red blood cells cannot make enough energy to maintain their structure, so they become damaged and are destroyed. With fewer healthy red blood cells available to carry oxygen, the body cannot make enough energy, leading to fatigue.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by pyruvate kinase deficiency-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Chronic fatigue is one of the most common symptom seen in Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)/ End Stage Renal Disease (ESRD). The development of fatigue is multifactorial. CKD is associated with chronic inflammatory response that may lead to systemic effects. It may contribute to progressive damage to pulmonary and alveolar membranes. This in turn will decrease the diffusion capacity in the lungs and cause hypoxia. Chronic inflammation also leads to muscle wasting and muscle protein loss especially respiratory musculature. The other well known mechanisms for developing fatigue in CKD is (1) deficiency of erythropoietin production in the kidneys and (2) dysregulation of iron by increased activity of hepcidin, which is due to chronic inflammation and decreased renal clearance. Elevated hepcidin inhibits iron absorption from the gut leading a microcytic anemia (impaired red blood cell production) presenting as fatigue.
RESOURCES:

https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1389854-overview

https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8729574/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When your kidneys are not working well, several things can happen that make you feel very tired. Long-term kidney disease can cause ongoing inflammation in the body, which can damage the lungs and make it harder to breathe in enough oxygen. This inflammation can also cause muscle wasting, making your muscles weaker. In addition, damaged kidneys cannot make enough of a hormone that tells your body to produce red blood cells, and inflammation causes a buildup of a protein that blocks your body from absorbing iron properly. Together, these problems lead to fewer red blood cells and less oxygen being delivered to your body's tissues, which causes fatigue.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by chronic renal disease-related fatigue.
