MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Insulin stimulates recruitment of the glucose transporter (GLUT4) to the plasma membrane, and is thus responsible for insulin-sensitive glucose uptake by muscle, adipose tissue. Loss of systemic insulin sensitivity is seen in type 2 diabetes and metabolic syndrome. 
 
Under normal conditions, Insulin stimulation of muscle ATP synthesis has been attributed to increased mitochondrial oxidative capacity as it coincides with enhanced mitochondrial protein synthesis and with increased mRNA levels and activities of mitochondrial enzymes involved with substrate catabolism. 
 
The consequences of insulin resistance, there is a decrease in metabolic fuel available due to absence of glucose uptake by muscle and lower signal transduction via insulin leading to decreased mitochondrial activity (both biogenesis and protein synthesis) and bioenergetic failure (reduced electron transport chain activity) These are the main mechanism involved in developing fatigue in these conditions.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Normally, insulin helps your muscle cells absorb sugar from the blood and use it for energy. It also helps your cells' energy-producing machinery work at full capacity. In type 2 diabetes, the body's cells stop responding properly to insulin. This means that muscle cells cannot take in enough sugar for fuel, and the energy-producing machinery inside cells slows down. With less fuel available and less energy being made, the body feels fatigued.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult patients are affected by type 2 diabetes mellitus-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Multiple factors can lead to hypothyroidism. Fatigue is commonly associated with hypothyroidism, which has been attributed to a direct effect of thyroid hormone (TH) on mitochondrial transcription. Mitochondrial DNA has promoter regions with response elements for TH receptors, so TH can influence the transcription of mitochondrial genes which encode proteins are required for oxidative phosphorylation of ATP in oxidative tissue.
RESOURCES:
Fischer S, Markert C, Strahler J, et al. Thyroid Functioning and Fatigue in Women With Functional Somatic Syndromes - Role of Early Life Adversity. Front Physiol. 2018;9:564.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5974249/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Thyroid hormone plays an important role in helping your cells make energy. It does this by directly influencing the activity of the parts of the cell responsible for energy production. When thyroid hormone levels are too low, this energy-producing process slows down in tissues throughout the body. With less energy being produced, the body feels persistently tired and fatigued.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by hypothyroidism-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: In MCAD deficiency (OMIM), long-chain fatty acids undergo β-oxidation to form medium-chain acyl-CoA; however, their further oxidation is impaired. A common clinical manifestation of this condition is recurrent episodes of fatigue during periods of low blood glucose, as fatty acids, which would typically be utilized for energy, cannot be adequately oxidized

SPECIAL NOTES: In the United States, MCAD deficiency is estimated to occur in 1 out of every 17,000 individuals, with a higher prevalence noted among individuals of northern European descent compared to other ethnic backgrounds.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body normally uses both sugar and fat for energy. When blood sugar is low, your body relies on breaking down fats to keep energy levels up. In MCAD deficiency, a specific step in breaking down certain fats is blocked, so the body cannot fully use those fats for energy. When blood sugar drops and the body cannot properly switch to burning fat as a backup fuel source, there is not enough energy available, which leads to episodes of fatigue.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric patients are affected by MCAD deficiency-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Deficiency of enzymes required for glycogen metabolism (called glycogen storage diseases; GSDs) in the liver lead to hypoglycemia and fatigue, particularly under conditions of fasting. Specific GSDs that lead to hypoglycemia include Type Ia (Glucose-6-Phosphatase deficiency/von Gierke disease; OMIM), Type Ib (Glucose-6-Phosphate transport deficiency; OMIM), Type III (debranching enzyme/Cori disease; OMIM), Type VI (liver glycogen phosphorylase deficiency/Hers disease; OMIM), and Type IXb (Phosphorylase kinase deficiency; OMIM).
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 24: Glycogen Metabolism and Glycogen Storage Diseases

Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 22e.  2025. Ch. 430: Glycogen Storage Diseases and Other Inherited Disorders of Carbohydrate Metabolism
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your liver stores sugar in a form called glycogen and releases it into the blood to keep your energy levels steady, especially when you have not eaten for a while. In von Gierke disease and related glycogen storage diseases, the liver is missing an enzyme it needs to release that stored sugar back into the blood. Without this release of sugar, blood sugar levels drop too low, and the body does not have enough fuel to make energy. This is what causes the fatigue, especially during periods of fasting.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric patients are affected by von Gierke disease (Type Ia)-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Undernutrition arising from various causes results in nutritional deficiencies and weight loss. This is predominantly due to decreased intake of protein and calories. The concomitant micronutrient deficiency, which have coenzymatic action in biochemical reactions/pathway (Glycolysis, TCA, ETC), increased catabolism of body store of fats and protein, eventually leading to fatigue.
RESOURCES:
Azzolino D, Arosio B, Marzetti E, Calvani R, Cesari M. Nutritional Status as a Mediator of Fatigue and Its Underlying Mechanisms in Older People. Nutrients. 2020;12(2):444.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When a person does not get enough food — especially enough protein and calories — the body does not have what it needs to make energy properly. Many vitamins and minerals that are also missing during malnutrition are needed to help the body convert food into energy. Without these nutrients, the body is forced to break down its own fat and muscle stores to try to keep going. All of these changes together lead to the body having less and less energy available, which causes fatigue.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by malnutrition-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (aka Pickwickian syndrome) is caused by a large habitus physically interfering with respiration. The lower oxygen delivery to the body thus leads to lower energy production and fatigue.
RESOURCES:
Hamdy O. Obesity. Published 2025 Sep 29. Accessed Dec 6, 2025.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In obesity hypoventilation syndrome, carrying a very large body size can physically press on the lungs and make it hard to breathe properly. When breathing is impaired, less oxygen gets into the body. With less oxygen available, the body's cells cannot make as much energy as they need, which leads to fatigue.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult patients are affected by metabolic syndrome-related fatigue.

SPECIFIC DIFFERENTIAL: Myoclonus epilepsy with ragged red fibers (MERRF)
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Inherited defects of components e.g. MELAS, OMIM; MERFF, OMIM) of the electron transport chain (ETC) leads to (inability to obtain energy via beta oxidation of medium chain fatty acids (FAs), and very long chain FAs, and to gluconeogenesis pathways. The basic defect in these conditions are due to mutations in ETC components encoded in the nuclear or mtDNA or in mitochondrial tRNA lys. These mutations impair the ability of the ETC to produce energy by oxidation of substrates in mitochondria; this decreased production of ATP from lack of ETC activity leads to fatigue and mainly affects organs with high energy demands.
RESOURCES:
Stenton SL, Prokisch H. Genetics of mitochondrial diseases: Identifying mutations to help diagnosis. EBioMedicine. 2020;56:102784.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7248429/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: MERRF is an inherited condition caused by mutations in the genetic instructions that control how cells make energy. These mutations impair the ability of the cell's energy-producing machinery to work properly, so cells cannot make enough energy from the fuels available to them. Because some organs — like the muscles and brain — need very large amounts of energy to function, they are hit the hardest when energy production falls short, leading to fatigue.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by MERRF-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Leigh syndrome is a severe neurological disorder that results from impaired energy production in mitochondria. Decreased ATP production particularly affects the cells that demand high ATP for their cellular functions. Those tissues are muscle (especially cardiac muscle) and neurons (brain). A decreased production of ATP in the neuronal cells and muscles can cause feeling of fatigue. Leigh syndrome is caused by diverse mutations in both the mitochondrial (OMIM) or nuclear genomes (OMIM).

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Leigh syndrome is an inherited condition caused by mutations that impair the ability of cells to produce energy in their mitochondria. When cells cannot make enough energy, the tissues that need the most energy — especially the brain and muscles, including the heart — are affected the most. This shortage of energy in the brain and muscles is what causes the feeling of fatigue.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric patients are affected by Leigh syndrome-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin B1 (thiamine), is known to be involved in several cell functions, including energy metabolism and the degradation of sugars and carbon skeletons. Thiamine in form of thiamine pyrophosphate acts as a cofactor for PDH and alpha KGDH, enzymes involved in oxidation of glucose and TCA cycle and metabolism of carbon skeleton. Thus, lack of thiamine can decrease the energy produced in key tissues like brain, muscles, cardiac muscles, causing fatigue. Additionally, heart failure, resulting from thiamine deficiency (wet beriberi) can further exacerbate fatigue by reducing blood flow and oxygen delivery to tissues.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin B1 (thiamine) is a nutrient that the body needs to convert sugar into energy and to keep several important energy-producing processes running. Without enough thiamine, key steps in turning food into energy slow down or stop, especially in the brain, muscles, and heart. This shortage of energy in these important tissues causes fatigue. In severe cases, the heart can also be weakened, which reduces the delivery of blood and oxygen to the rest of the body, making fatigue even worse.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by thiamine deficiency-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: The exact mechanism how deranged vitamin D levels can cause fatigue is not known, but several hypothesis have been presented. Vitamin D is required for proper functioning of mitochondria, by acting on vitamin D receptors, vitamin D is important for regulating several transcription factors, which can affect the availability of proteins, enzymes in mitochondria or tissues. This can decrease the ability of tissue to generate ATP during activity. also vitamin D is required for calcium homeostasis, which is important factor required for muscle contraction. By both these hypothesis, lack of vitamin D can accentuate muscle weakness or tone causing the feeling of fatigue.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin D plays a role in helping your cells — especially muscle cells — make energy and work properly. It does this by influencing the availability of important proteins inside cells that are needed for energy production. Vitamin D also helps control calcium levels in the body, and calcium is needed for muscles to contract properly. When Vitamin D levels are too low, muscle cells may not be able to produce energy efficiently and may not contract as well, leading to muscle weakness and the feeling of fatigue.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by Vitamin D deficiency-related fatigue.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: The kidney is the site of 1-hydroxylation of 25-hydroxycholecalciferol, which is the last step in forming the active form of Vitamin D (calcitriol). In late-stage chronic kidney disease (CKD), hyperphosphatemia develops due to the inability of the kidneys to excrete dietary phosphate, which in turn suppresses renal hydroxylation of vitamin D to the active form, calcitriol. Thus, CKD leads to calcitriol deficiency. Calcitriol favors intestinal Ca absorption, renal Ca reabsorption, and resorption of bone.  Accordingly, the CKD-induced calcitriol deficiency leads to hypocalcemia. In summary, abnormal vitamin D metabolism, hypoproteinemia, treatments for kidney disease, vascular calcification, and secondary parathyroid dysfunction are all causative factors for low bone mass in CKD patients. The low bone mass could increase the risk of fractures.

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 12: Collagen,Collagenopathies, and Diseases of Mineralization, and Ch. 31  Steroid Hormones and Vitamin D.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3592201/
SPECIAL NOTES: Despite the hypocalcemia, blood vessels can get calcified.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your kidneys play an important role in activating Vitamin D in your body. When your kidneys are not working well, they cannot properly activate Vitamin D. Without enough active Vitamin D, your body cannot absorb enough calcium from food, and your bones lose important minerals that keep them strong. On top of this, damaged kidneys also cannot remove enough phosphate from your blood, which makes the problem even worse. All of these changes together cause your bones to become weak, making them much more likely to break.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by chronic renal disease-related fractures.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Heritable osteogenesis imperfecta originates from mutations in collagen genes and/or genes involved in collagen processing (e.g. hydroxylation, glycosylation, chaperones; OMIM search) resulting in abnormal collagen that gets incorporated into bone and other connective tissue. With collagen being an important part of connective tissue, abnormalities lead to brittle bones (increased susceptibility to fractures), improper dentition, and blue (thin) sclera.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Osteogenesis imperfecta is an inherited condition caused by changes in the genes responsible for making collagen, which is a protein that acts like a scaffold to give bones their strength and flexibility. When these genes are not working properly, the collagen that is made is abnormal. Because bones rely heavily on collagen to stay strong, abnormal collagen leads to bones that are very brittle and break easily. This condition can also affect teeth and cause the whites of the eyes to appear blue.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric patients are affected by osteogenesis imperfecta.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: X-linked hypophosphatemic Rickets is due to defects in osteoid (bone tissue) mineralization by calcium and phosphate arising from mutations in the PHEX gene (Phosphate regulating endopeptidase X-linked; OMIM) which impacts phosphate homeostasis. The abnormal bone mineralization leads to increased risk of fractures.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets is an inherited condition caused by a change in a gene that controls how the body manages phosphate, which is a mineral that is essential for building strong bones. When this gene is not working properly, the body cannot use phosphate and calcium properly to harden and strengthen bone tissue. This leads to bones that are softer and weaker than normal, which makes them much more likely to break.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric patients are affected by X-linked hypophosphatemic rickets.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Gaucher disease is caused by an inherited deficiency of acid beta-glucosidase (glucocerebrosidase; OMIM) leading to accumulation of glucosylceramide in liver, spleen and bone marrow. This leads to an accumulation of glucosylceramide, especially in macrophages.  The disorder affects the functioning of osteoblasts and osteoclasts, leading to demineralization of bone, thus giving rise to fragility fractures.
RESOURCES:

SPECIAL NOTES: Enzyme replacement therapy is available.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Gaucher disease is an inherited condition in which the body is missing an enzyme that breaks down a certain type of fatty substance. Without this enzyme, the fatty substance builds up inside cells, especially in the liver, spleen, and bone marrow. This buildup disrupts the normal process of building and breaking down bone, causing bones to lose their mineral strength. As bones become weaker, they are much more likely to break.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by Gaucher disease-related fractures.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Rickets/osteomalacia are due to defects in osteoid (bone tissue) mineralization by calcium and phosphate arising from vitamin D deficiency. The active form of vitamin D (1, 25 dihydroxycholecalciferol) is important for intestinal Ca absorption, maintenance of bone (formation/resorption), and allows Ca reabsorption in the kidneys. Physical manifestations of vitamin D deficiency include softening and bowing of bones that can lead to fractures. Vitamin D deficiency can be caused by malnutrition or decreased exposure to sunlight, end organ failure, or anticonvulsant therapy.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025. Ch. 29: Micronutrients: Minerals

SPECIAL NOTES: Rickets is the pediatric condition and Osteomalacia is the adult condition.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin D is an important nutrient that helps your body absorb calcium and keep your bones strong and hard. When you don't get enough Vitamin D — whether from your diet, lack of sunlight, or other causes — your body cannot absorb enough calcium. Without enough calcium, bones become soft and weak instead of firm and strong. Soft bones can bend and bow, and are much more likely to break.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by Vitamin D deficiency-related fractures.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) promotes osteoclast activity indirectly by interacting with receptors on osteoblasts. This interaction triggers the production of RANK-L (receptor activator of nuclear factor kappa β ligand) and M-CSF (macrophage colony-stimulating factor), which together enhance bone resorption. RANK-L plays a critical role in activating transcription factors that drive osteoclast differentiation, while M-CSF supports RANK-L expression.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your parathyroid glands make a hormone that helps control calcium levels in your body. When too much of this hormone is produced, it sends signals to the cells that break down bone to become more active. When bone is broken down faster than it is built back up, your bones gradually lose their strength and density, making them much more likely to fracture.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult patients are affected by hyperparathyroidism-related fractures.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Renal artery atherosclerosis reduces blood flow to the kidneys, leading to decreased glomerular filtration rate (GFR) and activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS). This triggers the release of renin, which converts angiotensinogen to angiotensin II, a potent vasoconstrictor that raises blood pressure and stimulates aldosterone secretion. Aldosterone increases sodium and water retention, expanding blood volume and further elevating blood pressure. These combined effects lead to hypertension, which worsens over time due to persistent vascular and renal strain.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When cholesterol builds up inside the arteries that supply blood to the kidneys, it can narrow those arteries and reduce the amount of blood reaching the kidneys. When the kidneys sense that blood flow is reduced, they release a chain of chemical signals that cause blood vessels throughout the body to tighten and squeeze. These same signals also tell the kidneys to hold onto extra salt and water. Both of these responses raise blood pressure, and over time, the ongoing strain on the blood vessels and kidneys makes the high blood pressure worse.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adult patients are affected by atherosclerosis-related hypertension.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: 17-alpha-hydroxylase (OMIM) is essential for converting pregnenolone and progesterone into 17-hydroxylated precursors required for cortisol and androgen synthesis. A deficiency in this enzyme impairs cortisol production, removing negative feedback on the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and leading to excessive ACTH secretion. This drives overproduction of mineralocorticoid precursors, such as 11-deoxycorticosterone, which increases sodium retention and water reabsorption, causing expanded blood volume and hypertension. The condition, known as 'low renin hypertension,' is characterized by suppression of the renin-angiotensin system, with aldosterone levels ranging from low to high.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hyperuricemic conditions can cause hypertension by acting through crystal-dependent or independent pathways. Urate crystals can get deposited in the renal lumen and trigger proinflammatory reactions and acute renal failure. Soluble uric acid can cause endothelial dysfunction in renal vasculature by decreasing the activity of nitric oxide synthase and increasing the activity of aldose reductase causing upregulation of polyol pathway. This, in turn, leads to decreased nitric oxide production and increased fructose synthesis. Soluble uric acid can also activate the intrarenal renin/angiotensin system. The major consequence of these above actions is hypertension. Uric acid is known to cause mitochondrial dysfunction by depleting ATP (via stimulation of fructokinase causing phosphate trapping). Mitochondrial dysfunction can increase oxidative stress, which can trigger inflammation and lead to hypertension.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Pheochromocytoma is a tumor of the chromaffin cells of the adrenal gland characterized by excessive production and secretion of catecholamines (primarily norepinephrine and epinephrine). Norepinephrine stimulates α1-receptors in blood vessels, causing systemic vasoconstriction including the renal artery. It also activates β1-receptors in cardiac tissue, increasing contractility and cardiac output. Both these mechanisms contribute to sustained hypertension. Epinephrine also contributes to episodic elevations of blood pressure via similar mechanisms, with its primary action mediated by beta receptors.

These chemical messengers cause blood vessels throughout the body to tighten and squeeze, and they cause the heart to pump harder and faster. Both of these effects push blood pressure up significantly and can lead to persistent or episodic high blood pressure.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Patients who are dehydrated because of poor fluid intake, gastroenteritis, and diuretic or alcohol use can become hyperthermic.  Dehydration increases demand on Na⁺K⁺ ATPase pumps, which contribute 25-45% of basal metabolic rate. When demand for ATP is higher, more substrate is utilized, and more heat is generated. This is because the process of oxidative phosphorylation, which produces ATP, is not 100% efficient; some of the energy is converted to heat, which can, in turn, lead to hyperthermia. Additionally, when an individual is dehydrated, sweat production is reduced to conserve water, which further prevents heat loss.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When you're dehydrated, your body's cells have to work harder to maintain their normal functions, especially the pumps that move sodium and potassium. This extra work requires more energy (ATP), and making ATP creates heat as a byproduct—like how a car engine gets hot when running. Additionally, when dehydrated, you can't sweat as much, so your body can't cool itself down properly, causing your temperature to rise.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP) is an illegal drug, sometimes used for weight loss and bodybuilding, that facilitates the transfer of protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane in all tissues. As the ETC complexes pump protons out of the matrix into the inner mitochondrial space, DNP allows the protons to reenter the matrix down their concentration gradient.The potential energy in the protons is dissipated as heat rather than captured as ATP by ATP synthase, leading to hyperthermia. Hence, DNP uncouples the mitochondrial proton gradient from ATP synthesis.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Ecstasy (3, 4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine [MDMA, the Rave drug]), amphetamines, methamphetamines, and cocaine lead to hyperthermia by a variety of mechanisms. Drugs can generate heat by affecting the central nervous system (i.e., neurotransmitter release, metabolic activity), the cardiac system (i.e., tachycardia), and the periphery (i.e., shivering). These effects, combined with vigorous exercise, increase ATP substrate demand. When demand for ATP is higher, more substrate is utilized, and more heat is generated.  This is because the process of oxidative phosphorylation, which produces ATP, is not 100% efficient; some of the energy in the substrate is converted to heat, which can, in turn, lead to hyperthermia.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Pharmacological agents, such as anaesthetics, muscle relaxants (e.g., succinylcholine), anticholinergic drugs (atropine), neuroleptic drugs, and serotonin reuptake inhibitors, can trigger hyperthermia in patients with mutations in Ryanodine Receptor 1 (RYR1, OMIM) and Calcium Channel, Voltage-Dependent, L Type, Alpha-1S Subunit gene (CACNA1S, OMIM). In certain cases, the underlying genetic defect may not be known.  These deficiencies cause the RYR1 channel to open more easily and close more slowly in response to certain drugs. As a result, abnormally large amounts of calcium ions are released from storage within muscle cells. When demand for ATP is higher, more substrate is utilized, and more heat is generated.  This is because the process of oxidative phosphorylation, which produces ATP, is not 100% efficient; some of the energy in the substrate is converted to heat, which can, in turn, lead to hyperthermia.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: This is a combined effect of environmental and genetic factors. Autosomal dominant pathogenic variants in calcium ion channels, Ryanodine Receptor 1 (RYR1, OMIM) or Calcium Channel, Voltage-Dependent, L Type, Alpha-1S Subunit gene (CACNA1S, OMIM), use the RYR1 channel to open more easily and close more slowly in response to exercise. The abnormal increase in calcium ion concentration within muscle cells promotes contraction, leading to higher ATP substrate demands. When demand for ATP is higher, more substrate is utilized, and more heat is generated. This is because the process of oxidative phosphorylation in the production of ATP is not 100% efficient and some of the energy in the substrate is converted to heat, which can, in turn, lead to hyperthermia.

SPECIAL NOTES: Vigorous exercise and/or exposure to high heat is one of the common triggers for hyperthermia in individuals with this genetic susceptibility.  Malignant hyperthermia is an autosomal dominant genetic susceptibility disorder that may be present in as frequently as 1 out of 5000 individuals.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Some people have genetic changes in calcium channels in their muscle cells. During intense exercise or heat exposure, these faulty channels release too much calcium, causing muscles to contract excessively and demand large amounts of energy (ATP). Making this extra ATP generates excessive heat—like an overworked engine—causing dangerous overheating of the body.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Strenuous exercise increases ATP demand, leading to a higher rate of substrate utilization and more heat generation. This is because the process of oxidative phosphorylation, which produces ATP, is not 100% efficient, resulting in some energy being released as heat. This effect is particularly amplified in hot climates, in part because of the accompanying dehydration (see dehydration).

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: During strenuous exercise, your muscles need lots of energy (ATP) to keep working. Making ATP isn't perfectly efficient—some energy is always lost as heat, like how your phone charger gets warm when charging. The harder you exercise, the more ATP you need, and the more heat your body produces. In hot weather, this problem gets worse because you also become dehydrated and can't cool down as well.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult or Pediatric

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When a person drinks a large amount of alcohol without eating enough food, the body's ability to make new glucose is disrupted. Normally, the body can make glucose from other substances to keep blood sugar levels up. However, when alcohol is broken down in the body, it produces a substance that redirects those building blocks away from glucose production. Without enough glucose being made, blood sugar levels drop too low, causing hypoglycemia.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by alcohol-related hypoglycemia.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Improper management of blood glucose levels and inadequate regular monitoring can lead to hypoglycemia in diabetes mellitus. MECHANISM EXPLANATION: A defect in fatty acid oxidation will lead to increased reliance on glucose, resulting in hypoglycemia. Normally, long-chain fatty acids are metabolized by β-oxidation to a medium-chain-length acyl-CoA, but further oxidation is blocked in individuals with MCAD deficiency (OMIM), so the medium-chain acyl group is transferred back to carnitine. These acylcarnitines are water-soluble and appear in blood and urine.The most frequent manifestation of MCAD deficiency is intermittent hypoketotic hypoglycemia during fasting. Fatty acids normally would be oxidized to CO2 and H2O  but in MCAD deficiency, fatty acids are oxidized only until they reach medium-chain length. As a result, the body must rely to a greater extent on oxidation of blood glucose to meet its energy needs, and the patient becomes hypoglycemic.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Normally, when blood sugar starts to run low, the body switches to burning fat for energy so that it can save its glucose. In MCAD deficiency, the body cannot fully break down certain fats to use them for energy. Because fat cannot be used properly as a backup fuel, the body has to rely much more heavily on glucose to meet its energy needs. This causes blood sugar to be used up much faster than normal, leading to hypoglycemia, especially during periods of fasting.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric patients are affected by MCAD deficiency-related hypoglycemia.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The liver stores sugar and releases it into the blood to keep blood sugar levels steady, especially when a person has not eaten for a while. In Fanconi-Bickel syndrome, the transporter protein that moves glucose out of the liver and into the blood is missing or not working. Without this transporter, glucose gets trapped in the liver and cannot be released into the bloodstream. This causes blood sugar levels to fall too low, especially during fasting, leading to hypoglycemia.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric patients are affected by Fanconi-Bickel syndrome-related hypoglycemia.

SPECIFIC DIFFERENTIAL: Neonatal hyperinsulinism - infants of pregnant people with diabetes
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Neonatal hypoglycemia is a common complication in infants of diabetic mothers. The condition usually occurs in the first 4 hours after birth (postnatal). Uncontrolled hyperglycemia in the mother increases placental glucose transport and fetal hyperglycemia, which in turn stimulate the fetal pancreas to produce insulin. Once the baby is born, glucose influx from the mother stops, but the newborn still has a high insulin level, which leads to hypoglycemia. The blood glucose level usually normalizes gradually over 24 - 72 hours.

• 10-25% of infants of diabetic mothers (IDMs) show some degree of hyperinsulinism

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When a pregnant person has poorly controlled diabetes, their blood sugar is too high. Extra sugar passes through the placenta to the baby, causing the baby's blood sugar to be high as well. In response, the baby's pancreas learns to produce large amounts of insulin to manage this extra sugar. When the baby is born, the supply of extra sugar from the mother suddenly stops, but the baby's pancreas continues to produce high levels of insulin. This excess insulin removes too much sugar from the baby's blood, causing hypoglycemia in the first hours after birth. Blood sugar levels usually return to normal within one to three days.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily pediatric patients are affected by neonatal hyperinsulinism related to maternal diabetes.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Diabetes Insipidus (DI) is a condition characterized by the kidneys' inability to reabsorb water from urine. Central DI occurs due to a deficiency in antidiuretic hormone (ADH, also known as vasopressin), while nephrogenic DI is caused by the kidneys' resistance to ADH. When ADH binds to its receptor on the cells of the collecting ducts, it triggers the movement of Aquaporin 2-containing vesicles to the cell surface, enabling more water to be reabsorbed from the urine back into the bloodstream. A deficiency in ADH or aquaporins in the kidneys results in reduced water reabsorption and increased urine output (water diuresis). This loss of water reduces blood volume, which can lead to hypotension.
RESOURCES:
Robbins & Kumar. Basic Pathology 11e. 2023. Ch. 8: Blood vessels

Diabetes Insipidus. National Library of Medicine. Assessed Jan. 29, 2025
https://medlineplus.gov/diabetesinsipidus.html

Diabetes Insipidus. National Health Service. Assessed Jan. 29, 2025
https://www.nhs.uk/conditions/diabetes-insipidus
SPECIAL NOTES: Diabetes insipidus affects about 1 in 25,000 people in the general population.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Normally, the kidneys reabsorb water back into the body to help maintain a healthy blood volume. In diabetes insipidus, the kidneys cannot reabsorb water properly, either because the body does not make enough of the hormone that signals the kidneys to hold onto water, or because the kidneys do not respond to that signal. As a result, large amounts of water are lost in the urine. This loss of water reduces the volume of blood in the body, which can cause blood pressure to drop too low and lead to hypotension.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by diabetes insipidus-related hypotension.

Signal transduction

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Different drugs can cause hypotension through various mechanisms. Biochemistry-based examples include excessive intake of drugs that affect the Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone system (RAAS). These include ACE (angiotensin-converting enzyme) Inhibitors, Angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs), and aldosterone antagonists (e.g., spironolactone). Another category of drugs that can lead to hypotension includes beta-adrenergic blockers and calcium channel blockers, which affect cardiac output and/or vascular smooth muscle contraction. Beta-adrenergic blockers primarily block the effects of epinephrine and norepinephrine by inhibiting specific beta-adrenergic receptors.  Calcium channel blockers inhibit voltage-gated calcium channels, which reduces intracellular calcium levels in cardiac muscle cells and vascular smooth muscle cells. This decreases heart contractility and vascular resistance, leading to a decrease in blood pressure.

Drug-drug interaction between sildenafil (used in erectile dysfunction) and nitrates (used in angina) can also lead to hypotension. Nitrates are metabolized into nitric oxide (NO), which increases cGMP levels by promoting guanylyl cyclase. This leads to vasodilation. Sildenafil, a phosphodiesterase 5 inhibitor, causes vasodilation by inhibits breakdown of cGMP into GMP. Concurrent use of sildenafil and nitrates leads to excess accumulation of cGMP, leading to vasodilation and hypotension. Hence, concurrent use of these two drugs is contraindicated.

Some medications work by interfering with the body's system for controlling salt and water balance, causing the body to hold onto less fluid and reducing blood volume. Others work by slowing the heart down or making it pump less forcefully, which reduces the pressure of blood flowing through the vessels. Some medications cause the blood vessels themselves to relax and widen, which also lowers blood pressure. MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Generally, hypotension can lead to dizziness/lightheadedness through positional changes (orthostatic hypotension). This refers to a drop in blood pressure when a person stands up from a sitting or lying position, which can cause dizziness, lightheadedness, or fainting. Standing from a supine position causes blood to pool in the lower extremities due to gravity, reducing venous return to the heart and consequently lowering cardiac output. Normally, the body compensates by increasing sympathetic tone and decreasing vagal tone through the baroreceptor reflex. This raises peripheral vascular resistance, restoring venous return and cardiac output, and preventing a significant drop in blood pressure. When this compensatory mechanism fails, patients may experience orthostatic hypotension

Biochemical conditions that can lead to orthostatic hypotension include diabetes (via low blood sugar or peripheral neuropathy), vitamin B12 & B6 deficiencies and B6 toxicity (associated with neurological impact), B6, B9, & B12 deficiencies (associated with anemia).  For all of these cases, orthostatic hypotension might not be the primary presentation, but it can be associated.
RESOURCES: Ringer M, Hashmi MF, Lappin SL. Orthostatic Hypotension. [Updated 2025 Jan 17]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448192
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When a person stands up quickly from sitting or lying down, gravity causes blood to temporarily pool in the legs, reducing the amount of blood returning to the heart. Normally, the body quickly senses this and automatically adjusts by tightening blood vessels and speeding up the heart to restore blood pressure. When this automatic adjustment does not work properly, blood pressure stays too low after standing, causing dizziness or lightheadedness. Several biochemical conditions can interfere with this adjustment process, including low blood sugar, nerve damage from diabetes, and deficiencies of certain B vitamins.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric patients can be affected by orthostatic hypotension.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Alcohol Metabolism
SPECIFIC DIFFERENTIAL: Intoxication combined with low ambient temperatures
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Excess use of alcohol is often associated with cases of hypothermia. The vasodilatation caused by alcohol provides a sensation of warmth, but also increases heat loss through radiation. The impact of ethanol on vasodilation appears to originate in its effect on nitric oxide (NO) release from endothelial cells, but there is no clear mechanism, and it does not appear to relate to ethanol metabolism to acetyl-CoA. In addition, alcohol and other drugs impair hand-eye coordination, mobility, and decision-making abilities that could potentially leave these individuals stranded in colder environments, contributing to hypothermia.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Induced hypothermia is a therapeutic process (see Special Notes) that lowers body temperature by intentional exposure to cold. To prevent the body from re-warming, shivering is suppressed with sedation (opioids, dexmedetomidine, propofol). Shivering needs to be suppressed because it rewarms the body by increasing ATP demand during muscle contraction. If shivering were not suppressed, the body would reheat, since ATP production is not 100% efficient; some of the energy in the substrate is converted to heat.

In this treatment a device is used to promote conductive heat loss either by surface or intravascular cooling.  A feedback process is used to maintain the lowered body temperature.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: To treat certain medical emergencies, doctors intentionally cool the body using cold devices. To keep the body from warming itself back up through shivering, medications are given to stop the shivering. Without shivering (which normally creates heat by making muscles work and use energy), the body stays cold.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hypothermia results from decreased basal metabolic rate (BMR) due to decreased thyroxine production from either primary or secondary thyroid pathologies. MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Low birth weight (LBW) premature infants are prone to hypothermia, and this seems to be mainly due to the large body surface area in relation to weight and the relative lack of subcutaneous fat.   However, heat loss in the LBW premature infant is multifactorial. Brown fat is stored in the axillary, mediastinal, perinephric, paraspinal, and intrascapular regions and first appears around 26–30 weeks of gestation. A deficiency in non-shivering thermogenesis caused by low amounts of brown fat can contribute to hypothermia in the LBW premature neonate. This is because the electron transport chain is decoupled from ATP synthesis by the uncoupling protein in brown fat (UCP1), and protons coming down their gradient into the mitochondrial matrix generate heat rather than ATP.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Angelman syndrome results from mutations in the gene UBE3A (OMIM) or by paternal uniparental disomy for the region of chromosome 15 containing UBE3A (UBE3A is imprinted, thus inactivated during spermatogenesis). UBE3A is a ubiquitin ligase with many identified targets. Although the mechanism is not clear, a deficiency in UBE3A leads to severe intellectual disability, seizures, a happy demeanor, and a specific facial appearance.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Everyone has two copies of most genes — one from mom and one from dad. In Angelman syndrome, a gene called UBE3A — which helps the body get rid of damaged or unneeded proteins — is missing or broken. Normally, in brain cells, only the copy from mom is active (the dad's copy is "switched off"). When the mom's copy is also missing or broken, brain cells can't clean up proteins properly. This buildup causes problems with how brain cells work and grow, leading to intellectual disability, seizures, and a happy, excitable personality.

All those extra repeats cause a "cap" to form over the gene, shutting it off completely. The FMR1 gene normally makes a protein that helps brain cells send messages to each other and form proper connections. Without this protein, the brain's communication network doesn't develop correctly, leading to intellectual disability and behavioral challenges.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Our bodies need to break down a sugar called galactose (found in milk and dairy products) into forms the body can use for energy. In galactosemia, one of the enzymes needed for this process is missing or not working correctly. Without that enzyme, galactose and its breakdown products build up to toxic levels in the blood and organs, including the brain. This buildup damages brain cells, which can cause intellectual disability and other health problems. Avoiding galactose in the diet (especially early in life) can prevent much of the damage.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (newborn/infant onset; lifelong dietary management required)

Genetic Inheritance 
Inborn Errors of Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Guanidinoacetate methyltransferase (GAMT; OMIM) is required for the synthesis of the energy source creatine from glycine, arginine, and methionine. Creatine is synthesized in the body by the transfer of a methyl group to the glycine breakdown product guanidinoacetate (GAA) by GAMT. The absence of GAMT results in a deficiency of creatine, which has particularly detrimental effects on the brain, resulting in intellectual disability, as well as seizures, autism, and speech delay.
RESOURCES:
Hart K, Rohrwasser A, Wallis H, et al. Prospective identification by neonatal screening of patients with guanidinoacetate methyltransferase deficiency. Mol Genet Metab. 2021;134(1-2):60-64.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34389248/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Our bodies make a chemical called creatine, which acts like a rechargeable battery for cells — it helps store and release energy. To make creatine, the body uses an enzyme called GAMT. When GAMT is missing or not working, creatine cannot be made, and a building block called guanidinoacetate (GAA) builds up. The brain needs creatine especially badly because it uses a lot of energy. Without enough creatine (and with too much GAA), brain cells cannot work properly, leading to intellectual disability, seizures, and delayed speech.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (presents in infancy/early childhood; identified on newborn screening in some programs)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Homocystinuria (HCY) is caused by defects in a number of genes (the most common being cystathionine β-synthase (CBS), OMIM) that function in the catabolism of methionine. Enzyme deficiencies caused by mutations in these genes lead to the accumulation of homocysteine. The mechanism of homocysteine toxicity in the brain is not fully understood, but it is known to cause white matter abnormalities leading to intellectual impairment. Accumulation of methionine in the case of CBS deficiency could also contribute to this presentation.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch 20: Amino Acids: Degradation and Synthesis

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 36: One-Carbon Metabolism, Folate Deficiency, and Cobalamin Deficiency
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Normally, the body breaks down an amino acid (a building block of protein) called methionine step by step. In homocystinuria, one of the enzymes in this process — most often one called CBS — is missing or broken. This causes a substance called homocysteine to build up in the blood and body. High levels of homocysteine are toxic to brain cells and damage the white matter — the "wiring" of the brain that helps different parts communicate. This damage leads to intellectual disability and other health problems.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (presents in early childhood if not identified by newborn screening; lifelong condition)

Urea Cycle
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Deficiency of argininosuccinate lyase (OMIM) leads to the accumulation of argininosuccinic acid, resulting in argininosuccinic aciduria (ASA). While severe defects in ASA lead to hyperammonemia and acute changes in mental status, weaker alleles that retain some residual function display delayed intellectual development, among other phenotypes, presumably due to sublethal accumulation of ammonia in the brain.
Deficiency of argininosuccinate synthase (OMIM) leads to accumulation of citrulline and citrullinemia. Genetics are similar to ASA, with strong alleles resulting in overt hyperammonemia and weaker alleles showing more insidious delayed intellectual development.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch 20: Amino Acids: Degradation and Synthesis

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 35: Protein degradation, Amino Acid Metabolism, and Nitrogen Balance

Quinonez SC, Lee KN. Citrullinemia Type I. 2004 Jul 7 [Updated 2022 Aug 18]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 1993-2024. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1458/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When we eat protein, our bodies break it down and produce ammonia as a waste product. Normally, the body converts ammonia into a safe substance called urea through a series of steps called the urea cycle. In some urea cycle disorders, one of the enzymes in this cycle is missing or weak, so ammonia builds up in the blood. Ammonia is toxic to brain cells. In milder cases where the deficient enzyme still works a little bit, ammonia builds up slowly over time, gradually damaging the brain and leading to intellectual disability and developmental delays.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (newborn onset for severe forms; milder/partial forms may present later in childhood)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: The primary function of hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HPRT; OMIM) is in purine nucleotide recycling. HPRT deficiency causes Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, showing symptoms of moderate intellectual disability and repetitive behaviors, including self-mutilation. Since the HPRT gene is on the X chromosome, this condition is much more commonly seen in males, though some females can exhibit mild symptoms due to somatic mosaicism caused by X-chromosome inactivation. It is known that HPRT deficiency causes uric acid accumulation in all cells, but the exact biochemical link to the characteristic neurobehavioral disorder, Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, remains unknown.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 38: Gout and Other Diseases Related to the Metabolism of Purine Nucleotides

Ceballos-Picot I, Mockel L, Potier MC, et al. Hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyl transferase regulates early developmental programming of dopamine neurons: implications for Lesch-Nyhan disease pathogenesis. Hum Mol Genet. 2009;18(13):2317-2327.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2694685/

Jinnah HA. HPRT1 Disorders. 2000 Sep 25 [Updated 2020 Aug 6]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 1993-2024. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1149/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Our bodies constantly recycle the building blocks of DNA called purines, using an enzyme called HPRT. In Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, HPRT is missing or not working. Without it, purines cannot be recycled and instead are broken down into a waste product called uric acid, which builds up. While doctors know that uric acid builds up everywhere, it is still not fully understood exactly how this harms the brain. What is known is that certain brain circuits — especially those controlling movement and behavior — are severely disrupted, causing intellectual disability, repetitive movements, and self-injurious behavior. Because the gene for HPRT is on the X chromosome, this condition almost always affects males.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (symptoms appear in infancy/early childhood; lifelong condition; almost exclusively males)

Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Dietary iodine is required for the synthesis and function of thyroid hormones. In the fetus, a lack of dietary iodine results in the condition of congenital iodine deficiency syndrome. This syndrome manifests as global developmental delays, including intellectual disability. Iodine deficiency in early childhood could also lead to impaired growth and cognitive development. The exact role thyroid hormone plays in brain development is unclear, though, as in other tissues, it is probably responsible for regulating energy production and use.
RESOURCES:
Gastaldi R, Muraca M, Beltramo A, Poggi E. Iodine deficiency and its consequences for cognitive and psychomotor development of children. Ital J Pediatr. 2014;40(Suppl 1):A15.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4132543/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The thyroid gland — a small gland in the neck — uses iodine from food to make hormones that help control how the body grows and uses energy. When a pregnant mother or young child does not get enough iodine in their diet, the thyroid cannot make enough of these hormones. Thyroid hormones are especially important for the developing brain — they help brain cells grow, multiply, and connect properly. Without enough thyroid hormone at critical times of brain development, the brain does not develop correctly, leading to intellectual disability, slowed growth, and other developmental delays.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (fetal/neonatal onset when mother is iodine deficient; early childhood deficiency also impairs development)

Lysosomes and Peroxisomes
Macronutrients: Structure and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Lysosomal storage disorders are caused by mutations in lysosomal acid hydrolases and are characterized by the accumulation of lipids in lysosomes. The lipid in question depends on which enzyme is deficient: GM2 ganglioside in Tay-Sachs and Sandhoff diseases (affecting the enzyme subunits hexosaminidase A (OMIM) and B (OMIM), respectively); sphingomyelin in Niemann-Pick type A and B disease (affecting the enzyme sphingomyelin phosphodiesterase 1; OMIM); galactosylceramide in Krabbe disease (affecting the enzyme galactocerebrosidase; OMIM); cerebroside sulfate in metachromatic leukodystrophy (affecting the enzyme arylsulfatase A; OMIM); and glucocerebroside in Gaucher's disease (affecting the enzyme lysosomal glucocerebrosidase; OMIM)

In the CNS, accumulation of these lipids in lysosomes leads to neuronal cell death and myelin defects, both of which result in neurological problems. These problems can manifest as a range of symptoms, from learning and behavioral difficulties to rapid death caused by severe neural degeneration. These different symptoms depend on the affected gene and the severity of the mutation.
RESOURCES:
Rajkumar V, Dumpa V. Lysosomal Storage Disease. [Updated 2023 Jul 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK563270/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Inside our cells are tiny compartments called lysosomes that act like recycling centers, breaking down and reusing old materials. In Niemann-Pick type C, a protein that helps move cholesterol out of lysosomes is broken, so cholesterol gets trapped inside. This buildup is especially harmful to brain cells, causing them to malfunction and die. Over time, the loss of brain cells leads to worsening neurological problems, including intellectual disability. Depending on how much the protein still works, symptoms can range from mild learning difficulties to severe neurological disease.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (most commonly presents in childhood, though onset can range from neonatal to adult)

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Inside our cells are tiny recycling centers called lysosomes. In Niemann-Pick type A, an enzyme called sphingomyelin phosphodiesterase is missing, so a fatty substance called sphingomyelin cannot be broken down and builds up in lysosomes throughout the body — especially in the brain. This buildup damages and kills brain cells and the protective covering around nerve fibers (called myelin). Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Cells in the brain have a fatty protective coating around their "wires" (nerve fibers) called myelin, which helps signals travel quickly. In Krabbe disease, an enzyme called galactocerebrosidase is missing or broken, so a fatty substance called galactosylceramide builds up in the lysosomal recycling centers of brain cells. This buildup destroys the cells that make myelin (called oligodendrocytes), causing the myelin to fall apart. Without proper myelin, nerve signals slow down and fail, leading to rapidly worsening neurological problems and intellectual disability.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (most commonly infantile-onset, though late-onset forms exist in older children and adults)

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Similar to Tay-Sachs, Sandhoff disease involves the buildup of a fatty substance called GM2 ganglioside in brain cell lysosomes. In Sandhoff disease, both subunits (A and B) of the enzyme hexosaminidase are affected, meaning the enzyme cannot work at all. This causes GM2 ganglioside to accumulate even more broadly — in brain cells and other organs — leading to progressive destruction of the brain's nerve cells. The result is rapid neurological decline, intellectual disability, and loss of motor skills beginning in infancy.

In Gaucher disease, an enzyme called lysosomal glucocerebrosidase is missing or not working properly, so a fatty substance called glucocerebroside builds up inside lysosomes — mainly in immune cells. PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Amino Acid Metabolism
Genetic Inheritance
Inborn Errors of Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Maple syrup urine disease (MSUD) is caused by mutations in subunits of the mitochondrial branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex (BCKD), specifically the enzymes BCKDHA (OMIM), BCKDHB (OMIM), and DBT (OMIM). This complex is required to convert the toxic ketoacid intermediates of branched-chain amino acids (leucine, isoleucine, and valine) metabolism to non-toxic by-products. These ketoacids have a distinctive smell, akin to maple syrup, when secreted into the urine, giving the condition its name.
Accumulation of the ketoacids leads to brain edema. In cases of mutations leading to complete or almost complete loss of BCKD function, if untreated, this edema rapidly leads to coma and death. When the mutations in the BCKD are weaker, CNS toxicity of the ketoacids results in delayed intellectual development, amongst other non-specific phenotypes.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 35: Protein degradation, Amino Acid Metabolism, and Nitrogen Balance


Strauss KA, Puffenberger EG, Carson VJ. Maple Syrup Urine Disease. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, Pagon RA, Wallace SE, Amemiya A, eds. GeneReviews®. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; January 30, 2006.
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1319/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When we eat protein, the body breaks it down into amino acids. Three of those amino acids — leucine, isoleucine, and valine — are normally broken down further by an enzyme complex called BCKD. In maple syrup urine disease, this enzyme is missing or weak, so toxic breakdown products of these amino acids build up in the blood and brain. These toxins cause swelling in the brain. In milder cases where some enzyme still works, the brain is damaged slowly over time, leading to intellectual disability and developmental delays. The sweet, maple-syrup smell of the urine comes from these buildup products.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (newborn onset for classic severe form; milder forms present later in childhood; identified on newborn screening)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Extracellular Matrix
Genetic Inheritance 
Inborn Errors of Metabolism 
Lysosomes and Peroxisomes
Protein Synthesis, Trafficking, and Posttranslational Modifications
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Mucopolysaccharidoses (MPS) are caused by mutations in enzymes responsible for the degradation of glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) within the lysosome. GAGs are long carbohydrate chains that primarily function as structural and lubricating components of the extracellular milieu. In the absence of the enzymes required for their turnover, GAGs accumulate in lysosomes, leading to cellular dysfunction. When cellular dysfunction occurs in the brain, it results in intellectual delay and behavioral problems. This is accompanied by distinct facial features, short stature, and eye problems. There are 7 distinct subtypes of mucopolysaccharidoses caused by mutations in different enzymes, the majority of which lead to intellectual delay. The two best-known types are MPH I (Hurler syndrome; OMIM) and MPS II (Hunter syndrome; OMIM), both of which cause loss of the enzyme α-L-iduronidase and the accumulation of heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate in lysosomes.

I-cell disease (OMIM) shows symptoms very similar to MPS, but in this case is caused by defects not in the lysosomal enzymes themselves, but in trafficking the enzymes to the lysosome. To be trafficked to the lysosome, lysosomal enzymes are phosphorylated on specific mannoses in enzyme carbohydrate side chains. These mannose-6-phosphate residues are recognized by an intracellular receptor, which directs enzyme trafficking to the lysosome. I-cell disease is caused by mutations in the enzyme UDP-N-Acetylglucosamine-1-Phosphotransferase, which catalyzes mannose phosphorylation. The non-phosphorylated enzymes, instead of being trafficked to the lysosome, are instead excreted into the bloodstream. The lack of lysosomal enzymes leads to the accumulation of GAGs and symptoms similar to MPS diseases.
RESOURCES:
https://www.ninds.nih.gov/health-information/disorders/mucopolysaccharidoses

Abetalipoproteinemia. National Organization for Rare Disorders. Accessed November 24, 2025.
https://rarediseases.info.nih.gov/diseases/6749/i-cell-disease
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Our cells are constantly building and breaking down long sugar chains called glycosaminoglycans (GAGs), which act like the body's scaffolding and lubricant. In mucopolysaccharidoses, one of the enzymes needed to break down GAGs inside the cell's recycling center (the lysosome) is missing. Without that enzyme, GAGs pile up inside the lysosomes of cells throughout the body. When this happens in brain cells, it disrupts how they work, leading to intellectual disability, behavioral problems, and other health issues. The specific symptoms depend on which enzyme is missing and how severe the deficiency is.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (onset typically in infancy or early childhood; lifelong condition)

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Hurler syndrome, an enzyme called α-L-iduronidase is missing, so two types of long sugar chains — heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate — cannot be broken down and pile up in the lysosomes of cells throughout the body, including brain cells. This buildup is like having a recycling center that gets so clogged it stops working altogether. As brain cells fill up with these undigested sugar chains, they stop functioning properly, leading to progressive intellectual disability. Other symptoms include a distinctive facial appearance, short stature, and heart and eye problems.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (onset typically in infancy; most severe MPS type; progressive course)

Protein Synthesis, Trafficking, and Posttranslational Modifications
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Mucopolysaccharidoses (MPS) are caused by mutations in enzymes responsible for the degradation of glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) within the lysosome. GAGs are long carbohydrate chains that primarily function as structural and lubricating components of the extracellular milieu. In the absence of the enzymes required for their turnover, GAGs accumulate in lysosomes, leading to cellular dysfunction. When cellular dysfunction occurs in the brain, it results in intellectual delay and behavioral problems. This is accompanied by distinct facial features, short stature, and eye problems. There are 7 distinct subtypes of mucopolysaccharidoses caused by mutations in different enzymes, the majority of which lead to intellectual delay. The two best-known types are MPH I (Hurler syndrome; OMIM) and MPS II (Hunter syndrome; OMIM), both of which cause loss of the enzyme α-L-iduronidase and the accumulation of heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate in lysosomes.

I-cell disease (OMIM) shows symptoms very similar to MPS, but in this case is caused by defects not in the lysosomal enzymes themselves, but in trafficking the enzymes to the lysosome. To be trafficked to the lysosome, lysosomal enzymes are phosphorylated on specific mannoses in enzyme carbohydrate side chains. These mannose-6-phosphate residues are recognized by an intracellular receptor, which directs enzyme trafficking to the lysosome. I-cell disease is caused by mutations in the enzyme UDP-N-Acetylglucosamine-1-Phosphotransferase, which catalyzes mannose phosphorylation. The non-phosphorylated enzymes, instead of being trafficked to the lysosome, are instead excreted into the bloodstream. The lack of lysosomal enzymes leads to the accumulation of GAGs and symptoms similar to MPS diseases.
RESOURCES:
https://www.ninds.nih.gov/health-information/disorders/mucopolysaccharidoses

Abetalipoproteinemia. National Organization for Rare Disorders. Accessed November 24, 2025.
https://rarediseases.info.nih.gov/diseases/6749/i-cell-disease
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Normally, the cell's recycling center (the lysosome) gets its cleaning enzymes delivered by a special "address label" — a sugar tag called mannose-6-phosphate that guides the enzymes to the right place. In I-cell disease, the enzyme that puts this address label on lysosomal enzymes is broken. Without the label, the lysosomal enzymes get sent out of the cell into the bloodstream instead of into the lysosome. The lysosomes then have no enzymes to clean up waste materials like glycosaminoglycans (long sugar chains), which pile up and disrupt how cells work — especially in the brain — leading to intellectual disability and symptoms similar to Hurler syndrome, but often more severe.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (onset in infancy; severe progressive course)

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Like Hurler syndrome, Hunter syndrome is caused by the buildup of long sugar chains (heparan sulfate and dermatan sulfate) in the cell's lysosomes due to a missing enzyme called iduronate-2-sulfatase. Over time, this buildup clogs brain cells and prevents them from working properly, causing intellectual disability and behavioral problems in the more severe form of the disease. Because the gene for iduronate-2-sulfatase is on the X chromosome, Hunter syndrome almost exclusively affects males. Symptoms also include distinctive facial features, joint stiffness, and organ enlargement.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (onset typically between ages 2–4; almost exclusively males; X-linked inheritance)

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Sanfilippo syndrome, one of several enzymes needed to break down a specific long sugar chain called heparan sulfate is missing. Heparan sulfate accumulates in the lysosomes of brain cells, causing significant disruption to how those cells work. Sanfilippo syndrome primarily affects the brain — causing severe intellectual disability and behavioral problems — while physical symptoms are generally milder than in other mucopolysaccharidoses. There are four subtypes (A, B, C, D) depending on which enzyme is missing, but all cause a similar picture of progressive brain deterioration.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (onset typically in early childhood, ages 2–6; progressive neurological decline)

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Sly syndrome, an enzyme called beta-glucuronidase is missing, so several types of long sugar chains (heparan sulfate, dermatan sulfate, and chondroitin sulfate) cannot be broken down and accumulate in lysosomes throughout the body. When this buildup occurs in brain cells, it prevents them from functioning correctly, leading to intellectual disability. Sly syndrome is one of the rarest mucopolysaccharidoses, and severity can range widely — from very severe forms detectable before birth to milder forms diagnosed in childhood.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (can present prenatally, in infancy, or in early childhood depending on severity)

Genetic Inheritance 
Inborn Errors of Metabolism

Membrane Transport
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: ALD is caused by mutations in the peroxisomal membrane transporter protein ABCD1 (OMIM), which is required for the import of VLCFA into peroxisomes, while ZSDs (various genes) affect the biogenesis of peroxisomes themselves. Both conditions result in impaired peroxisomal metabolism of very long-chain fatty acids (FAs). In both cases, accumulation of these FAs leads to CNS demyelination and loss of white matter in the brain. Symptoms include progressive neurological defects, including intellectual disability.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 27: Fatty acids, Ketone Bodies, and Ketoacidosis

Wanders RJA, Baes M, Ribeiro D, Ferdinandusse S, Waterham HR. The physiological functions of human peroxisomes. Physiol Rev. 2023;103(1):957-1024.
https://journals.physiology.org/doi/full/10.1152/physrev.00051.2021
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Peroxisomes are tiny compartments inside cells that break down certain types of fats, including very long-chain fatty acids. In Zellweger spectrum disorders, mutations in genes needed to build peroxisomes mean that peroxisomes either don't form properly or don't form at all. Without working peroxisomes, very long-chain fatty acids cannot be broken down and accumulate in the body. In the brain, this buildup damages the protective myelin coating around nerve fibers and kills nerve cells, causing progressive neurological problems, including intellectual disability. Symptoms range from severe (classic Zellweger syndrome) to milder forms depending on how much peroxisome function remains.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (classic Zellweger syndrome presents in newborns; milder ZSD forms may present later in childhood)

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Peroxisomes are compartments in cells responsible for breaking down certain very long-chain fatty acids. In adrenoleukodystrophy, a transporter protein called ABCD1 — which normally carries very long-chain fatty acids into the peroxisome for breakdown — is broken. Without this transporter, very long-chain fatty acids build up in cells throughout the body, especially in the brain and adrenal glands. In the brain, this buildup destroys myelin — the protective coating on nerve fibers — causing progressive loss of neurological function, including intellectual decline. Because the ABCD1 gene is on the X chromosome, this condition mainly affects males, though carrier females can develop milder symptoms.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (childhood cerebral form typically presents in boys ages 4–10; adrenomyeloneuropathy form can present in young adults; X-linked)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Mutations in the phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH; OMIM) gene are required for phenylalanine metabolism. The loss of gene function leads to the accumulation of toxic levels of phenylalanine that damage neurons and lead to intellectual disability and other mental disorders.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 35: Protein degradation, Amino Acid Metabolism, and Nitrogen Balance

MedlinePlus [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Library of Medicine (US);  Phenylketonuria; [updated 2023 Apr 25; cited 2023 Sep 1]
https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/phenylketonuria/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Phenylalanine is an amino acid (a building block of protein) found in many foods. Normally, an enzyme called phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) converts phenylalanine into another amino acid called tyrosine. In classical PKU, this enzyme is missing or not working, so phenylalanine builds up to very high — and toxic — levels in the blood and brain. High levels of phenylalanine damage brain cells and interfere with the production of important brain chemicals, leading to intellectual disability and behavioral problems. A low-phenylalanine diet started early in life can prevent most of the brain damage.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (identified by newborn screening; dietary treatment must begin immediately to prevent intellectual disability; lifelong management required)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Mutations in the phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH; OMIM) gene are required for phenylalanine metabolism. The loss of gene function leads to the accumulation of toxic levels of phenylalanine that damage neurons and lead to intellectual disability and other mental disorders.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 35: Protein degradation, Amino Acid Metabolism, and Nitrogen Balance

MedlinePlus [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Library of Medicine (US);  Phenylketonuria; [updated 2023 Apr 25; cited 2023 Sep 1]
https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/phenylketonuria/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH) needs a helper molecule called tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) to work correctly. BH4 acts like a key that allows the enzyme to do its job. In non-classical PKU, the body cannot make or recycle BH4 properly. Without enough BH4, PAH cannot break down phenylalanine, so it builds up to toxic levels — just as in classical PKU. However, BH4 is also needed by other enzymes that make important brain chemicals (neurotransmitters). Because of this dual problem, non-classical PKU tends to be harder to treat and can cause more severe neurological problems than classical PKU even when phenylalanine levels are controlled.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (identified by newborn screening; requires more complex treatment than classical PKU; lifelong condition)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) is caused by maternal uniparental disomy for a region of chromosome 15 (OMIM). Genetics is complementary to Angelman syndrome, which is caused by paternal uniparental disomy of the same critical region. No single gene in the critical region has been identified that, on its own, results in PWS, though candidates include snoRNAs involved in RNA processing. PWS leads to mild to moderate intellectual impairment, including hyperphagia (constant hunger leading to overeating and obesity).
RESOURCES:
MedlinePlus [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Library of Medicine (US);  Prader-Willi syndrome; [updated 2022 May 13; cited 2023 Sep 1]
https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/prader-willi-syndrome/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Everyone normally has two copies of most genes — one from mom and one from dad. In Prader-Willi syndrome, both copies of a critical region on chromosome 15 come from the mother (called maternal uniparental disomy), meaning the genes that should come from dad are missing. Several genes and small RNA molecules in this region that are normally only active on the dad's copy end up being silenced. These missing gene products — including small RNAs that help process other RNAs in the cell — appear to be important for normal brain development and function. Without them, individuals experience mild to moderate intellectual disability, constant feelings of hunger (hyperphagia), and behavioral challenges.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (presents in infancy with poor feeding/low muscle tone; hyperphagia and intellectual disability become evident in early childhood; lifelong condition)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: The exact molecular mechanism by which trisomy of chromosome 21 leads to intellectual disability is not understood, but is possibly caused by increased gene dosage leading to general defects in cellular protein homeostasis. Defects include intellectual disability, along with heart problems, increased cancer risk, and characteristic morphological features.
RESOURCES:
Krivega M, Storchova Z. Consequences of trisomy syndromes - 21 and beyond. Trends Genet. 2023;39(3):172-174.
https://www.cell.com/trends/genetics/abstract/S0168-9525(22)00291-8
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Normally, people have two copies of each chromosome. In Down syndrome, there are three copies of chromosome 21 instead of two. Having an extra copy means that all the genes on chromosome 21 are made in larger amounts than normal. This extra gene "dosage" disrupts the careful balance of proteins in cells, which appears to interfere with how brain cells develop, communicate, and clear away damaged proteins. The exact reasons are still being studied, but the result includes intellectual disability, along with heart problems, characteristic physical features, and an increased risk of some diseases.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (present from birth; lifelong condition; degree of intellectual disability varies among individuals)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Breast milk jaundice may be seen in the period 1-2 weeks after birth. Multiple factors are known to cause jaundice. A progesterone metabolite, pregnane-3a,20ß-diol, present in breast milk, inhibits uridine diphosphoglucuronic acid glucuronyl transferase, leading to less conjugation of bilirubin, so it is not excreted and remains in circulation.
RESOURCES:
Robbins, Cotran & Kumar Pathologic Basis of Disease, 11e, Ch. 10: Diseases of infancy and childhood.

Flaherman VJ, Maisels MJ; Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine. ABM Clinical Protocol #22: Guidelines for Management of Jaundice in the Breastfeeding Infant 35 Weeks or More of Gestation-Revised 2017. Breastfeed Med. 2017;12(5):250-257.
https://www.liebertpub.com/doi/10.1089/bfm.2017.29042.vjf

Bratton S, Cantu RM, Stern M. Breast Milk Jaundice. [Updated 2023 Jan 17]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: 
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK537334
SPECIAL NOTES: Note that this should differentiated from Breastfeeding jaundice that results from low breastmilk intake and is associated with dehydration (which is not as biochemical in nature)
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When red blood cells break down, they release a yellow waste product called bilirubin. Normally, the liver processes and removes bilirubin from the body. Breast milk contains a substance that interferes with the liver's ability to process bilirubin properly. When bilirubin can't be removed efficiently, it builds up in the blood and causes the skin and eyes to turn yellow.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (newborns, typically appearing 1–2 weeks after birth)
