Heme Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Cholestasis can be due to gallstones (intraductal obstruction), a tumor in the head of the pancreas, jaundice of pregnancy, biliary atresia, primary biliary cholangitis, or primary sclerosing cholangitis.  Obstruction of bile flow leads to the accumulation of conjugated bilirubin in the blood, which in turn can lead to jaundice.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e., 2025, Ch 21: Amino Acids: Conversion to Specialized Products

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 14: Heme Metabolism, Porphyrias, and Hyperbilirubinemia
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: After the liver processes bilirubin, it is normally sent out of the body through a system of tubes called bile ducts. When something blocks these tubes — such as a gallstone or a tumor — the processed bilirubin gets backed up and spills into the bloodstream instead of being eliminated. This buildup causes the skin and eyes to turn yellow.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, though biliary atresia presents in newborns

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: G6PD deficiency (OMIM) impairs the ability of the cell to produce reducing equivalent NADPH, which is essential for the maintenance of the reduced glutathione (GSH) pool. Reduced GSH is required for detoxifying free radicals and peroxides. Decreased levels of reduced GSH can lead to increased oxidation of RBC membrane proteins, making them rigid, fragile, and prone to membrane damage and premature destruction, and removal from the circulation by macrophages of the reticuloendothelial system (liver, spleen). This leads to excess hemolysis and pre-hepatic unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e., 2025, Ch 13: Pentose Phosphate Pathway and Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide Phosphate

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 21: Pentose Phosphate Pathway, Oxidative Stress, and Glucose 6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Red blood cells need a certain protective system to defend themselves against damage. In G6PD deficiency, this protection is weakened, making red blood cells fragile and prone to breaking apart earlier than they should. When too many red blood cells break down at once, the liver gets overwhelmed with more bilirubin than it can process, and the excess builds up in the blood, causing jaundice.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages; episodes often triggered by infections, certain medications, or foods

Genetic Inheritance

Protein Structure and Folding
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hereditary spherocytosis is caused by deficiency or structural abnormalities in spectrin (OMIM; OMIM) or ankyrin (OMIM) proteins. This can weaken the link anchoring the erythrocyte membrane to the cytoskeleton, leading to abnormal spherical RBCs (normal shape is biconcave). This shape makes RBCs less deformable and more prone to lysis in the spleen/RES, leading to hemolytic anemia. This causes unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Hereditary elliptocytosis has a similar genetic abnormality to spherocytosis but differs in the shape of RBCs, which are elliptical.
RESOURCES:
Harper's Illustrated Biochemistry, 32e. 2023. Ch. 53: Red Blood Cells

Pathology: The Big Picture.  2008. Ch. 6: Genetic Disorders
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Red blood cells normally have a flexible, disc-like shape that allows them to squeeze through tiny blood vessels. In hereditary spherocytosis and elliptocytosis, gene changes cause the structural proteins that maintain the cell's shape to be faulty, making the red blood cells stiff and misshapen. These abnormal cells are fragile and get destroyed prematurely in the spleen. When too many red blood cells break down, the liver can't keep up with processing all the bilirubin produced, and it builds up in the blood, causing jaundice.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically identified in childhood, though severity varies)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Membrane Proteins and Lipids
Heme Metabolism
SPECIFIC DIFFERENTIAL: Immune ABO, Rh incompatibility, Rh incompatibility, anti-RBC autoAb
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: RBC's undergo hemolysis when they are exposed to incompatible antibodies present in incompatible blood ((ABO/Rh) e.g, transfusion reaction)). Rh incompatibility can also arise when the blood of Rh− mother is mixed with an Rh+ fetus during delivery or pregnancy (hemolytic disease of fetal and neonate (HDFN)). The antibodies produced in the mothers blood after the initial exposure to Rh+ fetal blood can react with antigen present on fetal RBCs in the subsequent pregnancy and agglutinate and destroy them, leading to hemolytic anemia. This will present as prehepatic hyperbilirubinemia.
RESOURCES:
Harper's Illustrated Biochemistry, 32e. 2023. Ch. 53: Red Blood Cells
 
Pathology: The Big Picture.  2008. Ch. 6: Genetic Disorders
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Red blood cells have markers on their surface that identify them as belonging to a specific blood type. When the immune system encounters red blood cells with unfamiliar markers — for example, during a mismatched blood transfusion or when a mother's immune system reacts to a baby's different blood type — it produces antibodies that attack and destroy those red blood cells. The mass breakdown of red blood cells releases large amounts of bilirubin, more than the liver can process, causing it to build up and turn the skin and eyes yellow.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages; Rh incompatibility typically affects newborns

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance 
Glycolysis 
Heme Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Pyruvate kinase deficiency (OMIM) causes reduced rate of glycolysis, leading to decreased ATP production. Due to the absence of mitochondria, erythrocytes are completely reliant on anaerobic glycolysis to produce ATP. Inherited deficiencies of the pyruvate kinase result in low ATP production. The insufficient amount of ATP production causes a net accumulation of Na+ inside the cells, which subsequently attracts water. This results in swelling of the cells and hemolysis through reticuloendothelial system. Symptoms typically present between birth and early childhood with signs of hemolytic anemia.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e., 2025, Ch 8: Introduction to Metabolism and Glycolysis 

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 19: Glycolysis and its Regulation by Hormones and Hypoxia

Enegela OA, Anjum F. Pyruvate Kinase Deficiency. [Updated 2023 Apr 27]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/sites/books/NBK560581/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Red blood cells have no power plant of their own — they rely entirely on breaking down sugar to make the energy they need to function and stay intact. In pyruvate kinase deficiency, a key enzyme in this energy-making process is missing, so red blood cells can't make enough energy. Without energy, the cells can't maintain their internal balance and eventually burst. When too many red blood cells break down, the liver is flooded with more bilirubin than it can process, causing it to build up and cause jaundice.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (symptoms typically appear between birth and early childhood)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Heme Metabolism

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Sickle cell disease is characterized by excessive sickling of red blood cells (RBCs) due to the formation of long polymers by hemoglobin S (due to the replacement of glutamate with more hydrophobic valine at position 6 of β-globin (OMIM)). Sickling predominantly occurs during episodes of deoxygenation/hypoxia. These irreversibly sickled cells are eventually hemolyzed (intravascular and extravascular hemolysis). This causes conjugated and unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 17: Hemoglobinopathies

Robbins Basic Pathology. 11e. 2023. Ch. 10: Hematopoietic and Lymphoid Systems

Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 22e. 2025. Ch. 103: Disorders of Hemoglobin
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In sickle cell disease, red blood cells change into a stiff, curved shape when oxygen levels drop. These misshapen cells are fragile and break apart, both inside blood vessels and in the spleen. When red blood cells break down in large numbers, they release bilirubin faster than the liver can process and remove it. This buildup of bilirubin causes the skin and eyes to turn yellow.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric and adults (a lifelong genetic condition diagnosed in childhood)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Hematopoiesis
Heme Metabolism
Heme Proteins
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Reduced synthesis of α or β-globin  (OMIM; OMIM)  chains leads to inadequate HbA formation and results in production of RBCs with toxic globin tetramers. This imbalance in the synthesis of β-globin and α-globin chains results in unpaired α or β chains in RBCs to form insoluble precipitates. The insoluble precipitate can also bind to and damage RBC membrane proteins, leading to RBC hemolysis and ineffective erythropoiesis. These abnormal RBCs also have a reduced lifespan and undergo hemolysis, eventually leading to unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. The presentation of jaundice is more common in thalassemia major versus thalassemia minor.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 17: Hemoglobinopathies

Robbins Basic Pathology. 11e. 2023. Ch. 10. Hematopoietic and Lymphoid Systems

Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 22e. 2025. Ch. 103: Disorders of Hemoglobin
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Red blood cells are built from two types of protein chains that need to be made in balanced amounts. In thalassemia, one type of chain is made in much smaller amounts than the other. The unmatched chains clump together, damage the red blood cell from the inside, and cause the cells to break down prematurely. When too many red blood cells break down, more bilirubin is released than the liver can process, causing jaundice. Jaundice is more common and more severe in the more serious form of the disease.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically identified in infancy or early childhood)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Alcohol Metabolism
Heme Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Ethanol has both direct and indirect toxic effects on the liver. The direct toxicity of ethanol to hepatic or other cells can increase the fluidity of biological membranes, disrupting cellular functions. In addition, accumulation of the toxic intermediate acetaldehyde increases production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), overwhelming antioxidant defenses and causing lipid peroxidation, mitochondrial damage, and hepatocyte injury. The indirect effects on the liver are a result of enhanced synthesis of fatty acids and cholesterol in the liver, causing hepatic steatosis, followed by alcoholic hepatitis. These factors contribute to the damage and death of hepatic cells. They can also alter the activity of enzymes involved in bilirubin metabolism.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 14: Heme Metabolism, Porphyrias, and Hyperbilirubinemia; Ch. 30: Metabolism of Ethanol and the Consequences of Alcohol Dependence Syndrome.

Pathophysiology of Disease: An Introduction to Clinical Medicine, 8e. McGraw Hill; 2019. Chapter 14. 

Heuman DM. Alcoholic Hepatitis. Medscape. Published 2019 Sep 19. Accessed Nov 24, 2025.
https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/170539-overview
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Alcohol is toxic to liver cells both directly and through a harmful byproduct it produces when it is broken down. This byproduct damages liver cells and triggers the production of harmful molecules that injure the liver further. Alcohol also causes fat to build up in the liver, which can progress to inflammation and liver cell damage. As liver cells are injured or die, the liver loses its ability to process and remove bilirubin from the body, causing it to build up and turn the skin and eyes yellow.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT; OMIM) is produced in the liver, travels in the blood to the lungs (its main function is to inhibit neutrophil elastase in the lungs to prevent lung damage); storage of nonfunctional proteins (abnormal AAT) in hepatocytes can cause apoptosis. Certain inherited variants of alpha 1 antitrypsin (S or Z) can accumulate in the liver, causing hepatocyte damage leading to jaundice.
RESOURCES:
Robbins, Cotran & Kumar Pathologic Basis of Disease, 11e. Ch. 18: The Liver and Biliary Tract

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 13: Pathologic Alterations of the Extracellular Matrix that Involve Fibrillin, Elastin, or Proteoglycans; Ch. 14: Heme Metabolism, Porphyrias, and Hyperbilirubinemia.

Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Deficiency. National Organization for Rare Disorders. Accessed November 18, 2025.
https://rarediseases.org/rare-diseases/alpha-1-antitrypsin-deficiency/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Alpha-1 antitrypsin is a protective protein made in the liver. In some people, a gene change causes an abnormal version of this protein to be made that gets stuck inside liver cells instead of being released. This buildup damages and destroys liver cells over time. As liver cells are lost, the liver's ability to process bilirubin is reduced, leading to a buildup that causes jaundice.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric and adults (liver disease can appear in infancy or develop over time)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Alcohol Metabolism
Extracellular Matrix
Heme Metabolism
Signal Transduction
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Chronic inflammation triggered by factors such as alcohol, viral infections, toxins, and autoimmune reactions can stimulate the release of transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β), which promotes collagen synthesis and cellular proliferation, leading to fibrosis. Chronic inflammation can also cause programmed cell death (apoptosis). Cycles of cell death, fibrosis, and hepatic nodule regeneration result in cirrhosis of the liver. This causes hepatocellular dysfunction, leading to impaired protein synthesis and altered enzymatic activity; all of these processes contribute to the development of hyperbilirubinemia.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 13: Pathologic Alterations of the Extracellular Matrix that Involve Fibrillin, Elastin, or Proteoglycans; Ch. 14: Heme Metabolism, Porphyrias, and Hyperbilirubinemia.

Pathology: The Big Picture. McGraw Hill; 2008. Chapter 15. Pathophysiology of Disease: An Introduction to Clinical Medicine, 8e. McGraw Hill. Chapter 14
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Long-term liver injury from any cause — such as alcohol, infection, or toxins — leads to ongoing inflammation and death of liver cells. Each time liver cells die, the body tries to repair the damage by forming scar tissue. Over time, this scar tissue replaces healthy liver tissue, making the liver stiff and less able to do its job. A scarred liver can no longer process bilirubin effectively, causing it to build up in the blood and produce jaundice.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance 
Heme Metabolism  
Membrane Transport
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Dubin-Johnson syndrome: A defect in the ABC family transport protein (ABCC2/Multidrug resistance-associated protein 2 (MRP2); OMIM), which is located on the canalicular part of liver cells, leads to impaired export of conjugated bilirubin and hence accumulation in the liver, resulting in a characteristic black color.

Rotor Syndrome: an autosomal recessive disorder caused by homozygous mutations in both the SLCO1B1 (OMIM) and SLCO1B3 (OMIM) genes, which encode organic anion transporting polypeptide 1B1 and 1B3 (OATP1B1 and OATP1B3, respectively). These proteins are found in liver cells and mediate sodium-independent cellular uptake of compounds including bilirubin glucuronide, bile acids, steroid and thyroid hormones, and numerous drugs, toxins, and their conjugates.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 14: Heme Metabolism, Porphyrias, and Hyperbilirubinemia.

Talaga ZJ, Vaidya PN. Dubin-Johnson Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: 
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK536994/

Hashmi MF, Mehta D. Rotor Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Feb 19]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: 
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532306/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: After the liver processes bilirubin, it normally uses specific transporter proteins to move the processed bilirubin out of liver cells and into the bile system for elimination. In Dubin-Johnson syndrome, one of these transporter proteins is faulty, so processed bilirubin gets trapped in the liver cells and backs up into the blood. In Rotor syndrome, different transporter proteins needed to take up and recycle bilirubin in liver cells are both faulty, also causing processed bilirubin to build up in the blood. Both conditions cause the skin and eyes to turn yellow.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Typically identified in adolescence or early adulthood, though can present earlier

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Heme Metabolism
Iron Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hereditary hemochromatosis is caused by inherited mutations in homeostatic iron regulator (HFE; OMIM). Hepcidin is an acute phase protein which regulates iron homeostasis in the body, it responds to signals mediated by HFE. Hepcidin primarily represses the absorption of iron by the intestinal enterocytes on its basolateral side by its interaction with ferroportin-1. Mutations in HFE protein reduce the hepcidin production in hepatocyte leading to increased dietary iron absorption. Accumulation of excess iron in the liver and pancreas may destroy the functions of these organs. Damage to hepatocytes causes decrease bilirubin conjugation.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 15: Iron Metabolism: Iron-Deficiency Anemia and Iron Overload.

Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 22e. 2025. Ch. 426: Hemochromatosis 

Pathology: The Big Picture. 1e. 2008. Ch.15: Pathology of the Liver, Gallbladder, and Pancreas
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The body normally absorbs just the right amount of iron from food and carefully regulates how much is kept. In hereditary hemochromatosis, a gene change disrupts this regulation, allowing the body to absorb far too much iron. Excess iron accumulates in the liver and other organs, damaging and destroying cells over time. As liver cells are damaged, the liver becomes less able to process bilirubin, causing it to build up and produce jaundice.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults (symptoms typically develop in midlife)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Heme Metabolism 
Iron Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Excessive iron intake and chronic transfusions can lead to Iron overload (Secondary) leading to iron deposition in liver parenchymal cells occurs in chronic disorders of erythropoiesis, those with defects in hemoglobin synthesis and ineffective erythropoiesis. Patients with these conditions require frequent and repeated blood transfusions, leading to iron overload. Excess iron may cause destruction of the hepatocytes, which decrease the bilirubin conjugation capacity of the liver leading to jaundice.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 15: Iron Metabolism: Iron-Deficiency Anemia and Iron Overload.

Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 22e. 2025. Ch. 426: Hemochromatosis

Kemp WL, Burns DK, Brown TG. eds. Pathology: The Big Picture. McGraw Hill; 1e, 2008, Ch.15: Pathology of the Liver, Gallbladder, and Pancreas
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When a person needs frequent blood transfusions — for example, to treat a blood disorder — iron from the transfused blood builds up in the body over time, since the body has no way to get rid of excess iron. This excess iron deposits in the liver, damaging and destroying liver cells. As liver cells are lost, the liver can no longer process bilirubin effectively, causing it to build up and turn the skin and eyes yellow.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages, depending on the underlying condition requiring transfusions

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Heme Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Viral infection can cause liver inflammation (hepatitis) and damage to hepatocytes, leading to impaired bilirubin conjugation. Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia in hepatitis is due to impaired intrahepatic bile flow or obstruction to bile flow from hepatic cells into the bile canaliculi and hepatobiliary system.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025. Ch. 21: Amino Acids: Conversion to Specialized Products   

Kemp WL, Burns DK, Brown TG. eds. Pathology: The Big Picture. McGraw Hill; 1e, 2008, Ch.15: Pathology of the Liver, Gallbladder, and Pancreas
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Certain viruses can infect and inflame the liver, damaging liver cells. When liver cells are injured, they can't properly process and move bilirubin out of the body through the bile system. Bilirubin backs up into the bloodstream, causing it to build up and turn the skin and eyes yellow.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages, depending on the type of virus

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance 
Heme Metabolism
Membrane Transport
Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Wilson's disease is a inherited disorder caused by mutation in the ATP7B gene (OMIM), the gene encodes for a membrane-bound copper-transporter ATPase. The ATP7B gene is responsible for producing a protein that helps transport copper from the liver to other parts of the body and into bile for excretion. When the ATP7B gene is mutated, it disrupts this process, leading to the accumulation of copper in the liver and other organs. Defect or deficiency of ATPase impairs copper excretion into the bile and also the incorporation of copper into ceruloplasmin. Excessive accumulation of copper in liver can lead to hepatitis, and eventually to cirrhosis. Altered liver function due to these processes can affect the bilirubin metabolism and secretion.
RESOURCES:
Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 22e. 2025. Ch. 427: Wilson’s Disease

Kemp WL, Burns DK, Brown TG. eds. Pathology: The Big Picture. McGraw Hill; 1e, 2008, Ch.15: Pathology of the Liver, Gallbladder, and Pancreas
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Copper is a mineral the body needs in small amounts, and the liver is normally responsible for sending excess copper out of the body through bile. In Wilson disease, a gene change causes a copper-transporting protein to be faulty, so excess copper builds up in the liver and other organs. The accumulated copper damages and destroys liver cells, causing inflammation and eventually scarring. As the liver is damaged, its ability to process bilirubin is impaired, leading to jaundice.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Typically identified in children, adolescents, and young adults

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance 
Heme Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
There are developmental and genetic causes of low UDP-glucuronyl transferase activity that result in jaundice.

Neonatal: Since hepatic UDP Glucuronyl Transferase levels are low at birth, a large number of newborns (especially those born preterm) show elevated levels of unconjugated bilirubin.  UDP Glucuronyl Transferase typically reaches adult levels by four weeks.

Mutations in the UDP Glucuronyl Transferase (UGT; OMIM) gene cause decreased conjugation of bilirubin, which can lead to the accumulation of unconjugated bilirubin. High levels of unconjugated bilirubin lead to neurotoxicity because excess unconjugated bilirubin crosses the blood-brain barrier.  

Crigler-Najjar syndrome type I is caused by a complete deficiency of UDP-glucuronosyltransferase (UGT), leading to very high levels of unconjugated bilirubin, and thereby, kernicterus ensues. Because conjugated bilirubin is absent, the feces of a Crigler-Najjar syndrome type I patient are colorless or clay-colored. Because of the severity of this disease, it is fatal without liver transplantation.

Crigler-Najjar type II has some UDP-glucuronosyl transferase activity. As a result, this syndrome is benign. Patients are responsive to phenobarbital induction therapy, which increases UDP-glucuronosyl transferase enzyme activity.

Gilbert Syndrome is relatively common in the US population and may go unnoticed for years. It is a benign condition caused by a mutation in the 5’ promoter region of the UGT gene, leading to reduced enzyme activity. The enzyme levels fluctuate in response to situations that are conducive to stress, such as illness, strenuous exercise, or fasting. Gilbert's Syndrome is milder than Crigler-Najjar Syndrome type II.
RESOURCES:
Crigler Najjar Syndrome. National Organization for Rare Disorders. Accessed November 18, 2025.
https://rarediseases.org/rare-diseases/crigler-najjar-syndrome/

Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025. Ch.  21: Amino Acids: Conversion to Specialized Products

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 14: Heme Metabolism, Porphyrias, and Hyperbilirubinemia.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The liver processes bilirubin using a specific enzyme that attaches a molecule to it, making it water-soluble so it can be removed from the body. In Crigler-Najjar syndrome, gene changes cause this enzyme to be completely missing (type I) or severely reduced (type II). Without this enzyme, bilirubin cannot be processed and removed, and it builds up to very high levels in the blood. Because unprocessed bilirubin can cross into the brain, very high levels can cause serious brain damage. Type I is life-threatening without a liver transplant; type II is milder and can be managed with medication.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (presents at birth or in early infancy)

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The liver processes bilirubin using a specific enzyme. In Gilbert syndrome, a gene change reduces the activity of this enzyme. The enzyme levels drop even further during times of stress, illness, fasting, or strenuous exercise. During these periods, the liver can't keep up with processing bilirubin, and mild, temporary jaundice can appear. This is a benign condition and often goes unnoticed for years.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Typically identified in adolescence or early adulthood

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: At birth, the liver's enzyme for processing bilirubin is not yet fully active. Because of this, many newborns — especially those born prematurely — temporarily cannot process bilirubin efficiently. Bilirubin builds up in the blood during this period, causing the skin and eyes to turn yellow. The enzyme activity normally reaches adult levels by about four weeks of age.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (newborns, particularly premature infants)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Amino Acid Metabolism
Genetic Inheritance
Inborn Errors of Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Alkaptonuria is an autosomal recessive deficiency of homogentisate oxidase (HGD; OMIM) that results in incomplete breakdown of tyrosine, leading to a buildup of homogentisate. Oxidation of homogentisate turns it into a pigmented polymer that can deposit in cartilage. This leads to clinical manifestations, including hip and knee arthritis and degeneration/calcification in intervertebral discs, which cause joint pain.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 35, Protein Degradation, Amino Acid Metabolism, and Nitrogen Balance

Bernardini G, Braconi D, Zatkova A, et al. Alkaptonuria. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2024;10(1):16.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38453957/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body normally breaks down a building block of proteins called tyrosine in a series of steps. In alkaptonuria, one of the enzymes needed for this process is missing, so a substance called homogentisate builds up. Over time, this substance gets converted into a dark pigment that deposits in joint cartilage. This buildup causes the cartilage to break down, leading to arthritis and joint pain, especially in the hips, knees, and spine.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults (joint symptoms typically develop in adulthood after years of buildup)

Genetic Inheritance
Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (CPPD) results from deposits of calcium pyrophosphate crystals in joints and soft tissues and can cause painful inflammation of one or more joints, as well as gradual destruction of joints. While CPPD can be caused by multiple mechanisms that are poorly understood, certain mutations in the ANKH gene (OMIM), which encodes a PPi transporter, can also cause CPPD.
RESOURCES:
Robbins, Cotran & Kumar Pathologic Basis of Disease, 11e 2026. Ch. 26: Bones, Joints, and Soft Tissue Tumors.

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 38, Gout and Other Diseases Related to the Metabolism of Purine Nucleotides

Voulgari PV, Venetsanopoulou AI, Drosos AA. Recent advances in the therapeutic management of calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease. Front Med (Lausanne). 2024;11:1327715.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38529115/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body normally keeps a careful balance of minerals in and around joints. In CPPD, calcium pyrophosphate crystals form and deposit inside joints and soft tissues. These crystals irritate the joint, causing painful swelling and inflammation. Over time, the crystals can also slowly damage the joint itself. In some cases, a faulty gene affects a transporter that moves a related mineral, contributing to crystal buildup.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, more common in older adults

Protein Structure and Folding 
Protein Synthesis, Trafficking and Posttranslational Modifications
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: There are many types of Ehlers-Danlos (EDS) syndrome. Types that cause joint hypermobility or joint instability may be associated with joint pain. Chronic joint pain is most commonly seen in classical EDS (OMIM) and mobility type EDS. Classical EDS is due to a mutation in the COL5A2, COL5A1, or COL1A1 collagen genes. The pain associated with EDS is due to frequent dislocations or subluxations of joints, which cause inflammation and arthritis; this is most often seen in the 2nd decade. In addition, pain is caused by activation of nociceptors due to ongoing joint damage.
RESOURCES:
Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 22e,  2025, Ch. 425: Heritable Disorders of Connective Tissue.

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 13, Pathologic Alterations of the Extracellular Matrix that Involve Fibrillin, Elastin, or Proteoglycans

Shirley ED, Demaio M, Bodurtha J. Ehlers-danlos syndrome in orthopaedics: etiology, diagnosis, and treatment implications. Sports Health. 2012;4(5):394-403.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3435946/

Malfait F, Symoens S, Syx D. Classic Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome. 2007 May 29 [Updated 2024 Feb 1]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 1993-2025. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1244/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Collagen is the main structural protein that holds your joints, skin, and tissues together — like the framework of a building. In Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, gene changes affect the collagen-making instructions, producing weaker collagen. With weaker collagen, joints become too flexible and are prone to slipping partially or fully out of place. These repeated dislocations cause inflammation and wear down the joint over time, leading to chronic joint pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adolescents and adults (joint pain most common in the 2nd decade of life and beyond)

Extracellular Matrix
Genetic Inheritance
Protein Structure and Folding  
Protein Synthesis, Trafficking and Posttranslational Modifications
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Marfan syndrome is a connective tissue disorder characterized by hypermobile joints (and many other problems). This can predispose to early osteoarthritis in joints due to pelvic dysplasia, hypermobile joints, or frequent joint subluxation. These can trigger inflammation and early arthritis episodes, leading to chronic joint pain. Patients with Marfan syndrome carry pathogenic mutations in the FBN1 gene, which encodes fibrillin-1, a protein in elastic fibers (OMIM).
RESOURCES:
Clinical Genomics: Practical Applications in Adult Patient Care, 1e,  2014, Ch. 28: Marfan Syndrome.

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 13, Pathologic Alterations of the Extracellular Matrix that Involve Fibrillin, Elastin, or Proteoglycans

Al Kaissi A, Zwettler E, Ganger R, Schreiner S, Klaushofer K, Grill F. Musculo-skeletal abnormalities in patients with Marfan syndrome. Clin Med Insights Arthritis Musculoskelet Disord. 2013;6:1-9.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3565546/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body's connective tissue acts like the scaffolding that holds everything in place, including your joints. In Marfan syndrome, a gene change affects a protein called fibrillin, which is part of the elastic fibers in this scaffolding. Without strong, properly formed elastic fibers, joints become overly flexible and can slip out of position easily. These repeated joint problems trigger inflammation and early arthritis, causing chronic joint pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adolescents and adults

Alcohol Metabolism
Genetic Inheritance
Inborn Errors of Metabolism 
Integration of Metabolism
Nucleotide Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Gout is always due to persistent (months-long or years-long) hyperuricemia. With hyperuricemia, crystals of sodium urate can form in joints and soft tissues. Crystals of sodium urate in joints can cause very painful inflammation of one or more joints (urate acting in this way is a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP)).

In most cases (>90%), chronic hyperuricemia is due to urate underexcretion, which can be due to frequent and substantial ethanol intake, chronic lactic acidemia or ketoaciduria, or ongoing treatment with a thiazide diuretic.

Only a minority of patients with hyperuricemia are overproducers of urate.  Such overproduction can be due to ethanol intake, high fructose consumption, increased tissue turnover, chronic hypoxia, a deficiency of HPRT (hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase; OMIM), or an overactive PRPP synthetase.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch. 22 Nucleotide Metabolism

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1st ed., 2018, Ch. 38, Gout and Other Diseases Related to the Metabolism of Purine Nucleotides

Reginato AM, Mount DB, Yang I, Choi HK. The genetics of hyperuricaemia and gout. Nat Rev Rheumatol. 2012;8(10):610-621. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3645862/
SPECIAL NOTES:
Gout is one of the most common causes of joint pain and primary can be idiopathic in nature

Crystals of uric acid can also form in the kidneys, giving rise to nephrolithiasis.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body produces a waste product called uric acid, which normally gets filtered out through the kidneys. When uric acid builds up in the blood over a long period of time, it can form sharp needle-like crystals that deposit in joints. These crystals cause intense inflammation and pain. The most common reason uric acid builds up is that the kidneys don't clear it fast enough, which can be influenced by diet, certain medications, or other health conditions.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, more common in middle-aged and older adults

SPECIFIC DIFFERENTIAL: Deficiency in clotting factor VIII (hemophilia A)
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Any condition that causes excessive bleeding into the joint space can cause hemarthrosis, leading to joint pain. Some of the common causes include trauma, hemophilia A (factor VIII deficiency), hemophilia B (factor IX deficiency), and von Willebrand disease (OMIM). Exposure of cartilage to extravascular blood can trigger the release of chemokines and inflammatory cytokines that are responsible for synovitis. Deposition of iron or hemoglobin in joints can also trigger the release of interleukins that can damage cartilage and subchondral bone, causing arthritic changes.
RESOURCES:
Tintinalli's Emergency Medicine: A Comprehensive Study Guide, 9e.,  2020, Chapter 235: Hemophilias and von Willebrand’s Disease

https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8808899/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Blood normally clots quickly when a blood vessel is injured. In hemophilia A, a key clotting protein is missing, so bleeding is harder to stop. When bleeding occurs inside a joint, the blood irritates and inflames the joint lining. Over time, blood breakdown products — including iron — deposit in the joint and damage the cartilage and bone, leading to painful, arthritic joints.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric and adults (a lifelong genetic condition, typically diagnosed in childhood)

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Blood normally clots quickly when a blood vessel is injured. In von Willebrand disease, a protein involved in clotting is missing or not working properly, so bleeding is harder to stop. When bleeding occurs inside a joint, the blood irritates and inflames the joint lining. Over time, blood breakdown products — including iron — deposit in the joint and damage the cartilage and bone, leading to painful, arthritic joints.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric and adults (a lifelong genetic condition, often identified in childhood or early adulthood)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Extracellular Matrix
Protein turnover
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Microbes (e.g., streptococcus, Lyme disease, gonorrhea, alphavirus, parvovirus B19, rubella, Epstein-Barr virus, and hepatitis B and C viruses) can trigger inflammation in joints when they gain entry into the joint space via the bloodstream or direct entry. These microbes will trigger the release of cytokines, such as interleukins, and proteases/collagenases from chondrocytes or synovial macrophages. This is followed by pannus formation on cartilage and obstruction of joint blood vessels. These changes lead to cartilage damage and fragmentation. Some microorganisms can release endotoxins that can promote cartilage damage.
RESOURCES:
Robbins, Cotran & Kumar Pathologic Basis of Disease, 11e 2026. Ch. 26: Bones, Joints, and Soft Tissue Tumors.

Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 22e,  2025, Ch. 135: Infectious Arthritis
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Normally, joints are protected from germs. When bacteria or viruses enter the joint — either through the bloodstream or directly — the immune system responds with inflammation to try to fight them off. This inflammatory response releases substances that damage the cartilage and joint lining. Some microbes can also release toxins that directly harm the cartilage. The result is a painful, swollen joint.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages, depending on the infecting organism

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Extracellular Matrix
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Joint pain in systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), can be from erosive or nonerosive arthritis. Erosive arthritis involves an inflammatory response that elevates receptor activator of nuclear factor κB ligand (RANKL) levels, leading to the overproduction of osteoclast precursors at the bone-pannus interface and joint damage. Nonerosive arthritis, often seen in patients with a history of SLE or the presence of anti-dsDNA or anti-CCP antibodies, is commonly referred to as Jaccoud's arthritis, although its exact biochemical pathogenesis remains unclear. Additionally, SLE patients typically experience reduced bone mineral density, partly due to an increased RANKL/osteoprotegerin ratio, which favors osteoclast formation and subsequent bone loss. Although the direct connection between bone loss and joint pain is less clear, elevated RANKL levels, particularly in pediatric SLE cases, suggest a potential biochemical link, as RANKL is also implicated in other inflammatory arthritis conditions, such as rheumatoid arthritis.
RESOURCES:
Siegel CH, Sammaritano LR. Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: A Review. JAMA. 2024;331(17):1480-1491.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38587826/ 

Ceccarelli F, Govoni M, Piga M, et al. Arthritis in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: From 2022 International GISEA/OEG Symposium. J Clin Med. 2022;11(20):6016.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9604412/ 

Papadaki M, Rinotas V, Violitzi F, et al. New Insights for RANKL as a Proinflammatory Modulator in Modeled Inflammatory Arthritis. Front Immunol. 2019;10:97.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6370657/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In lupus, the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own tissues, including the joints. This triggers ongoing inflammation that can damage the joint lining and surrounding bone. In some cases, the inflammation causes the body to produce too many cells that break down bone, leading to joint destruction and pain. Even when the joint lining isn't directly eroded, the inflammation can still cause significant joint pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, though can affect adolescents; pediatric cases are associated with more severe joint involvement

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Extracellular Matrix
Protein Structure and Folding
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Osteoarthritis is due to biochemical, biomechanical, environmental, or genetic factors that lead to abnormalities in intra-articular or extra-articular processes. The most obvious process is loss of joint cartilage.  The earliest change is due to loss of GAG's, causing fibrillation of joint cartilage, followed by cartilage fragmentation and degradation of matrix. In response, chondrocytes in cartilage release collagenases and other metalloproteinases, which will completely degrade the cartilage. Inflammation will also trigger the release of cytokines, which are known to inhibit the synthesis of type-2 cartilage, further limiting repair processes.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 13, Pathologic Alterations of the Extracellular Matrix That Involve Fibrillin, Elastin, or Proteoglycans.

Robbins, Cotran & Kumar Pathologic Basis of Disease, 11e 2026. Ch. 26: Bones, Joints, and Soft Tissue Tumors.

Abramoff B, Caldera FE. Osteoarthritis: Pathology, Diagnosis, and Treatment Options. Med Clin North Am. 2020;104(2):293-311. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32035570/
SPECIAL NOTES: Osteoarthritis is  the most common cause of joint pain.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Joints are cushioned by a layer of smooth cartilage that allows bones to glide over each other. In osteoarthritis, this cartilage gradually breaks down — first losing its springy quality, then cracking and wearing away entirely. As the cartilage breaks down, the joint cells release substances that further speed up the damage and block repair. Without proper cushioning, bones rub against each other, causing pain, swelling, and stiffness.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, more common with increasing age

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Rickets/osteomalacia are due to deficient osteoid (bone tissue) mineralization by calcium and phosphate arising from a deficiency of vitamin D, calcium, or phosphate. The active form of vitamin D (1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol) is important for intestinal calcium absorption, bone (formation/resorption), and kidney calcium reabsorption. Rickets is a pediatric condition, and osteomalacia is an adult condition. Physical manifestations include softening and bowing of bones, which can lead to fractures and, in turn, joint pain. Vitamin D deficiency can be caused by malnutrition or decreased exposure to sunlight, end-stage organ failure, anticonvulsant therapy, etc.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 12: Collagen, Collagenopathies, and Diseases of Mineralization

Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch. 29: Micronutrients: Minerals
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin D helps the body absorb calcium and use it to harden and strengthen bones. When vitamin D is lacking, bones don't mineralize properly and become soft. In children (rickets), soft bones can bend and bow. In adults (osteomalacia), bones weaken and are prone to fracture. Both the deformity and the fractures can put stress on nearby joints, causing joint pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Rickets: pediatric; Osteomalacia: adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Rheumatoid arthritis is an autoimmune inflammatory condition of the joints. In several disease states, free-radical release by neutrophils or macrophages during inflammation contributes to injury in the surrounding tissues.  Joint degradation is mostly irreversible.
RESOURCES:
Radu AF, Bungau SG. Management of Rheumatoid Arthritis: An Overview. Cells. 2021;10(11):2857.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34831081/

Marks' Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e., 2023, Ch. 47: The Extracellular Matrix and Connective Tissue

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 37: Pyrimidine Nucleotides and Chemotherapy
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In rheumatoid arthritis, the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own joints. Immune cells flood the joint space and release harmful molecules — including free radicals — that damage the joint lining and surrounding tissue. Over time this damage becomes permanent, causing ongoing pain, swelling, and joint deformity.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, though a juvenile form exists

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Hematopoiesis
Protein Structure and Folding
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Sickle cell disease is caused by an amino acid substitution in the beta-globin chain (OMIM). The major consequence of this substitution is the polymerization of deoxyhemoglobin, followed by red blood cell deformation and aggregation. This can lead to thrombosis and/or vascular occlusion, followed by tissue infarction and necrosis. Sickle cell disease also causes chronic anemia, leading to bone marrow hyperplasia, which in turn affects the shape and structure of bone. Both mechanisms contribute to joint and bone pain in the disease. Affected patients are predisposed to developing osteomyelitis, osteonecrosis, and osteoarthritis. The exact mechanisms are not yet established.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 17: Hemoglobinopathies

Williams Hematology, 10e.  2021, Chapter 50: Disorders of Hemoglobin Structure: Sickle Cell Anemia and Related Abnormalities
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In sickle cell disease, red blood cells change into a stiff, curved shape that can clump together and block small blood vessels. When blood flow is blocked, the tissues and bone that rely on that blood supply are starved of oxygen and begin to die. This can lead to bone damage, bone marrow overgrowth, and joint problems including bone death and arthritis — all of which cause significant joint and bone pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric and adults (a lifelong genetic condition diagnosed in childhood)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Protein Structure and Folding
Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin C is required for the hydroxylation of prolyl and lysyl side-chains in collagen. The absence of this hydroxylation, due to a lack of vitamin C, weakens the collagen structure, resulting in symptoms of scurvy. Deformed collagen in the vasculature can lead to bruising and bleeding in joints (hemarthrosis). Hemarthrosis leads to joint pain (see also Hemarthrosis). Weakening of collagen structure also leads to arthralgia (joint pain), osteopenia, and osteonecrosis.
RESOURCES:
Fraser IM, Dean M. Extensive bruising secondary to vitamin C deficiency. BMJ Case Rep. 2009;2009:bcr08.2008.0750. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3027858/ 

Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch. 28: Micronutrients: Vitamins

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 12: Collagen, Collagenopathies, and Diseases of Mineralization
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin C is needed to build strong collagen, the protein that gives structure to blood vessels, joints, and bones. Without enough vitamin C, collagen becomes weak and fragile. Weak blood vessels in and around joints can bleed easily, and blood pooling in the joint space causes pain and swelling. The weakened collagen in joints and bones also directly causes aching and tenderness.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages, though most common in those with very poor diets

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Cholesterol and Lipoprotein Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Atherosclerotic plaque buildup in peripheral blood vessels of extremities (example lower limbs) leads to reduced blood flow and ischemic pain (see CHEST PAIN for atherosclerosis mechanism). The phenomenon especially takes place while walking or exercising resulting in cramps and is referred to as intermittent claudication.
RESOURCES:
McDermott MM. Lower extremity manifestations of peripheral artery disease: the pathophysiologic and functional implications of leg ischemia. Circ Res. 2015;116(9):1540-1550.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4410164/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Imagine your blood vessels are like pipes that carry blood to your legs. Over time, a waxy, fatty substance called cholesterol can build up inside these pipes, making them narrower. When this happens in the blood vessels of your legs, your muscles don't get enough blood — and therefore not enough oxygen — especially when you're walking or exercising. This lack of oxygen causes cramping and pain in your legs, which often gets better when you rest. This is called "claudication."
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult. This condition typically affects older adults, especially those over 50 with risk factors such as smoking, diabetes, high blood pressure, or high cholesterol.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Cholesterol and Lipoprotein Metabolism 
TCA Cycle, Oxidative Phosphorylation and Energy Generation
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Certain drugs can induce myopathy and lead to muscle pain. 

Statin drugs inhibit HMG-CoA reductase in the cholesterol synthesis pathway. Statin-related myopathy could be multifactorial since it affects the levels of multiple intermediates in this pathway that are crucial for oxidative phosphorylation, signal transduction, etc. One of the intermediates in this pathway is farnesyl pyrophosphate (PP), whose levels are lowered due to use of statins. Farnesyl PP is a precursor of ubiquinone (CoQ) in the electron transport chain (ETC). Because of this, the rate of the ETC is decreased leading to decreased production of ATP. Lack of ATP leads to muscle damage and breakdown, partially due to decreased activity of Na/K pump, resulting in muscle pain and cramping. In rare cases it could also lead to rhabdomyolysis. 

HIV reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTI) cause mitochondrial dysfunction by inhibiting the mitochondrial DNA polymerase due to the structural similarity to reverse transcriptase, thus imparing cellular respiration and ATP formation. As explained above, this could lead to cramping and muscle pain. Mitochondrial dysfunction also leads to lactate accumulation, which could contribute to this presentation.
RESOURCES:
Morales De Guzman M. Rhabdomyolysis. Medscape. Published 2024 Feb 28. Accessed Nov 24, 2025.
https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1007814-overview

Di Stasi SL, MacLeod TD, Winters JD, Binder-Macleod SA. Effects of statins on skeletal muscle: a perspective for physical therapists. Phys Ther. 2010;90(10):1530-1542.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2949584/

Brinkman K. Mitochondrial toxicity of HIV nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors. UptoDate. Owen, C, Updated Apr 2024. Accessed Nov. 20, 2025
https://www.uptodate.com/contents/mitochondrial-toxicity-of-hiv-nucleoside-reverse-transcriptase-inhibitors

Patel PH, Zulfiqar H. Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors. [Updated 2023 Jun 25]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK551504/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your muscles need energy to work properly, and they get that energy from tiny power plants inside your cells called mitochondria. Certain medicines, like cholesterol-lowering drugs called statins or some HIV medicines, can interfere with how these power plants work. Statins lower the levels of an important helper molecule (called CoQ) that the power plants need to make energy. HIV medicines can directly damage the power plants themselves. When your muscle cells can't make enough energy, they start to break down, which causes muscle pain, weakness, and cramping in areas like your legs.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult. Statin-induced myopathy occurs in adults taking cholesterol-lowering medications. HIV NRTI-associated myopathy occurs in adults being treated for HIV infection.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: In Glycogen storage disorder V (GSD V or McArdle Disease), there is an inherited defect in muscle glycogen phosphorylase (OMIM),  due to which glycogen cannot be broken down to glucose and used for energy production. Lack of energy leads to muscle weakness. Lack of ATP availability during intense exercise leads to severe muscle damage and breakdown in these individuals, partially due to decreased activity of Na+/K+ ATPase pump and other pumps.  Intense exercise in these individuals can also lead to life-threatening rhabdomyolysis, which can also contribute to the pain in extremities.
RESOURCES:

Nagarajan E. Metabolic Myopathies. Medscape. Published 2021 Sep 14. Accessed Nov 24, 2025.
https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1173338-overview

https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2872126/
SPECIAL NOTES: Characterized by decreased lactic acid levels after exercise
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your muscles use a stored form of sugar called glycogen for fuel when you exercise. Normally, a special protein (called muscle glycogen phosphorylase) breaks this glycogen down into sugar that your muscles can use for energy. In McArdle Disease, this protein doesn't work properly because of an inherited gene change, so your muscles can't access their fuel during exercise. Without enough energy, your muscles get tired very quickly, cramp up, and can even start to break down during intense activity, causing significant pain — especially in the legs.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. Symptoms often first appear in childhood or adolescence, but the condition is managed throughout life into adulthood.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Glycogen storage disease VII/Tarui disease (OMIM) is caused due the defect in muscle phosphofructokinase, an enzyme involved in glycolysis. Impairment of  glycolysis due to this defect causes decreased production of ATP leading to muscle weakness. It can also cause cramping and muscle pain due to muscle breakdown partially due to decreased activity of Na+/K+ ATPase pump

SPECIAL NOTES: Characterized by decreased lactic acid levels after exercise.  This is a very rare autosomal recessive disorder with less than 100 reported cases worldwide.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your muscles break down sugar through a series of steps to make energy — this process is called glycolysis. In Tarui Disease, an inherited gene change causes a key step in this process (controlled by an enzyme called phosphofructokinase) to not work properly. Because this step is blocked, your muscles can't make enough energy during exercise. Without enough energy, your muscle cells start to break down, causing cramping, weakness, and pain — particularly in the legs during physical activity.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. Symptoms typically begin in childhood, but the condition continues into adulthood. It is an extremely rare disorder with fewer than 100 reported cases worldwide.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Hemostasis
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) results from clots in the venous system, usually in the deep large veins of lower extremities. Multiple factors such as immobility, injury, certain drugs, increased pressure on veins because of a mass/pregnancy, etc. can lead to DVT. Genetic causes of increased predisposition to DVT include Factor V leiden mutation, antithrombin deficiency, protein C deficiency and S deficiency. A clot in the deep veins results in increased back pressure in the venous system, which leads to swelling, stiffness, and pain. This could also trigger inflammation, which could increase the swelling. The resultant swelling and edema can obstruct blood flow, further contributing to pain.

Hirsh J, Hoak J. Management of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism. A statement for healthcare professionals. Council on Thrombosis (in consultation with the Council on Cardiovascular Radiology), American Heart Association. Circulation. 1996;93(12):2212-2245.
https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/01.cir.93.12.2212
SPECIAL NOTES: A serious complication of DVT is pulmonary embolism, which is caused due to the dislodgement of the clot.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your veins carry blood back to your heart, and sometimes a blood clot can form inside the deep veins of your leg. This can happen because of long periods of sitting or lying still, an injury, certain medications, pregnancy, or even inherited tendencies to form clots more easily. When a clot forms, it blocks the normal flow of blood returning from your leg. This causes pressure to build up in the vein, leading to swelling, stiffness, and pain in the leg. The clot can also trigger inflammation, which makes the swelling and pain even worse.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult. DVT is most common in adults, particularly those who are older, immobile for long periods, pregnant, or have inherited clotting disorders. It can rarely occur in children.

Mitochondrial Inheritance

SPECIFIC DIFFERENTIAL: Myoclonus epilepsy with ragged red fibers (MERRF)
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: In inherited mitochondrial disorders (for example MERRF Syndrome (Ragged Red Fiber Disease; OMIM) and Mitochondrial encephalomyopathy (MELAS; OMIM)) pathogenic variants in mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA), most commonly in the tRNA genes, affects the translation/function of mitochondrial proteins. This dysfunction impairs oxidative phosphorylation, which causes decreased production of ATP and accumulation of lactate. The lack of energy results in muscle weakness. Decreased production of ATP also results in muscle breakdown partially due to decreased activity of ATP dependant Na/K pumps and other transmembrane pumps. This may manifest as burning sensation or heaviness in extremities. Lactate associated acidosis could also contribute to muscle soreness.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 23: Oxidative Phosphorylation and Mitochondrial Diseases

Berardo A, DiMauro S, Hirano M. A diagnostic algorithm for metabolic myopathies. Curr Neurol Neurosci Rep. 2010;10(2):118-126.

https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9486707/

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Inside nearly every cell in your body are tiny power plants called mitochondria that make energy. In MERRF syndrome, a change in a special type of DNA (called mitochondrial DNA) that is passed down from your mother affects how the mitochondria work. Because of this, your cells — especially muscle cells — can't make enough energy. They also build up a waste product called lactate. Without enough energy, your muscles become weak and can start to break down, causing pain, a burning sensation, or heaviness in your legs. The buildup of lactate can also make your muscles feel sore.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. MERRF typically presents in childhood or early adulthood, though the age of onset can vary. It is inherited through mitochondrial (maternal) inheritance.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Inside nearly every cell in your body are tiny power plants called mitochondria that make energy. In MELAS syndrome, a change in a special type of DNA (called mitochondrial DNA) that is passed down from your mother affects how the mitochondria work. Because of this, your cells — especially muscle cells — can't make enough energy. They also build up a waste product called lactate. Without enough energy, your muscles become weak and can start to break down, causing pain, a burning sensation, or heaviness in your legs. The buildup of lactate can also make your muscles feel sore.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. MELAS typically presents in childhood or early adulthood (usually before age 40), though symptoms can vary. It is inherited through mitochondrial (maternal) inheritance.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin D plays a critical role in maintaining serum calcium levels by helping with absorption of calcium through intestines, reabsorption through kidneys and regulation of bone mineralization and resorption. Hence, deficiency of Vitamin D, which could arise due to multiple factors, leads to hypocalcemia. Lack of calcium leads to inefficient muscle contraction and relaxation, which can lead to muscle cramping and pain. Lack of serum calcium also leads to increased resorption from bones due to compensatory hyperparathyroidism, which leads to bone loss. Weakening of bones and potential fractures due to this could further contribute to the pain.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin D is like a key that helps your body absorb calcium from the food you eat. Calcium is important not just for strong bones, but also for helping your muscles contract and relax properly. When you don't have enough Vitamin D, your body can't absorb enough calcium, causing your muscles to cramp and ache — including in your legs. Your body tries to fix the low calcium by pulling it out of your bones, which weakens them over time. Weak bones are more prone to pain and injury, which can add to the leg pain you might feel.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. Vitamin D deficiency can affect people of all ages. In children, it causes rickets (soft, weak bones), and in adults, it causes osteomalacia (bone softening), both of which can present with leg pain and muscle cramps.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: The pathogenic variant that causes sickle cell disease results in a single amino acid in position 6 in hemoglobin beta (Hb) being changed from glutamic acid residue to valine (OMIM). This results in a hydrophobic patch being exposed on the surface of hemoglobin (Hb), which leads to polymerization of Hb. Polymerization of Hb disrupts the cellular structure of RBCs leading to sickling of RBCs. The sickle shaped RBCs are rigid and get stuck in the blood vessels  leading to vaso-occlusive events. Occlusion of vessels can lead to ischemic pain (sickle cell crisis). When the occlusion takes place arteries supplying lower extremities, it can result in leg pain.
RESOURCES:
Marks' Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e. 2023. Ch 42: The Biochemistry of Erythrocytes and Other Blood Cells

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 17: Hemoglobinopathies

Takaoka K, Cyril AC, Jinesh S, Radhakrishnan R. Mechanisms of pain in sickle cell disease. Br J Pain. 2021;15(2):213-220.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8138616/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Normally, red blood cells are round and flexible, like a donut without a hole, and they easily squeeze through blood vessels to deliver oxygen throughout your body. In sickle cell disease, a change in a single building block of hemoglobin (the protein that carries oxygen in red blood cells) causes the cells to become stiff and shaped like a crescent or sickle. These oddly shaped cells get stuck in blood vessels and block blood flow. When this happens in the blood vessels supplying your legs, the muscles and tissues don't get enough oxygen, causing severe pain. This is called a sickle cell crisis.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. Sickle cell disease is a genetic condition present from birth. Pain crises can begin in early childhood (as young as 6 months of age) and continue throughout life into adulthood.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Bassen-Kornzweig syndrome is a rare autosomal recessive (AR) inherited defect in the Microsomal TAG-transfer protein (MTTP/MTP; OMIM) that leads to an absence of Apo-B containing lipoproteins, including chylomicrons. The absence of chylomicrons leads to the malabsorption of dietary lipids and fat soluble vitamins (A,D,E,K). Unabsorbed dietary lipids leads to steatorrhea (loose, foul-smelling, fatty stools).

SPECIAL NOTES:
Incidence of less than 1 in a million.
Among the symptoms that appear in the first year are failure to gain weight and grow at expected rate (failure to thrive).
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body has a special protein that helps package fats from your food so they can travel through your blood. In this condition, you are born without this protein working properly because of a change in your genes that you inherited from both parents. Without this packaging system, fats from your food cannot be absorbed and instead pass through your intestines, causing greasy, loose, and bad-smelling stools.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric. Symptoms typically appear in the first year of life, with signs such as failure to thrive and fatty stools after feeding.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Chronic alcohol consumption can lead to atrophy of the GI lining resulting in malabsorption of macro and micronutrients, including vitamins  (e.g. niacin), which can lead to diarrhea. Malabsorption of macronutrients leads to diarrhea because unabsorbed nutrients draw water into the intestines osmotically and can be fermented by gut bacteria, producing osmotic byproducts that further increase fluid retention in the lumen resulting in osmotic diarrhea. Alcohol use can damage these organs and decrease liver and pancreatic secretions (e.g. bile and pancreatic enzymes).  Deficiencies in bile salts and pancreatic lipase lead to unabsorbed dietary lipids and steatorrhea (loose, foul-smelling, fatty stools).
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 30: Metabolism of Ethanol and the Consequences of Alcohol Dependence Syndrome

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20575826/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When someone drinks a lot of alcohol over a long period of time, it slowly damages the lining of the stomach and intestines so that food cannot be absorbed properly. It also damages the liver and pancreas, which are organs that make important juices that help break down food — especially fats. Without these juices working well, fats and other nutrients pass through the gut undigested, pulling extra water into the intestines and causing loose, greasy stools.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult. Chronic alcohol use disorder predominantly affects adults.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: The direct impacts of acute alcohol intoxication include increased GI motility and damage of GI lining, which lead to acute gastritis and enteritis.  As a consequence, there is decreased absorption of water, electrolytes and inhibition of Na+/K+ ATPase activity leading to osmotic diarrhea.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When someone drinks alcohol, even in a single occasion, it can irritate and damage the lining of the stomach and intestines right away. This speeds up the movement of food through the gut so that there is not enough time for water and salts to be absorbed properly. It also interferes with tiny pumps in the gut wall that normally move water and salts out of the intestine. The result is loose, watery stools shortly after drinking.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult. Acute alcohol intoxication primarily affects adults of legal drinking age.

SPECIAL NOTES: The precise prevalence of bile acid diarrhea (BAD) remains uncertain, yet mounting evidence suggests that it may be wrongly identified in as many as 30% of patients with diarrhea-dominant symptoms who have been diagnosed with irritable bowel syndrome.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your liver makes a liquid called bile that acts like a soap to break up fats in your food so your body can absorb them. Bile is stored in the gallbladder and released into your intestines when you eat. If you have had your gallbladder removed, or if part of your intestine has been removed or damaged, there may not be enough bile to properly break up fats. The undigested fats then travel to the large intestine, where they cause greasy, loose stools.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult. Conditions such as cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal) and ileal disease or surgery predominantly affect adults.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Patients with celiac disease display gluten sensitivity. Most patients (90%) carry mutations in HLA-DQ2 hetero-dimer, which has pockets for binding negatively charged peptides.  In celiac disease, gliadin, a 33 amino acid peptide in wheat, barley and rye that is normally deaminated by transglutaminase into a negatively charged peptide (Gln converted into Glu), will bind to HLA-DQ2 and activate an immune response. This immune response will lead to damaged intestinal villi causing malabsorption, steatorrhea and diarrhea.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When people with celiac disease eat foods containing gluten — a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye — their immune system mistakenly attacks the lining of their small intestine. This damages tiny finger-like projections called villi that are responsible for absorbing nutrients from food. When these villi are damaged, food — especially fats — cannot be absorbed properly, leading to loose, greasy stools and diarrhea.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. Celiac disease can present at any age, from infancy when gluten-containing foods are introduced, through adulthood.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Diarrhea can be caused by various infectious agents. Cholera is caused by Vibrio cholerae, which releases a toxin that activates adenylate cyclase in enterocytes. Downstream cAMP signaling results in increased water and electrolyte secretion that causes diarrhea (secretory). 

Enterotoxigenic E. coli and Enteric rotavirus produce proteins (heat-stable enterotoxin for E. coli, NSP4 for rotavirus) that activate calcium channels on the cell membrane. The influx of calcium stimulates chloride release from the cell, along with water, causing diarrhea (inflammatory - see special notes)

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18813221/
SPECIAL NOTES: Inflammatory diarrhea is caused by two groups of organisms--cytotoxin-producing, noninvasive bacteria (e.g. enteroaggregative Escherichia coli, enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli and Clostridium difficile), or by invasive organisms (e.g. Salmonella spp., Shigella spp., Campylobacter spp., Entamoeba histolytica). The cytotoxin-producing organisms adhere to the mucosa, activate cytokines and stimulate the intestinal mucosa to release inflammatory mediators.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Certain germs — like bacteria and viruses — can get into your intestines and cause your gut to release a lot of extra water and salts into the intestine, or they can damage and inflame the gut lining so it cannot absorb water properly. Some germs release poisonous substances called toxins that trick your gut cells into pushing out water and salts, leading to very watery diarrhea. Other germs directly invade and irritate the gut lining, causing inflammation and loose stools.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. Infectious diarrhea affects all age groups, though certain pathogens (e.g., rotavirus) are especially common in young children, while others (e.g., cholera) affect all ages.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Cystic fibrosis is caused by pathogenic variants in the CFTR gene (OMIM).  The CFTR protein is a chloride channel present in pancreatic duct cells and deficiencies in CFTR activity lead to blockage of ducts by mucous plugs. These mucous plugs block secretion of pancreatic lipase, amylase, trypsin, and chymotrypsin.  Decreased levels of these enzymes in the gut lead to malabsorption of fats, carbohydrates and proteins and pancreatic insufficiency.  Ineffective fat digestion can lead to malabsorption and steatorrhea (loose, foul-smelling, fatty stools).  In addition, the incomplete digestion of protein and carbohydrates can lead to their malabsorption, and osmotic diarrhea.
RESOURCES:

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK493206/

Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch 7: Introduction to Carbohydrates
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic condition — meaning it is caused by a change in a gene that is passed down from both parents. This gene change causes a thick, sticky mucus to build up and block the tubes of the pancreas, which is an organ that normally releases important enzymes — substances that help break down fats, proteins, and carbohydrates in your food. Without these enzymes, food cannot be properly digested, and undigested fats and other nutrients pass through the intestines, causing greasy, loose stools and diarrhea.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric. Cystic fibrosis is typically diagnosed in infancy or early childhood through newborn screening, though patients now commonly survive into adulthood.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Acarbose is a supplementary diabetic medication that inhibits pancreatic amylase and enzymes in the intestinal brush border, such as maltase and isomaltase. These enzymes are involved in carbohydrate digestion. Incomplete digestion of polysaccharides and oligosaccharides leads to malabsorption and causes osmotic diarrhea as a side effect.

SPECIAL NOTES: Acarbose is approved as an adjunct to diet alone, or diet and exercise, for treating adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Acarbose is a medicine used to help control blood sugar in people with type 2 diabetes. It works by blocking enzymes in your intestines that normally break down starchy foods — like bread, pasta, and rice — into simple sugars that your body can absorb. Because these starches are not fully broken down, they cannot be absorbed and instead travel to the large intestine, where they draw water in and are fermented by bacteria, causing bloating, gas, and loose, watery stools.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult. Acarbose is approved for use in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Drugs such as antibiotics disrupt the normal bacterial flora leading to overgrowth of opportunistic pathogens that may infect the gut lining, leading to diarrhea.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Antibiotics are medicines that kill harmful bacteria when you are sick. However, they can also kill many of the helpful bacteria that normally live in your intestines and help keep things balanced. When these good bacteria are wiped out, harmful bacteria can take over and infect the gut lining, causing inflammation and loose, watery stools.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. Antibiotic-associated diarrhea can affect anyone prescribed antibiotics, across all age groups.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Orlistat is a weight loss drug that is a pancreatic lipase inhibitor. Ineffective fat digestion can lead to malabsorption. Unabsorbed fats reaching the large intestine lead to steatorrhea (loose, foul-smelling, fatty stools).
RESOURCES:

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Orlistat is a weight loss medicine that works by blocking an enzyme in your intestines called lipase, which is normally responsible for breaking down fats from your food. When fats cannot be broken down properly, they cannot be absorbed by the body and instead travel through the intestines undigested. These unabsorbed fats reach the large intestine and cause greasy, oily, loose stools that may be hard to control.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult. Orlistat is approved for weight management in adults, though it is sometimes used in adolescents under medical supervision.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hartnup disease is caused by pathogenic variants in the SLC6A19 gene (OMIM), which encodes a system B(0) transmembrane protein that actively transports most neutral amino acids across the apical membrane of epithelial cells.  Loss of tryptophan transport is specifically responsible for the development of pellagra-like symptoms in Hartnup disease since tryptophan is a precursor of Niacin. Niacin deficiency leads to inflammation of GI tract, which can cause diarrhea. The other clinical features include dermatitis and dementia.  Note diarrhea in Hartnup is less severe than dietary niacin deficiency since the intestinal peptide transporter 1 (PepT1) that transports tripeptides and across the apical membrane of epithelial cells in the small intestine provides an alternative source of tryptophan.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Hartnup disease is a rare inherited condition — meaning it is caused by a gene change passed down from both parents — that affects special doorways (transporters) in the intestine and kidneys that normally let an amino acid called tryptophan into your body. Tryptophan is important because your body uses it to make a vitamin called niacin (vitamin B3). Without enough niacin, the lining of the intestines becomes inflamed and irritated, which can lead to diarrhea, a skin rash, and problems with thinking clearly.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric. Symptoms of Hartnup disease typically appear in childhood, often triggered by poor diet, sun exposure, or illness.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Insufficiency of the lactase enzyme results in the inability to convert dietary lactose to glucose and galactose, which can be absorbed in the small intestine.  The undigested lactose is used by bacteria which hyperproliferate and release H2 gas, and also generates osmotic diarrhea.  Primary lactase deficiency is due to lack of membrane-bound enzyme (inherited non persistence of lactase), secondary is due to damage to the intestinal mucosa by antibiotics, chemotherapy, inflammatory disease, or infection. Congenital lactase deficiency (OMIM), is an autosomal recessive condition resulting in reduced or absent lactase enzyme activity from birth and leads to symptoms when lactose containing products are consumed.
