About 70 percent of the world's population has lactose malabsorption that arises after weaning.

Congenital lactase deficiency is rare.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your small intestine normally makes an enzyme called lactase that breaks down lactose — the natural sugar found in milk and dairy products — into smaller sugars that your body can absorb. In primary lactase deficiency, your body gradually produces less and less lactase as you get older. Without enough lactase, the lactose in dairy foods cannot be broken down and instead travels to the large intestine, where bacteria ferment it, producing gas and causing bloating, cramps, and loose, watery stools.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult (and older children/adolescents). Primary lactase deficiency most commonly develops after childhood and affects a large proportion of the global adult population, particularly those of non-European descent.

SPECIAL NOTES:
About 70 percent of the world's population has lactose malabsorption that arises after weaning.

Congenital lactase deficiency is rare.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Congenital lactase deficiency is a very rare condition that babies are born with because of a gene change inherited from both parents. From the moment a baby is born, their intestines are unable to make the enzyme lactase, which is needed to break down lactose — the sugar in breast milk and formula. Because lactose cannot be broken down or absorbed, it causes watery diarrhea very early in life whenever the baby drinks milk.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric. This condition presents at birth or in the newborn period, as soon as the infant begins feeding on breast milk or lactose-containing formula.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Macronutrients: Digestion and Absorption 
pH, Water, Electrolytes
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: A deficiency in pancreatic digestive enzymes (pancreatic lipase, pancreatic amylase, trypsin, chymotrypsin, carboxypeptidase) arising due to various factors results in inability to digest dietary carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. Ineffective fat digestion can lead to malabsorption and steatorrhea (loose, foul-smelling, fatty stools). Incomplete digestion of protein and carbohydrates leads to their malabsorption and osmotic diarrhea.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch  7: Introduction to Carbohydrates;
Ch 15: Dietary Lipid Metabolism;  Ch 19: Amino Acids: Nitrogen Disposal

Marks' Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e. 2023, Ch. 21: Digestion, Absorption, and Transport of Carbohydrates

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 18: Carbohydrate Transport, Carbohydrate Malabsorption, and Lactose Intolerance

Tian C, Ghodeif AO, Arshad S, Gillespie E. Pancreatic insufficiency. [Updated 2025 Sep 14]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. 
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK555926/
SPECIAL NOTES: A leading cause in children is Cystic Fibrosis and in adults is chronic pancreatitis
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your pancreas is an organ that normally releases important enzymes — special proteins that help your body break down fats, proteins, and carbohydrates from your food. When the pancreas is not working properly, it does not make enough of these enzymes. Without them, food — especially fat — cannot be properly digested. The undigested fat and other nutrients travel to the large intestine, where they draw in water and cause greasy, foul-smelling, loose stools and sometimes watery diarrhea as well.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. In children, cystic fibrosis is a leading cause; in adults, chronic pancreatitis is the most common cause.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Neurotransmitter Synthesis
pH, Water, Electrolytes 
Vitamin and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Carcinoid tumors or neuroendocrine tumors are associated with the secretion of various hormones, peptides, and prostaglandins such as serotonin, histamine, somatostatin, bradykinin, substance P, etc. Increased secretion of serotonin leads to hypermotility of the gut, which reduces the time available for water and electrolyte absorption resulting in diarrhea. Serotonin secreting carcinoid tumors can lead to pellagra, since tryptophan, a precursor for serotonin is diverted towards serotonin synthesis. This can lead to NAD deficiency and pellagra. Pellagra can further contribute to diarrhea.
RESOURCES:
Naraev BG, Halland M, Halperin DM, Purvis AJ, OʼDorisio TM, Halfdanarson TR. Management of Diarrhea in Patients With Carcinoid Syndrome. Pancreas. 2019;48(8):961-972.
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6867674/

Menon G, Pandit S, Annamaraju P, Bhusal K. Carcinoid Syndrome. [Updated 2025 Feb 18]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-.
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448096/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A carcinoid tumor is an abnormal growth that can develop in the intestines or other parts of the body. This tumor releases large amounts of a chemical called serotonin, which causes the intestines to move food through much faster than normal. When food moves too quickly, there is not enough time for the gut to absorb water and salts properly, leading to frequent, watery diarrhea. The tumor also uses up a lot of tryptophan — an amino acid your body needs to make an important vitamin called niacin — which can further worsen diarrhea.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult. Carcinoid tumors most commonly present in adults, typically in middle age or later.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Vitamin and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Pellagra is result of niacin deficiency. Niacin is crucial for maintaining healthy cell function in the GI tract, and its deficiency disrupts this process, leading to inflammation and irritation through several biochemical mechanisms. Niacin is a precursor of NAD+/NADH which is used by redox enzymes in pathways such as the tricarboxylic acid cycle, the electron transport chain and fatty acid oxidation. The resulting NAD⁺/NADH deficiency impairs enterocyte ATP generation, causing villous atrophy and loss of brush‑border enzyme activity and active electrolyte transport, which manifests clinically as a watery, malabsorptive (loose‑stool) diarrhea. The other clinical features include dermatitis and dementia.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch 28: Micronutrients: Vitamins

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 19: Glycolysis and its Regulation by Hormones and Hypoxia

Redzic S, Hashmi MF, Gupta V. Niacin Deficiency. [Updated 2023 Jul 25]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. 
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK557728/
SPECIAL NOTES: In the 1900s, researchers discovered that maize-dependent populations in the American South developed Pellagra due to niacin deficiency. European colonists processed maize by grinding it into flour, which unintentionally removed niacin. In contrast, indigenous North Americans treated maize with alkaline solutions before grinding—a method that preserved niacin and enhanced its absorption. This difference in processing explains why European settlers developed niacin deficiency while indigenous populations did not.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Niacin (vitamin B3) is an important nutrient that your body uses to produce energy inside cells, including the cells that line your intestines. When you do not get enough niacin from your diet, the cells lining the intestines cannot make enough energy to work properly. This causes the tiny finger-like projections in the intestine (villi) to shrink and the gut lining to become inflamed and irritated. As a result, the intestines cannot absorb nutrients and water well, leading to watery, loose stools. Pellagra causes three main problems: diarrhea, a skin rash, and confusion — sometimes called the "three Ds."
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both Adult and Pediatric. Pellagra due to dietary niacin deficiency can affect any age group in populations dependent on maize-based diets with poor nutritional diversity, though it is rare in high-income countries today.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function 
Integration of Metabolism
Nutrition
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularity associated with Childhood overweight/obesity includes early menarche. 

Excess weight and obesity can affect onset and regular menstrual functioning via various mechanisms that involve insulin resistance, inflammatory response, and high leptin levels. Obesity is associated with insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia. Insulin synergistically acts with luteinizing hormone (LH) to increase androgen synthesis in ovaries. Additionally, inflammatory response in obesity also promotes excess androgen synthesis via various cytokines. Excess levels of androgens and estrogen (a downstream product of androgens) along with the abnormal high androgen:estrogen ratio interfere with proper ovarian functioning. Further, high levels of leptin resulting from leptin resistance is observed in individuals with obesity. High leptin affects central pulsatile gonadotropin releasing hormone (GnRH) secretion by binding receptors in neurons releasing GnRH, which are required for triggering onset of puberty and cyclical functioning of ovaries. Together these factors could lead to early menarche.
RESOURCES:
Li W, Liu Q, Deng X, Chen Y, Liu S, Story M. Association between Obesity and Puberty Timing: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. Int J Environ Res Public Health. 2017;14(10):1266.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5664767/

Blank SK, McCartney CR, Chhabra S, et al. Modulation of gonadotropin-releasing hormone pulse generator sensitivity to progesterone inhibition in hyperandrogenic adolescent girls--implications for regulation of pubertal maturation. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2009;94(7):2360-2366.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2708962/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Carrying excess weight affects several of the body's hormonal systems. Too much body fat can cause the body to stop responding normally to insulin, which then pushes the ovaries to make extra male-type hormones. Excess body fat also causes high levels of a hormone called leptin, which interferes with the brain's ability to send the normal signals that start and control the menstrual cycle. Together, these hormonal imbalances can cause periods to start earlier than expected.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

BROAD DIFFERENTIAL: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP)
SPECIFIC DIFFERENTIAL: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP)
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with CDGP include amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and hypomenorrhea. 

CDGP is considered a variant of normal growth and development rather than a pathological condition. Normally, puberty is initiated when there is pulsatile release of gonadotropin releasing hormone from hypothalamus. Both, genetic and environmental factors can control this. In individuals with CDGP, family history of delayed growth and puberty is often present. The catch-up of growth and development takes place a bit later in life as compared to normal population. The CDGP diagnosis is done by excluding other pathological conditions leading to similar clinical picture.  While the etiopathogenesis of CDGP is still being explored, underlying genetic makeup (such as genetic variation around LIN28B - Suppressor of microRNA synthesis, important for stem cell differentiation) has been shown to be associated with this delay. Delay is puberty may present as primary amenorrhea as well as other abnormalities listed above.
RESOURCES:
Gaudino R, De Filippo G, Bozzola E, et al. Current clinical management of constitutional delay of growth and puberty. Ital J Pediatr. 2022;48(1):45.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8944060/

Ong KK, Elks CE, Li S, et al. Genetic variation in LIN28B is associated with the timing of puberty. Nat Genet. 2009;41(6):729-733.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3000552/

Hu Z, Chen R, Cai C. Association of genetic polymorphisms around the LIN28B gene and idiopathic central precocious puberty risks among Chinese girls. Pediatr Res. 2016;80(4):521-525.
https://www.nature.com/articles/pr2016107
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The menstrual cycle is started and controlled by a specific signal released from the brain. In some individuals, the timing of this signal is simply delayed compared to most people — often running in families. There is no disease or damage causing this; the body just follows its own slightly later schedule. Puberty and menstrual cycles do eventually begin, just later than average. Because it looks similar to other conditions, doctors diagnose it by ruling out other causes first.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with Complete Androgen insensitivity syndrome are amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and hypomenorrhea. 

This is X-linked recessive condition caused due to loss-of-function mutations in the gene encoding androgen receptor (AR; OMIM), with most mutations being in the hormone-binding domain of the receptor. This results in androgen resistance in individuals with 46, XY genotype due to which body does not respond to any androgens, including testosterone and dihydrotestosterone. Action of testosterone and dihydrotestosterone are required for male sexual differentiation and development, which includes the development of male external genitalia and secondary sexual characteristics. Thus complete androgen insensitivity in individuals with 46, XY genotype leads to under masculinization, with testes remaining undescended. Additionally, these individual may present with female-like external genitalia with a blind vagina (atypical genitalia). In severe cases, these individuals may be assigned as females at birth and later may present with primary amenorrhea due to absence of a uterus.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 31: Steroid Hormones and Vitamin D

Gottlieb B, Trifiro MA. Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome. 1999 Mar 24 [Updated 2017 May 11]. In: Adam MP, Feldman J, Mirzaa GM, et al., editors. GeneReviews® [Internet]. Seattle (WA): University of Washington, Seattle; 1993-2024. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK1429/

Hughes IA, Davies JD, Bunch TI, Pasterski V, Mastroyannopoulou K, MacDougall J. Androgen insensitivity syndrome. Lancet. 2012;380(9851):1419-1428.
https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(12)60071-3/full text
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Hormones work by attaching to specific receivers called receptors inside cells — like a key fitting a lock. In complete androgen insensitivity syndrome, the receiver for male-type hormones is broken, so the body cannot respond to them at all. In individuals with XY chromosomes, this means the body does not develop male reproductive organs, and since there is no uterus, menstruation never begins. These individuals are typically raised as female and may not be aware of this condition until they do not get their first period.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically identified at puberty)

Genetic Inheritance
Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia include amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, hypomenorrhea. 

In 17-hydroxylase deficiency (OMIM), the downstream production of glucocorticoids and sex hormones is reduced. Lack of sex hormones, particularly estrogen in individuals with 46 XX genotype, leads to absent menses or other menstrual abnormalities. In severe cases, individuals with 46 XY genotype may be born with small male genitalia or female-like external genitalia (atypical genitalia). If undiagnosed until puberty, these individuals with female like genitalia may present to the clinic with primary amenorrhea.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 31: Steroid Hormones and Vitamin D

Oliveira JC, Sousa FC, Campos ST, et al. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia in adolescence - a gynecological perspective. Ginekol Pol. Published online March 22, 2022.
https://journals.viamedica.pl/ginekologia_polska/article/view/87514

Hubska J, Kępczyńska-Nyk A, Czady-Jurszewicz K, Ambroziak U. Characteristics of Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia Diagnosed in Adulthood: A Literature Review and Case Series. J Clin Med. 2023;12(2):653.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9866811/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The adrenal glands normally make a series of hormones in a step-by-step process. In 17-hydroxylase deficiency, one of the steps in this process is blocked, reducing the production of sex hormones. In individuals with XX chromosomes, the shortage of estrogen means the body can't go through normal female sexual development, so periods may never start or may be very irregular. In severe cases, individuals with XY chromosomes may be born with genitalia that appears female, and the lack of periods may be the first clue that something is different.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically identified at puberty)

Genetic Inheritance

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia include amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, hypomenorrhea. 

In 21-hydroxylase deficiency (OMIM), glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid synthesis is reduced and steroid hormone synthesis is diverted to androgen and sex hormone synthesis leading to excess levels of these hormones. In individuals with 46 XX genotype, this enzyme deficiency leads to high androgen to estrogen ratio, which results in lack of menses, oligomenorrhea or other menstrual irregularities.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 31: Steroid Hormones and Vitamin D

Oliveira JC, Sousa FC, Campos ST, et al. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia in adolescence - a gynecological perspective. Ginekol Pol. Published online March 22, 2022.
https://journals.viamedica.pl/ginekologia_polska/article/view/87514

Hubska J, Kępczyńska-Nyk A, Czady-Jurszewicz K, Ambroziak U. Characteristics of Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia Diagnosed in Adulthood: A Literature Review and Case Series. J Clin Med. 2023;12(2):653.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9866811/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The adrenal glands normally make a series of hormones in a step-by-step process. In 21-hydroxylase deficiency, one step is blocked, so instead of making the usual hormones, the body is rerouted into making too many male-type hormones. In individuals with XX chromosomes, this hormone imbalance — too many male hormones relative to female hormones — disrupts normal ovarian function, leading to absent or infrequent periods.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric and adults (a lifelong condition, severity varies)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with 5-alpha-reductase deficiency (OMIM) are amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and hypomenorrhea. 

5-alpha-reductase deficiency is an autosomal recessive condition that presents phenotypically in individuals with 46, XY genotype. In these individuals, deficiency of 5-alpha-reductase enzyme results in decreased synthesis of dihydrotestosterone (DHT). DHT is a potent form of testosterone that is required for male sexual differentiation and development, which includes the development of male external genitalia and secondary sexual characteristics. Thus, 5-alpha reductase deficiency in individuals with 46, XY genotype leads to under masculinization. In severe cases, these individuals may be assigned as females at birth and later may present with primary amenorrhea.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 31: Steroid Hormones and Vitamin D

Kumar G, Barboza-Meca JJ. 5-Alpha-Reductase Deficiency. [Updated 2022 Oct 17]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539904/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The body converts one form of male-type hormone into a more powerful form using an enzyme called 5-alpha-reductase. When this enzyme is missing, the more powerful form cannot be made. In individuals with XY chromosomes, this means the body cannot fully develop male external features before birth, and the individual may be raised as female. When they do not get a first period at puberty, it may prompt investigation that leads to this diagnosis.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically identified at puberty)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Some menstrual irregularities associated with endocrine disorders such as pituitary tumors (prolactinomas, craniopharyngiomas), Hypo/Hyperthyroidism, Cushing Syndrome include amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and hypomenorrhea. These disorders lead to hypogonadism originating from hypothalamus-pituitary-gonadal axis dysfunction. Pituitary tumors lead to hypersecretion of hormones from respective cells, with a loss of negative feedback mechanism. 40% of craniopharyngiomas are characterized by a deficiency of gonadotropic hormone (FSH and LH). Both situations affect the hypothalamus-pituitary-gonadal axis dysfunction leading to hypogonadism, which can lead to amenorrhea or oligomenorrhea. In prolactinomas, increased prolactin secretion by tumor suppresses GnRH secretion, resulting in disruption of the menstrual cycle or absent menses. Abnormal thyroid hormone levels can also affect the hypothalamus-pituitary-gonadal axis. Similarly, excess glucocorticoids in Cushing syndrome also suppress the central secretion of gonadotropic-releasing hormones. The resultant hypogonadism in these conditions leads to menstrual irregularities.
RESOURCES:
Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 22e. 2025. Ch. 392: Pituitary Tumor Syndromes; Ch. 298: Disorders of the Adrenal Cortex, Ch. 395: Hypothyroidism

Ortiz Torres M, Shafiq I, Mesfin FB. Craniopharyngioma. [Updated 2023 Apr 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459371/#
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The brain and pituitary gland work together as a control center that sends hormone signals to the ovaries to regulate the menstrual cycle. In Cushing syndrome, the body has too much of a stress hormone called cortisol, which suppresses this control center. When the brain can't send the right signals to the ovaries, the ovaries stop working normally, and periods become irregular or stop altogether.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, though can affect adolescents

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The pituitary gland is a small gland in the brain that controls the hormone signals sent to the ovaries. When a tumor grows in or near the pituitary, it can disrupt these signals in different ways. Some tumors cause the gland to make too much of one hormone, which shuts down the signals needed for normal ovarian function. Others destroy the cells that make the hormones needed to drive the menstrual cycle. Either way, without the right signals reaching the ovaries, periods become irregular or stop.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, though can affect adolescents

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
Integration of Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with extreme exercise include amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and hypomenorrhea. 

Extreme exercise coupled with inadequate nutrition can lead to overall energy scarcity and metabolic stress. This can suppress pulsatile secretion of GnRH from hypothalamus. The disturbance in hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis causes hypogonadotropic hypogonadism leading to amenorrhea. Additionally, imbalance of neurohormones (cortisol, ghrelin, leptin, insulin-like growth factors) could also contribute to amenorrhea and menstrual irregularities.
RESOURCES:
Mallinson RJ, Williams NI, Olmsted MP, Scheid JL, Riddle ES, De Souza MJ. A case report of recovery of menstrual function following a nutritional intervention in two exercising women with amenorrhea of varying duration. J Int Soc Sports Nutr. 2013;10:34.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3750722/

Huhmann K. Menses Requires Energy: A Review of How Disordered Eating, Excessive Exercise, and High Stress Lead to Menstrual Irregularities. Clin Ther. 2020;42(3):401-407.
https://www.clinicaltherapeutics.com/article/S0149-2918(20)30052-7/full text

Roberts RE, Farahani L, Webber L, Jayasena C. Current understanding of hypothalamic amenorrhoea. Ther Adv Endocrinol Metab. 2020;11:2042018820945854.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7418467/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The brain controls the menstrual cycle by sending out regular hormonal signals. When exercise is extreme and the body doesn't have enough fuel to support it, the body goes into a kind of energy-saving mode. One of the first things the body cuts back on is the hormonal signal that drives the menstrual cycle. Other hormones that help regulate energy balance also get disrupted. Together, these changes can cause periods to become irregular or stop completely.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adolescents and adults (athletes, dancers, military recruits)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function 
Transcription and Regulation of Gene Expression
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Fragile X Associated Primary Ovarian Insufficiency (FXPO) is associated with premature/ early menopause. 

Fragile X is a genetic condition caused due to unstable expansion of trinucleotide repeats (CGG) in the gene encoding for FMRP Translational Regulator 1 (OMIM). In females carrying allele in premutation range, ovarian failure is observed leading to premature menopause. The severity depends on the size of the expansion, with longer allele resulting in earlier onset of menopause. While the exact underlying mechanism is still being explored, aberrant translation arising from repeat regions could be leading to ovarian dysfunction.
RESOURCES:
Fink DA, Nelson LM, Pyeritz R, et al. Fragile X Associated Primary Ovarian Insufficiency (FXPOI): Case Report and Literature Review. Front Genet. 2018;9:529.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6278244/

Okeke T, Anyaehie U, Ezenyeaku C. Premature menopause. Ann Med Health Sci Res. 2013;3(1):90-95. 
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3634232/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Fragile X is a genetic condition caused by an abnormal expansion of a repeated section of DNA in a specific gene. In females who carry a certain version of this gene change, the ovaries may stop working properly much earlier than normal — before age 40. The exact reason this happens is still being studied, but the abnormal gene seems to interfere with normal ovarian function, causing early menopause.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults (premature menopause typically occurs in the reproductive years)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Hemostasis
Nutrition
Protein Synthesis, Trafficking and Posttranslational Modifications
Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with acquired coagulopathies/ bleeding disorders (Vitamin K deficiency and Warfarin pharmacotherapy) include menorrhagia, prolonged menses, and polymenorrhea. 

Impairment of blood coagulation due to Vitamin K deficiency can lead to heavy or prolonged bleeding during menses. Vitamin K is required for gamma-carboxylation of glutamate residues of proteins, which includes clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. The Vitamin K-dependent carboxylase modifies particular glutamic acid residues near the N-terminus of several clotting factors. This enables the clotting factors to bind calcium, and expose a hydrophobic surface that allows binding of enzymes and substrates to membrane surfaces. Vitamin K deficiency can result from malabsorption, decreased intake or damaged to gut flora (from antibiotic use). 

The anticoagulant drug, Warfarin, is a competitive inhibitor of Vitamin K epoxide reductase, which is required for reactivation of Vitamin K. Insufficient active Vitamin K leads to ineffective coagulation.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch 35: Blood Clotting

Zekavat OR, Fathpour G, Haghpanah S, Dehghani SJ, Zekavat M, Shakibazad N. Acquired Vitamin K Deficiency as Unusual Cause of Bleeding Tendency in Adults: A Case Report of a Nonhospitalized Student Presenting with Severe Menorrhagia. Case Rep Obstet Gynecol. 2017;2017:4239148.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5592002/

James AH. Heavy menstrual bleeding: work-up and management. Hematology Am Soc Hematol Educ Program. 2016;2016(1):236-242.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6142441/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Blood clotting is a process that requires several proteins to work in a specific order, like a chain reaction. Vitamin K is needed to activate several of these clotting proteins. Without enough Vitamin K, the chain reaction doesn't work well, and bleeding becomes harder to stop. During menstruation, this means periods can become much heavier or last longer than normal.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages; risk is higher in those with poor diet, malabsorption, or antibiotic use

Genetic Inheritance
Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with Kallmann Syndrome include amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and hypomenorrhea. 

Mutations in various genes involved in development of specific areas of brain, such as (ANOS1, CHD7, FGF8, etc.) have shown to give rise to Kallmann Syndrome. This syndrome is characterised by hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, which arises due to failure of migration of neuronal cell arising from nasal placode. These consist of cells that process smell sensation. Additionally, migration of neurohormonal cells secreting GnRH is also affected. Thus, in individuals with Kallmann Syndrome, production of sex hormones and normal sexual development is impaired. This could lead to amenorrhea or other menstrual irregularities.
RESOURCES:
Sonne J, Lopez-Ojeda W. Kallmann Syndrome. [Updated 2023 May 16]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538210/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The menstrual cycle depends on a hormone signal released from a specific area of the brain. In Kallmann syndrome, certain brain cells that are supposed to migrate to the right location during early development never make it there. Without these cells in place, the brain cannot send the hormonal signals needed to drive normal sexual development and ovarian function. This leads to absent or very irregular periods.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically identified at puberty when periods don't start)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Menstrual irregularities associated with Malnutrition, Weight loss and eating disorders (see Restrictive eating behaviours/ eating disorders) include amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and hypomenorrhea. GnRH secretion is controlled by multiple factors. One of them is overall energy scarcity either due to decreased caloric intake or excess energy expenditure leading to metabolic stress.The disturbance in hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis causes hypogonadotropic hypogonadism leading to amenorrhea and menstrual irregularities. Additionally, specific macronutrient (e.g. protein deficiency) and micronutrient deficiencies (e.g. iron deficiency) can lead to neurohormonal (cortisol, ghrelin, leptin, insulin-like growth factors) and inflammatory imbalance, which could also contribute to above listed mechanism.
RESOURCES:
Roberts RE, Farahani L, Webber L, Jayasena C. Current understanding of hypothalamic amenorrhoea. Ther Adv Endocrinol Metab. 2020;11:2042018820945854.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7418467/

https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8401547/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The brain controls the menstrual cycle by sending out regular hormonal signals. When the body doesn't get enough food or the right nutrients, it detects an energy shortage and goes into a kind of survival mode. One of the first things it cuts back on is the hormonal signal that drives the menstrual cycle. Imbalances in other hormones related to hunger and stress can make this worse, leading to absent or very irregular periods.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages across the reproductive lifespan

Various pathologies involving ovaries could be leading causes of premature/ early menopause.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The ovaries contain follicles that develop eggs and produce the hormones needed for regular periods. When the ovaries are damaged — by infection, by the body's own immune system attacking them, by cancer, or by treatments like radiation or chemotherapy — the follicles stop working properly. Without healthy follicle function, the hormones needed for regular periods are no longer produced, leading to early menopause.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults of reproductive age, though can affect adolescents depending on cause

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with physiological amenorrhea include amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and hypomenorrhea. 

Pregnancy and lactation are the most common causes of physiological secondary amenorrhea. High levels of progesterone and estrogen during pregnancy inhibit follicle stimulating hormone (FSH) and Luteinizing (LH) secretion from pituitary gland. Similarly, high prolactin during pregnancy and lactation have similar effect leading to inhibition of menstrual cycle.
RESOURCES:
Soma-Pillay P, Nelson-Piercy C, Tolppanen H, Mebazaa A. Physiological changes in pregnancy. Cardiovasc J Afr. 2016;27(2):89-94.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4928162/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: During pregnancy and breastfeeding, the body produces high levels of certain hormones — including one called prolactin during breastfeeding — that suppress the hormonal signals needed to restart the menstrual cycle. This is a normal physiological response. Until these hormone levels drop, periods remain absent or irregular.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults (and adolescents who have been pregnant)

Nutrition
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Menstrual irregularities associated with PCOS include amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, hypomenorrhea, menorrhagia, prolonged menses, and polymenorrhea.  PCOS has a multifactorial etiology, which includes genetic as well as environmental factors. This multisystem disorder is characterized by hyperandrogenism, abnormal folliculogenesis, and chronic anovulation. Excess androgen production by ovaries (adrenal and peripheral tissue can also contribute) leads to hormonal imbalance between androgens, follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), and anti-Mullerian hormone, which results in follicular arrest. The hormonal and ovarian dysfunction result in amenorrhea or other menstrual irregularities. Additionally, insulin resistance is considered to be an important factor leading to the pathophysiology of PCOS. Insulin resistance is known to increase androgen synthesis further contributing to androgen excess and thus menstrual irregularities such as, amenorrhea and oligomenorrhea. Irregular menses can lead to endometrial hyperplasia. This can cause unscheduled menses as well as heavy bleeding (menorrhagia).

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: PCOS is a complex condition with multiple causes. The ovaries produce too many male-type hormones, which disrupts the normal process of egg development and release. This hormonal imbalance prevents ovulation from happening regularly, which in turn leads to irregular, absent, or unpredictable periods. Many individuals with PCOS also have insulin resistance, which further drives up male-type hormone production and worsens the menstrual irregularities. Over time, the buildup of the uterine lining from skipped cycles can also lead to very heavy bleeding.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adolescents and adults of reproductive age

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Chromosomal abnormalities 
Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with Turner syndrome include amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, hypomenorrhea, premature/ early menopause. 

Turner syndrome is a chromosomal disorder most commonly caused due to monosomy of chromosome X (a sex chromosome aneuploidy). Individuals with Turner syndrome have gonadal dysgenesis (example streak ovaries). This chromosomal abnormality is characterized by loss of entire X chromosome (Monosomy X) or part of it (structural chromosomal abnormality). This results in impaired pairing of chromosomes during meiosis, which could lead to meiotic arrest of germ cells and apoptosis. Additionally, follicular development is also impaired. This results in hypergonadotropic hypogonadism. Thus, decrease in cyclical release of sex hormones may lead to primary amenorrhoea. Additionally, there is premature ovarian failure/ insufficiency due to ovarian dysgenesis. This leads to premature or early menopause.
RESOURCES:
Yoon JY, Cheon CK. Evaluation and management of amenorrhea related to congenital sex hormonal disorders. Ann Pediatr Endocrinol Metab. 2019;24(3):149-157.

https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3634232/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Turner syndrome is a chromosomal condition in which all or part of one X chromosome is missing. Without the full set of chromosomes, the ovaries do not develop properly and cannot produce eggs or the hormones needed for a regular menstrual cycle. In most cases, periods never start, and the ovaries fail very early — often before age 40 — leading to premature menopause.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (primary amenorrhea typically identified at puberty) and young adults (premature menopause)

BROAD DIFFERENTIAL: Uterine pathologies (Uterine fibroids, polyps, adenomyosis, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease or uterine malignancy)
SPECIFIC DIFFERENTIAL: Uterine pathologies (Uterine fibroids, polyps, adenomyosis, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease or uterine malignancy)
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Menstrual irregularities associated with uterine fibroids, polyps, adenomyosis, endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease or uterine malignancy include menorrhagia, prolonged menses, polymenorrhea and dysmenorrhea. 

These uterine pathologies affect endometrial lining. Occurrence of uterine fibroids and polyps are linked to high levels of estrogen. These pathologies are associated with increased local vasculature, increased surface area, local physical pressure changes and with high prostaglandin level, which could lead to menorrhagia. Similarly, adenomyosis, which is characterized presence of endometrial tissue in the myometrium results in hypertrophy of myometrium and leads to menorrhagia due to mechanisms similar to those mentioned above.

Dysmenorrhea (Secondary) with various uterine pathologies are associated with increased local secretion of prostaglandins (PGs), which are synthesized from arachidonic acid. High levels of PGs increase uterine contractility leading to cramping. Additionally, decreased blood supply due to increased contractility and vasoconstriction associated with PGs leads to hypoxia and ischemia, which can contribute to painful contractions. 

Dysmenorrhea can also be experienced without underlying structural pathology (Primary dysmenorrhea).  This has been linked to increased secretion of oxytocin, which leads to increased uterine contractility, which results in painful menses.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Several conditions can affect the lining or structure of the uterus. Fibroids, polyps, and adenomyosis are linked to high estrogen levels and cause changes in the blood supply and surface area inside the uterus, leading to heavier or longer periods. These and other uterine conditions also increase the levels of substances called prostaglandins, which make the uterus contract more strongly — causing cramping and painful periods. In some cases, increased contractions also reduce blood flow to the uterus, adding to the pain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults of reproductive age, though endometriosis and PCOS can affect adolescents

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Gluconeogenesis
Glycolysis
pH, Water, Electrolytes 
TCA Cycle, Oxidative Phosphorylation and Energy Generation
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Lactate-associated acidosis can trigger vomiting as a physiological response to remove H+ and thereby allow the stomach to add bicarbonate to the body. There are many drugs and toxins that can lead to lactate-associated acidosis - several are listed below:

Metformin is a drug used to lower blood glucose in type II diabetes.  Two mechanisms have been suggested to explain how Metformin overdose leads to lactic acidosis: 
1. Metformin leads to excess glucose uptake in the small intestine's splanchnic bed.  The intestinal cells may not have sufficient oxygen to fully metabolize the glucose leading to increased catabolism lactate through anaerobic glycolysis
2. Metformin is thought to inhibit mitochondrial respiratory chain complex 1, leading to decreased hepatic gluconeogenesis from lactate, pyruvate, and alanine, which results in additional lactate and substrate for lactate production.

Ethanol poisoning results in lactic acidosis due to the increased generation of NADH during ethanol metabolism. Excess NADH directs the lactate dehydrogenase reaction to produce lactate.

Drugs/Toxins that inhibit the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC), such as amytal, rotenone, cyanide, antimycin A, CO, sodium azide, and oligomycin. These inhibitors of the ETC prevent the flow of electrons through the ETC. As a result, the NADH will build up which in turn inhibits the tricarboxylic acid cycle and causes anaerobic pyruvate reduction to lactate. The increased serum lactate levels can be associated with acidosis.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Some medicines and chemicals can cause your body to make too much of a waste product called lactic acid. When this acid builds up in your blood, your body tries to get rid of it by making you vomit. For example, a diabetes medicine called metformin (if taken in too high a dose), drinking too much alcohol, or being exposed to certain poisons can all block the normal way your cells make energy. When cells can't make energy the normal way, they make lactic acid instead. Too much lactic acid makes your blood too acidic, and vomiting is one way your body tries to fix that.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Lactate-associated acidosis can trigger vomiting as a physiological response to remove H+ and thereby allow the stomach to add bicarbonate to the body. There are many drugs and toxins that can lead to lactate-associated acidosis - several are listed below:

Metformin is a drug used to lower blood glucose in type II diabetes.  Two mechanisms have been suggested to explain how Metformin overdose leads to lactic acidosis: 
1. Metformin leads to excess glucose uptake in the small intestine's splanchnic bed.  The intestinal cells may not have sufficient oxygen to fully metabolize the glucose leading to increased catabolism lactate through anaerobic glycolysis
2. Metformin is thought to inhibit mitochondrial respiratory chain complex 1, leading to decreased hepatic gluconeogenesis from lactate, pyruvate, and alanine, which results in additional lactate and substrate for lactate production.

Ethanol poisoning results in lactic acidosis due to the increased generation of NADH during ethanol metabolism. Excess NADH directs the lactate dehydrogenase reaction to produce lactate.

Drugs/Toxins that inhibit the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC), such as amytal, rotenone, cyanide, antimycin A, CO, sodium azide, and oligomycin. These inhibitors of the ETC prevent the flow of electrons through the ETC. As a result, the NADH will build up which in turn inhibits the tricarboxylic acid cycle and causes anaerobic pyruvate reduction to lactate. The increased serum lactate levels can be associated with acidosis.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Cholelithiasis refers to gallstones in the gallbladder, and choledocholithiasis refers to gallstones within the common bile duct. The latter can also lead to pancreatitis. Gallstone formation occurs when there is an imbalance in bile composition (an increased ratio of cholesterol to bile salts), such that cholesterol precipitates out. 

Cholecystitis is Inflammation of the gallbladder. Cholecystitis is associated with cholestasis (sluggish bile flow), reduced gallbladder motility, and possible infection.  The clinical presentation of cholecystitis includes nausea and vomiting, which can be triggered by multiple stimuli (chemical, infectious, physical, mechanical) and mediated by neurophysiological signaling (e.g. substance P, serotonin) through the brain-gut axis (neuroendocrine connections between GI tract and brain).

These stones can block the flow of bile and cause the gallbladder to become inflamed and swollen. When this happens, your body sends pain and chemical signals to your brain through a pathway connecting your gut and brain, making you feel sick and causing vomiting.

Serotonin can activate the enteric nervous system (ENS) and extrinsic vagal afferents to the brain that end in the nucleus tractus solitaries (NTS) in the brain stem, which acts as a vomiting center.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When certain viruses or bacteria get into your intestines, they irritate special cells in the lining of your gut. These irritated cells release a chemical called serotonin. Serotonin sends a signal through the nerves in your gut all the way up to a part of your brain that controls vomiting. Once that part of the brain receives the signal, it triggers you to vomit as a way to try to get rid of the harmful germ or toxin.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult and Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Absolute lack of insulin in patients with type I diabetes leads to increased lipolysis and release of free fatty acids from adipose tissue. In the liver, the flux of FA results in increased beta-oxidation and overproduction of acetyl CoA. Due to a lack of insulin inhibition on the HMG CoA synthase, acetyl CoA molecules are utilized for ketogenesis, which results in increased ketone body production and release in the circulation, leading to ketonemia. The carboxyl group of a ketone body has a pKa of ~4, so each ketone body loses a proton (H+), which circulates in the blood, lowering the overall pH and resulting in metabolic acidosis. In addition, urinary loss of glucose and ketone bodies results in dehydration and increased H+ circulating in a decreased volume of plasma that can cause severe acidosis (diabetic ketoacidosis or DKA). Vomiting is a physiological response to remove H+, allowing the stomach to add bicarbonate to the body.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In type 1 diabetes, the body doesn't make enough of a hormone called insulin. Without insulin, the body starts breaking down fat for energy instead of sugar. When fat is broken down this way, it produces waste products called ketones. Too many ketones make your blood too acidic. Your body tries to fix this by making you vomit, which is one way it can reduce the acid level in your blood. This serious condition is called diabetic ketoacidosis, or DKA.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult and Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Chemotherapy leads to the death of rapidly growing cells, damaging the epithelial lining of the gut and mouth.  This damage leads to pain and the release of substance-p, which results in changes to the tone and motility of the gut. These factors stimulate the afferent neuron endings within the lamina propria and activate the enteric nervous system (ENS) and extrinsic afferents to the brain stem (gut-brain axis). Additionally, some studies support that damage to enteric neurons, including neuronal loss and morphological alterations, may further contribute as underlying mechanisms for nausea and vomiting.

This damage causes pain and releases chemicals (like substance P) that upset the normal movements of your gut. MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Addison’s disease (referred to as an adrenal insufficiency), occurs when the adrenal glands are not producing enough of mineralocorticoid (aldosterone) and glucocorticoid hormone (cortisol). Cortisol is involved in the human stress response and has several different effects throughout the body such as regulation of blood glucose levels, causing vasoconstriction and inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines. Cortisol is essential in regulating blood glucose levels especially in prolonged fasting or stress by activating gluconeogenesis and in the liver. Aldosterone regulates water and salt levels in the body and hypoaldosteronism leads to hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypotension. Adrenal insufficiency leads to nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, or diarrhea. Vomiting and abdominal pain often are an indication for adrenal crisis, and the fluid loss due to vomiting or diarrhea may further precipitate the crisis. The cause of gastrointestinal symptoms in adrenal insufficiency is not known, however, it’s been suggested that electrolyte disturbances may be involved in their etiology.

In Addison's disease, these glands don't make enough of these hormones. Without enough aldosterone, the levels of salt and other minerals in your blood get out of balance. These imbalances are thought to upset your digestive system and send signals through your nervous system that cause nausea, vomiting, and stomach pain.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Pangastritis is the inflammation of the entire body of the stomach. This can be caused by a number of mechanisms, including bacterial infection (particularly by Helicobacter pylori), excessive alcohol consumption, stress, autoimmune conditions, and the use of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), such as aspirin. In the case of NSAIDs, the primary mechanism is the inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis. Prostaglandin signaling is required for cells of the stomach to excrete a layer of mucus that protects the stomach lining from stomach acid. Excessive NSAID use thus diminishes this lining, allowing acid to damage the stomach lining and results in inflammation.

Certain bacteria (like H. pylori), too much alcohol, or medicines like aspirin and ibuprofen can reduce this protective mucus layer. Without enough mucus, stomach acid irritates and inflames the lining of your stomach. This irritation sends signals through the nerves connecting your gut and brain, making you feel sick and causing vomiting.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Gastric ulcers result from damaged mucosa of the stomach lining and erosion which disrupts the protective barrier against hydrochloric acid and proteolytic enzymes found in the stomach juice. The two most common causes are H. Pylori and NSAID drugs.  In the case of NSAIDs, the primary mechanism is the inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis. Prostaglandin signaling is required for cells of the stomach to excrete a layer of mucus that protects the stomach lining from stomach acid. Excessive NSAID use thus diminishes this lining, allowing acid to damage the stomach lining and results in inflammation. Ulcers may lead to local tissue inflammation, hyperacidosis and further damage may result in perforation, bleeding, activation of gastric vagal afferents by certain molecules or changes to gastric tone that can stimulate the neurons in dorsal vagal complex and trigger nausea and vomiting ( often blood), however, the precise mechanisms are poorly understood.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Urea cycle disorders are a group of genetic conditions that affect the function of proteins and enzymes necessary to convert ammonia (a toxic waste product of amino acid catabolism) to urea that is disposed of in the urine.  High blood ammonia levels (hyperammonemia) result in ammonia crossing the blood-brain barrier and accumulating in the brain, where it is metabolized by glutamine synthase to form glutamine. The accumulation of glutamine leads to brain swelling and cerebral edema and may progress to encephalopathy, coma, and death. The damage to the CNS can impact the gut and trigger nausea and vomiting through uncharacterized mechanisms.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body breaks down proteins from food into smaller pieces called amino acids. When amino acids are used for energy, they produce a toxic waste product called ammonia. Normally, your liver uses a series of chemical steps (called the urea cycle) to turn ammonia into a harmless substance called urea, which you get rid of in your urine. In urea cycle disorders, gene mutations break one of these steps, causing ammonia to build up in the blood. Too much ammonia travels to the brain, causes swelling, and disrupts normal brain function, which leads to nausea and vomiting.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically presents in newborns or early childhood; some milder forms can present in adults)

When there is too much calcium in your blood, it affects how your nerve and muscle cells receive signals. This makes the muscles in your digestive tract move more slowly than normal, so food moves through your stomach and intestines more slowly. This sluggish movement causes your stomach contents to back up, which can make you feel sick and cause vomiting.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Lactose intolerance is caused by the lack or decreased expression of the enzyme lactase (OMIM) that catalyzes the breakdown of lactose to glucose and galactose.  Since only monosaccharides can be absorbed, undigested carbohydrates pass into the large intestine upon consuming lactose-containing products. The undigested lactose is used by bacteria that hyperproliferate and release H2 gas and also generate osmotic diarrhea. The resultant gas and bloating lead to nausea and vomiting.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When you are exposed to certain harmful substances (toxins), your liver — which is the body's main filter — works hard to break them down. Some toxins directly damage liver cells, while others cause the immune system to react and inflame the liver. This liver inflammation sends chemical and nerve signals through the gut-brain connection, causing nausea and vomiting.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Acute hepatitis can be caused by infectious agents (e.g. or viral (e.g.. hepatitis A/B/C/D), protozoal (e.g. amoeba), bacterial (e.g. salmonella) as well as non infectious agents such as drugs/toxins, alcohol, metabolic disorders (e.g. Wilson's disease). Acute hepatitis is characterized by hepatocellular inflammation lasting less than 6 months. The clinical presentation of acute hepatitis includes nausea and vomiting, which are triggered by multiple stimuli (chemical, infectious, physical, mechanical) and mediated by neurophysiological signaling (e.g. substance P, serotonin, prostaglandins) through the brain-gut axis (neuroendocrine connections between GI tract and brain).

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Drinking too much alcohol over time causes the liver to become inflamed and damaged. Alcohol is broken down in the liver, but heavy or long-term drinking creates harmful by-products and puts too much stress on liver cells, causing them to become inflamed or die. This liver inflammation disrupts normal body chemistry and sends signals through the nerves connecting your gut and your brain, causing nausea and vomiting.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Maple syrup disease (MSD) is caused by mutations in components of the branched-chain alpha-ketoacid dehydrogenase complex (BCKD), specifically the enzymes BCKDHA (OMIM), BCKDHB (OMIM) and DBT (OMIM) (subunits of the mitochondrial branched-chain alpha-keto acid dehydrogenase complex). This complex is required to convert the toxic keto acids intermediates of branched-chain amino acids (leucine, isoleucine, and valine) metabolism to non-toxic by-products. These keto acids have a distinctive smell, akin to maple syrup, when secreted into the urine, giving the condition its name. Accumulation of the keto acids leads to brain and may results in delayed intellectual development, amongst other non-specific phenotypes. Elevated levels of Leucine is neurotoxic lead to multiple abnormalities primarily resulting from leucine toxicity which has been also associated with epigastric pain, anorexia, and vomiting. Nausea and Vomiting/poor appetite with this condition have been observed in the more rare severe forms of MSD in which there is a metabolic crisis.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Maple syrup disease is a rare condition that babies are born with because of changes in their genes. Normally, the body breaks down certain building blocks of protein (called branched-chain amino acids — leucine, isoleucine, and valine) step by step. In Maple syrup disease, a key enzyme needed for one of these steps is missing or doesn't work properly, so toxic waste products build up in the blood and brain. These toxic substances — especially high levels of leucine — can damage the brain and cause stomach pain, loss of appetite, and vomiting, particularly during a metabolic crisis. The urine and earwax of affected babies often smells like maple syrup, which is how the disease got its name.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Inflammation of the pancreas can be caused due to various factors such as pancreatic or common bile duct blockage, alcohol consumption, smoking, drugs, etc. The inflammatory response can result in trypsinogen activation and release of pancreatic enzymes. Cellular injury to the pancreas can lead to necrosis, which makes the site highly susceptible to infection. The surrounding structures and lining membranes (such as peritoneum) can also get affected.

Clinical presentation includes nausea and vomiting, which are triggered by multiple stimuli (chemical, infectious, physical, mechanical) and mediated by neurophysiological signaling (e.g. substance P, serotonin) through the brain-gut axis (neuroendocrine connections between GI tract and brain). Inflammatory mediators such as prostaglandins are released in response to gallbladder inflammation that can further propagate the inflammation.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your pancreas is a gland that makes digestive juices and hormones like insulin. Normally, the digestive enzymes made in the pancreas only become active after they reach your small intestine. In pancreatitis, these enzymes get activated too early — while still inside the pancreas — and start digesting the pancreas itself, causing painful inflammation. This can happen because of gallstones, too much alcohol, or certain medicines. The inflammation and damage release chemicals that signal through the nerves connecting your gut and brain, causing nausea and vomiting.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Phenylketonuria (PKU) is caused by pathogenic variants in the phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH; OMIM) gene, required for phenylalanine metabolism. Loss of gene function leads to accumulation of toxic metabolites (high levels of phenylalanine and phenylalanine-based products), which leads to metabolic crisis, of which one symptom is vomiting.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: PKU is a condition that babies are born with because of a change in a gene that makes an important enzyme called phenylalanine hydroxylase. This enzyme normally breaks down an amino acid called phenylalanine (found in protein-containing foods). Without this enzyme working properly, phenylalanine builds up to toxic levels in the blood and brain. Too much phenylalanine is harmful to brain development and can cause a metabolic crisis, one symptom of which is vomiting.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Heme Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Deficiency in the liver isozyme of PBG deaminase (OMIM 609806) leads to a buildup of porphobilinogen (PBG) and aminolevulinic Acid (ALA). The underlying mechanism of why this deficiency results in nausea is not fully understood. One theory is that a specific porphyrin precursor (most likely ALA and PBG) are neurotoxins that damage nerve tissue. The regulation of nausea by the gut-brain axis could be at the root of the nausea.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Porphyria is a condition caused by a gene mutation that disrupts how your body makes heme — a molecule found in red blood cells. Because of this gene change, certain toxic substances called ALA and PBG build up in the body. Scientists believe these substances act as neurotoxins, meaning they can damage nerve tissue, including the nerves that control your digestive system. This nerve damage disrupts the normal signals between your gut and brain, causing episodes of severe nausea and vomiting.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult (typically presents in young adult women, though can occur in men and older adults; rare before puberty)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Nausea and vomiting is a common clinical presentation in pregnant people. While the exact pathogenesis is still being explored, hormonal changes are thought to be the underlying cause. An increase in levels of human choriogonadotropin (hCG) is thought to be a major factor based on the temporal association between the time of presentation of these symptoms and peaking of hCG production. The pregnant individuals experiencing these symptoms may also have underlying conditions that are associated with higher levels of hCG. Additionally, hCG can lead to prostaglandin E2A stimulation, which can trigger vomiting. Changes in the levels of other hormones such as estrogen and progesterone have also been suggested to lead to nausea and vomiting.
RESOURCES:

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: During pregnancy, the body produces high levels of a hormone called human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), which helps support the pregnancy. This hormone — along with rising levels of estrogen and progesterone — affects the way the stomach and intestines move and can stimulate the body's vomiting response. hCG can also trigger the release of another chemical called prostaglandin E2, which is a known trigger for vomiting. Together, these hormonal changes are the main reason why many pregnant people experience nausea and vomiting, especially in the first trimester.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult (pregnant individuals)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Megaloblastic anemia, induced by a folate or B12 deficiency, is characterized by impaired DNA replication due to an inability to synthesize purines and dTMP.  The resulting cell cycle arrest in erythroblasts reduces the red blood cell count. The lack of red blood cells decreases the ability of the blood to carry oxygen, resulting in a pale appearance. In addition to dietary B12 deficiency, there are also various genetic, autoimmune (e.g. Crohn's), and surgical (e.g. bariatric surgery) reasons for poor B12 absorption or processing.

SPECIAL NOTES: Megaloblastic anemia is not rare, but data are insufficient regarding prevalence; incidence is higher in the elderly. Patients following a strictly vegan or Mediterranean diet and after bariatric surgery are at high risk for vitamin B12 deficiency
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body needs folate (a B vitamin found in leafy greens and beans) to build the DNA required to make red blood cells. Without enough folate, red blood cells can't divide properly and grow too large without maturing. Fewer working red blood cells means less oxygen is delivered around your body, making your skin and lips look pale.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. Adult cases are most common, particularly in the elderly and pregnant women. Pediatric cases occur in infants of folate-deficient mothers or children with poor diets.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Iron deficiency can result from many causes including: lack of dietary iron; loss of iron due to bleeding; a genetic deficiency in the divalent metal ion transporter (DMT1/SLC11A2; OMIM)  that transports Fe2+ from endosome leading to insufficient release of iron for biosynthetic reactions; GI inflammation due to celiac disease causing decreased iron absorption; and various parasitic infections (especially in developing countries) that result in blood loss and iron deficiency.

Insufficient iron decreases heme biosynthesis by the sequence-specific translational down regulation of delta-aminolevulinic acid synthase 2.  The heme deficiency down regulates the global translation of globin proteins in reticulocytes via the heme regulated inhibitor kinase (HRI).  The decreased production of hemoglobin results in a pale appearance.

SPECIAL NOTES: Approximately 25% of people worldwide have anemia. Iron deficiency, the most common cause, is responsible for 50% of all anemias.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Red blood cells carry oxygen using hemoglobin, a protein that requires iron at its core. When your body doesn't have enough iron — from poor diet, blood loss, or poor gut absorption — it can't make enough hemoglobin. Without enough hemoglobin, red blood cells become small and pale, and your skin and lips look pale too.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. It is the most common cause of anemia worldwide. Adult cases are most common in women of reproductive age and the elderly. Pediatric cases are most common in toddlers and adolescent girls.

Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. Severe forms (thalassemia major) typically present in infancy or early childhood. Milder forms (thalassemia trait) may go undiagnosed until adulthood.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Sickle cell disease results from a single amino acid mutation from glutamic acid to valine in both copies of the β-globin (OMIM) gene.  This mutation creates a hydrophobic patch on the surface of hemoglobin leading to polymerization of hemoglobin and the sickle shape of the red blood cells (RBCs).  Sickling of RBCs and their premature breakdown leads to anemia and a pale appearance.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: A single inherited gene change causes hemoglobin proteins to clump together into rigid rods when oxygen is low, forcing red blood cells into a stiff sickle shape. Sickle cells get stuck in blood vessels and break apart early, reducing the number of red blood cells and oxygen delivery, causing pale skin and lips.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. It is a congenital condition diagnosed at birth. Symptoms appear in the pediatric population after 6 months of age, and patients require lifelong management into adulthood.

Genetic Inheritance

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Genetic methemoglobinemia originates from pathogenic variants in the alpha (OMIM) or beta chain (OMIM) of hemoglobin that lead to the oxidation of the heme iron to Fe3+, which cannot bind O2 (aka methemoglobin).  

Additionally, genetic methemoglobinemia can originate from pathogenic variants that lead to a deficiency in the enzyme NADH-cytochrome b5 reductase (OMIM), which reduces heme Fe3+ to Fe2+ (the form that binds O2).  These variants also lead to the formation of methemoglobin.

Acquired methemoglobinemia  is caused by exposure to oxidizing agents such as aniline dyes, nitrobenzene, nitrate, nitrite, benzocaine, prilocaine, dapsone, pyridium, nitric oxide, nitrous oxide and naphthalene, which overwhelm the capacity of reducing enzymes and lead to the formation of methemoglobin.

In all cases, the oxidized Fe of methemoglobin results in poor oxygen delivery. Cyanosis causes bluish skin and eventually the conjunctiva will exhibit pallor.

1.  The incidence of genetic methemoglobinemia is unknown

2.  Acquired methemoglobinemia is much more common than genetic causes.

3. Treatment of methemoglobinemia with methylene blue reverses the Fe redox state (but not used in G6PDH treatment).
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Hemoglobin needs its iron in a specific form (Fe2+) to carry oxygen. In methemoglobinemia, the iron gets stuck in the wrong form (Fe3+) and cannot carry oxygen. This can result from an inherited gene change or exposure to certain chemicals or medicines. When enough hemoglobin is affected, the skin and lips look pale or bluish.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. Inherited forms affect the pediatric population from birth and persist into adulthood. Acquired forms can affect any age, but the pediatric population — especially infants — is particularly vulnerable due to immature protective enzyme systems.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Porphyrias include congenital erythropoietic porphyria (deficiency in UROS (uroporphyrinogen III synthase) gene (OMIM), erythropoietic protoporphyria (deficiency in the FECH (ferrochelatase) gene), hepatoerythropoietic porphyria and porphyria cutanea tarda (both caused by deficiency in uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase (UROD) gene). These erythropoietic porphyrias result in deficiencies in the  heme synthesis pathway, which leads to reduced production of heme. The heme deficiency down regulates the global translation of globin proteins in reticulocytes via the heme regulated inhibitor kinase (HRI).  The decreased production of hemoglobin results in a pale appearance.  In some cases the build up of toxic porphyrin intermediates and exposure to sunlight excerberates skin blistering.  Hence the pale appearance of these individuals can in part be attributed to avoidance of sunlight.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body builds heme — the oxygen-carrying core of hemoglobin — through a step-by-step process. In porphyria, an inherited enzyme defect blocks this process, causing toxic byproducts to pile up and too little heme to be made. Without enough heme, hemoglobin production falls and skin looks pale. In some types, toxic buildup causes sun-sensitive blistering, so sun avoidance further contributes to pallor.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. Congenital erythropoietic porphyria often presents in the pediatric population in infancy. Other types, like porphyria cutanea tarda, more commonly present in the adult population, often triggered by alcohol use or infection.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Acquired copper deficiency associated with gastrointestinal (GI) surgery, malabsorption, or nutritional deficiency can lead to peripheral neuropathy, but the exact biochemical mechanism is unclear. Copper plays a crucial role in the central nervous system as an essential component of various proteins and metalloenzymes. Copper deficiency has long been known to cause sensory ataxia, commonly referred to as swayback disease. Common enzymes requiring Cu are cytochrome oxidase, dopamine beta-hydroxylase (DBH), superoxide dismutase, and tyrosinase. Cytochrome oxidase deficiency impairs cellular respiration, which may lead to central nervous system degeneration and ataxia. Additionally, autonomic symptoms such as hypotension and hypothermia arise from abnormal catecholamine synthesis, which is caused by a deficiency in dopamine beta-hydroxylase. These effects eventually progress to demyelination of CNS and peripheral nervous system leading to myelopolyneuropathy.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Copper is a mineral your body needs to keep nerves healthy. It helps power several important enzymes, including ones that produce energy in nerve cells and help make chemical messengers. Without enough copper, nerve cells can't make enough energy, the protective coating around nerves breaks down, and chemical signaling goes wrong. This leads to tingling, numbness, and balance problems.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. Adult cases are most common after GI surgery or with malabsorption. Pediatric cases occur in rare inherited disorders such as Menkes disease.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Multiple, non-mutually exclusive mechanisms have been proposed for peripheral neuropathy resulting from diabetes. One model is that the high levels of glucose (primarily in type 1 diabetes) or lipids (primarily in type 2 diabetes) leads to accumulation of lactate or acylcarnitine, respectively, in Schwann cells. These products are then shuttled to peripheral nervous system (PNS) neurons, causing mitochondrial dysfunction and cell death. An alternative model is that excess glucose is converted by the enzyme aldose reductase in the Schwann cells to sorbitol. Sorbitol cannot be further metabolized and puts osmotic stress on the cell, resulting in cell damage, and decreased innervation in the peripheral tissues. Finally, high levels of glucose can lead to non-enzymatic modification of proteins. These glycation end products are thought to bind RAGE (Receptor for advanced glycation end products) and trigger inflammatory cascades. Glycation end products can also form crosslinks with collagen and disrupt PNS structure.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In diabetes, blood sugar or fat levels stay too high for too long. This damages the cells that wrap around and protect your nerves in several ways: toxic byproducts build up inside them, excess sugar draws water in causing cells to swell, and sugar-coated proteins trigger inflammation. All of these effects slowly destroy the nerve's protective covering and the nerve itself, causing tingling, numbness, and pain — usually starting in the feet.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Predominantly an adult population condition. The pediatric population with poorly controlled diabetes can be affected after years of high blood sugar, but symptoms are far more common in adults.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Many drugs may induce peripheral neuropathy, including amiodarone, antimycobacterial, chemotherapy drugs, immunosuppressors, nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and statins. Depending on the drug, several mechanisms are possible that lead to symptoms, such as a metabolic disturbance, an inflammatory response, or a disruption of axonal transport disruption. An example that is particularly relevant to biochemistry is use of statin class of drugs that may disturb cholesterol levels thus affecting membrane integrity and reduced ubiquinone levels decreasing the energy available for neurons. Another example is Isoniazid, an antimycobacterial drug, that can lead to vitamin B6 deficiency and thus induce peripheral neuropathy. (A deficiency of B6 may lead to improper myelin sheath formation, and thus to numbness, paresthesias, or burning pain in the feet which then ascends to affect the legs and eventually the hands.). A third example is a drug such as paclitaxel, a chemotherapy drug used to treat various types of cancer,  that inhibits microtubule disassembly thus affecting axonal transport in neurons.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Some medicines can accidentally damage the nerves in your hands and feet. Different drugs do this in different ways. For example, cholesterol-lowering drugs can disrupt the fats that make up nerve cell membranes and reduce energy production in nerve cells. The antibiotic isoniazid can drain your body of vitamin B6, which nerves need to build their protective coating. Some chemotherapy drugs block the internal transport system that nerves use to move important materials along the cell. All of these effects can cause tingling, numbness, or weakness.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Predominantly an adult population condition. The pediatric population receiving chemotherapy may also be affected.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Uremic neuropathy is a toxicity caused by excessive levels of uremic toxins which are biologically active compounds that are retained due to kidney impairment. 
The mechanisms by which the toxins damage neurons is not clear, and is likely to be multifactorial. Uremic toxins, such as guanidine compounds, indoxyl sulfate, and parathyroid hormone fragments, can directly damage peripheral nerves by altering neuronal membranes and disrupting ion channel functions. These toxins also promote oxidative stress, increasing reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, which leads to lipid peroxidation, protein modification, and DNA damage in nerve tissues. Additionally, uremic toxins impair axonal transport and contribute to chronic inflammation, both of which disrupt the normal structure and function of peripheral nerves. Over time, these effects result in nerve fiber degeneration, demyelination, and sensory and motor deficits characteristic of uremic neuropathy.

SPECIAL NOTES: Uremic neuropathy usually progresses slowly and causes typical symptoms of a peripheral neuropathy, such as paresthesia, artificial heat sensation, and increased pain sensation. Symptoms start in the more distal body regions and progress proximally.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your kidneys filter waste products out of your blood. When the kidneys aren't working well, toxic waste products build up in the body. These toxins directly damage the nerves in your hands and feet by disrupting normal nerve signaling, causing oxidative damage to nerve cell membranes, and blocking the internal transport system inside nerve cells. Over time, this leads to nerve fiber breakdown, causing tingling, numbness, and weakness that typically starts in the feet and moves upward.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Predominantly an adult population condition, as chronic kidney disease is most common in adults. The pediatric population with kidney failure can also develop uremic neuropathy.
