MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Deficiencies in glycolipid and sphingolipid metabolism, such as metachromatic leukodystrophy (arylsulfatase A deficiency; OMIM); Krabbe disease (β-Galactosylceramidase deficiency; OMIM); Gaucher disease (glucosylcerebrosidase deficiency; OMIM); Tay-Sachs and Sandhoff GM2 Gangliosidosis (β-Hexosaminidase deficiency; OMIM and OMIM); and Niemann-Pick types A and B (Sphingomyelinase; OMIM and OMIM) lead to peripheral neuropathy. The primary mechanism is the accumulation of non-degraded lipids in lysosomes disrupt overall lysosomal function and lead to cell dysfunction. The specificity of each disease comes mainly from which cell type produces each glycolipid and sphingolipid.  Note that in the majority of cases, peripheral neuropathy is not observed because the disease is typically fatal in earlier stages.
RESOURCES:
Pastores GM, Maegawa GH. Clinical neurogenetics: neuropathic lysosomal storage disorders. Neurol Clin. 2013;31(4):1051-1071. 
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3988112/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body has tiny compartments inside cells called lysosomes that act like recycling centers, breaking down old fats. In Gaucher disease, a specific enzyme needed to break down a certain fat doesn't work properly. This fat builds up inside the lysosomes, causing the recycling center to malfunction and the cell to stop working correctly. When this happens in nerve cells, it leads to nerve damage and symptoms like tingling and numbness.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. Severe neuropathic forms present in the pediatric population in infancy. The most common form affects both adult and pediatric populations.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Heavy metals such as cadmium, arsenic, lead, thallium, and mercury can induce peripheral neuropathy through several mechanisms. These metals interfere with normal neuronal function by disrupting ion channels, impairing synaptic transmission, and altering calcium homeostasis, which are critical for nerve signaling. Additionally, heavy metals generate oxidative stress by promoting the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), leading to lipid peroxidation, protein damage, and DNA dysfunction in peripheral nerves. They can also impair mitochondrial function and energy production in neurons, further contributing to nerve degeneration and dysfunction.
RESOURCES:
Heavy Metal Poisoning. National Organization for Rare Disorders. Accessed December 5, 2024.
https://rarediseases.org/rare-diseases/heavy-metal-poisoning/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Toxic metals like lead, mercury, and arsenic can damage the nerves in your hands and feet in several ways. They disrupt the electrical signals nerves use to communicate, interfere with how nerve cells produce energy, and generate harmful molecules that damage the fats and proteins that make up nerve cell membranes. Over time, this leads to nerve breakdown, causing tingling, numbness, and weakness usually starting in the hands and feet.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. Adult cases are most common in occupational settings. Pediatric cases occur through environmental exposure such as contaminated water or soil.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: An overdose of pyridoxine, typically from pharmacological sources, can cause neurological symptoms that resemble those of pyridoxine deficiency. In the peripheral nervous system (PNS), this toxicity causes neurodegeneration that manifests as paresthesia in the distal extremities and, in severe cases, difficulty in walking. The mechanism(s) of pyridoxine toxicity are not well understood. One potential mechanism is that pyridoxine acts as a competitive inhibitor of its bioactive derivative, pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP), thereby interfering with biological processes that depend on PLP (see Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) deficiency)
RESOURCES:
Hemminger A, Wills BK. Vitamin B6 Toxicity. [Updated 2023 Feb 7]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554500/

Hadtstein F, Vrolijk M. Vitamin B-6-Induced Neuropathy: Exploring the Mechanisms of Pyridoxine Toxicity. Adv Nutr. 2021;12(5):1911-1929.
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC8483950/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin B6 is a nutrient your body needs to keep nerves healthy. However, taking too much B6 — usually from high-dose supplements — can actually damage nerves. Very high levels of B6 may block its own active form from doing its normal job, disrupting important chemical reactions that nerves depend on. This paradoxically produces the same tingling and numbness in the hands and feet that is seen when B6 levels are too low.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Predominantly an adult population condition, as high-dose B6 supplementation is most common in adults. The pediatric population receiving high-dose B6 therapy for certain conditions may also be at risk.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Thiamine deficiency is primarily due to alcohol use disorder (leading to both less intake and impaired absorption of thiamine). Thiamine is converted to thiamine diphosphate (TDP) and TDP is an essential cofactor in cellular respiration, ATP production, the synthesis of the neurotransmitters glutamate and γ-aminobutyric acid, and for the maintenance of the myelin sheath. In the peripheral nervous system (PNS), thiamine deficiency manifests as distal sensory loss, burning pain, and paraesthesias. This starts off primarily in the feet, but can advance to the hands and eventually to the laryngeal nerves.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Thiamine (vitamin B1) is converted in the body into an essential helper molecule used to produce energy inside cells, make important chemical messengers, and maintain the protective coating around nerves. Without enough thiamine — most commonly due to heavy alcohol use, which reduces both intake and absorption — nerve cells can't produce enough energy, the protective coating breaks down, and nerve signaling fails. This causes burning pain, tingling, and numbness that starts in the feet and can spread upward.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Predominantly an adult population condition, most commonly seen in adults with alcohol use disorder or severe malnutrition. Rare pediatric cases have been reported in the context of malnutrition.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: For function, pyridoxine is first converted to its active form pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP.) PLP is a coenzyme required for numerous biochemical reactions, such as neurotransmitter synthesis (serotonin, GABA, Dopamine etc). Additionally, in the nervous system, PLP is required for the rate-limiting step in the de novo synthesis of sphingolipids. Sphingomyelin is an essential component of myelin sheath. A deficiency of B6 may lead to improper myelin sheath formation, and thus to numbness, paresthesias, or burning pain in the feet which then ascends to affect the legs and eventually the hands. The most frequent clinical cause of pyridoxine deficiency is from Isoniazid treatment for tuberculosis (TB).

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin B6 must be converted into its active form to do its job in the body. This active form is needed for making important chemical messengers in the nervous system and for building the protective coating around nerves. Isoniazid, a drug used to treat tuberculosis, interferes with this conversion, effectively creating a vitamin B6 deficiency. Without enough active B6, the protective coating around nerves breaks down, causing tingling, numbness, and burning pain that starts in the feet and moves upward toward the hands.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Predominantly an adult population condition. The pediatric population treated with isoniazid for tuberculosis is also at risk and should receive B6 supplementation.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin B12 is important for the methylmalonyl coA mutase reaction involved in odd-chain FA oxidation and branched chain amino acid metabolism.  B12 deficiency is primarily caused by autoimmune destruction of the gastric mucosa which is responsible for making intrinsic factor, which in turn is needed for B12 absorption in the gut. Other causes are restricted dietary choices, intestinal surgery, nitrous oxide use (which breaks down methyl-B12), etc. B12 deficiency results in accumulation of methylmalonyl CoA and propionyl CoA, interfering with myelin synthesis. B12 deficiency thus leads to a neuropathy called subacute combined degeneration (SCD). PNS (peripheral nervous system) symptoms of SCD include reduced proprioception and vibration sensation and weakness in a corticospinal tract distribution. In the CNS (central nervous system) SCD results in increased muscle tone, brisk knee and arm reflexes, and the extensor plantar response in the toes (called the Babinski reflex).

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin B12 is needed for a chemical reaction that helps your body process certain fats and amino acids. Without enough B12, harmful byproducts build up and interfere with the production of the protective coating around your nerves. When this coating breaks down, nerves in the spinal cord and peripheral nervous system can no longer send signals properly. This causes reduced ability to feel vibration and body position, as well as weakness, starting in the hands and feet.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. Adult cases are most common in the elderly, strict vegans, and those with autoimmune gastric conditions. Pediatric cases occur in infants of B12-deficient mothers or in children with rare inherited disorders.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin E deficiency may result from malnutrition as well as other pathologies that could lead to decreased absorption. Vitamin E (primarily as its bioactive derivative alpha-tocopherol) is an antioxidant and prevents oxidation of lipid membranes. In the absence of this antioxidant function, free radicals can damage the nerve cell membrane and cause neurodegeneration. Symptoms include ataxia, hyporeflexia, and loss of proprioception/vibration sensation.

SPECIAL NOTES: Since vitamin E is fat soluble, these symptoms can take years to manifest. Deficiency is primarily due to fat malabsorption due to intestinal pathologies, and rarely due to genetic causes such as mutations in abetalipoproteinemia or alpha-tocopherol transfer protein.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin E is an antioxidant that protects the fats making up nerve cell membranes from being damaged by harmful molecules. Without enough vitamin E, these harmful molecules attack and break down nerve cell membranes, causing progressive nerve damage. This leads to loss of balance, reduced reflexes, and an inability to sense vibration or the position of your own limbs, which can make walking difficult.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. Pediatric cases occur in the context of inherited conditions affecting fat absorption. Adult cases are more commonly associated with fat malabsorption due to intestinal or liver disease.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Alcohol is a diuretic. Alcohol reduces the amount of vasopressin produced by the body through a variety of mechanisms. Vasopressin is an antidiuretic hormone. Consuming alcohol without drinking enough water may lead to dehydration.
RESOURCES:

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Alcohol acts as a natural diuretic — meaning it makes your body produce more urine. It does this by reducing the amount of a hormone that normally tells the kidneys to hold onto water. When your kidneys can't hold onto water properly, you urinate more frequently. If you don't drink enough water alongside the alcohol, this can lead to dehydration, which makes you feel thirsty.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your kidneys normally use a hormone signal to know when to hold onto water instead of letting it pass out as urine. In diabetes insipidus, this signal is either missing (the brain doesn't make enough of the hormone) or the kidneys can't respond to it. Without this signal working properly, the kidneys release large amounts of very dilute urine instead of conserving water. Losing so much water makes you extremely thirsty, leading to the urge to drink large amounts of fluid.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages, depending on the underlying cause

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Dysregulation of insulin release.  MODY2 and MODY3  are most common.  Primary causes include inherited mutations in glucokinase (MODY2; OMIM) and mutations in Hepatocyte Nuclear Factor 1 (HNF1/MODY3; OMIM) leading to lower insulin response and delayed or diminished release of insulin from the pancreas. Resultant hyperglycemia overwhelms the renal glucose transporters, which leads to osmotic diuresis (Urine Osmolarity  > Serum Osmolarity ) resulting in polyuria. The loss of fluids causes the individual to feel excessive thirst (polydipsia).

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body uses a hormone called insulin to move sugar from the blood into cells for energy. In MODY, inherited gene changes cause the pancreas to produce less insulin than it should. Without enough insulin, blood sugar rises too high. The kidneys can only handle a certain amount of sugar — when the level in the blood gets too high, sugar spills into the urine and pulls water with it, causing frequent urination. Losing all that fluid makes you feel very thirsty.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric and young adults (typically diagnosed before age 25)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Individuals with Type 1 diabetes do not produce insulin due to an autoimmune event which leads to destruction of pancreatic beta cells. Resultant hyperglycemia and ketonemia from uncontrolled diabetes leads to osmotic diuresis in the kidney (Urine Osmolarity > Serum Osmolarity) resulting in polyuria. The loss of fluids causes the individual to feel excessive/abnormal thirst (polydipsia) leading to increased drinking and frequent urination.

SPECIAL NOTES: Individuals with Type 1 diabetes often present with a triad of polydipsia (excessive thirst), polyuria (frequent urination) and polyphagia (excessive hunger).
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In type 1 diabetes, the immune system mistakenly destroys the cells in the pancreas that make insulin. Without insulin, blood sugar rises very high. The kidneys can only handle a certain amount of sugar — when the level in the blood gets too high, sugar and ketones spill into the urine and pull water with them, causing frequent urination. Losing all that fluid makes you feel very thirsty, leading to drinking large amounts and urinating frequently.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric and young adults (though can occur at any age)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Individuals with Type 2 diabetes have varying degrees of insulin resistance and fail to secrete an adequate amount of insulin to maintain normal blood glucose levels. Higher insulin levels are needed for insulin-dependent uptake of glucose into cells.  Uncontrolled type 2 DM leads to hyperglycemia which overwhelms the renal glucose transporters leading to spillage of glucose in the urine and osmotic diuresis (Urine Osmolarity  > Serum Osmolarity) resulting in polyuria. The loss of fluids causes the individual to feel excessive thirst (polydipsia)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Addison Disease is a form of primary adrenal insufficiency that is primarily (70%) autoimmune in etiology.  Other causes include infection, infarctions, hemorrhage, rapid weaning from exogenous steroids, and cancer.  Resultant damage to the adrenal gland results in decreased production of glucocorticoids (cortisol) and mineralocorticoids (aldosterone). Lack of aldosterone results in loss of sodium, retention of potassium, and loss of water with increased sodium excretion. This results in the need to urinate frequently, dehydration and increased thirst.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Aspirin induces high anion gap metabolic acidosis and respiratory alkalosis by increasing salicylic acid and generating lactic acid through disrupted oxidative phosphorylation, leading to anaerobic respiration. Rapid breathing and respiratory alkalosis occur due to direct stimulation of the respiratory center.
RESOURCES: Arif H, Aggarwal S. Salicylic Acid (Aspirin) [Updated 2023 Jul 5]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK519032/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Aspirin breaks down in the body into a product that disrupts the normal way cells make energy, forcing them to switch to a less efficient backup process that produces lactic acid as a waste product. This makes the blood more acidic than normal. Aspirin also directly stimulates the part of the brain that controls breathing, causing the body to breathe faster to try to correct the acid buildup.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected, typically in the context of aspirin overdose or toxicity.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: A number of genetic conditions activate anaerobic glycolysis and lead to elevated lactate and an associated acidosis. Examples of metabolic conditions that lead to lactate associated acidosis are: Von Gierke glycogen storage disease I (a deficiency in glucose-6-phosphatase (OMIM) or glucose-6-phosphate translocase (OMIM) results in excess substrate for anaerobic glycolysis), and Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (OMIM) deficiency (during glycemic stress (i.e. fasting) glycogen stores are depleted and gluconeogenic substrates such as pyruvate, alanine, and glycerol accumulate.  The conversion of these substrates to lactate and acetyl CoA result in lactic acidosis and ketosis.  The rapid breathing ensues, which is mediated by respiratory center, to compensate the acidosis associated with these conditions.

In one example, too much sugar builds up and gets pushed into this backup process. In another example, when food is not available, the body can't properly make new sugar and instead produces acidic waste products. In both cases, the blood becomes too acidic, and the brain's breathing control center responds by making the body breathe faster to help correct the acid level.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Predominantly a pediatric population condition, as these inherited metabolic disorders typically present in infancy or early childhood.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Carbonic anhydrase deficiency, caused by a mutation in the CA5A gene (OMIM), leads to less CO2 being absorbed and converted to bicarbonate. Bicarbonate is necessary to maintain proper acid-base balance. During episodes of metabolic crisis and the subsequent abnormal brain function (acute encephalopathy), rapid breathing may occur for a variety of reasons.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When the body breaks down protein, it produces ammonia as a waste product. Normally the liver converts ammonia into a safe form that is removed in the urine. When ammonia builds up — due to liver problems, inherited enzyme defects, or certain drugs — it directly stimulates the part of the brain that controls breathing, causing the body to breathe faster than normal.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both adult and pediatric populations are affected. Inherited causes predominantly affect the pediatric population, while liver dysfunction and drug-related causes more commonly affect the adult population.

Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Predominantly an adult population condition, occurring in the context of accidental or intentional ingestion of methanol.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Multiple causes can lead to anemia.  Anemia reduces the oxygen carrying capacity of blood.  This can be compensated by greater deoxygenation of hemoglobin in tissues, and by increased blood flow, along with prompt reoxygenation in the lungs. When the sympathetic nervous system detects hypoxia, it increases the rate of breathing.  Anemia without adequate compensation leads to shortness of breath.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Red blood cells carry oxygen through your blood to every part of your body. When you have anemia, there aren't enough healthy red blood cells to carry the oxygen your body needs. Your body tries to make up for this by breathing faster and increasing blood flow, but when it can't keep up, you start to feel short of breath.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages, depending on the underlying cause

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: CFTR (Cystic Fibrosis Transmembrane Regulator; OMIM) deficiency;  seven classes and over 2000 different mutations have been identified, with delta F508 being the most common one. Defective chloride channel causes decreased secretion of chloride and increased sodium and water reabsorption, leading to dehydration of the mucus layer coating epithelial cells, defective mucociliary action, and mucus plugging of airways, making it difficult to breathe. It also increases susceptibility to infections, which further contribute to shortness of breath. The same disorder of ion transport is also seen in the epithelial lining of the gastrointestinal and reproductive tracts.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your airways are normally coated with a thin, slippery layer of fluid that helps sweep out germs and debris. In cystic fibrosis, a faulty protein causes too much salt and water to be pulled back into the body, leaving the mucus thick and sticky. This thick mucus clogs the airways, makes it hard to breathe, and makes infections more likely — which makes breathing even harder.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (typically diagnosed in infancy or early childhood)

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: During diabetic ketoacidosis, sensing of low pH by the central and peripheral chemoreceptors in medulla oblongata and the carotid bodies, respectively, will lead to Kussmaul respiration. This labored deep breathing helps in removal of excess CO2 as a compensatory mechanism to move the pH towards normal.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When the body can't use sugar for energy, it breaks down fat instead, which produces acidic byproducts that build up in the blood and make it too acidic. The body senses this and tries to correct it by breathing very deeply and rapidly to blow off extra acid through the lungs. This deep, labored breathing — while it's the body trying to help — can feel like shortness of breath.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults with type 1 diabetes, though can affect adolescents

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: TCA Cycle, Oxidative Phosphorylation and Energy Generation
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: DNP transports protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane and thus uncouples the electron transport chain from ATP production.  At a low dose, DNP increases the basal metabolic rate, enhances glycolysis, and causes marked amplification of thermogenesis. The increased metabolic rate can result in rapid breathing (in order to get more oxygen) and shortness of breath.
RESOURCES:

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Normally, the body converts food into usable energy in a very controlled way. DNP disrupts this process, causing the body to burn energy very rapidly and produce a large amount of heat instead. This dramatically speeds up the body's overall activity level, causing the body to demand much more oxygen than usual, leading to rapid, labored breathing and shortness of breath.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily young adults (often associated with misuse as a weight loss agent)

Inborn Errors of Metabolism
Lysosomes and Peroxisomes
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Type II glycogen storage disease (Pompe disease) is caused by an inherited deficiency in lysosome alpha 1-4 glucosidase (acid maltase; OMIM). Type II GSD is the only Glycogen Storage Disease that is also a Lysosome Storage Disease. Excess glycogen accumulation in lysosomes leads to muscle weakness, hepatomegaly, cardiomegaly, and respiratory insufficiency. Accumulated glycogen in motor neurons of the spinal cord and brainstem also contributes to muscle weakness.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body normally breaks down a stored sugar called glycogen to release energy. In Pompe disease, a specific enzyme inside cells is missing, so glycogen builds up and can't be cleared out. Over time this buildup damages muscle cells throughout the body, including the muscles used for breathing, making it increasingly difficult to breathe.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (infantile form) and adults (late-onset form)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: TCA Cycle, Oxidative Phosphorylation and Energy Generation
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Multiple factors could lead to hypophosphatemia. Severe hypophosphatemia causes acute respiratory failure and cardiac dysfunction by decreasing the production of ATP (ADP + phosphate --> ATP) in muscles.
RESOURCES:
Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 21e, 2022. Ch. 409: Bone and Mineral Metabolism in Health and Disease

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/10919539/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Phosphate is a mineral your body uses to make energy in every cell. When phosphate levels drop too low, your muscle cells — including the muscles that power your breathing and your heart — can't make enough energy to work properly. When the breathing muscles weaken severely, it becomes very hard to breathe.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, particularly those who are critically ill or malnourished

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Extracellular Matrix
Genetic Inheritance
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: The protease inhibitor locus that encodes alpha-1-antitrypsin (also called SERPINA1) has many variants. Inheritance of null alleles like the Z allele (mutations in SERPINA1; OMIM) results in markedly reduced level of alpha-1-antitrypsin, which is made in the liver, travels in blood to lungs. The main function of alpha-1-antitrypsin is to inhibit neutrophil elastase, and protect lungs from elastases, collagenases and proteases. Absence of alpha-1-antitrypsin results in lung damage leading to shortness of breath. Different factors such as smoking and infection can increase the severity.  Storage and inability to release the mutated nonfunctional proteins in hepatocytes causes apoptosis and liver disease.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your lungs contain a protective protein that acts like a shield, preventing immune cells from accidentally destroying healthy lung tissue. When a gene change causes this protein to be missing or not work properly, the protective shield is gone and the lung tissue gets broken down over time. This damage makes the lungs less able to move air in and out, causing shortness of breath. Smoking and infections can make it worse.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults (early-onset lung disease, typically younger adults with severe variants)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Nutrition
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Protein-energy deficiency (malnutrition) leads to degradation of skeletal and cardiac muscle protein (and adipose tissue fat).  It frequently also leads to anemia.  Protein deficiency can lead to weakness of respiratory muscles and thus shortness of breath. Underlying anemia can further impact respiratory function.
RESOURCES:

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body needs protein and calories to maintain muscle throughout the body, including the muscles that help you breathe. When someone is severely malnourished, the body breaks down its own muscle for fuel, including the breathing muscles. Weakened breathing muscles can't move air in and out effectively, causing shortness of breath. Anemia, which often develops alongside malnutrition, makes breathing even harder.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages, though risk is higher in the very young, elderly, and those with chronic illness

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Membrane Proteins and Lipids
MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Insufficient surfactant production from type 2 pneumocytes in premature infants leads to shortness of breath (production of surfactant starts around 24-28 weeks). Surfactant is required to reduce the surface tension of lung, in absence of which the lungs can collapse making it difficult to breathe. 

Diagnosis is based on L/S ratio (lecithin/sphingomyelin, one of fetal maturity tests).  Normal L/S >2.  L/S < 2 leads to respiratory distress.

Prophylactic treatment for pregnant mother in advance of planned premature delivery is corticosteroids.

Premature infants can be given exogenous surfactant treatment.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The lungs are coated with a slippery substance called surfactant that keeps the tiny air sacs in the lungs from collapsing after each breath. In premature babies, the lungs haven't had enough time to make this substance yet. Without it, the air sacs collapse, making it extremely difficult to breathe.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric (premature newborns)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Heme Proteins
TCA Cycle, Oxidative Phosphorylation and Energy Generation
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Cyanide binds to cytochrome c oxidase and prevents electron transport to O2. Oxidative phosphorylation ceases, and cell death can occur rapidly. Tissues are poisoned in direct proportion to their metabolic rate.  The chemoreceptors in the carotid body, the metabolically active tissues in the body, get stimulated and send a signal to the brainstem to modify breathing response. The inability of the body to utilize oxygen leads to rapid breathing followed by labored breathing thus leading to shortness of breath.
RESOURCES:

Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry. 9e, 2025. Ch. 6: Bioenergetics and Oxidative Phosphorylation.

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12226056/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your cells need oxygen to make energy. Cyanide blocks the last step of this energy-making process, so cells can no longer use the oxygen being delivered to them — even though there is plenty of it in the blood. The body senses something is very wrong and triggers rapid, labored breathing in an attempt to compensate, leading to severe shortness of breath.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): All ages (exposure-dependent)

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Alcohol Metabolism
Integration of Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: As a central nervous system depressant, ethanol binds to and activates GABA receptors, thereby inhibiting neurotransmission.  Its effects range from mildly impaired coordination and euphoria, marked ataxia, nausea or vomiting, and memory lapse, to severe respiratory depression, coma, and death. At a very high concentration of ethanol in the blood, respiratory depression develops, which can be followed by coma and death.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch.  30: Metabolism of Ethanol and the Consequences of Alcohol Dependence Syndrome

LaHood AJ, Kok SJ. Ethanol Toxicity. In: StatPearls. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; June 21, 2023. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32491313/ 

Nakayama K, Hasegawa H. Blood Vessels as a Key Mediator for Ethanol Toxicity: Implication for Neuronal Damage. Life (Basel). 2022;12(11):1882. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36431016/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Alcohol acts as a "slowdown" signal in the brain, reducing the activity of nerve cells. At low levels it affects coordination and judgment, but at very high levels it slows down the part of the brain that controls breathing. This causes breathing to become slower and shallower — a dangerous condition that can lead to respiratory failure, coma, and death.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults and adolescents

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
TCA Cycle, Oxidative Phosphorylation and Energy Generation
Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin B1 (thiamine) is required for pyruvate dehydrogenase and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, which are necessary for aerobic respiration.  A vitamin B1 deficiency mainly affects tissues that depend on aerobic respiration, such as the heart (and the brain).  Heart failure leads to pulmonary hypertension and then pulmonary edema, which causes shortness of breath.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 22: Citric Acid Cycle and Thiamine Deficiency

Wiley KD, Gupta M. Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Deficiency. In: StatPearls. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; July 17, 2023.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30725889/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin B1 is essential for the heart and other tissues that need a constant supply of energy to keep working. Without it, the heart becomes weak and can't pump blood effectively. This causes pressure to build up in the blood vessels of the lungs, leading to fluid leaking into the lung tissue. Fluid-filled lungs make it very hard to breathe, causing shortness of breath.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults, particularly those with poor nutrition or alcohol dependence

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Lysosomes and Peroxisomes
Protein Synthesis, Trafficking and Posttranslational Modifications
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: One of the characteristics of Chediak-Higashi syndrome is oculocutaneous albinism, which causes abnormally light pigmentation of the skin.  Chediak-Higashi syndrome is caused by pathogenic variants in the Lysosomal trafficking Regulator (LYST) gene (OMIM) that results in improper trafficking of proteins, including granules that transport melanin.  Melanin becomes trapped in large melanosomes and is unable to contribute to skin pigmentation, leading to the abnormally light pigmentation of the skin.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry. 9e, 2025. Ch 20: Amino Acids: Degradation and Synthesis

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 35: Protein Degradation, Amino Acid Metabolism, and Nitrogen Balance

MedlinePlus [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Library of Medicine (US). Chediak-Higashi syndrome; [updated 2014 Jan 1; cited 2024 Sep 21].
https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/chediak-higashi-syndrome/
SPECIAL NOTES:
1. Rare autosomal-recessive disorder. About 200 cases reported worldwide.
2. Very serious immunological and neurological symptoms accompany the albinism
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Chédiak-Higashi syndrome, a faulty gene causes problems with how the body moves and delivers important packages (called granules) inside cells. These packages are supposed to carry melanin — the substance that gives your skin its color — to where it is needed. Because of this gene problem, the melanin gets stuck in oversized storage compartments and cannot do its job of coloring the skin. This is why people with Chédiak-Higashi syndrome have unusually light skin.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Insulin resistance in type 2 diabetes is often accompanied by acanthosis nigricans, a dermatological condition consisting of symmetrical hyperpigmentation and thickened skin with a rough, velvety texture.  Although the mechanism is not entirely understood, it is thought that the hyperinsulinemia associated with insulin resistance may increase the synthesis of insulin-like growth factor (IGF) in the liver. Activation of the IGF-1 receptor by increased circulating IGF may trigger dermal fibroblast and epidermal keratinocyte proliferation, resulting in acanthosis nigricans.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry. 9e, 2025. Ch 25 Diabetes Mellitus

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 39: Diabetes

Brady MF, Rawla P. Acanthosis Nigricans. [Updated 2023 Aug 11]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. 
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK431057/
SPECIAL NOTES: Other potential causes of acanthosis nigricans include: familial (mutations in fibroblast growth factor receptor 3); medications; obesity; malignant (gastrointestinal adenocarcinomas and genitourinary cancers such as prostate, breast, and ovary); and autoimmune.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In type 2 diabetes, the body stops responding well to insulin, so the body makes more and more of it. All that extra insulin signals the liver to make another growth-related substance. When that substance builds up in the blood, it can cause skin cells to grow and multiply too fast in certain areas, leading to patches of skin that become darker, thicker, and have a rough, velvety feel.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

PRESENTATION DEFINITION: Hormones: Structure, Synthesis and Function
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: In Addison disease, the adrenal glands don't make enough of the hormones cortisol and aldosterone. Adrenal insufficiency could be caused due to multiple primary, such as autoimmune conditions, infections, hemorrhages, etc. In autoimmune conditions, antibodies are often made against enzymes, such as 21-hydroxylase and 17-hydroxylase, which are key enzymes in the synthesis pathway from cholesterol to cortisol. Decreased cortisol and aldosterone will eventually result in increased adrenocorticotropic (ACTH) and melanocyte-stimulating hormone (MSH) due to the loss of negative feedback inhibition.  When stimulated, the melanocyte changes the color of pigment to a dark brown or black.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry. 9e, 2025. Ch 18 Cholesterol, Lipoprotein and Steroid Metabolism

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 31: Steroid Hormones and Vitamin D

Munir S, Quintanilla Rodriguez BS, Waseem M. Addison Disease. [Updated 2024 Jan 30]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-.
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK441994/
SPECIAL NOTES:
1. Addison disease is rare with the total number of people impacted ranging from 4 to 11 per 100,000 of the population. The common age of presentation is 30 to 50 years.

2. Hyperpigmentation is typically but most often prominent on the sun-exposed areas of the skin, extensor surfaces. Hyperpigmentation, however, may not be present in every long-standing case or in cases of short duration.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Addison disease, the adrenal glands do not make enough of certain important hormones, including cortisol. Normally, cortisol acts like a "stop sign" that tells the body to slow down the production of other hormones. Without enough cortisol, that stop sign disappears, and levels of another hormone called MSH rise. MSH tells the skin's color-making cells to produce more dark pigment, which is why people with Addison disease can develop darker patches of skin, especially in areas exposed to the sun.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Genetic Inheritance
Iron Metabolism
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hereditary hemochromatosis is most often caused by homozygosity for the C282Y mutation of HFE gene (High Fe gene = Human Homeostatic Iron Regulator Protein; OMIM), which fails to stimulate hepcidin secretion.  Without hepcidin, ferroportin survives and moves an excessive amount of absorbed iron from the intestine to the extracellular space.  Accumulation of hemosiderin gives skin a bronze appearance. Additionally, Iron excess can cause hypermelanosis (increased activation of melanocytes), but the mechanism is not fully understood.  A study in mice suggests brownish discoloration of the skin in hemochromatosis may be a result of hemosiderin accumulation activating melanocytes.

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/6155014/
SPECIAL NOTES:
1. Type 1 hemochromatosis, HFE deficiency, is one of the most common genetic disorders in the United States and is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.  It affects about 1 million people. It most often affects people of Northern European descent.

2. The skin discoloration associated with hemochromatosis often precedes liver disease.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In hereditary hemochromatosis, a faulty gene causes the body to absorb too much iron from food. Normally, a protein called hepcidin acts like a gatekeeper to control how much iron gets into the bloodstream. Without it working properly, too much iron builds up in the body. This extra iron collects in the skin, giving it a bronze color. The buildup of iron may also wake up the skin's color-making cells, causing them to produce more dark pigment, though exactly how this happens is not yet fully understood.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adult

Vitamins and Minerals
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: ATP7A (OMIM) is a transmembrane protein and an active copper (Cu) transporter predominantly expressed in enterocytes, the placenta, and the central nervous system.  ATP7A directs Cu to Cu-dependent enzymes.  The inability of pathogenic variants in the ATP7A gene to load Cu in Cu-dependent enzymes leads to many of the clinical features of Menkes disease.  One of these Cu-dependent enzymes is Tyrosinase (TYR, OMIM).  A deficiency in TYR can lead to a defect in the biosynthesis of melanin from tyrosine and decreases in melanin resulting in decreased pigmentation of the skin.
RESOURCES:
Gruewald, J. M., & Paller, A. S. (2018). Melanocyte biology. In J. L. Bolognia, J. L. Jorizzo, & J. V. Schaffer (Eds.), Dermatology (4th ed., pp. Ch 65). Elsevier Limited.

Ramani PK, Parayil Sankaran B. Menkes Disease. [Updated 2023 Nov 14]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-.
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560917/
SPECIAL NOTES:
1.  Menkes syndrome is an x-linked recessive condition estimated to occur in 1 in 100,000 newborns.

2.  Although hypopigmentation is often observed in Menkes syndrome, the features that cause most children with Menkes syndrome to die before the age of 3 are failure to thrive, deterioration of the nervous system, hypotonia and seizures.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Menkes syndrome, a faulty gene prevents the body from properly moving copper to the enzymes that need it. One of those enzymes requires copper to help make melanin — the pigment that gives skin its color. Without enough copper reaching this enzyme, the body cannot make enough melanin, which leads to lighter than normal skin color.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

Signal Transduction
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Neurofibromin is encoded by the NF1 gene (OMIM) and functions as a Ras-GTPase-activating protein (RasGAP).  Pathogenic variants in NF1 lead to overactivation of the Ras signalling pathway. Neurofibromin regulates melanin synthesis and keratinocyte differentiation through a currently unknown mechanism. Mis-regulation of these pathways in Neurofibromatosis 1 leads to hyperpigmentation in spots that are referred to as "café-au-lait spots"
RESOURCES:
Medical Genetics (Jorde, Carey and Bamshad), 6th ed., Chapter 11 Cancer Genetics

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 33: Signaling

Peltonen S, Kallionpää RA, Peltonen J. Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) gene: Beyond café au lait spots and dermal neurofibromas. Exp Dermatol. 2017;26(7):645-648.
https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/exd.13212
SPECIAL NOTES:
1. Neurofibromatosis 1 occurs in 1 in 2000 births
2. Neurofibromatosis 1 patients, versus the general population, have a 5X increased risk of cancer and a 2000-fold increased risk for neurogenic malignancies
3. Mutations in the NF1 genes are found in many tumor types in patients that do not have Neurofibromatosis 1
4. Condition inherited in an autosomal dominant manner
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In neurofibromatosis 1, a faulty gene causes an important "off switch" inside cells to stop working. This switch normally helps control a signaling pathway that, when left unchecked, becomes overactive. This overactive signaling affects how the skin's color-making cells produce pigment and how certain skin cells grow, though the exact steps are not yet fully known. The result is patches of darker skin called "café-au-lait spots," which look like the color of coffee with milk.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome is a lysosome-related organelle (LRO) biogenesis dysfunction caused by pathogenic variants in one of ~11 HPS genes (OMIM list).  One of the characteristics of Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome is oculocutaneous albinism, which causes abnormally light pigmentation of the skin.  Melanosomes are an LRO and Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome leads to melanin becoming trapped in dysfunctional melanosomes, such that it is unable to contribute to skin pigmentation.
RESOURCES:
MedlinePlus [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Library of Medicine (US). Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome; [updated 2014 May 1; cited 2024 Sep 21].
https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/hermansky-pudlak-syndrome/

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 35: Protein Degradation, Amino Acid Metabolism, and Nitrogen Balance
SPECIAL NOTES:
1. Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome is a rare autosomal recessive disorder is thought to affect 1 in 500,000 to 1,000,000 individuals worldwide.
2. Type 1 is more common in northwestern Puerto Rico, where approximately 1 in 1,800 people are affected.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Hermansky-Pudlak syndrome, faulty genes cause problems with how certain storage compartments inside cells are built and work. Melanin — the pigment that gives skin its color — is supposed to be packaged and delivered by one of these compartments, called a melanosome. Because the melanosomes do not work properly, the melanin gets trapped inside and cannot color the skin the way it should, leading to unusually light skin.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Pathogenic variants in a number of genes involved in the biosynthesis of melanin from tyrosine can lead to decreases in melanin and decreased pigmentation of the skin.  Genes implicated include tyrosinase (TYR, OMIM), OCA2 melanosomal transmembrane protein (OMIM), tyrosinase related protein 1(TYRP1, OMIM), and solute carrier family 45, member 2 (SLC45A2, OMIM). Variants in the TYR gene cause type 1; variants in the OCA2 gene cause type 2; variants in the TYRP1 gene cause type 3; and variants in the SLC45A2 gene cause type 4.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry. 9e, 2025. Ch 20: Amino Acids: Degradation and Synthesis

MedlinePlus [Internet]. Bethesda (MD): National Library of Medicine (US). Oculocutaneous albinism; [updated 2023 Mar 17; cited 2024 Sep 21].
https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/oculocutaneous-albinism/
SPECIAL NOTES:
1. In total, approximately 1 in 20,000 people worldwide are affected with oculocutaneous albinism, which is inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.
2. The different types are more frequent in certain ancestries.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In oculocutaneous albinism, faulty genes disrupt the process the body uses to make melanin — the pigment responsible for skin color. The body normally uses a series of proteins to turn a building block called tyrosine into melanin. When any one of these proteins is not working correctly because of a gene change, the body cannot make enough melanin, which leads to very light skin color. Different gene changes cause different types of this condition.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Classical PKU is an autosomal recessive condition that results from a deficiency in the catabolism of phenylalanine due to pathogenic variants in the phenylalanine hydroxylase gene (OMIM).  Without proper management (restriction of phenylalanine, and supplementation with tyrosine), an inability of the defective phenylalanine hydroxylase to synthesize tyrosine from phenylalanine results in poor melanin production since tyrosine is the starting substrate for the pathway that makes melanin.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry. 9e, 2025. Ch20 Amino Acids: Degradation and Synthesis

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 35: Protein Degradation, Amino Acid Metabolism, and Nitrogen Balance

https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/condition/phenylketonuria/
SPECIAL NOTES:
1. PKU occurs in 1 in 25,000 U.S. newborns.
2. PKU is usually detected by newborn screening, and treatment is started promptly. Hence, many of the symptoms of classic PKU are rarely seen.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In classical PKU, a faulty gene means the body cannot properly break down a substance called phenylalanine, which comes from protein in food. Normally, this process makes another substance called tyrosine, which the body then uses as the starting point to make melanin — the pigment that colors the skin. Without the enzyme working properly, not enough tyrosine is made, so the body cannot produce enough melanin, leading to lighter skin color.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Abetalipoproteinemia (Bassen-Kornzweig syndrome) is a rare autosomal recessive defect in the Microsomal TAG-transfer protein (MTTP/MTP; OMIM 200100) that leads to the absence of Apo-B containing lipoproteins. This deficiency affects the absorption of dietary lipids and the malabsorption of fat soluble vitamins (A,D,E,K). One of the presenting features of abetalipoproteinemia is retinitis pigmentosa, which could result from deficiency of Vitamin A and/or Vitamin E. For the mechanistic connection between vitamin A/ E deficiency and vision loss, see below  for "Vision Loss - Vitamin A (retinoids) deficiency and Vision Loss- Vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol)."
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 28, Triglycerides and Hypertriglyceridemia

Edwards G, Olson CG, Euritt CP, Koulen P. Molecular Mechanisms Underlying the Therapeutic Role of Vitamin E in Age-Related Macular Degeneration. Front Neurosci. 2022;16:890021. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35600628/

Nagappa M, Bindu PS, Adwani S, et al. Clinical, hematological, and imaging observations in a 25-year-old woman with abetalipoproteinemia. Ann Indian Acad Neurol. 2014;17(1):113-116. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24753676/

Junaid SZS, Patel K. Abetalipoproteinemia. [Updated 2023 Jul 31]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513355/

Abetalipoproteinemia. National Organization for Rare Disorders. Accessed November 24, 2025.
https://rarediseases.org/rare-diseases/abetalipoproteinemia/
SPECIAL NOTES: Abetalipoproteinemia is a rare autosomal recessive genetic disorder that is estimated to affect less than 1 in 1,000,000 people in the general population.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body needs a special protein to help move fats from your digestive system into your blood. In abetalipoproteinemia, this protein does not work properly, so your body cannot absorb certain vitamins that travel with fat — specifically vitamins A and E. Without enough of these vitamins, the light-sensing cells in the back of your eye (the retina) can become damaged over time, leading to a condition called retinitis pigmentosa and eventual vision loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric. This rare genetic disorder typically presents in infancy and early childhood.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Marfan syndrome is a connective tissue disorder caused by mutations in the fibrillin (FBN1) gene (OMIM 154700). It follows autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. The resultant deformed connective tissue in zonular fibers of the suspensory ligament of the lens leads to subluxation of the lens. This defect along with secondary complications such as glaucoma and retinal detachment can result in vision loss in patients with Marfan syndrome. Additionally, changes in the shape of the eyeball could result in astigmatism or flat eye. These changes can further affect the acuity of vision.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 13, Pathologic Alterations of the Extracellular Matrix that Involve Fibrillin, Elastin, or Proteoglycans

Akram H, Aragon-Martin JA, Chandra A. Marfan syndrome and the eye clinic: from diagnosis to management. Ther Adv Rare Dis. 2021;2:26330040211055738. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37181104/

Esfandiari H, Ansari S, Mohammad-Rabei H, Mets MB. Management Strategies of Ocular Abnormalities in Patients with Marfan Syndrome: Current Perspective. J Ophthalmic Vis Res. 2019;14(1):71-77. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30820290/

Kjeldsen S, Andersen N, Groth K, et al. Ocular morbidity in Marfan syndrome: a nationwide epidemiological study. Br J Ophthalmol. 2023;107(8):1051-1055. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35318224/
SPECIAL NOTES: Occular manifestations are common in Marfan sundrome. In a natonwide study, among individuals with Marfan Syndrome, 56% had atleast one documentation of a diagnosis realted to ophthalmology.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Marfan syndrome, a mutation in a gene causes the body to make faulty connective tissue — the material that holds structures in the body together. In the eye, tiny fibers that hold the lens in place become weak and stretchy. This can cause the lens to slip out of position (a condition called lens subluxation), which blurs vision. The shape of the eyeball may also change, and there is a higher risk of the retina detaching from the back of the eye or fluid pressure building up in the eye (glaucoma), all of which can lead to vision loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both pediatric and adult patients are affected, as Marfan syndrome is a lifelong condition diagnosed across all ages.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Diabetic Eye Disease includes diabetic retinopathy, cataracts, and glaucoma, which can lead to vision loss over time. Hyperglycemia induces the accumulation of advanced glycation end products (AGEs), which lead to the pathogenesis of diabetic eye disease. AGE accumulation in the microvasculature of the retina can result in retinopathy. Similarly, nonenzymatic glycation of lens proteins can result in cataracts. Production of sorbitol through the polyol pathway leads to osmotic stress, which can also contribute to retinopathy as well as cataracts. Additionally, inflammation and oxidative stress can also result in the pathogenesis of diabetic eye disease.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 39, Diabetes

Wang W, Lo ACY. Diabetic Retinopathy: Pathophysiology and Treatments. Int J Mol Sci. 2018 Jun 20;19(6):1816. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29925789/

Pollreisz A, Schmidt-Erfurth U. Diabetic cataract-pathogenesis, epidemiology and treatment. J Ophthalmol. 2010;2010:608751. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20634936/

Lundeen EA, Burke-Conte Z, Rein DB, et al. Prevalence of Diabetic Retinopathy in the US in 2021. JAMA Ophthalmol. 2023;141(8):747-754. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10273133/
SPECIAL NOTES: U.S. prevalence of diabetic retinopathy (DR) is estimated at 9.6M people (26.4% of those with diabetes). Prevalence of those with vision-threatening DR is 1.84M people (5.1% of those with diabetes).
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When blood sugar levels stay too high for too long, sugar molecules stick to proteins and build up in the tiny blood vessels of the eye, damaging them. This damage to blood vessels in the retina (the back of the eye) is called diabetic retinopathy and can cause vision loss. High blood sugar can also cause the lens of the eye to become cloudy (a cataract) or increase pressure inside the eye (glaucoma), both of which can also harm vision.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, though adolescents with long-standing diabetes may also be affected.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Galactosemia arising due to galactokinase deficiency (GALK; OMIM 230200) or galactose 1 phosphate uridyltransferase (GALT; OMIM 230400) prevents proper metabolism of galactose, which is present in milk. This leads to galactitol accumulation. In the lens, this can result in osmotic changes leading to vision loss due to cataract.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 20, Fructose and Galactose Metabolism

Chhapan RJ, Yerramneni R, Ramappa M. Diagnosing the oil drop: A case report and review of the literature. Indian J Ophthalmol. 2019;67(10):1705-1706. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31546521/
SPECIAL NOTES: Cataract is one of the most common features of Galactosemia.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In galactosemia, the body cannot properly break down galactose, a sugar found in milk. Because of a missing or faulty enzyme, galactose builds up and gets converted into a substance called galactitol. Galactitol accumulates in the lens of the eye and draws in water, causing the lens to swell and become cloudy. This clouding of the lens is called a cataract and leads to vision loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric. Symptoms typically appear in newborns and infants when they begin consuming milk.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hereditary fructose intolerance, an autosomal recessive condition, arising due to aldolase B deficiency (OMIM 229600), which prevents proper metabolism of fructose. This can lead to sorbitol accumulation, which can result in osmotic changes in the lens leading to cataract thus causing vision loss.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 20, Fructose and Galactose Metabolism

Ananth N, Praveenkumar GS, Rao KA, Vasanthi, Kakkilaya S. Two cases of hereditary fructose intolerance. Indian J Clin Biochem. 2003;18(2):87-92. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23105397/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In hereditary fructose intolerance, the body cannot properly break down fructose (a sugar found in many fruits and sweeteners) because of a faulty enzyme. This causes a buildup of a related substance called sorbitol in the lens of the eye. Sorbitol draws water into the lens, causing it to swell and become cloudy — forming a cataract — which leads to vision loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric. Symptoms typically appear when fructose-containing foods are introduced into the infant's diet.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Classic homocystinuria, an autosomal recessive condition, is caused due to cystathionine beta-synthase (CBS) deficiency (OMIM 236200) and is associated with a multi-system disorder involving mostly four major organ systems: eye, skeletal, central nervous system, and vascular system. Accumulation of homocysteine interferes with disulfide bond formation within connective fibers such as collagen and elastin, leading to connective tissue defects and Marfanoid features. This includes subluxation of the lens, which can result in vision loss. Additionally, retinopathy, macular degeneration, cataracts, and glaucomas could also be observed in patients with homocystinuria, which could arise due to connective tissue defects in the vasculature, oxidative stress, and inflammatory response.
RESOURCES:
Swanson TA, Kim SI, Glucksman MJ. Underground Clinical Vignettes: Biochemistry. 5th ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2008.

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 36, One-Carbon Metablism, Folate Deficiency, and Cobalamin Deficiency

Ajith TA, Ranimenon. Homocysteine in ocular diseases. Clin Chim Acta. 2015;450:316-321. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26343924/
SPECIAL NOTES: Homocystineuria can also arise due to methionine synthase deficiency.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In homocystinuria, a faulty enzyme causes a substance called homocysteine to build up in the body. High levels of homocysteine weaken the connective tissue fibers that hold structures together throughout the body, including the tiny fibers that keep the lens of the eye in place. When these fibers weaken, the lens can slip out of position (lens subluxation), causing blurry or lost vision. Damage to blood vessels and other parts of the eye from homocysteine buildup can also lead to conditions like cataracts, glaucoma, and retinal damage, all of which may further affect vision.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both pediatric and adult patients are affected, though initial diagnosis often occurs in childhood.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Sphingolipidoses are lysosomal storage disorders characterized by accumulation of sphingolipids (GM2 ganglioside accumulation due to hexosaminidase A deficiency in Tay Sachs disease (OMIM 272800) or hexosaminidase B in Sandhoff disease (OMIM 268800)), and sphingomyelin accumulation (due to sphingomyelinase deficiency in Niemann pick diseases type A & B (OMIM 257200 & OMIM 607616)). Excessive accumulation of sphingolipids in retinal ganglionic cells leads to cell death and gliosis giving rise to the appearance of cherry-red spot in the retina (fovea of the retina spared). Other retinal cells and optic nerve neurons may also be affected. Together these can eventually lead to vision loss.
RESOURCES:
Tripathy K, Patel BC. Cherry Red Spot. [Updated 2023 Aug 25]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539841/

Chen H, Chan AY, Stone DU, Mandal NA. Beyond the cherry-red spot: Ocular manifestations of sphingolipid-mediated neurodegenerative and inflammatory disorders. Surv Ophthalmol. 2014;59(1):64-76. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24011710/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Niemann-Pick Type A, a missing enzyme causes a fatty substance called sphingomyelin to build up inside the cells of the body, including the nerve cells in the retina (the back of the eye). As this fatty material accumulates, it kills the retinal nerve cells, which causes a characteristic appearance called a "cherry-red spot" and leads to progressive vision loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric. Niemann-Pick Type A typically presents in infancy.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: LHON (OMIM 308905) is an inherited mitochondrial disorder, which results from mutations in mitochondrial genes that mainly affect the functioning of complex 1 of the electron transport chain. This leads to degeneration of ganglionic cells of retina. The resultant neuropathy of the optic nerve results in vision loss.
RESOURCES:
Shemesh A, Sood G, Blair K, et al. Leber Hereditary Optic Neuropathy (LHON) [Updated 2024 Mar 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482499/    

Emery's Elements of Medical Genetics and Genomics, 16th ed. 2022, Ch. 18: Inborn Errors of Metabolism.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: LHON is caused by mutations in the DNA found in mitochondria — the parts of cells that make energy. These mutations mainly interfere with how cells in the retina (the back of the eye) and the optic nerve produce energy. Without enough energy, these cells degenerate and die, causing the optic nerve to stop working properly and leading to vision loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily affects young adults, with vision loss most commonly occurring in the teens through thirties. Males are more frequently affected.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Mucopolysaccharidosis, such as Hurler syndrome (OMIM 607014), a lysosomal storage disorder following autosomal recessive inheritance pattern, is characterized by the accumulation of glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) due to the deficiency of enzyme alpha-L-iduronidase, which is required for the metabolism of GAGs. Corneal clouding is a common finding and may arise due to GAGs (mainly heparan sulfate) accumulation in the cornea and disruption of collagen fibers of the corneal stroma. Additionally, Hurler syndrome may also be associated with retinopathy and optic neuropathy. All these pathologies could lead to vision loss.
RESOURCES: Fenzl CR, Teramoto K, Moshirfar M. Ocular manifestations and management recommendations of lysosomal storage disorders I: mucopolysaccharidoses. Clin Ophthalmol. 2015;9:1633-1644. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26379420/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Hurler syndrome, a missing enzyme causes complex sugar-like molecules called glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) to build up in cells throughout the body. In the eye, these molecules accumulate in the clear front surface of the eye (the cornea) and disrupt its normal structure, causing it to become cloudy. This cloudiness blocks light from entering the eye clearly and can lead to vision loss. The buildup can also damage the retina and optic nerve, further contributing to vision problems.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric. Hurler syndrome typically presents in infancy and early childhood.

PRESENTATION DEFINITION:
Cell Cycle
Genetic Inheritance
MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Most cases of retinoblastoma arise due to loss of function mutations in the RB1 gene, a tumor suppressor gene (OMIM 180200). Mutations in this gene result in loss of cell cycle regulation (G1/S transition check point), which leads to uncontrolled cell division and malignancies. Retinoblastoma, a malignancy involving the retina leads to vision loss. On physical examination, this may present as leukocoria (white pupillary reflex). In hereditary cases, children may have bilateral or multifocal presentation of retinoblastoma. These individuals are also susceptible to other malignancies including bone and soft tissue tumors.
RESOURCES:
Emery's Elements of Medical Genetics and Genomics. 16th ed. 2022, Ch. 14: The Genetics of Cancer... and Cancer Genetics

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Retinoblastoma is a cancer of the retina — the light-sensing tissue at the back of the eye — that occurs when a gene that normally controls how cells grow and divide stops working. Without this control, cells in the retina divide uncontrollably and form a tumor. This tumor can damage the retina and other parts of the eye, leading to vision loss. One early sign is a white glow seen in the pupil (called leukocoria) instead of the normal red-eye appearance.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric. Retinoblastoma is primarily a childhood cancer, most often diagnosed in children under 5 years of age.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Accidental consumption or inhalation of methanol can have serious consequences such as permanent, irreversible vision loss. Methanol is metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase into formaldehyde, which is further metabolized by aldehyde dehydrogenase into formic acid. Accumulation of these toxic metabolites, especially formic acid, can lead to optic neuropathy and retinal damage. Formic acid inhibits cytochrome oxidase and thus affects mitochondrial functioning leading to optic neuritis. There is also resultant accumulation of lactic acid leading to metabolic acidosis, which further contributes to the damage. There can be impairment of other structures of the eye such as optic chiasma.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 36, One-Carbon Metabolism, Folate Deficiency, and Cobalamin Deficiency

Figuerola B, Mendoza A, Roca M, Lacorzana J. Severe visual loss by inhalation of methanol. Rom J Ophthalmol. 2021;65(2):176-179. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34179584/

Liberski S, Kaluzny BJ, Kocięcki J. Methanol-induced optic neuropathy: a still-present problem. Arch Toxicol. 2022;96(2):431-451. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34988610/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When methanol (a toxic type of alcohol) is accidentally swallowed or inhaled, the body breaks it down into harmful substances, including formic acid. Formic acid interferes with how cells in the eye — especially those in the retina and optic nerve — produce energy, causing them to stop working and die. This damage to the optic nerve and retina can result in permanent vision loss. The buildup of toxic acids in the blood also adds to this damage.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Primarily adults, though accidental exposure can occur at any age.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin A deficiency could arise due to multiple acquired and hereditary causes. Vitamin A deficiency could lead to various ocular manifestations, such as night blindness, xerophthalmia, Bitot spots, keratomalacia, corneal ulceration, and perforation. Retinal form of vitamin A is part of the retinal pigment rhodopsin, the major component of the visual cycle. The visual cycle involves recycling between 11-cis-retinal and all-trans-retinal. A deficiency of vitamin A affects the visual cycle. This gives rise to the loss of dark adaptation or night blindness. The retinoic acid form of vitamin A regulates transcription through RARE (retinoic acid response element), which is required for the growth and maintenance of epithelial tissue. Lack of transcriptional regulation results in squamous metaplasia of the epithelium of skin, hair, and eyes. Epithelial changes can affect goblet cells in the eyes, as well as can lead to blockage of lacrimal ducts, which can result in xerophthalmia or dry eyes. Corneal xerosis may lead to impairment of vision. Severe epithelial alterations can also lead to keratomalacia and corneal ulceration, which could result in loss of vision.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Review: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025, Ch. 28: Micronutrients: Vitamins

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 28: Triglycerides and Hypertriglyceridemia

Gilbert C. The eye signs of vitamin A deficiency. Community Eye Health. 2013;26(84):66-67. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24782581/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin A plays two important roles in eye health. First, a form of vitamin A is part of a pigment in the eye called rhodopsin, which is needed to see in low-light conditions. Without enough vitamin A, this pigment cannot work properly, making it hard to see in the dark — a condition called night blindness. Second, another form of vitamin A helps control the growth and health of the cells that line the surface of the eye. Without it, these cells can break down, leading to dry eyes (xerophthalmia), corneal damage, and in severe cases, ulceration and permanent vision loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both pediatric and adult patients are affected. Severe deficiency leading to vision loss is more common in children in low-resource settings.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Vitamin E (alpha-tocopherol) is a fat soluble vitamin that acts as an antioxidant by scavenging lipid peroxyl radicals in the membrane and preventing their downstream effects. Vitamin E is found in high concentrations in the retinal tissue. Nutritional deficiency of Vitamin E or hereditary conditions, such as abetalipoproteinemia or cystic fibrosis which lead to absorption disorders, are associated with Vitamin E deficiency and often present with vision loss. Lack of protective function of Vitamin E leads to oxidative stress in the photoreceptors of the retina causing retinal damage. This could result in conditions like macular degeneration and retinitis pigmentosa.
RESOURCES:
Edwards G, Olson CG, Euritt CP, Koulen P. Molecular Mechanisms Underlying the Therapeutic Role of Vitamin E in Age-Related Macular Degeneration. Front Neurosci. 2022;16:890021. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35600628/ 

Latib F, Zafendi MAI, Mohd Lazaldin MA. The use of vitamin E in ocular health: Bridging omics approaches with Tocopherol and Tocotrienol in the management of glaucoma. Food Chem (Oxf). 2024;9:100224. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39415777/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Vitamin E acts as a protector in the body by neutralizing harmful molecules called free radicals that can damage cells. The retina — the light-sensing tissue at the back of the eye — contains high amounts of vitamin E because it is especially vulnerable to this kind of damage. When vitamin E levels are too low (due to poor diet or conditions that prevent fat absorption), the retina loses this protection and becomes damaged by oxidative stress. Over time, this can lead to conditions like retinitis pigmentosa and macular degeneration, causing vision loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Both pediatric and adult patients are affected depending on the underlying cause of deficiency.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Exogenous cortisol intake or misregulation of the HPA axis can result in excess cortisol resulting in Cushing Syndrome. Elevated cortisol can lead to increasing appetite and weight gain by the following mechanisms.
1. Cortisol induces induces Neuropeptide Y (NPY). NPY leads to reduced sensitivity of brain cells to leptin, and thereby increasing appetite leading to weight gain/obesity.
2. Increased cortisol promotes proteolysis in muscle to support gluconeogenesis resulting in hyperglycemia. The increased glucose leads to increased insulin promoting fatty acid synthesis and lipogenesis. Cortisol also promotes lipolysis in peripheral adipose stores causing a redistribution of fat and central obesity.
3. Cortisol also has mineralocorticoid activity causing sodium and water retention resulting in weight gain.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 31, Steroid Hormones and Vitamin D

Macfarlane DP, Forbes S, Walker BR. Glucocorticoids and fatty acid metabolism in humans: fuelling fat redistribution in the metabolic syndrome. J Endocrinol. 2008;197(2):189-204. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18434349/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your body makes a hormone called cortisol to help handle stress. In Cushing syndrome, there is too much cortisol in the body — either from taking steroid medicines or because the body is making too much on its own. This extra cortisol causes weight gain in three ways. First, it increases a brain chemical called NPY, which makes your brain ignore the signal that tells you you're full, so you feel hungrier and eat more. Second, extra cortisol breaks down muscle to raise your blood sugar, which then causes your body to make more fat and store it — especially around your belly. Third, cortisol acts like another hormone that makes your body hold onto salt and water, which also adds to your weight.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hypothyroidism generally develops due to several causes, such as autoimmune diseases or iodine deficiency in the diet. Low thyroid hormone levels can result in weight gain through two different mechanisms. One is through reduced energy expenditure by decreasing BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate), reducing lipolysis, gluconeogenesis, and mitochondrial biogenesis. Decreased expression of mitochondrial Uncoupling Protein 1 (UCP-1) decreases energy use, all of which together lead to weight gain and obesity. The other mechanism is increased water retention due to deposition of mucopolysaccharides in skin and subcutaneous tissue, which results in non-pitting edema.
RESOURCES:
Yau WW, Yen PM. Thermogenesis in Adipose Tissue Activated by Thyroid Hormone. Int J Mol Sci. 2020 Apr 24;21(8):3020. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32344721/

Marks' Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e. 2023, Ch. 41: Actions of Hormones That Regulate Fuel Metabolism

Sanyal D, Raychaudhuri M. Hypothyroidism and obesity: An intriguing link. Indian J Endocrinol Metab. 2016;20(4):554-557. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27366725/ 

Wilson SA, Stem LA, Bruehlman RD. Hypothyroidism: Diagnosis and Treatment. Am Fam Physician. 2021;103(10):605-613. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33983002/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Your thyroid gland makes hormones that help control how fast your body uses energy. When the thyroid is not working well — due to reasons like an immune system problem or not getting enough iodine in your diet — it makes too little thyroid hormone. This causes weight gain in two ways. First, your body slows down: it burns less energy, breaks down less fat, and your cells produce less energy overall, so the calories you eat are more likely to be stored as fat. Second, a substance builds up in the skin and tissue under your skin that traps water, causing a kind of puffiness or swelling that also adds to your weight.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Leptin deficiency or resistance disrupts the brain's appetite regulation, causing reduced metabolic rate and increased hunger signals that promote overeating. This hormonal dysfunction leads to a cycle where the brain fails to recognize adequate energy stores, resulting in continued food intake and progressive weight gain. Consequently, individuals with leptin pathway abnormalities experience difficulty maintaining a healthy weight due to impaired satiety signals and metabolic inefficiency. Rarely, leptin deficiency is caused by inherited mutations in the leptin gene (OMIM), but most cases are caused by obesity-related leptin resistance.
RESOURCES:
Obradovic M, Sudar-Milovanovic E, Soskic S, et al. Leptin and Obesity: Role and Clinical Implication. Front Endocrinol (Lausanne). 2021;12:585887. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34084149/

Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025 Ch. 26: Obesity.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Leptin is a hormone that tells your brain when you have eaten enough and have enough energy stored in your body. When leptin is missing or your body stops responding to it properly, your brain never gets the message that you are full. This makes you feel hungry even when you have eaten plenty, so you keep eating more than your body needs. At the same time, your body's metabolism slows down, meaning it burns fewer calories. Together, these two problems — feeling constantly hungry and burning less energy — cause your body to store more and more fat over time, making it very hard to keep a healthy weight. In rare cases, the body cannot make leptin because of an inherited gene change, but most of the time leptin stops working properly because of obesity itself.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults and Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Excess caloric intake (relative to caloric expenditure), especially from carbohydrates, increases the synthesis and storage of triacylglycerols in adipose and eventually other tissues leading to weight gain/obesity.
RESOURCES: Marks' Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e. 2023, Ch. 1: Metabolic Fuels and Dietary Components

Over time, this storage of extra fat leads to weight gain and obesity.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults and Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Prader Willi Syndrome (OMIM) is caused by a genetic cluster of mutations on paternal copy of chromosome 15 or maternal disomy (SNRPN gene region). Clinical phenotype includes low muscle tone, poor feeding during early infancy, immature genitalia followed by a tremendous appetite between 2 and 6 years of age. Although, the causal mechanism for a potential weight gain/obesity is still under investigation, recent in vivo neuroimaging studies have shown that the hypothalmic dysfunction and its associated impairment in the regulation of energy intake can be linked to poor eating behaviors which are neurodevelopmental in nature.
RESOURCES:
Scheimann A. Prader-Willi Syndrome. Medscape. Published 2025 Apr 25. Accessed Nov 24, 2025.
https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/947954-overview
