Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Prader-Willi Syndrome is caused by changes in a specific region on chromosome 15 that a child inherits from their father, or by getting two copies of that chromosome from the mother instead of one from each parent. Babies with this condition are born with weak muscles and have trouble feeding at first. However, between the ages of 2 and 6, a major change happens — children develop an extremely strong urge to eat that is very difficult to control. This happens because a part of the brain called the hypothalamus, which normally helps control hunger and fullness, does not work properly. This brain-based problem with hunger regulation leads to overeating and significant weight gain.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION:
Stress induced cortisol release can lead to increasing appetite and weight gain by the following mechanisms:
1. Cortisol induces induces Neuropeptide Y (NPY). NPY leads to reduced sensitivity of brain cells to leptin, and thereby increasing appetite leading to weight gain/obesity.
2. Increased cortisol promotes proteolysis in muscle to support gluconeogenesis resulting in hyperglycemia. The increased glucose leads to increased insulin promoting fatty acid synthesis and lipogenesis. Cortisol also promotes lipolysis in peripheral adipose stores causing a redistribution of fat and central obesity.
3. Cortisol also has mineralocorticoid activity causing sodium and water retention resulting in weight gain.

Stress is also associated with high circulating levels of ghrelin that can lead to weight gain by the following mechanisms: 
1. Ghrelin interacts with ghrelin receptors (growth hormone secretagogue receptor, GHSR) found in catecholaminergic neurons in the brain. Activation of these neurons by ghrelin triggers changes that promote food-reward behavior and overeating (orexigenic effect), resulting in increased consumption of calorie-dense comfort foods and weight gain.
2. Ghrelin increases intracellular Ca2+ release, and thus promotes AMPK phosphorylation in NPY neurons thereby leading to increased appetite and weight gain.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When you are stressed, your body releases a hormone called cortisol. Too much cortisol causes weight gain in three ways: it increases a brain chemical called NPY that makes your brain ignore fullness signals so you feel hungrier; it breaks down muscle to raise blood sugar, which leads your body to make and store more fat, especially around the belly; and it makes your body hold onto salt and water, adding more weight. Stress also raises the levels of another hormone called ghrelin. Ghrelin causes weight gain in two ways: it activates certain brain cells in a way that makes food feel like a reward, which leads to overeating — especially high-calorie comfort foods; and it triggers a chain reaction inside cells that increases appetite even further.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is characterized by impairment in the ability to stop or control alcohol use despite adverse social, occupational, or health consequences. Chronic alcohol consumption has widespread effects on various organs and systems that may result in weight loss. Some of these include GI effects - alcohol impairs nutrient absorption and utilization due to gut irritation, resulting in deficiencies of essential vitamins and minerals. The metabolism of alcohol increases the body's energy expenditure, which can contribute to weight loss if caloric intake is insufficient. Alcohol can disrupt leptin signaling, which often results in decreased appetite and reduced food intake. Additionally, GI issues, such as nausea and vomiting, further diminish nutritional intake. Alcohol can also elevate corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) levels, which in turn can suppress appetite and contribute to weight loss.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 30: Metabolism of Ethanol and the Consequences of Alcohol Dependence Syndrome

Weinland C, Tanovska P, Kornhuber J, Mühle C, Lenz B. Serum lipids, leptin, and soluble leptin receptor in alcohol dependence: A cross-sectional and longitudinal study. Drug Alcohol Depend. 2020;209:107898.
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0376871620300636

Nehring SM, Chen RJ, Freeman AM. Alcohol Use Disorder. [Updated 2024 Mar 16]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK436003/


Thompson W. Alcoholism. Medscape. Published 2024 Jun 28. Accessed Nov 24, 2025. https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/285913
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When someone drinks alcohol heavily over a long period of time, it can cause weight loss in several ways. First, alcohol irritates the gut and makes it harder for the body to absorb important vitamins and minerals from food. Second, the body uses extra energy to break down alcohol, which can burn more calories than a person takes in. Third, alcohol can mess with a chemical in the body called leptin, which helps control hunger — when this signal doesn't work properly, a person may not feel hungry and eats less. On top of that, alcohol can cause nausea and vomiting, making it even harder to eat enough food. Finally, alcohol raises levels of a stress chemical in the body that can also reduce appetite. All of these things together can lead to significant weight loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Celiac disease is an autoimmune disorder triggered by an immune reaction to dietary gluten, a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye.  Proteolysis of gluten generates multiple peptides, including the glycopeptide gliadin. The posttranslational modification of gliadin by tissue transglutaminase (tTg) makes them both (gliadin and tTg) autoantigenic. The resulting inflammation and cytotoxicity damage the small intestine over time. This leads to malabsorption of macro- and micronutrients and causes weight loss.

SPECIAL NOTES: Prevalence in the worldwide population is 1%; risk increases in individuals with another autoimmune disorder
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Celiac disease is an autoimmune condition, meaning the body's immune system accidentally attacks itself. When someone with celiac disease eats foods that contain gluten — a protein found in wheat, barley, and rye — the body breaks gluten down into smaller pieces. One of those pieces gets changed by a protein in the intestine, which makes the immune system treat it as an enemy and attack. This attack causes damage and inflammation in the small intestine over time. Because the small intestine is damaged, the body can no longer properly absorb nutrients from food. Without enough nutrients being absorbed, the body loses weight.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults and Pediatric

In cholestatic liver disease, the liver either has trouble making bile or something is blocking bile from flowing properly through the tubes that carry it. Without enough bile reaching the intestines, the body cannot properly break down or absorb fats from food. Since fat is an important source of calories and energy, not being able to absorb it means the body takes in far fewer calories than it needs, which leads to weight loss.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Cystic Fibrosis (CF) is caused by mutations in a gene that encodes for a protein called transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR, OMIM: 602421 ), which functions as a cAMP-activated chloride channel transporter. Mutations in the gene can impair the normal production or functioning of the CFTR protein  The symptoms of CF are manifested as thickened secretions due to defective chloride transport. This leads to obstruction of the pancreatic ductules and defective secretion of pancreatic enzymes, resulting in dietary malabsorption, which in turn leads to weight loss.
RESOURCES:
Sharma, GD. Cystic Fibrosis. Medscape. Published 2024 Oct 3. Accessed Nov 24, 2025.
https://emedicine.medscape.com/article/1001602-overview

Marks' Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e. 2023, Ch. 16: Use of Recombinant DNA Techniques in Medicine.
SPECIAL NOTES: CF is the most common autosomal recessive disorder in the U.S. where it has an incidence of greater than 1 in 6000 births among whites from northern Europe ancestry.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a genetic condition caused by a change in a gene that controls a protein responsible for moving chloride (a component of salt) in and out of cells. When this protein doesn't work correctly, the body produces abnormally thick, sticky secretions — like mucus — in many organs. In the pancreas, these thick secretions block the small tubes that normally carry digestive enzymes into the intestines. Without these enzymes, the body cannot properly break down and absorb nutrients from food. This leads to malabsorption — meaning the body isn't getting what it needs from food — which causes weight loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Type I diabetes is characterized by the destruction of pancreatic beta cells and inability to produce insulin. There are three possible mechanisms of weight loss: 1) Impaired insulin signaling in diabetes prevents the uptake of glucose by muscle cells and adipocytes due to defective expression and/or translocation of GLUT 4 (insulin-sensitive glucose transporter) in the plasma membrane. Due to the imbalance in insulin/glucagon ratio and the inability of the body to utilize glucose as the energy source, there is a shift towards lipolysis and subsequent fatty acid oxidation leading to weight loss. 2) An imbalance in the insulin/glucagon ratio activates gluconeogenesis, leading to muscle atrophy and weight loss. 3) Accumulation of acetyl coA from increased fatty acid oxidation leads to increased ketone bodies. Elevated glucose and ketone bodies lead to osmotic diuresis leading to polyuria, glycosuria and dehydration.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025 Ch. 25: Diabetes Mellitus

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 39: Diabetes

Chiasson JL, Aris-Jilwan N, Bélanger R, et al. Diagnosis and treatment of diabetic ketoacidosis and the hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state. CMAJ. 2003;168(7):859-866.
https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC151994/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: In Type 1 diabetes, the body cannot make a hormone called insulin because the cells in the pancreas that produce it have been destroyed. Insulin normally helps the body's muscles and fat cells absorb sugar (glucose) from the blood to use as energy. Without insulin, cells can't take in glucose, so the body starts breaking down fat for energy instead — causing weight loss. At the same time, the body also begins breaking down muscle to make new sugar in the liver, leading to muscle loss and further weight loss. Additionally, the buildup of sugar and other substances in the blood causes the kidneys to flush out large amounts of fluid through urine, leading to dehydration, which also contributes to weight loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Orlistat is an anti obesity drug that inhibits gastric and pancreatic lipases, causing a decrease in fat absorption and resulting in weight loss.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Orlistat is a medication used to treat obesity. It works by blocking enzymes in the stomach and intestines called lipases, which are normally responsible for breaking down fat from food so it can be absorbed into the body. When these enzymes are blocked, the fat from food cannot be absorbed and instead passes out of the body. Because less fat is absorbed, the body takes in fewer calories, which leads to weight loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) agonists (aka incretin mimetics) are medications that help with weight loss and type 2 diabetes by mimicking the GLP-1 hormone. They bind to the GLP-1 and leptin receptors in the brain and induce a feeling of satiety. GLP- agonists also increase insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, slow gastric emptying, and reduce appetite. Collectively, these effects lead to decreased caloric intake, improved satiety, and weight loss.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: GLP-1 agonists are medications that mimic a natural hormone in the body called GLP-1. They work in several ways to cause weight loss. They attach to receptors in the brain that control hunger, making a person feel full and satisfied. They also cause the body to release more insulin (which lowers blood sugar), reduce the release of a hormone called glucagon (which raises blood sugar), and slow down how quickly food leaves the stomach. Together, these effects cause a person to feel full sooner, eat less food, and take in fewer calories, all of which lead to weight loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Weight loss in Addison disease refers to adrenal insufficiency that results in decreased cortisol and aldosterone levels. Adrenal insufficiency could be caused due to multiple causes - autoimmune conditions, infections, hemorrhages, etc. In autoimmune conditions, antibodies are often made against enzymes, such as 21-hydroxylase and 17-hydroxylase, which are key enzymes in the synthesis pathway from cholesterol to cortisol.  Reduced cortisol and aldosterone levels can lead to disruptions in metabolism, appetite regulation, electrolyte balance, and gastrointestinal function. These factors together contribute to the weight loss. Aldosterone is essential for sodium retention and potassium excretion. Its deficiency leads to hyponatremia and hyperkalemia. This imbalance can cause symptoms like fatigue, dizziness, and nausea, potentially reducing appetite and contributing to weight loss.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The adrenal glands sit on top of the kidneys and make important hormones, including cortisol and aldosterone. In Addison's disease, these glands don't make enough of these hormones. In many cases, this happens because the immune system mistakenly attacks certain proteins needed to make these hormones. Without enough cortisol, the body's ability to manage energy, regulate appetite, and control digestion is disrupted. Without enough aldosterone, the body cannot properly balance salt and water levels in the blood, leading to low sodium and high potassium. This imbalance causes symptoms like fatigue, dizziness, and nausea, which reduce appetite and make it hard to eat enough — all contributing to weight loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Hyperthyroidism, including Graves disease, results from excess thyroid hormone, increasing basal metabolic rate and causing weight loss. In Graves disease, autoantibodies (TSI) continuously stimulate the thyroid, driving hormone overproduction, metabolism acceleration, and gland growth.

SPECIAL NOTES: Symptoms mimic catecholamine excess, and adrenergic blockade can help manage them.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The thyroid gland makes hormones that control how fast or slow the body uses energy — this is called the metabolic rate. In hyperthyroidism, the thyroid makes too much thyroid hormone, which speeds up the body's metabolism significantly. The body ends up burning energy much faster than normal. In a condition called Graves' disease, the immune system makes antibodies that constantly tell the thyroid to keep making more and more hormone, which keeps the metabolism running too fast. Because the body is burning so much energy so quickly, a person loses weight even if their food intake stays the same.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Chronic infections such as TB and HIV can lead to significant weight loss due to ongoing inflammatory response and increased metabolic demands. The immune response to infection causes the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (e.g., TNF-α, IL-1, and IL-6), increases resting energy expenditure (REE), and promotes the breakdown of muscle tissue and fat stores. These cytokines also dysregulate appetite by affecting the hypothalamus, causing anorexia and reduced food intake. Infections can also result in hormonal changes, such as increased levels of cortisol and catecholamines, further contributing to the catabolism of muscle and fat stores.
RESOURCES:
Rook GA, Lowry CA. The hygiene hypothesis and psychiatric disorders. Trends Immunol. 2008;29(4):150-158.
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18328783/
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When the body is fighting a long-term infection like tuberculosis (TB) or HIV, the immune system releases proteins called cytokines that cause ongoing inflammation. These cytokines cause the body to burn more energy even while resting. They also affect the part of the brain that controls hunger, causing a person to lose their appetite and eat less. At the same time, infections cause the body to release stress hormones like cortisol and catecholamines, which further break down muscle and fat stores for energy. Together, eating less while the body burns more energy leads to significant weight loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults and Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Injury due to (e.g., general categories - surgery, trauma, burns) elicits a hormonal response, the net effect of which is an increased secretion of catabolic hormones, which stimulate the breakdown of carbohydrates, fats, and proteins. Cortisol and catecholamines facilitate hyperglycemia due to increased hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis. Concomitantly, there is a reduction in peripheral use of glucose due to decreased insulin signaling. Increased cortisol levels stimulate protein catabolism. Predominantly, skeletal muscle and some visceral muscle proteins are also catabolized to release the constituent amino acids, which may be used for energy or in the liver to form new proteins, particularly acute-phase proteins. The resultant protein catabolism results in marked weight loss and muscle wasting. Concurrent inflammatory response further contributes to the hypercatabolic state and weight loss.

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: When the body experiences a serious injury — such as surgery, trauma, or burns — it releases large amounts of stress hormones, including cortisol and catecholamines. These hormones cause the body to break down its own stored energy. The liver increases its production of sugar by breaking down stored carbohydrates and making new sugar from other sources. At the same time, the body breaks down muscle proteins for energy and to make important proteins needed for healing. Because so much muscle is being broken down, a person loses significant weight and muscle mass. The body's inflammatory response during healing adds to this increased energy-burning state and further contributes to weight loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults and Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Cachexia is a wasting syndrome characterized by weight loss, anorexia, asthenia and anemia. It is prevalent in patients with cancer  (and other conditions-see special notes column). The underlying mechanism of this syndrome is multifactorial.  It is primarily associated with alterations in resting energy expenditure (REE) and reduction in levels of hormonal anabolic mediators such as growth hormone, insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1), testosterone, and ghrelin. Additionally, there is an onset of acute phase response (APR), a series of changes in liver protein synthesis, where there is a reduction in albumin synthesis and an increase in the synthesis of acute phase proteins (APP) such as C-reactive protein (CRP), α1‐antitrypsin, fibrinogen, and α2‐macroglobulin resulting in reduced serum albumin. Increase in APP generation accelerates the decomposition of muscle proteins for utilization in gluconeogenesis in the liver, which further aggravates muscle wasting. Inflammatory mediators such as cytokines which include tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF alpha) and interleukins induce anorexia while increasing glucagon, cortisol, and catecholamines producing a catabolic, hypermetabolic state. Elevated IL-6 levels promote thermogenesis in brown adipose tissue by increasing the expression of the uncoupling protein 1 (UCP-1) and other genes associated with fatty acid oxidation. The loss of muscle and elevated thermogenesis leads to weight loss.
RESOURCES:
Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 35: Protein Degradation, Amino Acid Metabolism, and Nitrogen Balance

Baker Rogers J, Syed K, Minteer JF. Cachexia. [Updated 2023 Aug 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from:
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470208/
SPECIAL NOTES: Other chronic diseases such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), chronic heart failure (CHF), and chronic kidney disease (CKD) and chronic infectious and inflammatory diseases including AIDS could also be associated with cachexia.
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Cachexia is a serious condition where the body wastes away, causing significant weight loss, loss of appetite, weakness, and anemia. In people with cancer, this happens for several reasons. The body's levels of hormones that normally help build and maintain muscle — such as growth hormone, IGF-1, testosterone, and ghrelin — become too low. At the same time, the liver changes the types of proteins it makes, producing less of the proteins that nourish the body and more of the proteins involved in inflammation. To make these inflammation proteins, the body breaks down muscle for raw materials, causing muscle wasting. Inflammatory signals called cytokines reduce appetite while causing the body to release more stress hormones like glucagon, cortisol, and catecholamines, putting the body in a state where it is constantly breaking things down. Additionally, these inflammatory signals cause the body to generate more heat by burning fat, which uses up even more energy. All of these processes together cause the body to lose muscle and fat, leading to severe weight loss.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults and Pediatric

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Marasmus and Kwashiorkor are protein-energy malnutrition disorders. Marasmus occurs when there is an insufficient energy intake to match the body's requirements. As a result, the body draws on its own stores, resulting in emaciation and weight loss. In Kwashiorkor, there is an increased reliance on carbohydrate consumption, and reduced protein intake leads to muscle atrophy and breakdown as well as decreased synthesis of visceral proteins, which, in turn can lead to weight loss.
RESOURCES:
Lippincott Illustrated Reviews: Biochemistry, 9e. 2025 Ch. 27. Nutrition: Overview and Macronutrients.

Marks' Basic Medical Biochemistry, A Clinical Approach, 6e. 2023, Ch. 1:  Metabolic Fuels and Dietary Components.
SPECIAL NOTES: In Kwashiorkor,resulting hypoalbuminemia contributes to extravascular fluid accumulation, edema, and impaired synthesis of B-lipoprotein, producing a fatty liver. The resulting abdominal bloating could mask the weight loss (In this case arm/thigh circumference and other methods need to be used instead of BMI and weight to detect nutritional status impairment).
Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Marasmus and Kwashiorkor are both conditions caused by not getting enough nutrients from food. In Kwashiorkor, a person eats mostly carbohydrates but does not get enough protein in their diet. Because the body doesn't have enough protein coming in, it starts breaking down its own muscles for protein. The body also makes fewer important proteins that it normally produces, including proteins in the blood. This breakdown of muscle and reduced protein production leads to weight loss. (Note: Kwashiorkor can cause swelling in the belly due to low protein levels in the blood, which may hide the weight loss, making it important to use other methods to check nutritional status.)
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Pediatric

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: Marasmus and Kwashiorkor are both conditions caused by not getting enough nutrients from food. In Marasmus, a person does not take in nearly enough calories (energy from food) to meet what the body needs to function. Because there isn't enough energy coming in from food, the body is forced to use up its own stored energy — breaking down fat and muscle — to survive. Over time, this causes the body to become very thin and wasted, leading to severe weight loss.

MECHANISM EXPLANATION: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency can be caused by chronic pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis, and conditions leading to pancreatic duct obstruction, resection (e.g., pancreatic tumors), etc. It can also result in weight loss due to a deficiency in pancreatic lipase, an enzyme that facilitates fat absorption. Dietary malabsorption leads to weight loss.

Netter's Essential Biochemistry, 1e. 2018. Ch. 18: Carbohydrate Transport, Carbohydrate Malabsorption, and Lactose Intolerance; Ch. 34: Digestion of Dietary Protein and Net Synthesis of Protein in the Body

Mechanistic Explanation for Patients: The pancreas normally produces digestive enzymes that are released into the small intestine to help break down food, including fats. In exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, the pancreas does not produce enough of these enzymes — particularly an enzyme called lipase, which is needed to break down fat. This can happen due to conditions like chronic pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis, or a blockage or removal of part of the pancreas. Without enough lipase, the body cannot properly absorb fat and other nutrients from food. Because the body is not absorbing enough nutrients, it loses weight.
Patient Population (Adult or Pediatric): Adults and Pediatric
